# Generated research ideas

320 generated ideas across 5 seed papers.

## A Simple Rule-Based Part of Speech Tagger

*seed paper `a-simple-rule-based-part-of-speech-tagger-1992`, 12 sources, 64 generated ideas*

### Human idea (ground truth)

**Motivation.** Automatic part-of-speech tagging has become dominated by stochastic methods because their statistics can be acquired automatically with little handcrafted knowledge, whereas existing rule-based taggers require rules that are difficult to construct, brittle, and substantially less accurate. This has led to a belief that tagging is inherently better suited to probabilistic techniques. Yet stochastic taggers carry real costs: they store tens of thousands of lines of opaque statistical tables, rely on hand-tuned pre/postprocessing (e.g., idiom lists) that do not transfer across tag sets, genres, or languages, and offer little human insight into why a decision was made. It matters to determine whether a rule-based tagger that automatically learns its rules can match stochastic accuracy while being more compact, transparent, and portable.

**Method.** Build a rule-based tagger that automatically learns its rules through error-driven correction. First, apply a simple lexical tagger that assigns each word its most likely tag from a training corpus, ignoring context, with two non-contextual heuristics for unknown words: capitalized unseen words are treated as proper nouns, and unseen words inherit the most common tag for their three-letter suffix. Then acquire ordered correction rules ('patches') using a held-out patch corpus: tag the patch corpus, compile a list of error triples (mistagged tag a, correct tag b, count), and for each error consider a fixed set of patch templates that change tag a to tag b conditioned on nearby tags, capitalization, or positional context. For each candidate patch, compute net error reduction (errors corrected minus new errors introduced) on the patch corpus, greedily select the single best patch, apply it, and repeat until improvement stops. To tag new text, run the lexical tagger and then apply the learned patches in order, permitting a change to tag b only if the word was seen with tag b in training. Because a poor template simply produces no selected patches, the template set can be freely extended. Evaluate on held-out portions of the Brown Corpus and compare accuracy, storage requirements, and portability against stochastic taggers, optionally augmenting lexical coverage with a dictionary.

### LLM ideas

#### [method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 13 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across these works, the dominant paradigm for tagging unrestricted text is a hidden Markov model over word "ambiguity classes" (equivalence classes of possible tags), trained by Baum-Welch on untagged text with only a lexicon and suffix-based guessing for unknown words (Church 1988; Kupiec 1989, 1992). Its great virtue is portability: no pre-tagged corpus, few parameters, vocabulary independence, and ~96% accuracy. Its central limitation is context depth. A uniform first-order model cannot capture many real constraints (determiner–noun–X sequencing, "to" as infinitive vs. preposition, subject–verb number agreement across a prepositional phrase), while a uniform higher-order model explodes to n^k parameters and cannot be reliably estimated (Kupiec 1989; Jelinek 1985 makes exactly this point about partitioning the past).

Kupiec's 1989 augmented HMM shows the right answer is *selective* higher-order conditioning: keep the first-order backbone and graft in extra "state chains" only where deeper context matters, with state tying to share sparse data. This yields accuracy gains without parameter blow-up. But the network topology — which ambiguity classes to split, and on which preceding contexts — is produced by hand, from manual error analysis plus linguistic expertise, and is re-derived for each language, genre, and tagset. This manual bottleneck is precisely what makes the otherwise portable, resource-light tagger un-portable in its most powerful configuration. Jelinek (1985) frames the underlying open problem as choosing "appropriately chosen equivalence classes" of the past and explicitly calls for an *automatic* mechanism instead of hand-tailoring; the rule-acquisition work (1989) likewise argues that disambiguation knowledge should be induced from text rather than hand-written. No source provides an automatic, unlabeled procedure to decide where to grow selective higher-order structure in the tagger itself. That gap is the opportunity.

**Method.** Build STAGE (Selective Topology Auto-Growth Engine), a tagger-construction system/toolkit whose central contribution is an artifact that automatically induces a variable-order, context-selective HMM topology — the hand-crafted "state chains" of Kupiec 1989 — from only a lexicon and untagged text.

Components:
1. Baseline builder. Reduce the lexicon to ambiguity classes (as in Kupiec 1992), assign unknown words ambiguity classes via a suffix trie, and train a first-order ambiguity-class HMM with Baum-Welch on raw text. This is the portable backbone every subsequent stage refines.

2. Unsupervised diagnostic module. This is the key novelty: a label-free criterion for *where* deeper context pays off. Run forward-backward to obtain expected state-occupancy and expected transition counts. For each (ambiguity class C at position i, current context state) that is genuinely ambiguous and frequent, estimate the information gain of conditioning the state distribution on one additional layer of preceding context: the expected reduction in conditional entropy of the tag/state given the extended history versus the base history, weighted by the context's expected frequency. High gain means the language actually distinguishes those histories (Kupiec's observation that augmentation only helps when usage patterns are truly distinct), and it is computable entirely from model expected counts — no gold labels.

3. Structure-growth module. Greedily instantiate state chains for the top-ranked context expansions that exceed a minimum expected-count threshold (guaranteeing reliable estimation), automating exactly the hand construction Kupiec drew by hand: clone the target state, wire the augmented sub-network into and out of the first-order backbone, and apply automatic state tying so cloned emission distributions pool data. Candidate expansions include longer left-context chains and simple skip/phrase templates (optional determiner/adjective + noun) to reach across intervening phrases, addressing the agreement-across-PP cases.

4. Iterate train→diagnose→grow under a global parameter budget until held-out likelihood and expected ambiguity (posterior entropy over the untagged development set) plateau, so growth stops automatically and stays parsimonious.

5. Outputs. A compiled tagger plus a human-readable report of every induced expansion, i.e., the linguistic contexts the system discovered on its own (a byproduct connecting to the rule-acquisition line of work).

How it addresses the gap: it replaces manual error-analysis-plus-linguistic-knowledge network design with an automatic, unsupervised, data-driven procedure, operationalizing Jelinek's call to automatically partition the past into equivalence classes; it keeps the selective (not uniform) higher-order structure that gives accuracy without parameter explosion; and because structure is re-derived from data it transfers to new languages, genres, and tagsets with no hand engineering, restoring the portability the manually augmented model sacrificed.

Evaluation: on a held-out tagged test set, compare STAGE against (a) the uniform first-order ambiguity-class HMM, (b) a uniform second-order HMM with smoothing, and (c) a Kupiec-style hand-augmented network, measuring overall accuracy, accuracy on ambiguous and on unknown words, parameter count, and how closely the auto-induced expansions match the published hand-designed state chains (e.g., determiner–noun–X, the "to" infinitive case). Feasibility rests entirely on machinery present in the sources: ambiguity classes, Baum-Welch/forward-backward, suffix guessing, state tying, and selective state chains.

#### [unsteered (blank)] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** HMM POS taggers train from a lexicon plus unlabeled text but use a fixed low order context. Kupiec adds selective higher order state chains chosen by hand from linguistic knowledge and error analysis, and Cutting stays first order with manual hints. No prior work automatically decides from unlabeled text which higher order contexts to model, which limits portability across domains and languages.

**Method.** Induce selective higher order state chains for an unlabeled text HMM tagger. From a Baum Welch first order model with category states and ambiguity class observations, propose state splits that add one more category of history, allow splits to compound into chains, and add a closed class skip template for cross phrase agreement. Score each split by soft count occupancy times the KL divergence between the refined and pooled transition distributions. Accept splits above a reliability threshold, retrain with emission tying, and iterate until the likelihood gain is small. Evaluate by held out perplexity versus first order, uniform second order, and Kupiec hand built networks, by Brown Corpus accuracy and accuracy versus parameter count, and by portability to a new domain and a second language.

#### [method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 26 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works tackle part-of-speech disambiguation with different paradigms: rule and context-frame coding without a dictionary (1963, 1971), n-gram and HMM statistical models (1985, 1988, 1992), unsupervised HMMs with word equivalence classes and phrase-dependent state chains (1989, 1992), and automatic rule acquisition (1989). Each reports a single headline accuracy on different corpora, tagsets, and lexicons with different definitions of an error, so the numbers are non-comparable and hide where and why systems fail. Church claims most errors come from lexicon defects and few from the trigram grammar; Cutting et al argue a tagger must be tunable to fix systematic errors, yet neither claim rests on a controlled cross-paradigm error analysis. The gap: no shared diagnostic testbed neutralizes tagset, corpus, and lexicon confounds, decomposes accuracy into lexicon-coverage versus contextual-model versus annotation-boundary contributions, and maps which ambiguity types are hard and whether that hardness is universal or paradigm-specific.

**Method.** Build an empirical map of grammatical-category disambiguation: a controlled comparative benchmark plus an error taxonomy. Assemble a multi-genre evaluation set (scientific prose, newswire, balanced Brown-style text) hand-adjudicated against one reference tagset, with a tagset-mapping hierarchy collapsing it onto each historical inventory so systems are scored at multiple commensurable granularities. Define a four-way error taxonomy: lexicon-gap, genuine contextual-ambiguity, tagset-boundary or annotation-inconsistency, and context-scope. Apply one representative system per paradigm (context-frame rule coder, supervised trigram optimizer, unsupervised HMM with ambiguity classes, phrase-dependent state-chain HMM, rule-acquisition tagger) all sharing one common lexicon and training corpus, holding lexicon coverage constant so the contextual model is the only variable, directly testing Church's lexicon claim. Ablate axes never compared head-to-head: Markov order, tagset granularity, degree of equivalence-class collapsing, training-set size, and cross-genre transfer. Deliver a per-ambiguity-type accuracy matrix crossed with systems and settings, an accuracy budget attributing each system's errors to the four bins, and a curated challenge set of hard contexts where paradigms diverge. Analyses quantify how much of the historical accuracy spread is real versus artifact, whether hard ambiguity types are universal or paradigm-specific, and whether higher-order conditioning and equivalence-class collapsing help on hard cases or only on aggregate.

#### [method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical part-of-speech taggers in the provided literature converge on hidden Markov models trained with or without tagged text (Church 1988; Cutting et al. 1992; Jelinek's Markov-source and phoneme-to-text modeling; Kupiec 1989). All of them face the same core tension around CONTEXT ORDER. A uniform second- or third-order model captures more constraint but needs n^3 transition parameters and, because contextual frequencies follow Zipf's law (Church 1988), most of those contexts are never reliably estimable, so blunt smoothing (add-one in Church; deleted interpolation in Cutting) is required. Kupiec (1989) makes the pivotal observation that most higher-order contexts are useless (e.g. determiner-determiner-determiner) while a few matter enormously (adjective-or-noun after a determiner; subject/verb number agreement across a prepositional phrase), and shows that SELECTIVELY deepening only those contexts via hand-built "state chains" fixes systematic errors without parameter explosion. But the decision of WHICH histories to refine is made entirely by human linguistic insight plus post-hoc error analysis; likewise Cutting's heuristic of giving the 100 most frequent words their own emission classes while pooling the rest into ambiguity classes is asserted, not derived. There is no formal criterion that says which context refinements are worth their parameters, how much error they can remove, or when to stop — and no such criterion in the unsupervised Baum-Welch setting these authors prize (no pre-tagged corpus). This gap matters because the whole appeal of selective augmentation (accuracy of high order at the cost of low order) hinges on choosing the right contexts, and manual design does not scale across genres, tagsets, or languages. The sources therefore leave open a principled, derivable account of selective context refinement.

**Method.** Provide a formal derivation that turns Kupiec's hand-built "augmented networks" and Cutting's frequent-word heuristic into a single, data-driven refinement objective with provable guarantees. (1) Formal model: represent an HMM tagger as a partition of the space of category histories into a finite set of "context states," i.e. a context tree over past categories/words; the basic first-order model is the root partition, uniform k-th order is the fully expanded tree, and a state chain is an intermediate refinement of a single leaf. Emission is defined analogously as a partition of the vocabulary into equivalence (ambiguity) classes, with lexicalization = splitting a class into an individual word. Both transition and emission structure become instances of one operator: SPLIT a state h into refined children {h·a}. (2) Objective and theorem: derive that the expected reduction in per-token cross-entropy (log-loss) from splitting context h equals the context-probability-weighted expected KL divergence between the refined and pooled predictive distributions, which sums to the conditional mutual information G(h)=P(h)·I(C_i ; refinement | h). Then, via Pinsker's and Fano's inequalities, prove an upper bound showing that the achievable reduction in Bayes (tagging) error is monotone in G(h): refining the highest-mutual-information contexts most tightens the error bound, giving formal justification for Kupiec's observation that only a few contexts help. (3) MDL trade-off and stopping rule: charge each split its parameter cost using a two-part / description-length penalty, so a split is accepted iff N·G(h) exceeds (½·log N)·(#added free parameters). This yields a derived, automatic growth of the augmented network and a principled analogue of Cutting's top-100 rule (lexicalize a word exactly when its emission carries mutual information about its tag beyond its ambiguity class, above the MDL threshold). (4) Unsupervised instantiation: because a tagged corpus is unavailable, replace true counts by the current model's Baum-Welch expected counts, so G(h) and the MDL test are computed under the model's own posterior; interleave greedy splitting with re-estimation, giving an EM-with-structure-search procedure that starts from the basic first-order model and grows only the state chains the data support. (5) Deliverables: a taxonomy of refinement operators (deepen category history, cross-phrase agreement chains, lexicalize emission class) all scored by the same expected-KL/MI objective; the cross-entropy = mutual-information identity; the error-bound theorem; and the MDL stopping criterion. The approach is testable on the Brown corpus by checking that the automatically grown network recovers Kupiec's hand-designed chains (determiner-adjective/noun, subject-verb-across-PP) and matches or exceeds uniform second-order accuracy with far fewer parameters, thereby deriving from first principles what prior work supplied by hand.

#### [method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** HMM part-of-speech taggers reach high accuracy from only a lexicon and unlabeled text (Cutting et al. 1992; Kupiec 1989; Jelinek 1985), their appeal being minimal resources and language independence. But residual errors are systematic and are fixed by MANUAL engineering: Kupiec adds hand-designed higher-order and phrase-spanning state chains from error analysis and linguistic knowledge and hand-splits the top words into their own equivalence classes; Cutting et al. add hand-specified biases. This human loop is labor-intensive, corpus- and language-specific, and needs expertise, while uniform higher-order models explode parameters. No source automates choosing which selective higher-order contexts to add, which words to promote out of their ambiguity class, or how to trade accuracy against parameter cost. Hindle (1989) automates rule learning but needs a hand-built parser and tagged text. The gap: an automatic, data-driven search for tagger structure that keeps the low-resource, language-independent character.

**Method.** Cast tagger construction as a guided search over HMM topologies. Start from the basic first-order ambiguity-class HMM trained by Baum-Welch on unlabeled text. Objective: minimize description length (negative held-out log-likelihood plus a parameter-count penalty), optionally plus accuracy on a tiny dev set, enforcing a parameter budget. Operators: (i) split a state into a selective higher-order state chain conditioned on previous k tags; (ii) promote a frequent word into its own ambiguity class; (iii) add phrase-spanning chains from frequent tag n-grams; all with state tying. Adaptive screening: rank candidate contexts by forward-backward posterior tag entropy and Viterbi margin, and words by deviation from their class average, so only high-uncertainty, frequent contexts are tried (automating error analysis). Search: greedy beam hill-climbing; per candidate, warm-start Baum-Welch and run a few localized re-estimation iterations, score on held-out text, keep best B, stop when no operator beats the penalty. Efficiency: warm-start plus localized updates and emission-matrix sparsity make evaluations cheap; MDL controls stopping. Evaluate against the first-order baseline and Kupiec's hand-augmented network on accuracy and parameter count on Brown text, and rerun on another domain/language to show manual engineering is eliminated.

#### [method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Every tagger in these sources, both rule-based (Greene and Rubin 1971; Klein and Simmons 1963) and statistical HMM taggers (Church 1988; DeRose 1988; Garside and Leech 1987; Jelinek 1985; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992), rests on two strong assumptions. First, a reliable closed lexicon: even the unsupervised HMM taggers still require a hand-built dictionary listing each word's permissible tags, its ambiguity class. This is brittle, because if a word's true tag is omitted, Viterbi can never recover it, ambiguity classes are frozen, and unknown words fall into a crude default open-class set from suffixes alone. No component repairs the lexicon from data, yet for new languages, domains, or tagsets such a dictionary may be absent or incomplete. Second, clean observed tokens: all taggers assume the token stream is the intended text, but OCR, ASR, and corrupted corpora are noisy. The only source addressing noisy input, Derouault and Merialdo's phoneme-to-text noisy-channel Markov source, targets word recovery rather than tagging and never touches lexicon uncertainty. The gap is that no tagger works when the lexicon is incomplete and the input is uncertain. Since tagging is the standard first step for phrase recognition, sense disambiguation, and parsing, a tagger that degrades gracefully under weak resources and noise would broaden where these methods apply.

**Method.** We relax the two core assumptions of the ambiguity-class HMM tagger while keeping its efficient forward-backward and Viterbi machinery. First, replace the hard word-to-ambiguity-class map with a feature-conditioned emission of word given tag that is nonzero for every tag but shaped by suffixes, prefixes, capitalization, and distributional context. Words are grouped into learned equivalence classes by feature signature, generalizing Kupiec's dictionary-derived classes and preserving parameter tying and vocabulary independence without a dictionary. Any available partial dictionary is used only as a prior, giving listed tags high initial mass and unlisted tags a small escape mass governed by a learned per-word confidence, so missing or wrong entries remain recoverable. Second, following Derouault and Merialdo, insert a noisy channel of observed token given true word between word emissions and surface tokens to model OCR, phoneme, and spelling corruption. The composed model runs from tag to word to token, and one Viterbi pass over the composed lattice jointly recovers the tag and word sequences, unifying transcription and tagging. Third, estimate all parameters with Baum-Welch on unlabeled and possibly noisy text, needing no tagged corpus, using deleted-interpolation smoothing and selective higher-order state chains only where error analysis shows cross-phrase dependencies. After each EM sweep the method updates each word's posterior tag-set and confidence, bootstrapping and completing the lexicon from data so the dictionary becomes an output rather than a requirement. Finally, we evaluate graceful degradation against the standard closed-lexicon ambiguity-class HMM while varying lexicon completeness and input noise, reporting tagging accuracy and joint word-plus-tag recovery as functions of missing-lexicon and noise rates.

#### [method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The prior works tag with HMMs trained on unlabeled text plus a lexicon (Jelinek 1985; Church 1988; Kupiec 1989/1992), reaching about 96% accuracy but with three unfixed brittleness sources. Hard lexicon constraints: a word's ambiguity class makes any missing correct tag unreachable at probability zero, a silent systematic failure that worsens across genres, and out-of-vocabulary words get a crude open-class default. Fragile training: Baum-Welch needs hand-tuned a-priori hints and biased starts to avoid bad local optima. No uncertainty: one best tag per token, so downstream stages propagate near-tie errors silently. No source quantifies confidence, lets a correct-but-unlisted tag win, or self-repairs its lexicon. This leaves room for a robustified tagger that degrades gracefully under noisy or incomplete lexicons, trains without hints, and reports calibrated uncertainty.

**Method.** A robustified unsupervised HMM tagger reusing ambiguity classes, forward-backward, Viterbi, deleted-interpolation smoothing, and suffix models. First, soft ambiguity classes: replace the hard zero-emission probability for unlisted tags with a small suffix-derived floor, so the lexicon is a prior not a gate and an omitted correct tag can still win on strong context. Second, lexicon self-repair: during each Baum-Welch pass accumulate posterior tag mass per word type, then add unlisted tags with high mass and flag listed tags with negligible mass, re-estimate, and log edits for domain adaptation. Third, hint-free initialization: run several perturbed restarts, combine by posterior averaging, and use cross-restart disagreement as an instability signal instead of manual biasing. Fourth, calibrated confidence and abstention: score each token by chosen-tag posterior marginal, top-1 versus top-2 Viterbi margin, and restart agreement, calibrate on a small labeled sample, and let downstream stages abstain on low-confidence tokens. Fifth, evaluation on Brown, encyclopedia, and journalistic text: stress-test with progressively corrupted or incomplete lexicons versus the hard-constraint baseline, report repair precision and recall, calibration, and downstream phrase-recognition error with and without abstention. Success is flatter degradation under lexicon noise and genre shift, high-precision repair, and well-calibrated confidence, with no hand-tuned hints.

#### [method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 19 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical HMM taggers (Church 1988, DeRose 1988, Jelinek 1985, Derouault and Merialdo 1986, Cutting 1992) train from unlabeled text but use fixed local context and are opaque; symbolic rule taggers (Klein and Simmons 1963, Greene and Rubin 1971, Hindle 1989) capture interpretable long-distance rules but need tagged corpora and a parser. Kupiec 1989 hand-designs phrase-dependent state chains for an unsupervised HMM; Derouault and Merialdo glue a Markov source and grammar as two modules. Gap: no framework automatically discovers which contexts need rule-like phrase-level conditioning, folds them into one statistical model, and still trains without a pre-tagged corpus. This matters because tagging feeds phrase recognition, sense disambiguation, and parsing, forcing a choice between hand-built rules and shallow statistics.

**Method.** Error-Driven Induction of Rule-Augmented HMMs unifies symbolic disambiguation rules and HMMs by treating each induced rule as an automatically compiled state-chain augmentation, trained from mostly unlabeled text, bridging unsupervised HMM estimation, Kupiec state chains, and Hindle rule acquisition. Steps: one, baseline first-order HMM with Kupiec word-equivalence classes trained by Baum-Welch on unlabeled text plus a small hand-tagged dev set. Two, diagnosis: run Viterbi, compute per-token posterior marginals, flag high-entropy decision points, and use the dev set to tabulate systematic confusions such as to-infinitive versus preposition, turning model uncertainty into an automatic error signal without a parser. Three, rule induction: for each confusion search symbolic context templates over surrounding categories and equivalence classes, including non-adjacent phrase-level positions, for the one best separating confused cases, i.e. Hindle acquisition grounded in HMM error statistics. Four, compilation: compile each accepted rule into the model as an added state chain with edited transitions per Kupiec, giving selective higher-order conditioning only where it fires, so parameters grow only where needed and decoding stays one Viterbi pass. Five, re-estimate with Baum-Welch, re-diagnose, iterate greedily, accepting an augmentation only if it improves held-out likelihood and dev accuracy. Evaluate on Brown-style corpora against plain first-order HMM, uniform higher-order HMM, Kupiec hand-augmented model, and Hindle rules, measuring accuracy, parameter count, and interpretability, with ablations on unsupervised versus dev-guided diagnosis and adjacent versus phrase-level templates. This automates Kupiec manual augmentation, frees Hindle acquisition from needing a parser and full tagging, and replaces the Derouault-Merialdo two-module glue with a single model where rules and statistics are the same object.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 14 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two research streams in the provided corpus independently converged on the same core device — "equivalence classes" to defeat data sparsity — yet never connected into a single working system.

Stream A (statistical POS tagging): the 1963 computational grammar coder, Church (1988), the phrase-dependent HMM (Kupiec 1989), and the practical HMM tagger (Cutting/Kupiec 1992) show that (i) parts of speech can be assigned to unrestricted text with >95% accuracy, (ii) training needs only a lexicon plus *untagged* text (Baum-Welch), (iii) words can be collapsed into a small, vocabulary-independent set of ambiguity/equivalence classes (129 classes cover a 30k-word dictionary), and (iv) open-vocabulary coverage is obtainable from morphology/suffix tries — no dictionary lookup required. These taggers are, however, evaluated only on tagging accuracy; their output is never fed back into anything.

Stream B (Markov language modeling for recognition): the Markov source modeling paper (1985) explicitly speculates that "grammar could be used not just to assign parts of speech to words, but to place a past word string into a grammatical equivalence class," and that such f(w_k | φ(history)) counts could be interpolated with word trigrams — but it had no automatic tagger to supply φ. The phoneme-to-text transcription work (1986) calls for "combining the advantages of statistical modeling with those of a grammatical approach," yet its grammatical knowledge lives in a hand-built 250k-form dictionary with 92 POS labels and its statistics are essentially word n-grams over known-boundary words; out-of-vocabulary and sparse-context prediction remain weak.

The bridge that no source builds: the unsupervised, open-vocabulary HMM tagger from Stream A produces exactly the automatic grammatical equivalence classes and P(word|tag)/P(tag|context) distributions that Stream B wanted for smoothing sparse word predictions and for scoring word lattices — and the acquired symbolic disambiguation rules of Hindle (1989) could add interpretable pruning where statistics are weakest. Because sparse-data language modeling was the recognized bottleneck for large-vocabulary transcription, uniting these disconnected communities into one artifact is both natural and consequential.

**Method.** Build GLM-Decode, an integrated open-vocabulary, class-based language-modeling and decoding system that operationalizes the bridge between statistical POS tagging and Markov recognition-time language modeling. The artifact has five composable modules, all trainable from a lexicon plus untagged text (no hand-tagged corpus).

1. Morphology-driven ambiguity-class builder. Implement a suffix/affix trie (uniting the 1963 coder's suffix tests with Kupiec's suffix-based guessing) that maps any token — including OOV words and, in the transcription setting, competing dictionary entries for a phoneme string — to a small ambiguity/equivalence class. This yields the vocabulary-independent class inventory (~100–200 classes) and guarantees open-vocabulary coverage.

2. Unsupervised HMM tagger core. Train an HMM over these equivalence classes with Baum-Welch on untagged text, using Kupiec-style selective higher-order "state chains" for phrase-dependent conditioning. Output: P(word|tag) emission distributions and P(tag | tag-history) transition distributions, plus per-token tag posteriors.

3. Factored class-based language model. Construct the predictor P(w_i | history) = λ·P_trigram(w_i | w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}) + (1−λ)·Σ_t P(w_i | t)·P(t | tag-history), i.e., the grammatical-equivalence-class model that Markov source modeling (1985) proposed but never instantiated. Estimate λ by deleted interpolation. The class term supplies non-zero, linguistically informed probabilities precisely for the rare/unseen word contexts and OOV items where word n-grams collapse.

4. Decoder integration. Plug the factored LM into a lattice/A* search over the phoneme-to-text hypothesis graph (the confluent-node word lattice of the 1986 stenotypy task, and analogous English pseudophonetic input). Each path is scored by combining the pseudophonetic/acoustic cost with the factored class LM; the tagger runs jointly so that word-boundary and category ambiguity are resolved together rather than in separate stages.

5. Optional self-supervised symbolic layer. Following Hindle (1989), induce deterministic disambiguation rules from the tagger's own high-confidence output and use them to prune lattice arcs where the statistical model is near-uniform, giving an interpretable hybrid and reducing search.

Evaluation deliberately spans both communities the artifact bridges: (a) tagging accuracy on standard text (comparable to Church/Cutting), and (b) transcription word-error-rate and LM perplexity on the French stenotypy pseudophonetic data and matched English corpora, with ablations isolating the contribution of the class term versus pure word trigrams, of open-vocabulary suffix classes on OOV rates, and of the symbolic pruning layer. The central test is whether automatically induced grammatical equivalence classes — free, from untagged text — reduce transcription error and sparse-context perplexity, validating the long-conjectured but never-built link between unsupervised POS tagging and recognition-time language modeling.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity] — claude-opus-4-8, 5 turns / 13 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bridge opportunity between two parallel, largely disconnected sub-communities of statistical NLP that grew up around part-of-speech disambiguation.

Stream 1 — stochastic/HMM taggers (Church 1988; Cutting, Kupiec, Pedersen & Sibun 1992; Kupiec 1989 augmentation; and the source-modeling lineage of Markov-source-modeling-of-text-generation 1985 and natural-language-modeling-for-phoneme-to-text-transcription 1986). These are attractive because they train with essentially no manual annotation (only a lexicon plus raw text via Baum-Welch), use equivalence classes for vocabulary independence, and reach ~96% accuracy. But they are opaque: the model yields no human-readable linguistic account of its decisions, and its known weakness — the first-order Markov assumption — is repaired only by hand. Tellingly, Kupiec (1989) states that his higher-order "state chain" augmentations were designed manually "based on linguistic considerations and also upon an analysis of tagging errors made by the basic network." That manual error-analysis-and-linguistic-hand-editing loop is the real bottleneck.

Stream 2 — symbolic rule acquisition (Hindle 1989; and the older hand-coded tradition of Klein & Simmons 1963 and TAGGIT/Greene & Rubin 1971). Hindle automatically acquires interpretable, contextual disambiguation rules that halve the error of hand-written rules and rival probabilistic taggers. But his procedure requires two expensive prerequisites the stochastic stream has already learned to do without: (a) pre-tagged text, and (b) a hand-built deterministic parser whose encoded grammar the rules exploit.

The gap: neither stream uses the other. The statistical side has solved annotation-free training but cannot explain or self-diagnose itself; the symbolic side produces exactly the interpretable, localizable knowledge the statistical side needs to fix its Markov limitation, yet still depends on manual tags and a parser. Nobody has closed the loop. This matters because a method that is simultaneously annotation-free AND interpretable AND self-improving would give both accuracy and a linguistic explanation, and would automate Kupiec's hand-tuning — the practical barrier to scaling these models to new tagsets, corpora, and languages (the "reusable" desideratum of Cutting et al. 1992).

**Method.** Propose a self-supervised, closed-loop tagger that bridges unsupervised HMM estimation with symbolic disambiguation-rule acquisition, so each side supplies what the other lacks.

Stage A — Annotation-free statistical base. Train a baseline HMM tagger exactly in the Cutting/Kupiec style: lexicon + raw text, Baum-Welch estimation, equivalence classes (e.g., noun-or-verb) to keep parameters vocabulary-independent. Run forward-backward to obtain, for every token, not just the Viterbi tag but the posterior probability over its candidate categories.

Stage B — Confidence-gated pseudo-labels replace Hindle's prerequisites. Treat high-posterior tokens (near-1.0 margin) as reliable "labeled" data. This substitutes the HMM's confident output for Hindle's two missing prerequisites: it supplies tagged text without human annotation, and it supplies category-in-context judgements without a hand-built parser.

Stage C — Interpretable rule induction. From the confidence-gated corpus, induce symbolic contextual disambiguation rules in Hindle's template style (conditions over surrounding categories/equivalence classes and lexical cues within a small window), but score each candidate rule by the HMM posteriors of the tokens it covers rather than by a parser's grammar. Keep only rules whose statistical support and coverage exceed thresholds. Output: a compact, human-readable rule set that explains the tagger's choices.

Stage D — The diagnostic bridge (the novel core). Compare the two views token-by-token to find systematic conflicts: (i) contexts where a well-supported symbolic rule confidently disagrees with the HMM's Viterbi choice, and (ii) contexts where the HMM posterior is a near-tie yet a symbolic rule is decisive. Because such conflicts concentrate exactly where local first-order context is insufficient, they automatically localize the failures Kupiec found by hand (e.g., adjective-or-noun after a determiner, "to" as preposition vs. infinitive marker).

Stage E — Automatic structural augmentation and iteration. For each recurring conflict context, synthesize a higher-order "state chain" (selective conditioning) targeting just that context — automating Kupiec's manual state-chain editing — re-estimate the enlarged HMM with Baum-Welch (using state tying to control parameters), and loop back to Stage A. Iterate until the rule set and the HMM stop disagreeing.

Evaluation. Use the Brown corpus (Francis & Kucera 1982) and its tagset, with no manual tags fed to training. Report: (1) tagging accuracy versus the plain unsupervised HMM (test whether the auto-synthesized augmentations recover Kupiec's hand-built gains), (2) the acquired rule set's error rate and readability versus Hindle's parser-and-tag-dependent rules, and (3) an ablation showing that confidence gating and the conflict-driven augmentation each contribute. Deliverables are a single artifact that is annotation-free, self-diagnosing, self-augmenting, and accompanied by an explicit linguistic rule set — uniting the stochastic and symbolic traditions that the prior works pursued separately.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the provided works, three research communities independently attacked the same core problem — resolving the grammatical category (part of speech) of words in running text — yet they developed in near-isolation and were never placed on common ground.

(1) A linguistic/rule community built symbolic disambiguators grounded in morphology and hand or acquired rules: Klein & Simmons' 1963 computational grammar coder that classifies words by suffix "batteries" and small exception lists without a large dictionary; Greene & Rubin's automated tagging; and Hindle's 1989 acquisition of disambiguation rules from a deterministic parser (Fidditch).

(2) A statistical-NLP tagging community treated the task as decoding a hidden Markov model: Church (1988), DeRose (1988), Kupiec (1989) and Cutting et al. (1992), the last reporting >96% accuracy from only a lexicon and unlabeled text, using ambiguity/equivalence classes and suffix guessing for unknown words.

(3) A speech / information-theory community used the very same Markov-source machinery for a different downstream goal: Jelinek's 1985 Markov source modeling annotates POS as a by-product of a 40-tag language model built by Forward–Backward for continuous speech recognition, and the phoneme-to-text transcription work fuses statistical Markov decoding with an explicit grammar to pick transcriptions.

These literatures share almost identical mathematics (n-gram/HMM scoring, Viterbi, equivalence classes) and even cite common corpora (Francis & Kučera's Brown Corpus), but they are disconnected in three consequential ways. First, they are evaluated on incompatible tagsets (Brown's fine tags vs. Fidditch's collapsed PRO; ad hoc speech alphabets) and disjoint texts, so their headline numbers (90% on scientific text, <2%/5% training error, "96%") are not comparable and no one knows which paradigm actually wins where. Second, they make radically different resource assumptions (hand-tagged corpus vs. lexicon-only vs. parser vs. morphology rules), yet no study controls for supervision, lexicon coverage, or morphological knowledge to isolate what each ingredient buys. Third, the "tagging as a tool" and "tagging as a by-product of a language model for transcription" framings have never been cross-evaluated, even though they are the same noisy-channel model. This bridge gap matters because the paradigms appear complementary — morphology-based rules should excel exactly on the low-frequency/unknown words where HMM lexicon statistics are weakest (the Zipf tail Church highlights), and parser-derived rules encode long-distance agreement that fixed HMM windows miss (the very errors the phoneme-to-text grammar was added to fix). Without a common empirical map, the field cannot see these complementarities or justify principled hybrids.

**Method.** We propose an empirical mapping study: a unified diagnostic benchmark and comparative analysis that places the rule/morphology, statistical-HMM, and speech-oriented Markov paradigms on identical footing, then charts where each succeeds, fails, and complements the others.

1. Common substrate. Anchor everything on the Tagged Brown Corpus (Francis & Kučera). Define a canonical tagset plus an explicit, documented many-to-one mapping to each paradigm's native tagset (fine Brown tags; Fidditch-style collapsed tags; the 40-tag speech alphabet), so all systems are scored under one normalized label space via the mapping, and separately under their own granularity.

2. Re-implemented, normalized systems. Build faithful, minimal reimplementations of one representative per paradigm, sharing I/O and lexicon interfaces: (a) a morphology/rule coder in the Klein–Simmons style (suffix tests + small exception list, no large dictionary); (b) an HMM tagger with ambiguity/equivalence classes and suffix-based unknown-word guessing (Cutting/Kupiec/Church); (c) a Jelinek-style Markov-source model trained by Forward–Backward that emits POS as a Viterbi by-product; and (d) a parser-rule disambiguator approximated by Hindle-style acquired context patterns. Each is trained/tuned only with resources native to its paradigm, all recorded.

3. Diagnostics (the map). Rather than a single accuracy number, produce a multi-axis empirical map: (i) per-category accuracy and full confusion matrices; (ii) stratification by word-frequency band and known-vs-OOV status, to test the hypothesis that morphology dominates in the Zipf tail while HMM statistics dominate on frequent words; (iii) genre robustness across Brown's sub-corpora; (iv) resource curves that sweep amount of labeled data, unlabeled data, lexicon coverage, and presence/absence of morphology rules, isolating each ingredient's marginal contribution; (v) context-window sensitivity, contrasting fixed HMM windows with parser/state-chain long-distance dependencies on hand-selected agreement cases; and (vi) inter-system agreement/complementarity analysis (pairwise disagreement, oracle-combination ceiling) to quantify how much each paradigm could gain from the others.

4. Cross-community bridge task. Reconnect tagging with the transcription line by evaluating every model both as a tagger and as the language model in a controlled noisy-channel decoder over deliberately ambiguated input (e.g., devoweled or homophone-confused text mimicking the phoneme-to-text/stenotypy setting). We measure whether design choices that help tagging accuracy also reduce transcription word-error, and vice versa, mapping transfer between the two objectives that the sources treated separately.

5. Deliverables. (i) A reusable, open evaluation harness with the tagset-mapping layer, resource-controlled protocols, and OOV/frequency stratification; (ii) an empirical map of the design space (linguistic features vs. statistics vs. supervision vs. context length) with confusion and complementarity findings; and (iii) a data-driven specification for a hybrid — e.g., morphology-first backoff for the OOV tail feeding HMM decoding, with parser-derived long-distance corrections — whose predicted gains are read directly off the oracle-combination ceiling.

This addresses the bridge gap by making the three literatures directly comparable for the first time, attributing performance to specific mechanisms rather than to incomparable corpora/tagsets, and empirically demonstrating (or refuting) their complementarity across both the tagging and transcription tasks.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 24 turns / 33 tool calls

**Motivation.** Symbolic dictionary-free morphological taggers such as Klein and Simmons 1963 and the probabilistic HMM taggers such as Kupiec 1989 and 1992 are disconnected literatures, and no source unifies morphological affix tests with probabilistic emission and automatic context selection.

**Method.** Derive a factored HMM emission in which morphological affix tests become per-category feature factors, prove it is the maximum-entropy conditional-independence emission, add an information-theoretic feature and state-chain selection rule with a monotonicity theorem, and prove zero-temperature decoding recovers the deterministic Klein and Simmons tagger.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 21 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two streams here tackle part-of-speech ambiguity but never connect on how model structure is built. Statistical HMM taggers (Kupiec, Church, DeRose, Jelinek) train automatically on untagged text but face parameter proliferation; Kupiec's state chains add selective higher-order conditioning only where needed, yet their structure is hand-crafted based on analysis of errors and linguistic knowledge. Separately, Hindle automatically acquires disambiguation rules by scanning tagged text and creating a new rule wherever an error occurs. The bridge opportunity: Kupiec's manual error-analysis-driven structure design is conceptually identical to Hindle's automatic error-driven rule acquisition, yet the two are disconnected. Automating HMM structure discovery would remove the human from the loop, make taggers portable across domains and languages, and allocate parameters only where data demand, crucial because hand design does not scale to noisy new domains like Kupiec's Lisp-email corpus.

**Method.** Automate discovery of an HMM tagger's selective higher-order state chains via error-driven structure search. Start from a base first-order equivalence-class HMM trained with Baum-Welch on untagged text, with a small dev set for scoring. Define one operator: a state-chain split cloning a state into context-conditioned copies. Generate candidates Hindle-style: diagnose the states and contexts causing the most residual errors, or highest-entropy Viterbi decisions and highest-perplexity contexts when tags are absent, and only split those, keeping the search small. Run greedy or beam hill-climbing under an MDL objective, held-out likelihood minus a parameter-count penalty, re-estimating each candidate with warm-started Baum-Welch and stopping when no split helps. Efficiency comes from diagnostic screening, sparse re-estimation of only new states, and the MDL penalty as an explicit parameter budget. Evaluate versus the plain HMM and Kupiec's hand-built chains on accuracy, parameter count, and cross-domain portability, checking whether interpretable augmentations such as the to-infinitive split emerge automatically.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bridge opportunity across three streams never joined. The probabilistic HMM tagging line (Church 1988; Kupiec 1989; Cutting 1992) reaches about 96 percent but assumes a reliable lexicon listing each word's possible tags; unknown words are patched by a suffix heuristic and a default open-class ambiguity class. The lexicon-free morphological-coding line (Klein and Simmons 1963) predicts word class at about 90 percent with no dictionary using affix tests plus context-frame rules, but is hand-built and non-probabilistic. The speech language-modeling line (Markov source modeling 1985; phoneme-to-text 1986) decodes noisy uncertain word streams and speculated that grammatical or POS equivalence classes could sharpen decoding, but had no trainable morphological tagger to plug in. Why it matters: leaving clean in-domain text for new genres, ASR or phoneme output, OCR, or historical text breaks both assumptions at once, since the lexicon is absent or wrong and OOV soars, and tokens are uncertain. No work connects the morphological-generative and probabilistic-HMM traditions into one trainable model that also tolerates a noisy channel.

**Method.** Relax the two assumptions of statistical taggers, a complete lexicon and clean tokens. Keep the HMM tag-transition structure, optionally with Kupiec selective state chains, but replace the lexicon plus ambiguity-class plus default-class scaffolding with a generative morphological emission model for word given tag: a per-tag character or morpheme sub-model that decomposes a token into stem plus affixes and scores it with a within-tag character n-gram generator in a trie. Any token, seen or unseen, gets a nonzero form-informed probability, subsuming the ambiguity-class trick and suffix heuristic into one process, with Klein and Simmons affixes seeding the priors. Train by Baum-Welch EM on unlabeled text with no lexicon, or with only a small seed affix list as priors, giving an open-vocabulary tagger whose parameters do not grow with vocabulary. Extend to noisy input by composing with a channel model for observation given word, such as phoneme-confusion or OCR errors, so decoding jointly recovers tags and words in one Viterbi pass, with POS transitions acting as the grammatical equivalence-class constraint the IBM work called for. Evaluate tagging accuracy versus rising OOV and domain shift against lexicon-dependent baselines, joint tag and transcription accuracy on pseudophonetic input versus a word n-gram source, and ablations against suffix-heuristic, default-open-class, and hand-rule Klein and Simmons baselines.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 14 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bridge Opportunity across two grammatical-tagging traditions that grew up in parallel and rarely inform each other at run time.

(1) The statistical tradition: hidden Markov taggers trained on unlabeled text (Kupiec 1989; Cutting/Church-style "Practical Part-of-Speech Tagger" 1992) are fast, portable, and "robust," reaching ~96% accuracy with only a lexicon and raw text. But their errors are not uniform — they concentrate in identifiable pockets: unknown words (handled only by crude open-class/suffix biasing), closed-class and idiom ambiguities, and dependencies that cross phrase boundaries, which the local Markov window cannot capture. These errors are also opaque and degrade under genre shift.

(2) The linguistic/rule tradition: Hindle (1989) shows disambiguation rules can be automatically ACQUIRED from tagged text and that they beat hand-written rules precisely because they exploit the parser's linguistic structure; Klein & Simmons (1963) do dictionary-free coding using morphology; Church (1988) adds noun-phrase structure. Kupiec's "augmenting an HMM" work is the clearest evidence the bridge pays off — he used error analysis plus linguistic knowledge to design higher-order "state chains" modeling phrase-crossing dependencies — but he did this by hand, once, offline, baked into the model topology. Meanwhile the speech language-modeling papers (phoneme-to-text 1986; Markov source modeling 1985) argued in the abstract for "combining statistical modeling with a grammatical approach," but never closed the loop for tagging robustness.

The gap: no work lets the statistical model's own uncertainty decide WHEN and WHERE to invoke automatically-acquired symbolic linguistic knowledge. Bridging these disconnected communities matters because it converts Kupiec's manual, static linguistic augmentation and Hindle's standalone rule sets into a targeted, automatic mechanism for repairing exactly the brittle points of an otherwise cheap, portable statistical tagger — and yields a more trustworthy language-model component for downstream decoding (speech/transcription).

**Method.** A selective hybrid "uncertainty-gated" tagger that robustifies an unsupervised HMM tagger by triggering automatically-acquired symbolic rules only at its brittle decision points.

Step 1 — Baseline statistical tagger. Train an HMM tagger on unlabeled text using ambiguity-class word representations (Kupiec/Cutting style) so it stays vocabulary-independent and portable.

Step 2 — Per-token confidence estimate. Beyond Viterbi, run forward–backward to obtain the marginal posterior over tags at each position. Define brittleness signals: (a) the margin/entropy between the best and second-best tag posterior; (b) disagreement between the Viterbi tag and the marginal-max tag; (c) unknown-word / rare-ambiguity-class flags. These convert the model's internal probabilities into an explicit "where am I likely wrong" map — the robustification trigger.

Step 3 — Acquire targeted repair rules. From held-out tagged text (or bootstrapped self-tagged text), automatically mine contexts where the HMM is simultaneously low-confidence AND wrong. Using Hindle-style acquisition, learn symbolic disambiguation rules that use richer context than the HMM window: long-distance and phrase-crossing features (operationalizing Kupiec's hand-built state chains automatically), noun-phrase boundary features (Church NP parser), and morphological/suffix features for unknown words (Klein & Simmons). Rules are indexed by the HMM's residual error signatures, so the rule library specifically covers the statistical model's failure modes rather than duplicating what it already gets right.

Step 4 — Uncertainty-gated inference. Tag with the fast HMM; wherever a token's brittleness signal exceeds a threshold, consult the rule library to override or re-rank the local tag distribution, then re-decode locally. Elsewhere, trust the HMM. This preserves HMM speed and coverage while intervening only where it is unreliable, and it can abstain when no rule fires.

Step 5 — Evaluation of robustification. Measure error reduction specifically inside the identified failure pockets: unknown-word accuracy, closed-class/idiom ambiguity accuracy, phrase-crossing cases, and cross-genre transfer (train on one corpus, test on Brown fragments/ungrammatical text). Compare against the pure HMM and the pure acquired-rule system to show the gated hybrid beats both, and report calibration (does low confidence actually locate errors?). Finally, plug the tagger into the phoneme-to-text/linguistic-decoding setting to test whether targeted robustification of tagging improves downstream transcription — demonstrating the statistical↔grammatical bridge end to end.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across these works, part-of-speech disambiguation for unrestricted text developed as two largely disconnected research communities that rarely share machinery. One is the probabilistic/Markov community: Church's stochastic tagger (1988) and practical HMM tagger (1992), Kupiec's augmented HMM (1989), Jelinek-style Markov source modeling (1985), and the CLAWS-style empirical labelling (1991). These systems self-organize from unlabeled text plus a lexicon, optimize globally, and degrade gracefully, but they condition on fixed, uniform n-gram windows, are opaque, and cannot easily incorporate specific linguistic constraints — Kupiec had to hand-design "state chains" from error analysis and linguistic intuition to get selective higher-order conditioning without parameter explosion. The other is the symbolic/linguistic community: Klein & Simmons' morphology-and-context coder without a dictionary (1963), Greene & Rubin's TAGGIT (1971), and especially Hindle's automatic acquisition of interpretable disambiguation rules grounded in a parser's grammar (1989). These are linguistically transparent and grammar-grounded, and Hindle even reaches accuracy comparable to probabilistic taggers, but they are deterministic: they lack probabilistic conflict resolution and a principled statistical fallback for unseen contexts.

The single work that bridges the two — Derouault & Merialdo (1986) — showed that a "suitable combination of the Markov source and the grammar" beats either alone, but only for French phoneme-to-text speech decoding, with a hand-built 200-rule CFG, inside a task-specific decoding pipeline. No one has unified *automatically acquired* symbolic disambiguation rules (Hindle/Klein–Simmons) with the HMM/Markov sequence model in a single probabilistic tagger for unrestricted English text. This bridge matters because each community holds exactly what the other lacks: symbolic rules supply the interpretable, linguistically motivated conditioning structure that Kupiec built by hand, while the Markov model supplies the probabilistic reconciliation of conflicting rules and the robust fallback the symbolic systems lack.

**Method.** Build a unified probabilistic–symbolic tagger that reconciles the two literatures in one model and one decoding pass.

1) Statistical backbone. Start from an HMM over POS categories with Kupiec-style word equivalence classes, trainable from unlabeled text plus a lexicon via Baum–Welch (as in Church 1992 / Kupiec 1989). This is the robust, self-organizing component.

2) Automatic rule acquisition. From the same corpus, automatically induce two families of interpretable disambiguation rules: (a) affix/morphology rules for unknown and ambiguous words in the Klein–Simmons tradition, and (b) context/grammar-grounded rules in the Hindle tradition, mining local configurations in which a specific categorial ambiguity is resolved with high reliability. Each rule is a triggering context plus a predicted resolution, with an empirically measured reliability.

3) Unification mechanism (the core contribution). Rather than applying rules deterministically (Hindle) or hand-designing state chains (Kupiec), treat each acquired rule as a *data-derived* state-chain augmentation that turns on selective higher-order/linguistic conditioning only where the rule fires. Formally, express the tagger as a learned combination of the base Markov-source probability and rule-conditioned probabilities, generalizing Derouault & Merialdo's statistics-plus-grammar combination from French speech decoding to English tagging. Mixture/interaction weights and rule-conditioned transition/emission parameters are estimated on training text (Baum–Welch plus held-out tuning). Where a reliable rule applies, its conditioning dominates; where none applies, the model falls back to robust n-gram statistics — solving both the rule-conflict and unseen-context problems while avoiding uniform higher-order parameter explosion.

4) Joint decoding. Run a single global Viterbi search over the rule-augmented state space so statistical and symbolic evidence are optimized together rather than pipelined.

5) Evaluation and analysis. Tag unrestricted English (e.g., the Brown corpus of Francis & Kučera 1982), reporting overall accuracy, unknown-word accuracy, and — to demonstrate the bridge — which rules fired for each decision (interpretability) alongside probabilistic scores. Ablations isolate the contribution of statistics versus acquired rules, and compare against pure-HMM baselines (Church/Kupiec) and the pure-rule baseline (Hindle). Success criteria: match or beat both baselines while providing human-readable justifications, and show that automatically learned rule-driven state chains recover or exceed the benefit of Kupiec's hand-built ones.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 14 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the prior works, part-of-speech disambiguation is attacked by strikingly different paradigms: computational rule/dictionary coders (Klein & Simmons 1963; Greene & Rubin's TAGGIT), stochastic hidden Markov models (Church 1988; Cutting et al. 1992; Jelinek's Markov source modeling 1985), phrase-dependent HMMs with hand-designed state chains (Kupiec 1989), and disambiguation rules automatically acquired for a deterministic parser (Hindle 1989). Each reports an impressive-sounding headline number — "over 90%" (Klein & Simmons on scientific text), "exceeds 96%" (Cutting et al.), "less than 2% training / higher test error" (Hindle) — but the field has no way to turn these numbers into comparable, accumulating evidence. The reported accuracies are computed on different corpora (Brown, scientific text, WSJ-style text), different tagsets whose granularity is known to change difficulty (Church notes larger tagsets are kept only to aid corpus search; Cutting notes accuracy depends on how the lexicon is mapped to the tagset; Hindle contrasts Brown's fine pronoun tags with Fidditch's collapsed PRO), and different training regimes (labeled vs. unlabeled text, hand-written vs. acquired rules). Crucially, a single token-level accuracy hides the things practitioners actually need to observe: how much of the score is just the most-frequent-tag baseline (most tokens are unambiguous), which ambiguity classes (e.g., noun/verb "program," preposition/particle "out") actually drive errors, how performance degrades on unknown words and on the genuinely hard cases these papers keep citing ("Time flies like an arrow"), and how accuracy scales with the amount and type of training data or with context-window/model order. Because there is no shared, controlled measurement instrument, claims cannot be audited, paradigms cannot be fairly compared, and evidence about *what actually determines tagging accuracy* cannot be accumulated. This is an evidence gap, not a modeling gap.

**Method.** Build a concrete, open tagging-evaluation workbench: a software platform that makes tagger results observable, comparable, and cumulative. It has four components. (1) A shared, granularity-controlled benchmark corpus: take a single hand-tagged text collection and define a hierarchical tagset lattice, so any experiment can be scored after projecting predictions and gold labels to a chosen granularity level (fine Brown-style tags down to collapsed classes). This lets the platform report the same system at several granularities and thereby neutralizes the tagset-comparability problem that currently makes 90% and 96% incommensurable. (2) A common tagger interface behind which we re-instantiate one representative from each paradigm in the corpus: a dictionary/rule coder (Klein & Simmons / TAGGIT style), a first-order HMM with ambiguity classes (Church / Cutting), a phrase-dependent state-chain HMM (Kupiec), and an acquired-rule disambiguator (Hindle). All are trained and tested through the same harness on the same splits and lexicon, with a plug-in slot for new systems. (3) A standardized diagnostic battery run automatically for every system: overall accuracy alongside the most-frequent-tag baseline; accuracy restricted to ambiguous tokens; a per-ambiguity-class confusion breakdown (using ambiguity classes as the shared diagnostic unit across paradigms); unknown-word accuracy as a function of lexicon coverage; sensitivity curves over context window / model order; and learning curves over amount of labeled vs. unlabeled training text (so the "no pre-tagged corpus needed" claims can be quantified). A curated "hard case" probe set of locally ambiguous constructions the papers highlight is scored separately. (4) A results registry: every run is logged with corpus, tagset level, training regime, and full diagnostic profile into a versioned database with a public leaderboard, so evidence accumulates and any headline number can be traced back to the conditions that produced it. The central contribution is the artifact itself — a reusable measurement instrument and its accumulated evidence base — which directly closes the gap by letting the community observe error structure, audit accuracy claims, and compare paradigms under controlled, reproducible conditions.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the prior work, a decade-plus of part-of-speech taggers report seemingly comparable headline accuracies but there is no shared evidence base that makes those numbers actually comparable or diagnostic. Klein & Simmons (1963) report ~90% on scientific text; Church (1988) reports ~95–99% and 99.5% for NP bracketing, attributing "most errors" to lexicon defects rather than the grammar; Cutting et al. (1992) report 96% and merely assert it is "comparable to the best achieved by Church [1988], Merialdo [1991]"; Kupiec (1989) reports 10% error on ambiguous words and 2–3% overall; Hindle (1989) reports ~10% ambiguous-word error and explicitly states "it is harder to compare performance directly to other reported disambiguation procedures, since the part of speech categories used are different," and can only say he "believes" Church has a "somewhat smaller" error rate.

This exposes a concrete Evidence Gap. (1) Tagsets differ (21 categories in Kupiec, 46 Fidditch categories in Hindle, the full Brown tagset in Cutting/Church), so cross-paper accuracy numbers are not on the same scale. (2) The accuracy metric itself is inconsistent — overall per-token accuracy (inflated by trivially unambiguous words) versus error on genuinely ambiguous tokens — with no shared most-frequent-tag baseline to reveal the true disambiguation headroom. (3) Error attribution is anecdotal and hand-picked (Church's "lexicon defects," Kupiec's "The period of..." and "to"-infinitive cases), so the field cannot tell whether gains come from the contextual model, the lexicon, the tagset granularity, the training regime (supervised vs. unlabeled), or the corpus. Because the field cannot observe or attribute where taggers succeed and fail, it cannot accumulate cumulative evidence about which mechanisms matter — precisely the question these rule-based, supervised-stochastic, and unsupervised-HMM approaches implicitly debate.

**Method.** Build a shared diagnostic benchmark and error-attribution protocol that puts heterogeneous taggers on equal footing and localizes their errors, rather than proposing yet another tagger.

1) Common evaluation substrate. Use the tagged Brown Corpus (already the common denominator for Cutting, Church, Kupiec via the 30k dictionary, and Hindle's training text) with fixed train/test splits. Define one canonical coarse tagset and publish documented many-to-many projection maps from each system's native tagset (Kupiec's 21, Hindle's 46 Fidditch, the full Brown set) onto it. All predictions are projected to the canonical tagset before scoring, removing tagset-granularity as a confound; report accuracy at both native and canonical granularity to quantify how much apparent accuracy is a tagset artifact.

2) Decomposed metric suite. For every system report: (a) overall per-token accuracy; (b) accuracy restricted to genuinely ambiguous tokens (words whose lexical ambiguity class has >1 tag); (c) per-ambiguity-class accuracy for the recurring hard classes named in the sources (noun-or-verb, adjective-or-noun, past-tense/past-participle, the "to" preposition/infinitive contrast); and (d) a mandatory most-frequent-tag unigram baseline that establishes the real headroom — exposing how much of a "96%" is trivial. This converts Kupiec's and Hindle's isolated "ambiguous-word error" numbers into a standard, always-reported quantity.

3) Stratified challenge sets. Curate held-out subsets stratified by ambiguity class and by context type, including cross-phrase-boundary dependencies (the phenomena Church's trigram window and Kupiec's augmented state-chains target). This lets the benchmark measure exactly the long-distance conditioning that current window/HMM models claim to handle.

4) Error-attribution instrument. Define an automatic protocol that classifies each error as (i) lexicon/coverage error — the correct tag is absent from the word's ambiguity class or the word is unknown; (ii) contextual-disambiguation error — correct tag present in the class but the model chose wrong; or (iii) mapping/tagset artifact introduced by projection. This turns Church's anecdotal "errors are lexicon defects" claim into a measured fraction and cleanly separates lexicon quality from contextual-model quality across systems.

5) Comparative accumulation. Wrap representative approaches from the corpus of prior work — a rule/deterministic disambiguator, a supervised trigram/HMM tagger, and an unsupervised HMM with ambiguity classes — run them on identical splits and projected tags, and record results in a versioned, reproducible leaderboard with the full decomposition. Feasibility is high: the data (Brown), the models, and the tagsets are all specified in the sources; the only new artifacts are the projection maps, the metric/attribution scripts, and the stratified challenge sets. The payoff is the missing evidence: whether stochastic really beats rule-based once tagset and baseline are controlled, and whether reported gains stem from the lexicon or the contextual model.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Early POS taggers report incomparable accuracies due to different tagsets, denominators, genres, and training regimes. There is no controlled benchmark or error taxonomy to observe which factor drives results.

**Method.** Build a controlled benchmark on the Brown corpus with nested tagsets, graded lexicons, and reimplemented taggers from each paradigm, sweeping factors and classifying errors to map where accuracy comes from.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 21 turns / 31 tool calls

**Motivation.** POS disambiguation systems in these works are each judged by one aggregate accuracy figure measured on a different corpus, tagset, and lexicon, so they are neither comparable nor attributable. No work decomposes an error rate into irreducible ambiguity given observable context, model-class limits, lexicon gaps, and estimation noise. This is an evidence gap. The 1963 paper only qualitatively distinguishes unnecessary from inherent ambiguity, and Church quantifies ambiguity in bits but stops short of a bound on achievable accuracy or an error-source audit.

**Method.** A formal error-attribution framework. Fix an observation window and the Bayes-optimal tagger given that window. Decompose expected tagging error into an irreducible term, a model-class term, a lexicon term, and an estimation term. Prove a theorem that these sum to the total, each is non-negative, and the total is at least the irreducible term, yielding a certifiable accuracy ceiling. Derive an information-theoretic floor via the Fano inequality from conditional entropy as a function of window size. Provide an empirical audit estimating each term and validate on rule-based, HMM, and augmented state-chain taggers over a shared corpus and tagset.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical part-of-speech taggers of this era (Church's stochastic parts program, 1988; Cutting/Kupiec et al.'s Practical Tagger, 1992; Kupiec's equivalence-class and phrase-dependent HMMs, 1989) are trained on unlabeled text with Baum-Welch and reported with a single aggregate accuracy figure (~96%). Yet the real gains come from expert, hand-driven refinement: in Augmenting a Hidden Markov Model, Kupiec derives improvements by manually inspecting errors and then splitting categories (breaking the coarse auxiliary state into be/have/do/modal), promoting the 100 most frequent words to their own equivalence classes, and editing the transition structure by hand (zeroing bad transitions out of the singleton to/to-infinitive state). This is an evidence gap: the field has no systematic, reproducible way to observe where an unsupervised HMM's parameters are unreliable versus merely data-sparse, to attribute the aggregate error to specific ambiguity classes and transition contexts, and to compare candidate refinements on equal footing before spending scarce annotation to verify them. Because Baum-Welch can silently mis-estimate parameters, and because equivalence classes trade reliability against resolution, practitioners cannot tell which of the huge space of possible refinements will actually help. Tagger construction stays artisanal and does not accumulate as measurable evidence across corpora, tagsets, and languages.

**Method.** Turn manual error analysis into a measurable, budget-aware optimization that improves the tagger and emits reusable diagnostics. First, diagnostics as measurements: for a base equivalence-class HMM trained by Baum-Welch, compute a reliability score for each ambiguity class and transition context from its expected counts and posterior variance in the forward-backward pass, and an error-contribution score from a small held-out annotated validation set plus the model's posterior entropy on abundant unlabeled text. Second, an operator library mirroring Kupiec's manual moves, each with an explicit parameter cost: split or merge an equivalence class, promote a frequent word to its own class, split a category state, insert a selective higher-order state chain over a specific context, and zero or constrain a transition. Third, search: run greedy/beam search over sequences of operators, proposing operators targeted at high-error, low-reliability units, scoring candidates by marginal validation-error reduction per added parameter (a description-length or accuracy-per-parameter penalty), warm-starting a few Baum-Welch iterations after each accepted move, and keeping a beam to escape local optima. Fourth, active annotation as resource allocation: since validation labels are the binding budget, use an acquisition function (expected error reduction or disagreement among beam models) to pick the minimal text spans to hand-tag, allocating annotation where it most resolves which competing refinement to accept. Fifth, outputs: not only a tuned tagger but an auditable diagnostic report listing per-class and per-context error contributions, reliability flags, and the accepted/rejected operator trace, serving as a benchmark to compare taggers beyond aggregate accuracy and to transfer refinement decisions to new corpora and languages. Evaluate on the Brown corpus and on out-of-domain text such as the noisy Lisp-email corpus Kupiec used, measuring accuracy, parameters added, and annotation spent, and test whether the automatically discovered refinements recover and surpass the hand-crafted ones.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The tagger works report roughly 90 to 96 percent accuracy (Klein/Simmons 1963; Church 1988; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992; Hindle 1989), but the supporting evidence is non-comparable and optimistic. Every tagger is measured on clean orthographic text, with a complete lexicon, on a single genre, and unknown words are often excluded (Kupiec reports 95.6 percent correct, ignoring unknown words). Meanwhile the phoneme-to-text and Markov source works show real input is an uncertain signal, with homophone confusions and word-candidate lattices, not clean tokens. The field has no shared way to observe, measure, or compare how the tagger families degrade when the clean-input, complete-lexicon, single-genre assumptions are relaxed, nor to diagnose which error sources dominate. This is an evidence gap: we cannot audit robustness, compare rule-based versus ambiguity-class HMM versus equivalence-class HMM on equal footing, or tell whether reported accuracy reflects the uncertain-input conditions taggers face as front-ends to speech, OCR, and parsing systems, which are exactly the use cases their authors advertise.

**Method.** Relax the clean-input, complete-lexicon assumption shared by these taggers and build the evidence base this enables. First, a parametric degradation generator turns any gold-tagged corpus into paired clean and noisy versions with known ground truth, via tunable operators grounded in the sources: homophone and phoneme-confusion candidates and word-boundary ambiguity from phoneme-to-text lattices, OCR-like character corruption, lexicon dropout at rate r to synthesize unknown words, and genre or domain shift by training on one corpus and testing on another. Intensity knobs yield degradation curves rather than single points. Second, a generalized soft-evidence tagging formulation lets existing HMMs run under these conditions: each position carries a confidence-weighted distribution over candidate words rather than one clean token, and the emission term becomes a mixture over the candidate words equivalence classes, a minimal extension of the Cutting and Kupiec models that reduces to the original tagger on clean one-best input; unknown words are handled in-model via open-class ambiguity classes plus suffix cues rather than excluded. Third, a unified harness with a common tagset mapping across corpora, interchangeable re-implementations of the three tagger families, and an error taxonomy scoring unknown-word, lexical-confusion, and context-disambiguation errors separately. Experiments include accuracy-versus-noise curves per tagger per operator and their combinations, attribution of the ignoring-unknown-words gap to each error source, and a test of whether the soft-evidence lattice extension recovers accuracy over naive one-best decoding, isolating the value of propagating input uncertainty. The deliverable is comparable, diagnostic evidence on tagger robustness under realistic uncertain-input regimes, plus a concrete relaxation extending these taggers to the speech and OCR front-ends their authors targeted.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 5 turns / 13 tool calls
