**Motivation.** The provided works collectively established statistical part-of-speech tagging as the dominant paradigm: stochastic n-gram/HMM taggers (Church 1988; DeRose 1988; Cutting 1992), equivalence-class HMMs with selective higher-order state chains (Kupiec 1989), acquired symbolic disambiguation rules (Hindle 1989), and earlier rule/frequency systems (Klein & Simmons 1963; Greene & Rubin 1971). Each reports strong headline numbers — Cutting "exceeds 96%," Kupiec "95.6% correct... (ignoring unknown words)." Yet these numbers are essentially non-comparable and non-diagnostic. They are measured on different corpora (Grolier vs. Dave Barry vs. Brown), different tagset granularities (21 vs. 38 tags vs. others), with different and often unstated treatment of the very cases that break taggers: Kupiec explicitly excludes unknown words, and unknown-word handling elsewhere is an ad hoc backoff to a default open-class ambiguity class or suffix cue.

This is an evidence gap. The field accumulates taggers but not evidence about *where and why* they fail. Error analysis in these papers is informal and manual — Kupiec inspected outputs by hand to decide where to add state chains; Hindle relied on parser errors — with no shared, repeatable way to observe which error sources dominate (out-of-lexicon words, intrinsically hard ambiguity classes, contexts that exceed a bigram's reach, genre/domain shift, or tagset choices), nor to know when an emitted tag should be trusted. Consequently, robustness is asserted rather than measured, gains from added model complexity (e.g., higher-order state chains) cannot be attributed to the phenomena they target, and cross-paper comparison is impossible. This matters because these taggers feed downstream shallow analysis (phrase recognition, sense disambiguation, grammatical-function assignment in Cutting; parsing in Hindle), where silent tagging failures on unknown words and unfamiliar genres propagate unchecked.

**Method.** Two coupled contributions: a stratified diagnostic harness that makes tagger failure observable and comparable, and a diagnostic-guided robustified tagger that reduces the failure modes the harness exposes.

(1) Diagnostic stress-testing harness (the evidence instrument). Given any tagged corpus and any of the paradigms above, decompose error into interpretable strata rather than a single aggregate: (a) known-to-lexicon vs. out-of-lexicon tokens; (b) per ambiguity class (e.g., adjective-or-noun, noun-or-verb), so intrinsically hard classes are isolated; (c) by required context order — for each token, perturb the surrounding tags/words and record whether the correct decision flips, separating cases solvable by local bigram context from those needing phrase-level context (directly probing Kupiec's motivation); (d) by genre via train-on-one/test-on-another transfer. Add two controlled interventions: lexicon ablation (drop k% of lexicon entries to synthesize known vs. simulated-unknown copies of the *same* tokens, giving a clean, quantified unknown-word robustness curve), and a tagset-normalization mapping (collapse/refine to a common inventory, exploiting Cutting's flexible lexicon-to-tagset mapping) so that Church-, Cutting-, Kupiec-, and Hindle-style systems can be scored on the same footing. Output is a per-stratum error profile plus robustness curves under perturbation — the missing shared evidence base.

(2) Robustified tagger driven by the profile. Use the stratum profile as a control signal to build a backoff/mixture tagger that targets identified weaknesses instead of raising average accuracy blindly: start from an equivalence-class HMM baseline (Kupiec/Cutting); automatically insert higher-order state chains only for the ambiguity classes and contexts the harness flags as context-hungry (data-driven placement replacing Kupiec's manual design); handle out-of-lexicon tokens with an explicit suffix/affix ambiguity-class model whose parameters are fit to maximize accuracy on the ablation-simulated unknown stratum; and add Hindle-style acquired correction rules as a final layer aimed at the residual systematic errors the profile surfaces. Attach a calibrated per-token confidence (from the posterior margin and agreement among the mixture components), thresholded so low-confidence tokens are flagged "uncertain" rather than silently guessed — converting silent failures into auditable abstentions for downstream modules.

Evaluation reuses the harness: report improvement per stratum, especially the unknown-word robustness curve and cross-genre transfer, plus calibration quality (does flagged-uncertain correlate with actual error?). Success is defined as reduced brittleness — flatter degradation under lexicon ablation and genre shift and reliable uncertainty flags — not merely a higher single-corpus score. All components are implementable with the resources and methods present in these sources (lexicon + unlabeled/tagged text, HMM training, equivalence classes, acquired rules), making the study feasible and grounded.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works split into statistical taggers (Church 1988, Cutting and Kupiec 1992, Kupiec 1989, Derouault and Merialdo 1986, Jelinek 1985) and rule-based ones (TAGGIT 1971, Klein and Simmons 1963, Hindle 1989). Their reported accuracies are incommensurable because of different corpora, tagset granularity, and metrics. Claims of comparability cannot be verified, and errors cannot be attributed to lexicon gaps, ambiguity classes, context models, or tagset mapping. The one within-study comparison by Derouault and Merialdo hints the paradigms are complementary but is a single data point. This is an evidence gap: no shared instrument exists to measure, benchmark, audit, and diagnose these systems on common ground.

**Method.** Build a unifying framework. First, a tagset-reconciliation layer mapping each system inventory onto a shared coarse-to-fine hierarchy and scoring all systems at matched granularities on common corpora. Second, a unified constraint-scoring representation, following Jelinek and Kupiec equivalence classes, expressing every method as a scoring function over one lattice of ambiguity-class sequences so HMMs, rules, and dictionary-free coders run on identical inputs and can be combined by interpolation. Third, a diagnostic benchmark with an error taxonomy attributing each mistake to lexicon coverage, ambiguity inventory, context-model failure, tagset artifact, or genuine ambiguity, using ablations. The result is a reproducible protocol reporting per-class, per-genre, per-granularity performance with error attribution.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 24 turns / 31 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical taggers with radically simplified models tag nearly as well as rich ones, but no work explains why. Church (1988) is surprised a local trigram works so well and blames errors on the lexicon. Kupiec (1989) drops word identity into about 129 ambiguity classes with little loss, unexplained. Cutting et al. (1992) get high accuracy from unsupervised Baum-Welch class models, unexplained. This is an explanation gap: why is context nearly sufficient and why do residual errors persist.

**Method.** Build a glass-box tagging platform reimplementing the shared HMM with switchable axes such as word versus class emissions, Markov order, tagset, and supervised versus Baum-Welch, plus a rule-parser wrapper on one corpus. Instrument each token with emission versus context probability mass via ablation, contextual-determinacy entropy given only ambiguity-class context, gain from higher-order chains, and an oracle context-solvable versus lexicon-bound flag. Output a causal error taxonomy explaining why simplified models match rich ones.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** A striking fact recurs across these taggers but is never explained. The unsupervised HMM family (Jelinek 1985; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992) collapses tens of thousands of words into a few hundred part-of-speech ambiguity classes, discarding each word's individual tag bias. Kupiec asserts word context provides significant constraint so the trade-off is remarkably favorable, and Cutting et al. say the collapse comes without significant accuracy loss, yet neither explains why it is favorable, when it fails, or how much context replaces the lost lexical prior. The word-specific-emission family (Church 1988; DeRose 1988) keeps per-word probabilities and reaches nearly the same 96 percent, and Merialdo bootstrapping (Derouault and Merialdo 1986) reaches it from another regime, a convergence with no causal account. The field patched this with heuristics: Kupiec modeled the top-100 frequent words individually, zeroed specific to-infinitive transitions, and hand-designed state chains from manual error analysis. These are symptoms of a missing theory. Without a causal account we cannot predict which words need individual modeling, which classes need richer context, or when the class-based trade-off breaks on a new corpus or language.

**Method.** Build a mechanistic, information-theoretic explanation of why and when context substitutes for per-word lexical priors, using resources these papers assume (Brown corpus as gold reference, a lexicon, standard HMM Viterbi and Baum-Welch). First, factorize each ambiguous token's decision into three predictors of the true tag: per-word prior, class prior, and context posterior given class plus neighbors; build taggers toggling word-specific versus class-based emissions crossed with zeroth, first, and second-order context so accuracy differences isolate each factor. Second, quantify the trade-off per class via conditional entropy of the true tag given word alone, class alone, and class plus context: the class-minus-word gap measures information destroyed by collapsing, the class-minus-context gap measures what context recovers, and the trade-off is favorable exactly when recovery exceeds discarded word information. Third, adjudicate token by token where per-word prior and context posterior disagree, measuring which wins against gold. Fourth, explain the frequency heuristic by testing whether residual per-word information concentrates in high-frequency function words deviating most from their class average, deriving Kupiec's top-100 rule from a principled entropy-deviation criterion. Fifth, explain systematic errors as low-context-information cases where target class and neighbor have low mutual information, and show the manual state chain works by raising it. Sixth, explain supervised versus unsupervised convergence by comparing Baum-Welch and supervised estimates against the entropy diagnostics. Deliverable: a predictive score forecasting which words need individual modeling, which classes need augmented context, and cross-corpus robustness.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 5 turns / 11 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the represented literature, statistical part-of-speech taggers achieve strikingly high accuracy with linguistically impoverished models — Church's stochastic parts program reports ~95-99% and explicitly remarks that "it is surprising that a local bottom-up approach can perform so well," attributing residual errors informally to "defects in the lexicon" rather than the trigram grammar; Cutting et al.'s HMM tagger exceeds 96% using only a lexicon and untagged text; Kupiec introduces equivalence classes and selective higher-order "state chains" motivated by ad hoc error analysis; Jelinek/Derouault-Merialdo train hidden Markov models with Baum-Welch on untagged data; and Greene-Rubin/Hindle represent the rule-based lineage. Yet there is no shared, causal account of WHY these systems work and where their accuracy actually comes from. Each design choice — Markov order, word vs. equivalence-class representation, supervised counts vs. unsupervised Baum-Welch training, lexicon completeness — is justified by localized error observations on different corpora and tagsets, so the field cannot say which factor drives performance, whether unsupervised training recovers genuine linguistic categories or merely distributional proxies, or whether the errors that remain are truly lexicon-bound (as claimed) versus caused by the models' inability to capture cross-phrase dependencies. This is an explanation gap: high performance is reported and celebrated as "surprising," but its mechanism is unmeasured and its failures are diagnosed anecdotally.

**Method.** Construct a unified diagnostic benchmark and comparative empirical map that isolates the mechanisms behind statistical tagging performance, holding data constant. (1) Reimplement the representative model families on a single common corpus and tagset (e.g., the Brown/LOB material used across these papers): a rule-based baseline in the TAGGIT/Hindle-acquired-rule tradition, and HMM variants spanning Church-style trigrams, Kupiec-style selective state chains, and Cutting-style ambiguity-class models. (2) Run a controlled ablation grid crossing four factors each paper varies in isolation: Markov order (bigram/trigram/selective higher-order state chains); token representation (individual words vs. equivalence/ambiguity classes); training regime (supervised counts vs. Baum-Welch unsupervised bootstrap in the Jelinek/Derouault-Merialdo style); and lexicon quality (complete, degraded, and suffix-guessing for unknown words). (3) Decompose every model's errors along an explicit taxonomy — ambiguity class of the target word, local vs. cross-phrase dependency distance, word-frequency band, and known-vs-unknown status — so that aggregate accuracy is resolved into interpretable strata. (4) Directly test the folklore claim that "most errors are due to the lexicon" by measuring the marginal error contribution attributable to lexicon gaps versus contextual-model limits versus long-distance dependencies. (5) Probe whether unsupervised Baum-Welch actually induces linguistic categories by building a hidden-state-to-tag confusion mapping and measuring state-tag alignment entropy, comparing induced states against supervised categories. The output is an "error map" and factor-contribution profile that converts the informal, per-paper explanations into measured, causal accounts of when, why, and how statistical taggers succeed and fail.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 12 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the statistical tagging literature represented here, a striking empirical claim recurs but is never explained. Kupiec (1989) and Cutting et al. (1992) replace individual words by "ambiguity classes" (the set of parts of speech a word can take), thereby discarding all word-specific tag preferences — e.g. "dog" and "see" are pooled into a single noun-or-verb class even though "dog" is usually a noun and "see" usually a verb. This drastically cuts parameters (a 50,000-word Brown lexicon collapses to ~400 classes) yet is reported to work "without significant reduction in accuracy," and Kupiec calls the trade-off "remarkably favorable." Church (1988) and Derouault & Merialdo (1986), by contrast, keep word-level emission probabilities P(tag|word), i.e. they retain exactly the lexical bias that class pooling throws away, and also reach high accuracy.

This is an unresolved Explanation Gap: why is discarding word-level lexical bias almost free, i.e. under what conditions does local context recover the tag information that pooling destroys, and when does it not? The papers themselves reveal that it is not always free — they patch the model exactly where pooling hurts: Cutting et al. and Kupiec give the ~100 most frequent words their own singleton classes; Kupiec introduces "state chains" for selective higher-order conditioning and hand-edits transitions (e.g. the "to" infinitive-vs-preposition error) after an "analysis of the errors." These fixes are justified by error inspection and linguistic intuition, not by any principle that predicts, in advance, which words must be broken out of their class and where higher-order context is required. Without such an account we cannot explain why the trade-off is favorable, cannot predict when it will fail (new domains, new languages, richer tagsets), and cannot replace ad-hoc, per-corpus tuning with a principled design procedure.

**Method.** Provide a formal, information-theoretic account of why ambiguity-class pooling is (or is not) accuracy-preserving, and derive from it a predictive criterion for the empirical fixes the papers apply by hand.

1. Formal setup. Model tagging as recovering a hidden tag T from an observation. Distinguish two emission representations of a token at position i: the word identity W and its ambiguity class Q(W) (a deterministic coarsening of W). Let C denote the observable context (neighboring ambiguity classes / tags as used by a first-order HMM). Define the Bayes-optimal error of the word-level tagger e_word = E[1 - max_t P(t | W, C)] and of the class-level tagger e_class using P(t | Q, C).

2. Decomposition theorem. Prove that the excess error Δ = e_class − e_word is governed by the conditional mutual information I(T ; W | Q, C): the tag information carried by the exact word beyond what its class and context already provide. Concretely, derive (i) a lossless-pooling condition — pooling costs nothing exactly when T ⟂ W | (Q, C), i.e. within each class the word identity is conditionally independent of the correct tag given context — and (ii) a two-sided bound on Δ in terms of the residual conditional entropy gap H(T | Q, C) − H(T | W, C) (via Fano-type inequalities). This makes precise the intuition that "context provides significant constraint": the trade-off is favorable exactly to the extent that context C makes lexical bias redundant.

3. A taxonomy of disambiguation regimes derived from the decomposition, classifying each (ambiguity-class, context) situation as: (a) context-recoverable — H(T|Q,C) already near zero, so pooling is safe (the common case, explaining the favorable trade-off); (b) lexically-dominated — word identity, not context, carries the information (large I(T;W|Q,C) with C uninformative), predicting exactly the high-frequency, skewed-distribution words that must be given singleton classes; (c) higher-order-dependent — first-order C is insufficient but a longer context C′ would make the residual entropy vanish, predicting precisely where Kupiec's state chains / phrase-dependent conditioning are needed; (d) irreducibly ambiguous — entropy stays high under any available observation.

4. Operationalization and validation on the Brown corpus (Francis & Kučera 1982), whose tags let all entropy/mutual-information terms be estimated empirically. (i) Rank words by the estimated I(T;W|Q,C) and show the theory predicts, without error inspection, the same set of words that the prior work broke out into singleton classes (the frequent, distributionally skewed words), and predicts the "to" and noun/verb-before-participle failure points as regime (c). (ii) Show that the bound on Δ tracks the measured accuracy difference between a word-level model (Church-style) and an ambiguity-class model (Cutting/Kupiec-style) as tagset granularity and context order are varied. (iii) Turn the criterion into a design rule: iteratively refine the model by splitting a class or lengthening context only where the estimated residual entropy exceeds a threshold, and demonstrate this reproduces the hand-crafted refinements at lower parameter cost. This converts the papers' empirical "remarkably favorable trade-off" into an explained, quantitatively predictive principle.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** The statistical POS taggers here converge on one recipe: an HMM over lexical categories trained on unlabeled text with Baum-Welch, words collapsed into a few hundred ambiguity classes, plus hand-added hints and selective higher-order state chains. All report about 96 percent accuracy, yet none explains why it works or why it plateaus. Key design choices are made by intuition: which words to pool versus individualize, where to inject hints, and where to add state chains. Crucially, EM maximizes likelihood of the ambiguity-class stream, not accuracy, and unsupervised fixed points are only weakly identified by unlabeled text, so why some categories are learned well and others fail systematically, and why extra EM iterations stop helping, is uncharacterized. This is an explanation gap: no mechanistic decomposition of what causes the achieved accuracy, its variation across corpora and tagsets, and its ceiling.

**Method.** Build a search-driven attribution framework that turns the tagger's ad hoc levers into an explicit optimization space and uses the search trajectory to decompose accuracy. First, parameterize the design space: a merge and split lattice over ambiguity classes, a continuous knob per hint via biased initial probabilities, candidate state-chain context extensions, and the EM stopping point. Second, score candidates with two objectives at once, held-out tagging accuracy on a small labeled probe set and Baum-Welch likelihood, exposing the likelihood versus accuracy mismatch. Third, run adaptive greedy or bandit search that allocates evaluation budget to promising levers; the marginal accuracy change per move gives an automatic ablation quantifying how much accuracy comes from lexicon coverage, class-pooling loss, hints, and state chains. Fourth, add an identifiability diagnostic: run multi-restart Baum-Welch from perturbed initializations and measure variance of per-class tag decisions; high-variance parameters reveal what unlabeled text cannot pin down, explaining where and why EM fails and which hints break symmetry. Fifth, close the loop with active design: allocate a minimal annotation budget to the flagged high-variance high-error contexts and measure accuracy recovered per token. The output is a quantified causal account of the accuracy ceiling plus a reusable optimizer that discovers the best class granularity, hints, and state chains for a new corpus, feasible with the Brown corpus, a lexicon, and a first-order HMM tagger.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The unsupervised, vocabulary-independent HMM taggers central to this literature (Cutting et al. 1992; Kupiec 1989; the Markov-source lineage of Jelinek 1985 and Derouault & Merialdo 1986) all rest on one pivotal but unexplained assumption: the equivalence-class (ambiguity-class) hypothesis, that all words sharing the same set of possible tags have identical category-emission distributions, so only local transition probabilities distinguish them. Kupiec calls this a trade-off ("dog" leans noun, "see" leans verb, yet both are pooled as noun-or-verb) but the field merely asserts it is "remarkably favorable." No paper explains WHY collapsing a 50,000-word lexicon into ~400 classes barely reduces accuracy (~96%), WHEN and WHY it produces systematic errors (e.g., "period" tagged adjective after a determiner), or WHY modeling only the ~100 most frequent words individually is the right cut point. Reported accuracy is thus a black box: we cannot say how much comes from lexical prior structure versus contextual disambiguation, nor predict how the assumption holds across genres, languages, training-set sizes, or unknown suffix-guessed words. This explanatory gap matters because tuning, error correction, and porting are currently guided only by anecdotal error analysis rather than by understanding of the mechanism.

**Method.** Reframe full ambiguity-class pooling (Kupiec/Cutting) and fully word-specific emissions (Church-style models) as two endpoints of one continuum, relaxing the strict equivalence-class assumption into a weaker, evidence-graded one. (1) Replace class-level emission P(class|tag) with a hierarchical shrinkage estimate: each word's emission interpolates between its own distribution and its class distribution, with a per-word mixing weight that grows with available evidence. This is the deleted-interpolation idea (Jelinek & Mercer) already used for transition smoothing, now applied for the first time to the word/class emission hierarchy; it recovers full pooling when data are scarce and word-specific behavior when abundant, so the top-frequency-individual heuristic emerges as a hard-thresholded special case. Training stays unsupervised via Baum-Welch on unlabeled text plus a lexicon, preserving low-resource operation. (2) Use the continuum as an analytical instrument: sweeping the shrinkage weight decomposes tagging error into a pooling-bias term (ignoring word-specific preferences), an estimation-variance term (sparse word counts), and a contextual-constraint residual, giving a quantitative causal account of what produces the ~96% and why pooling nearly matches word-specific modeling. (3) Derive and validate a per-word predictor of when the assumption fails, from the divergence between a word's own and its class's emission distribution weighted by frequency and by the local contextual disambiguating power of its class, testing whether it flags documented systematic errors. (4) Relax scope and stress-test: rerun the decomposition across cross-genre Brown splits, small training sets, a second language (French), and unknown words handled by suffix-based classes, measuring how the assumption's validity and the optimal shrinkage weight vary with conditions. Evaluate against held-out gold Brown tags to confirm accuracy and that the error decomposition and failure predictor track observed per-word and per-condition errors, turning the anecdotal favorable trade-off into a measured mechanistic explanation while yielding a model strictly more general than either endpoint.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 23 turns / 31 tool calls

**Motivation.** POS taggers all plateau near 95-96 percent (Church 1988; the 1992 equivalence-class HMM tagger; Kupiec 1989; the 1989 rule-acquisition tagger), yet none explains the residual errors. Kupiec adds second-order states by hand, noting extra context does not forcibly fix an error since usage must be genuinely distinct in the language, but never says which contexts, how much, or how to predict this; the 1992 tagger leaves systematic errors to manual tuning. Gap: are residual errors intrinsic ambiguity or model artifacts, and what predicts whether more context helps?

**Method.** Three steps. (1) Align errors of a trigram HMM, an equivalence-class HMM, and a rule tagger on one corpus; shared errors isolate the Markov/locality failure. (2) Per ambiguity class, measure conditional-entropy reduction from expanding context one position with a significance test; large reduction predicts a fixable context, near-zero an intrinsic ceiling. (3) Split only diagnostic-flagged states, backing off elsewhere with smoothing. Validate that the diagnostic predicts error reduction, that selective augmentation beats uniform higher-order models with fewer parameters, and that residual errors sit in low-information contexts.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** These works share an unexplained puzzle. Church 1988, DeRose 1988, Jelinek 1985, and Cutting et al 1992 reach 95 to 99 percent POS accuracy using only local surface statistics and crude independence assumptions, with no syntactic structure. Hindle 1989 calls this surprising, noting earlier categorical surface-rule systems by Klein and Simmons 1963 and Greene and Rubin 1971 did only moderately on the same information, yet his corpus-acquired rules inside a deterministic parser match the probabilistic systems. Kupiec 1989 shows a first-order HMM systematically fails on constructions and needs hand-designed state chains. These facts co-occur with no causal account, and the field compares systems only by one aggregate accuracy number. Missing is a mechanistic explanation of what information actually drives disambiguation, why frequency-weighted models beat hard rules on identical features, and why residual errors persist.

**Method.** Build a framework unifying the statistical HMM and symbolic rule-based traditions in one representation, then decompose the accuracy puzzle causally. First, cast every tagger as a map from a bounded context window to a category choice: the HMM Viterbi decision decomposes into transition and lexical log-scores, while Klein-Simmons, Greene-Rubin, and Hindle rules are ordered hard predicates over the same window, making all comparable as weighted versus hard disambiguation functions over identical features on the tagged Brown Corpus. Second, extract implicit rules from a trained HMM by probing each ambiguity class using Kupiec equivalence classes, ablating and perturbing context to find the minimal context that flips a decision, aligning them with hand-written and acquired rules. Third, run matched swap experiments separating three mechanisms: representation surface versus syntactic, knowledge source hand-authored versus corpus-estimated, and conflict resolution hard ordering versus soft frequency-weighted preference, for instance giving categorical rules frequency-weighted resolution and stripping the HMM to hard majority rules, to test whether the stochastic advantage is soft corpus-derived preference rather than richer representation. Fourth, build a shared error taxonomy classifying each residual error as local-surface-resolvable, phrase-level, full-syntax-requiring, or genuinely ambiguous, showing Kupiec state chains and Hindle parser rules solve the same phrase-dependency phenomenon. Fifth, synthesize into a single explanatory account plus a hybrid that adds explicit non-local constraints only where the taxonomy proves local statistics insufficient, demonstrating the two literatures are complementary regions of one design space.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** All statistical taggers and language models in these sources output a single most-likely decision and are judged by one aggregate accuracy number (~96% in Cutting et al. 1992; Church 1988; Jelinek 1985), which masks a serious reliability/failure gap. Errors are concentrated, not uniform: the augmenting-HMM paper reports ~89% on ambiguous words vs 95.6% overall, over a third of Brown-corpus tokens are categorially ambiguous (Hindle 1989), and the phoneme-to-text paper attributes failures to out-of-dictionary proper names/technical terms and bad homonym choices. Yet every system commits to one Viterbi-best tag with no per-token indication of trustworthiness, and unknown words are handled by crude suffix/open-class guessing with no reliability signal. Three risks follow: silent-error brittleness, since downstream consumers built on the tagger (NP parsing, word-sense disambiguation, grammatical-function assignment, deterministic parsing, phoneme-to-text) inherit confident-looking wrong tags indistinguishable from correct ones and propagate errors; domain fragility and non-reproducibility, since aggregate accuracy is corpus/genre specific and a Brown-tuned tagger gives no warning when unreliable on new genres, titles, tables, or ungrammatical text; and no mechanism to defer or abstain when unsure, though a modest hand-tagged bootstrap sample is already assumed available. None of the twelve works produce calibrated per-token uncertainty or a selective/abstaining workflow, leaving room for a system that makes reliability explicit and controllable.

**Method.** Build a deployable uncertainty-aware tagging pipeline as an artifact on top of the existing HMM tagger, turning a single-answer tagger into one that quantifies, calibrates, and acts on its reliability. First, posterior decoding: instead of only the Viterbi best path, reuse the already-computed forward and backward quantities to compute, per token, the full posterior marginal over tags given the whole sentence, essentially free given training code. Second, confidence signals: derive the chosen-tag posterior, top-1-vs-top-2 margin, and posterior entropy, plus provenance flags for unknown words and rare ambiguity classes. Third, a calibration module: using a modest held-out hand-tagged bootstrap sample, fit a reliability map (binning/isotonic) from confidence signal to empirical correctness so the score becomes a calibrated probability of correctness; report expected calibration error and fit per-genre maps to expose and correct domain fragility. Fourth, a selective-tagging/abstention controller: at a user-set risk threshold, emit a single tag when calibrated confidence is high, else abstain by emitting the candidate ambiguity class, route to a symbolic disambiguation-rule backoff in the spirit of Hindle's acquired rules, or flag for human review, producing a tunable risk-coverage tradeoff. Fifth, confidence-annotated output and an audit log: each token carries tag plus calibrated confidence plus provenance plus whether backoff was used, so downstream modules can down-weight or ignore unreliable tags and errors are reproducibly traceable. Sixth, evaluation: report risk-coverage and calibration curves rather than only aggregate accuracy, with breakdowns on high-risk subsets (ambiguous and unknown words) and across genres/corpora, showing the system converts silent errors into flagged low-confidence decisions and recovers accuracy on hard cases at controlled coverage. The contribution is the engineered pipeline itself: a robust, uncertainty-reporting, abstaining tagger with calibration and audit infrastructure that addresses the silent-error, domain-fragility, and reproducibility risks left open by prior single-answer taggers.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Every tagger here—rule-based (TAGGIT 1971), statistically optimized (DeRose 1988; Church 1988), and HMM-based (Jelinek 1985; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992)—commits to a single best tag per word and passes it downstream with no measure of reliability. This is a failure/risk gap. Errors are silent: at ~96% accuracy the ~4% wrong tags look identical to correct ones, and the Cutting phrase recognizer and CatchWord WSD explicitly rely on tagger output, so each wrong tag silently corrupts every layer above it. The models are brittle under distribution shift: trained on one genre (Brown, Grolier's) and handling unknown words only via suffix-guessed ambiguity classes, they degrade silently on new genres with no warning of being out-of-distribution. They also carry a hidden genre bias invisible to users. Crucially, the forward-backward algorithm already computes full posterior tag marginals, but these systems discard that information to pick one path, leaving calibrated reliability, graceful abstention, and automatic failure detection unexplored.

**Method.** Build a confidence-aware, selectively-abstaining tagger that exposes an HMM tagger's own uncertainty. (1) Signals: run standard forward-backward to get per-token posterior tag distributions and derive posterior entropy, top-1/top-2 margin, ambiguity-class size, and an unknown/suffix-guessed flag. (2) Calibration: fit a monotone calibrator on a small held-out hand-tagged set so the score is an interpretable probability of correctness; validate with reliability diagrams and risk-coverage curves. (3) Selective tagging with symbolic fallback: below an abstention threshold, flag tokens instead of silently committing, and route them to automatically-acquired symbolic disambiguation rules (Hindle 1989) that use richer syntactic context; measure how often the hybrid corrects HMM errors on the high-risk cases. (4) Distribution-shift detection: aggregate calibrated confidence over a document; a drop relative to the in-domain baseline is a label-free out-of-distribution signal, tested by shifting across genres. (5) Confidence-driven active learning: send the least-confident sentences to annotation and re-estimate, quantifying accuracy recovered per labeled sentence versus random selection. (6) Downstream evaluation: feed confidence-filtered tags into the Church/Cutting phrase recognizer and a homograph WSD step and show that abstaining on low-reliability tags reduces downstream false positives at controlled coverage cost. This needs only resources these papers already use, adds negligible cost over forward-backward, and turns an opaque, brittle single-best tagger into one that reports when it is likely wrong, abstains, flags out-of-domain input, and directs effort where it reduces risk.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** These works validate POS taggers almost entirely with one aggregate accuracy figure (about 96 percent in Church 1988 and Cutting 1992, 90 percent in Klein and Simmons 1963, 95.6 percent in Kupiec 1989), which hides reliability risks that matter because taggers feed downstream systems such as NP and phrase parsing in Church and Cutting, phoneme-to-text in the Markov work, and word-sense and grammatical-function assignment in Cutting. Kupiec reports 95.6 percent ignoring unknown words but only 89 percent on ambiguous words, so errors concentrate exactly where disambiguation is needed yet stay invisible in the headline number. Evaluation is in-domain, since Church and Cutting train and test on halves of the same Brown Corpus and Klein and Simmons on scientific text, leaving genre and domain-shift brittleness untested. Unsupervised HMM taggers depend on seeded lexicons, ambiguity and equivalence classes, and biased Baum-Welch initialization, with no study of sensitivity to lexicon coverage, corpus size, or initialization. Kupiec also notes the correct tag is not always obvious and idioms were ruled incorrect, revealing gold-standard inconsistency that inflates error and breaks cross-study comparison. No source maps where, why, and how reliably taggers fail.

**Method.** Build an Empirical Failure Map, a standardized stratified diagnostic benchmark comparing the paradigms in these sources: rule-based coding (Klein and Simmons, Greene-Rubin, Hindle), supervised HMMs (Church), unsupervised equivalence-class HMMs (Cutting, Kupiec), and Markov-source models (Jelinek). Step 1: stratified error decomposition, re-scoring each tagger by lexical ambiguity class, word-frequency band, unknown status, and sentence position to convert one number into a competence profile. Step 2: domain-shift stress test, training on one genre and evaluating cross-genre to quantify accuracy loss and error-profile shift. Step 3: training-reliability audit, varying lexicon coverage, ambiguity-class granularity, corpus size, and Baum-Welch initialization and reporting the variance of overall and per-class error. Step 4: gold-standard reliability analysis, doubly annotating a hard-cases subset of ambiguous words, idioms, and fragments with an explicit adjudication protocol to separate genuine errors from ill-defined labels and estimate the ceiling. Step 5: downstream propagation, feeding outputs into the NP and phrase recognition of Church and Cutting to measure which upstream error types most damage chunks. Deliverables: a reusable stratified evaluation protocol, an error-annotated test set, and a paradigm-by-error-axis failure map with reliability intervals.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Stochastic POS taggers of this era — Church's stochastic parts program (1988), Kupiec's augmented phrase-dependent HMM (1989), and Cutting et al.'s practical HMM tagger (1992) — are judged by a single aggregate figure ("accuracy exceeds 96%") that masks a reliability/brittleness problem the papers themselves document but do not solve. First, errors are concentrated, not uniform: Kupiec reports ~95.6% overall but only ~89% on genuinely ambiguous words, and unknown/out-of-lexicon words are handled by ad hoc suffix heuristics and biased initial probabilities. The aggregate score gives downstream consumers (the noun-phrase parser, word-sense disambiguator, and the phoneme-to-text/speech decoders of Jelinek 1985 and the French stenotypy work) no way to know which tags to trust, so a wrong tag propagates silently. Second, all these systems emit a single Viterbi-best sequence with no calibrated per-token confidence, even though the forward-backward (Baum-Welch) machinery they already run makes exact posterior marginals available for free; reliability information present in the model is discarded. Third, model improvement is reactive and unprincipled: Kupiec builds his augmented state network by hand from post-hoc analysis of errors, fixing one systematic mistake at a time (the "to"-infinitive case, adjective-or-noun-after-determiner). This is not reproducible or transferable and gives no account of where the model is uncertain versus merely wrong. The gap: there is no formal characterization of when, where, and why a stochastic tagger's decisions are unreliable, and no principled rule for when a tag should be trusted, abstained on, or deferred. Without this, "96% accuracy" is an unsafe contract for any downstream system.

**Method.** A formal, decision-theoretic reliability framework layered on existing forward-backward/Viterbi machinery, so it runs on Church-, Kupiec-, and Cutting-style HMMs. (1) Formal model: recast tagging as Bayesian decision-making over the HMM posterior rather than MAP-sequence decoding. Using the forward (alpha) and backward (beta) quantities already computed in Baum-Welch, define per-token posterior marginals gamma_i(t)=P(tag_i=t | full sentence) over each word's ambiguity class, replacing the single Viterbi label with a full posterior at every position. (2) Uncertainty taxonomy (formal core): prove a chain-rule decomposition of each token's marginal entropy H(gamma_i) into additive sources — lexical/emission ambiguity from B, contextual/transition uncertainty from A and neighbors, and coverage/epistemic uncertainty from open-class unknown-word mass and sparse counts. This yields a principled distinction between aleatoric uncertainty (genuine ambiguity) and epistemic uncertainty (OOV/data-sparsity) that prior heuristics conflate. (3) Confidence and risk bound (theorem): define token confidence as the posterior margin m_i = gamma_i(top1) - gamma_i(top2). Prove a bound linking margin and H(gamma_i) to token-level Bayes error, P(error_i) <= f(m_i). Under misclassification cost c_e and abstention cost c_a, derive that the Bayes-optimal selective tagger abstains exactly when m_i is below threshold theta*=g(c_a,c_e), giving a provable risk-coverage tradeoff that converts aggregate accuracy into a tunable guarantee. (4) Principled deferral replacing hand-built augmentation: route low-confidence tokens by uncertainty type — high aleatoric tokens are abstained/marked; high epistemic (OOV/sparse) tokens are deferred to a secondary resolver (rules or a locally higher-order state chain). Higher-order states are added precisely at the states the decomposition flags as carrying resolvable contextual uncertainty, making Kupiec's ad hoc augmentation systematic and reproducible. (5) Evaluation on the Brown corpus with the Cutting/Kupiec HMM: verify the risk bound empirically; plot risk-coverage curves and calibration/reliability diagrams for the margin; confirm abstained/deferred tokens concentrate the documented ambiguous- and unknown-word errors; and show uncertainty-targeted augmentation automatically recovers the manual corrections. Deliverables: the entropy-decomposition theorem, the margin-to-error bound, the optimal abstention rule, and a taxonomy of tagger failure modes.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Unsupervised HMM POS taggers (Jelinek 1985; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992) need only a lexicon and unlabeled text and reach 96% accuracy, but their reliability rests on fragile human crutches. Baum-Welch is sensitive to starting values: Cutting et al. manually bias initial probabilities (asserting most unknown words are nouns, or hand-setting infinitive-marker transitions so indistinguishable states like the two tags of "to" separate) to avoid poor local optima. Kupiec builds the higher-order state-chain structure by hand from error analysis and linguistic knowledge. These interventions are brittle, non-reproducible, corpus and language specific, and hard-code human bias. Errors concentrate where the crutches are weakest (rare ambiguity classes, degenerate state pairs, unknown words), yet the tagger gives no signal of when a decision is untrustworthy. No prior work here offers a principled automatic way to pick initializations or structure, quantify variance, or flag unsafe outputs.

**Method.** Build a reliability-driven optimization pipeline replacing hand-tuned initialization and structure with automatic search plus calibrated uncertainty, reusing existing primitives (ambiguity classes, Baum-Welch, Viterbi, lexicon, small held-out set). First, initialization search: run multi-start Baum-Welch, sampling K starting points from a structured space of hyperparameters (per-class tag-realization skew, unknown-word open-class priors, transition-bias magnitudes), selecting the model that maximizes held-out agreement or, without labels, held-out likelihood plus a stability score, via coarse-to-fine greedy search so biases become data-selected auditable quantities. Second, uncertainty screening: across restarts measure variance of each token's Viterbi tag and of parameters, and expose forward-backward posterior entropy as per-token confidence so the tagger flags low-confidence outputs. Third, automatic structure search: start from the first-order ambiguity-class HMM and greedily split states or extend context only where a split reduces held-out error or entropy beyond a description-length penalty. Fourth, active supervision: rank decisions by posterior uncertainty times corpus frequency and spend a tiny human-tagging budget only on those to anchor the search. Evaluate on Brown and a mismatched genre against hand-tuned baselines on accuracy (rare classes and unknown words), reproducibility across seeds, confidence calibration, and labels per accuracy point.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The statistical POS taggers here (Kupiec's Baum-Welch HMM tagger, the phrase-dependent augmented HMM, Church's stochastic parts program) rest on two strong assumptions that create a failure and risk gap. First, closed-lexicon brittleness: each word is confined to the ambiguity class its dictionary entry permits, and the model gives zero probability to any tag outside that class. If a lexicon omits a legitimate tag for a known word, or a token is malformed by OCR or ungrammatical text, the correct tag is unreachable, so the tagger fails silently with no way to detect or repair the defect. Unknown words get suffix fallbacks but known words with incomplete entries do not, and reported accuracy is measured on clean text with curated lexicons while ignoring unknown words, hiding this brittleness. Second, unreliable uncalibrated output: Baum-Welch is seeded with hand-crafted biased starting values, so estimates depend on subjective priors and are initialization-sensitive, a reproducibility and bias concern, and the taggers emit one Viterbi sequence with no per-token confidence, so downstream users inherit unflagged errors. These works leave room for a tagger that stays accurate under incomplete lexicons and noisy text and quantifies its own reliability.

**Method.** Extend the unsupervised HMM tagger to weaker assumptions, a noisy incomplete lexicon and noisy text, while exposing calibrated uncertainty, reusing Baum-Welch and forward-backward. Soft leaky ambiguity classes replace the hard restriction to listed tags with a mixture where most emission mass comes from the lexicon class and a small escape mass may emit any tag via a suffix and morphology model applied to every token, so the correct tag is reachable when an entry is incomplete or a token corrupted, with escape mass re-estimated per class. Posterior confidence computes per-token marginals via forward-backward and outputs the max-marginal tag plus posterior as a confidence score, flagging or abstaining on low-confidence tokens. Initialization-robust ensembling trains several models from perturbed random starts, uses inter-model disagreement as an uncertainty estimate, and averages posteriors. A self-correcting lexicon loop aggregates posterior evidence so a word repeatedly drawing high mass on an unlisted tag triggers adding it while an unsupported listed tag is flagged spurious, then re-runs Baum-Welch and iterates. Evaluation on the Brown-corpus lexicon and tagset injects lexicon corruption by dropping and adding tags and compares degradation of the leaky model versus the hard-constraint HMM, injects OCR-style noise, measures confidence calibration and error-detection AUC on held-out hand-tagged text, and measures recovery of dropped and spurious tags. Success is graceful degradation under noise, calibrated confidence, and lexicon recovery within the same lexicon-plus-unlabeled-text budget.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 21 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior statistical POS taggers such as Church 1988, Cutting 1992, Kupiec 1989, and Jelinek 1985 emit one best tag per word with no per token reliability, so about four percent of errors propagate silently downstream. Errors cluster at brittle points: unknown words, sparse contexts needing ad hoc smoothing, and systematic mistakes Kupiec fixed by hand built state chains, while Baum Welch training is unstable across initializations. No listed work provides calibrated per token risk, leaving room for a robustification contribution.

**Method.** Build a confidence calibrated self monitoring HMM tagger using forward backward posterior marginals for per token confidence, calibrated on held out data via reliability diagrams and expected calibration error. Add a disagreement signal across the HMM posterior, a deleted interpolation category ngram, and acquired disambiguation rules, plus a low confidence unknown word suffix model. Use an abstention threshold to defer uncertain tokens, yielding a risk coverage curve, and run multiple Baum Welch initializations to quantify and reduce training variance. Evaluate on the Brown corpus for error concentration in low confidence tokens, calibration quality, risk coverage AUC, capture of the systematic errors Kupiec fixed by hand, downstream noun phrase parsing gains, and training reproducibility.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** POS tagging is the foundational first step feeding parsing, word-sense disambiguation, and phoneme-to-text decoding (Cutting 1992; Church 1988; the stenotypy work). Yet every tagger here commits to one answer with no signal of trustworthiness, and the two paradigms are brittle in complementary ways. Statistical HMM taggers (Church, DeRose, Cutting/Kupiec) use only local bigram/trigram context and silently fail on phrase-dependent long-distance dependencies (Kupiec 1989: about 89 percent on ambiguous words), idioms, and unknown words. Rule-based disambiguators (Klein-Simmons 1963; Greene-Rubin TAGGIT, which left about 23 percent of Brown for manual correction) are interpretable but less accurate and fragile. Hindle 1989 shows acquired rules can rival probabilistic methods, and the phoneme-to-text work shows Markov plus grammar beats either alone, but via one global interpolation still emitting a single committed tag. The failure/risk gap: no method quantifies per-decision reliability, flags at-risk tokens, or reconciles disagreeing statistical versus symbolic evidence where each is unreliable. Tagging errors propagate invisibly downstream, so this missing calibrated, abstaining, cross-paradigm reliability is a real risk the prior works leave open.

**Method.** Build a unified probabilistic-symbolic tagger that reconciles the two literatures with calibrated reliability and selective abstention. Step one, uncertainty-aware statistical layer: run an HMM tagger (Cutting/Church/DeRose) but compute per-token posterior marginals via forward-backward rather than a single Viterbi choice; derive token uncertainty from posterior entropy and top-two margin and calibrate it on held-out tagged text so confidence matches accuracy, keeping Kupiec equivalence classes for vocabulary independence. Step two, symbolic evidence layer: automatically acquire disambiguation rules from tagged text (Hindle 1989), augmented with phrase-dependent state-chain features (Kupiec) for long-distance context and morphological suffix cues for unknown words, each rule giving an interpretable justification. Step three, context-adaptive reconciliation: for tokens flagged at-risk by calibrated uncertainty, fuse statistical posterior and symbolic evidence with a per-token log-linear combiner, generalizing the phoneme-to-text global Markov-plus-grammar interpolation into a decision reweighted by each stream local reliability. Step four, selective prediction: when fused confidence stays low, abstain and flag the token for review, making the TAGGIT manual pass targeted. Step five, evaluation: calibration curves, error reduction on ambiguous and unknown and long-distance words, coverage-versus-accuracy of abstention, cross-genre robustness, and reduced error propagation into a downstream task such as Church noun-phrase parsing or WSD.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle gap: unsupervised HMM taggers are sold on minimal resources (only a lexicon plus unlabeled text, vocabulary independence via ambiguity classes, easy retargeting to new languages; Kupiec 1989; Cutting 1992), yet in practice accuracy comes from manual expert engineering: category splits, zeroed transitions, hand-picked word classes, and hand-designed augmented state chains for selective higher-order context. This contradicts the minimal-resource claim. Hindle 1989 automates discovery of disambiguation distinctions and halves error versus hand rules, but needs a hand-built parser and the tagged Brown Corpus. So automation exists only under supervision while the unsupervised regime stays stuck with hand surgery. This matters because manual tuning blocks cheap tagging of new genres and languages, and uniformly raising HMM order causes parameter proliferation.

**Method.** Build a self-refining tagger builder that automatically induces selective higher-order structure and equivalence-class granularity for an HMM tagger from only a lexicon and raw unlabeled text, using Baum-Welch, Viterbi, state tying, and an MDL criterion. Loop: initialize ambiguity classes from the lexicon and train a first-order HMM with Baum-Welch; run label-free diagnosis over held-out unlabeled text to find stress points such as low top-two Viterbi margin or high posterior entropy over the ambiguity class, aggregated by surrounding category-sequence template; propose refinements for stressed templates by inserting an augmented state chain, promoting a frequent word to its own class, or pruning transitions; accept a candidate only if held-out unlabeled likelihood gain exceeds the description-length cost of added parameters; re-estimate with Baum-Welch and state tying and iterate. Output a compact selectively-higher-order HMM tagger plus a readable log of induced chains and splits, built with zero tagged training and retargetable by rerunning. Evaluate accuracy and parameter count versus first-order, uniform second-order, and Kupiec hand-augmented HMMs, test recovery of hand-designed refinements, and test retargeting on a new genre.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle/contradiction. The same HMM tagger reaches ~95-96% both when trained supervised on the tagged Brown corpus (Church 1988) and when trained fully unsupervised with Baum-Welch on raw text (Jelinek 1985; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992), seeming to show annotation is dispensable. Yet the unsupervised systems only reach parity by re-injecting heavy human labor elsewhere: Kupiec hand-designs state-chain topology from manual error analysis, and Cutting et al. hand-bias the initial transition/emission matrices from linguistic priors. So "no tagged corpus" hides a migration of effort into error analysis and topology engineering.

The deeper contradiction: Baum-Welch maximizes text likelihood, not tagging accuracy. Kupiec says adding structure "does not forcibly correct the error - the actual patterns of category usage must be distinct in the language." Thus a structural class of systematic errors (adverbial vs prepositional, agreement across phrases, idioms) is invisible to the likelihood objective and cannot be fixed by any unsupervised enrichment. Meanwhile Hindle (1989) shows a little targeted signal turned into automatically acquired symbolic rules halves error versus hand rules. The field oscillates between a misaligned unsupervised objective plus manual patching, or full annotation. No one has aligned model refinement with tagging accuracy while automating the error-analysis/topology labor the unsupervised methods secretly rely on. That is the gap.

**Method.** A self-diagnosing, margin-targeted, minimally-supervised refinement loop that spends annotation exactly where unsupervised likelihood maximization is blind.

1. Baseline: train an ambiguity-class HMM by Baum-Welch on raw text, no labels.

2. Diagnose via posterior margin: run forward-backward to get each token's tag posterior. Flag decisions with low top-two margin, and near-degenerate state pairs whose emission distributions collapsed (the to-inf vs to-prep pathology). These automatically locate where likelihood and accuracy diverge, the cases Kupiec found by hand.

3. Discover error templates: cluster flagged tokens by target ambiguity class crossed with surrounding ambiguity-class context, ranked by frequency times uncertainty, replacing manual error analysis with data-driven surfacing of confusable contexts.

4. Targeted correction on top clusters only, two operators. Operator A: draw a tiny annotated sample preferentially from flagged clusters and acquire Hindle-style symbolic rules for just those contexts. Operator B: automatically instantiate extra states/state-chains for those contexts, re-estimate with tied distributions, keep only if held-out accuracy improves. A cost model chooses A or B per cluster to maximize corrected errors per unit effort.

5. Apply rules/expanded model as a correction layer over Viterbi; iterate 2-4 until flagged mass stops shrinking.

Evaluation on Brown: plot accuracy versus number of annotated tokens for pure Baum-Welch, full supervision, random small-sample supervision, and this loop. Hypotheses: EM-uncorrectable errors concentrate in low-margin clusters; a few hundred targeted annotations recover most of the gap to full supervision, beating equal random annotation; auto-grown state-chains match Kupiec's hand topology without manual error analysis. This resolves the puzzle by aligning refinement with accuracy and automating the hidden human labor. All components exist in the cited works, so it is feasible.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** A striking contradiction runs through this body of work on part-of-speech disambiguation. Purely surface stochastic taggers—first-order HMMs over ambiguity classes trained on unlabeled text (Church 1988; Cutting et al. 1992) and statistical optimizers (DeRose 1988)—report ~95–99% word accuracy while succeeding, in Hindle's words, essentially without reference to syntactic structure. Yet methods built on deep linguistic machinery reach the same aggregate accuracy: Hindle (1989) shows automatically acquired rules embedded in a deterministic parser cut error rates in half versus hand rules and match the stochastic taggers, and Kupiec (1989) shows that fixing an HMM's residual errors ("to" as infinitive-marker vs. preposition; adjective-vs-noun after a determiner) requires precisely the cross-phrase, higher-order syntactic conditioning the surface models lack. Hindle names the puzzle: it is tempting to conclude that robust handling of unrestricted text demands general probabilistic methods in preference to deeper linguistic knowledge—yet the same papers show linguistic structure is what fixes the hardest cases. This near-parity of aggregate accuracy is treated as evidence the paradigms are interchangeable, but no one has tested the question hidden beneath the headline number: do surface-statistical and structure-based methods succeed and fail on the same tokens, or on complementary ones? The 4–5% residual matters—Hindle notes that at these rates almost every sentence still contains a tagging error, so word accuracy is a misleading aggregate. If errors coincide, deeper linguistic knowledge is redundant; if complementary, the field's convergence on surface statistics discards recoverable signal. The prior works, each optimizing its own paradigm on its own tagset and reporting only a scalar accuracy, leave this unresolved.

**Method.** We propose an empirical-mapping study: a controlled, head-to-head diagnostic comparison of the disambiguation paradigms in these works, to locate and characterize where each succeeds and fails rather than report a single accuracy. (1) Common testbed and tagset harness: use the tagged Brown Corpus (shared across Church, Cutting, Hindle) with a fixed train/test split, plus an explicit tag-mapping layer projecting all outputs onto a canonical coarse tagset and, separately, a fine one, so errors are comparable and tagset granularity becomes an experimental factor. (2) Representative implementations: (a) a surface first-order HMM over ambiguity classes trained on unlabeled text (Church/Cutting); (b) a selectively higher-order state-chain HMM adding cross-phrase conditioning driven by error analysis (Kupiec); (c) a rule-based disambiguator combining Greene-Rubin categorical surface rules with Hindle-style rules automatically acquired from tagged text and tied to parser state. (3) Diagnostic partitioning: annotate every ambiguous token with attributes operationalizing the paradigms' claims—its ambiguity class; the minimal disambiguating context (estimated by progressively masking left/right context and by whether resolution needs information beyond the local phrase, i.e., long-distance/cross-phrase); whether surface cues alone suffice; lexicon status (known vs. unknown/open-class guessed by suffix); and genre. (4) Empirical maps: per-paradigm accuracy as a function of each attribute, per-ambiguity-class confusion matrices, and word-level vs. sentence-level accuracy curves exposing how the 96% word figure translates into sentence-level failure rates. (5) Complementarity analysis: pairwise error-overlap (agreement matrices, Venn decompositions) and an oracle-combination upper bound quantifying how much of each system's residual error the others would have avoided; isolate the hard core all paradigms miss and characterize it linguistically (long-distance dependency, semantic disambiguation, true ambiguity). (6) Controlled sweeps: vary training-set size (Cutting's ~3,000-sentence regime and larger), lexicon coverage, and tagset granularity to test whether the aggregate parity is coincidental—whether error profiles diverge or converge as resources change. This replaces a single misleading accuracy number with an error map revealing whether surface statistics and linguistic structure are redundant or complementary, and pinpoints the phenomena (e.g., Kupiec's determiner-noun/adjective cross-phrase cases) where deeper knowledge remains necessary—yielding a reusable diagnostic benchmark and error taxonomy that reframes how tagging progress is measured.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 20 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** A puzzle runs through statistical POS tagging. Church (1988) measures that the word is the dominant cue: tag entropy given the word is about 0.25 bits versus about 2 bits given the two neighboring tags, so lexical identity carries roughly eight times more disambiguating information than context. Yet the design that made HMM taggers practical does the opposite: Kupiec (1989) and Cutting et al. (1992) collapse fifty thousand words into a few hundred ambiguity classes, so dog and see become indistinguishable, then invest effort in the weaker signal via hand-built state chains adding selective higher-order context. The patches reveal the tension: Cutting keeps common words individual because data are sufficient, and Church notes see is not really class-ambiguous. The field thus suppresses its most informative feature and manually props up its least informative one, using frequency thresholds and intuition rather than a derived principle. Gap: no formal account explains why this favorable trade-off holds despite the entropy numbers, nor turns the hand-tuned choices of which words to lexicalize and which contexts to expand into an automatic criterion, reintroducing the manual engineering that unsupervised HMM training (Jelinek 1985) aimed to remove.

**Method.** A formal refinement-lattice theory of conditioning in Markov taggers that unifies lexicalization, ambiguity classes, and state chains and derives when each is optimal. Ontology: place the tag conditioning state on a lattice with a lexical axis from a word ambiguity class down to the word, and a context axis of state-chain order zero, one, two; each existing tagger is a frontier, Cutting being class plus first-order with common words individual, Kupiec being class plus hand-picked chains. Objective: for a node with count N and tag-alphabet size K, write expected excess risk as a bias-variance and minimum-description-length decomposition, approximation error N times the node conditional tag entropy plus estimation cost about K minus one over two N nats; refining a parent into children changes code length by N times the mutual-information gain minus a penalty proportional to added parameters times log N. Theorem: greedy refinement of every positive-change split yields the code-length-minimizing frontier, lexicalizing or extending context only when information gain beats a penalty scaling as one over N; with Church lexical mutual information of about 1.75 bits versus weaker context, this predicts lexicalizing frequent ambiguous words, pooling rare words, and adding higher-order context only where residual entropy stays high, making Cutting threshold and Kupiec chains corollaries and resolving the paradox because pooled classes are dominated by low-count words whose lexical estimates would be unreliable. Soft version: define emission and transition probabilities as a deleted-interpolation mixture over lattice paths, generalizing Jelinek interpolation of Markov sources, with a finer node weight rising with count and gain, weights trained by forward-backward on held-out text so no pre-tagged corpus is needed. Validation: on the Brown corpus with only a lexicon, grow the frontier automatically and compare accuracy, parameter count, and per-word error against a uniform ambiguity-class HMM, Cutting model, and Kupiec chains, testing that it matches or beats hand-engineered frontiers, that lexicalized words are the predicted high-frequency high-information ones, and that gains track the predicted code-length change.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle. Statistical POS taggers of this era advertise being annotation-free and vocabulary-independent: an HMM trained on raw text with Baum-Welch given only a lexicon (A Practical Part-of-Speech Tagger, 1992; Augmenting a HMM for phrase-dependent word tagging, 1989). Yet their reported accuracy depends on heavy hand-crafted knowledge engineering that reintroduces the expert labor the approach claims to remove, now hidden in the model structure. Cutting et al. hand-set initial biases (unknown words biased to noun; transitions out of the infinitive-marker zeroed). Kupiec refines his model through many manual moves driven by human error analysis and linguistic knowledge: splitting auxiliary/verb categories, individualizing about 100 frequent words, editing specific transitions, and hand-designing higher-order state chains. This exposes an unresolved tradeoff: uniform higher-order conditioning (trigram, Church 1988) proliferates parameters that cannot be reliably estimated, while compact equivalence-class models discard word-specific evidence. The right model is a heterogeneous mixture that only a human currently finds by inspecting errors. Worse, Baum-Welch maximizes likelihood, not accuracy, so more capacity or iterations need not help. Acquiring Disambiguation Rules from Text (1989) shows automatically acquired rules cut a parser's error about 50% versus hand-written rules, evidence automation should win. The gap: no one has automated the design of the tagger's structure, leaving the decisive step manual, unprincipled, and non-portable across corpora and tagsets.

**Method.** Turn manual HMM-tagger refinement into a budget-constrained structure search that automatically allocates parameters where they most reduce tagging error. Base model: a compact first-order equivalence-class HMM (Kupiec 1989), initialized from lexicon frequencies and trained by Baum-Welch and Viterbi. Operator library: small automatically instantiable local operators reproducing the human moves: split an equivalence class or promote a frequent word to its own class; split a category state; insert a selective second-order state chain for one specific left context; insert a phrase-skip state chain across a simple intervening phrase. Each operator has a known parameter cost. Automatic error attribution replaces human error analysis: on unlabeled text, compute posterior tag distributions and localize systematic ambiguity via tag-posterior entropy and context-conditioned confusion counts to generate concrete operator instances. Efficiency scoring: rank candidates by expected error reduction per added parameter on a development signal, either a small tagged seed set or a self-agreement signal (Viterbi-tag stability across bootstrap-resampled Baum-Welch runs). Operators are accepted on validation error, never likelihood, countering the likelihood-not-accuracy misalignment. Greedy or beam search under a parameter budget: each round instantiate candidates, warm-start Baum-Welch from current parameters, keep operators whose validation error strictly decreases, discard the rest, repeat until budget is spent; apply Jelinek-Mercer state tying automatically to duplicated states. Initialization search: treat hand-set biases as hyperparameters set by coarse coordinate ascent. Evaluation: compare against a fixed first-order equivalence-class HMM, a uniform smoothed trigram HMM, and Kupiec's hand-crafted augmented network across at least two corpora and tagsets, testing that search matches or beats hand-tuned structure at equal or lower parameter count, ports without re-engineering, and avoids the more-capacity-hurts failure because operators are gated on accuracy.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** A puzzle runs through this body of work on grammatical category assignment. The modern statistical taggers are advertised as needing "few resource requirements — only a lexicon and some unlabeled training text" (Cutting et al. 1992), and Kupiec (1989) even claims a "vocabulary-independent" model via equivalence classes. Yet three facts from these same sources contradict that framing:

1. The dictionary is actually the load-bearing resource, not the model. Church (1988) reports that "most errors are attributable to defects in the lexicon; remarkably few errors are related to the inadequacies of the extremely over-simplified grammar." So the component treated as a cheap given (the word→possible-tags lexicon) is simultaneously (a) the dominant error source and (b) unavailable or wrong for new domains, genres, and languages — exactly the "robust, unrestricted text" settings these papers target.

2. The very trick used to claim vocabulary independence undermines itself. Kupiec's equivalence classes (129 classes covering a 30,000-word dictionary) still require the dictionary to assign every word to its class, and by collapsing words they explicitly discard the word-specific lexical bias that is the single strongest predictor of a tag ("dog" prefers noun, "see" prefers verb, as Kupiec notes). So the mechanism that reduces the parameter cost of the dictionary still depends on the dictionary and throws away the information the dictionary is most valuable for.

3. The oldest source dissolves the assumption entirely: Klein & Simmons (1963) coded over 90% of running text with NO dictionary, using only suffix/orthographic tests, a tiny closed-class word list, and a context-frame test. Cutting and Kupiec quietly rely on this same signal — suffixes "provide strong cues to words' possible categories" — but only as a last-resort fallback for unknown words.

The contradiction: morphology plus context is strong enough to be the primary evidence (90% with zero dictionary in 1963), errors in statistical taggers come precisely from the hand-built lexicon, and yet the field kept the lexicon as the indispensable core and demoted morphology/distribution to a patch for out-of-vocabulary words. No source has asked whether a statistical tagger can be trained with no dictionary at all, inducing each word's ambiguity class AND its lexical bias from raw text. This gap matters because it determines whether these taggers can truly travel to new domains, noisy input (OCR, phoneme/speech transcription as in the French stenotypy study), and languages without months of lexicon construction.

**Method.** Relax the strongest hidden assumption shared by Church (1988), Cutting et al. (1992), and Kupiec (1989) — that a word→possible-tags lexicon is given — and make HMM tagging function from raw, unlabeled, possibly noisy text alone. The approach induces both the ambiguity classes and the per-word lexical bias that the lexicon used to supply, then trains the standard HMM by forward–backward (Baum-Welch) as before.

Step 1 — Data-driven equivalence classes (replacing dictionary-derived ones). For every word type in the raw corpus, build two signatures: (a) a morphological/orthographic signature generalizing Klein & Simmons' suffix tests and Cutting's suffix fallback — inflectional/derivational endings, capitalization, hyphenation, digits, prefix — and (b) a distributional context signature: the distribution over which high-frequency "anchor" tokens immediately precede and follow it. Cluster word types on the joint signature to obtain induced equivalence classes, directly realizing the "grammatical equivalence class" idea sketched in the Markov-source paper (1985) but obtained without any grammar or dictionary. A small seed set of unambiguous closed-class function words (determiners, prepositions, pronouns — few, stable, and the same list Klein & Simmons hand-listed) is used only to give the induced classes interpretable part-of-speech identities, not to tag content words.

Step 2 — Soft membership to recover per-word bias (fixing Kupiec's tradeoff). Instead of a hard class per word, represent each word type as a learned mixed-membership distribution over the induced classes, so the model retains that "dog" leans noun while "see" leans verb even when both belong to a noun-or-verb region. This restores the word-specific lexical preference that hard equivalence classes discard, at controlled parameter cost (the mixture is estimated jointly during training).

Step 3 — Unsupervised HMM training. Initialize emission/transition probabilities from the induced classes and morphological priors (e.g., bias open-class-looking words toward noun, as Cutting does for unknowns), then run Baum-Welch on the raw corpus to refine transition and emission probabilities and the soft memberships. This keeps the well-understood, cheap machinery of the existing taggers; only the source of the lexical parameters changes.

Step 4 — Broaden the operating regime. Because there is no dictionary to be "defective," evaluate exactly where the prior systems were assumed but never stress-tested: (i) a new domain/genre with no lexicon; (ii) a second language (French, matching Cutting's and the stenotypy study's setting) using only language-specific suffix rules and a closed-class seed list; and (iii) noisy input — degrade the text with OCR-like or phoneme-confusion errors (as in the phoneme-to-text transcription work) so that word identity itself is uncertain, and let the induced morphological+distributional evidence propose ambiguity classes robustly for corrupted tokens.

Evaluation and falsifiable claims. Compare against a lexicon-based HMM (Church/Cutting style) on held-out hand-tagged text. The central test of the puzzle: if Church is right that lexicon defects dominate errors, then a well-induced, corpus-matched lexicon should match or beat a generic hand-built one on out-of-domain and noisy text, even if it slightly trails on in-domain clean text. Ablations isolate the contribution of morphology alone (the Klein-Simmons regime), distributional clustering alone, and their combination, and measure how much the soft-membership lexical bias recovers relative to Kupiec-style hard classes. Success is defined as approaching the ~96% lexicon-based accuracy on matched text while retaining most of that accuracy under domain shift, language change, and input noise — regimes where the lexicon-dependent predecessors have no defined behavior.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Unsupervised HMM taggers (Church 1988; Kupiec 1992) report about 96 percent accuracy and are branded robust, and rule acquisition (Hindle 1989) reports under 2 percent training error, called a path toward more robust analyzers. Yet the same papers contradict these claims once failures are localized. Headline accuracy is dominated by trivially unambiguous frequent words; the augmenting-HMM paper (1989) drops from 95.6 percent overall to 89 percent on genuinely ambiguous words, and Hindle ambiguous-word error rises from 5 percent on training to 10 percent on test while overall test error is only 3 percent. Unknown words are excluded from reported accuracy and handled by weak suffix and open-class heuristics. And the HMM no-tagged-data advantage evaporates exactly at rare and ambiguous words where estimation is least reliable.
