The deeper gap: these taggers commit deterministically to one tag even when evidence is weak, and none can tell downstream consumers which tags to trust. Robustness is asserted globally but never quantified or managed where errors actually concentrate, so tail errors propagate silently.

**Method.** Build a risk-aware, uncertainty-calibrated tagger that replaces single-best commitment with abstention and concentrates linguistic resources where the statistical model is weak. First, keep the unsupervised equivalence-class HMM as base but run forward-backward for full per-token tag posteriors, and define a risk score from the top-two posterior margin, ambiguity-class entropy, and an unknown-word flag. Second, selective prediction: below a risk threshold emit the single tag; above it, abstain and emit a ranked shortlist with probabilities, converting silent errors into flagged uncertainty. Evaluate with risk-coverage curves and disaggregated accuracy for unambiguous versus ambiguous versus unknown words, attacking the measurement illusion. Third, targeted rule robustification: instead of Hindle global rules that overfit and still miss the tail, acquire a small library of local context rules only for the HMM high-risk or abstained contexts, with conditioning chosen by error analysis (the state-chain idea of the augmenting-HMM paper). Rules fire only when the HMM abstains, so the base covers the easy mass and rules generalize only over information-rich hard contexts, shrinking the train/test gap. Fourth, calibrated unknown words: replace the single suffix guess with a calibrated distribution over ambiguity classes folded into the same risk score. Validate under distribution shift (train on one Brown genre, test on another), reporting risk-coverage, disaggregated accuracy, and calibration. Success means higher accuracy-at-fixed-coverage on ambiguous and unknown words than any single paradigm, a smaller train/test gap than global rule acquisition, and graceful degradation under domain shift where naive single-best taggers fail silently.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** A sharp contradiction runs through this literature. Surface stochastic taggers such as Church 1988, DeRose 1988, the Baum-Welch HMM of Jelinek 1985 and Cutting 1992, and Kupiec reach very high accuracy essentially without reference to syntactic structure, and the Lancaster position claims enough quantitative data compensates for lack of linguistic knowledge. Earlier categorical rule systems such as Klein and Simmons 1963 and Greene and Rubin 1971 did worse, seemingly confirming structure is unnecessary. Yet the same successful systems, once pushed past their ceiling, smuggle structure back in: Kupiec's augmenting HMM adds phrase-dependent state chains designed from error analysis to capture word dependency across phrases, and Hindle 1989 shows symbolic disambiguation rules tied to a deterministic parser grammar match or beat probabilistic methods and improve when other linguistic parser components improve. The residual errors concentrate on exactly the phrase-dependent, long-distance phenomena a first-order surface Markov model cannot see. The puzzle is whether syntactic structure is needed for robust tagging: surface statistics say no while the error patterns say yes. Each tradition patches the same structural errors in isolation and in incompatible representations, and no work reconciles them or measures how much error genuinely requires structure.

**Method.** Unify the surface-statistical and symbolic-structural traditions into one self-supervised, error-driven tagging loop needing no pre-tagged corpus. A baseline HMM tagger uses word equivalence classes from Kupiec trained by Baum-Welch on raw text following Jelinek, plus a morphological analyzer following Klein and Simmons 1963 for unknown words. A deterministic partial parser in the Hindle Fidditch style annotates the same unlabeled corpus with phrase boundaries and complementation as weak supervision. The crux is a shared representation: structural context predicates such as crosses-phrase-boundary, precedes-past-participle, is-complement-of-verb, and infinitival-versus-prepositional uses, instantiable both as symbolic disambiguation rules following Hindle and as selective higher-order state-chain splits in the HMM following Kupiec, making rules and state augmentations two views of one object. In an error-driven loop each iteration compares HMM surface tags to the parser structural analysis; where they systematically disagree the parser constraint acts as weak supervision, the predicate best explaining the disagreement is induced and compiled at once into a lattice constraint and a targeted state-chain refinement, and HMM parameters are re-estimated by constrained Baum-Welch, iterating until no profitable refinement remains. Evaluation on held-out gold tags decomposes accuracy gains into surface versus structural contributions and catalogs which phenomenon each refinement resolves, thereby measuring how much error truly needs structure and yielding an interpretable tagger where parser improvements automatically upgrade the statistical model.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Every tagger in this literature buys accuracy with an expensive human-built resource. Church (1988) needs the manually tagged Brown corpus (TAGGIT disambiguated only 77 percent, the rest hand-corrected over years), and word-level models require large tagged corpora. The HMM line (Jelinek 1985, Kupiec 1989, Cutting et al. 1992) drops the tagged corpus via Baum-Welch and Kupiec vocabulary-independent equivalence classes, but all still require a manually constructed lexicon of ambiguity classes plus hand-crafted, language-specific suffix tables and a default open-class fallback for unknown words. Derouault and Merialdo (1986) still require full manual correction of the tagger output. Klein and Simmons (1963) avoid a dictionary using morphology and a small function-word table, but with static hand-written rules and no learning to resolve residual ambiguity. Retargeting a tagger to a new language, domain, or tagset thus remains gated by building or licensing a broad dictionary and authoring unknown-word heuristics, a deployment and scalability bottleneck. No source offers an end-to-end path from raw untagged text alone to a working tagger plus lexicon with minimal, targeted human effort.

**Method.** Build a self-contained tagger-bootstrapping workbench whose only inputs are a raw untagged corpus and a short seed list of closed-class function words with their categories. Outputs are an induced ambiguity-class lexicon, a tagged corpus, and a trained HMM tagger. Module 1, automatic ambiguity-class induction replacing the manual dictionary: for each word type compute a distributional signature from contexts anchored on the unambiguous seed function words, plus a morphological suffix and prefix signature in a trie (extending Cutting suffix guesser and Klein and Simmons morphotactics), then cluster types into a small set of Kupiec-style equivalence classes so unknown words fall into an existing class by suffix and context, removing hand-authored unknown-word rules. Module 2, unsupervised HMM: estimate transition and class-emission parameters via Baum-Welch on raw text with deleted-interpolation smoothing over the induced classes. Module 3, confidence-driven active-correction loop replacing whole-text manual correction: run forward-backward for per-token posteriors, rank types by uncertainty using posterior margin or entropy, show only the most uncertain types with sample contexts to a human who confirms or edits their class, then re-cluster and re-estimate in small batches, so human labor scales with genuine ambiguity rather than corpus size. Module 4, a tagset-mapping layer to collapse or split induced classes onto any desired tagset without re-labeling, then export the tagger. Evaluation with available resources: bootstrap from raw Brown text while ignoring gold tags in training, and measure accuracy versus the number of human class-confirmations; compare against a full hand-lexicon HMM as an upper bound and a no-correction unsupervised run as a lower bound; report the labeling budget needed to reach 95-96 percent accuracy and demonstrate cross-domain retargeting. The central contribution is the artifact: an integrated, reusable pipeline turning raw text into a tagger with only a seed function-word list and minimal uncertainty-targeted supervision.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** Every accurate tagger in this literature rests on one expensive hand-built resource. The HMM taggers of Cutting et al. (1992), Kupiec (1989/1992), DeRose (1988), Church (1988), and Jelinek (1985) removed the tagged training corpus via Baum-Welch training on unlabeled text, but replaced it with a second manual resource: a wide-coverage lexicon listing each word's set of possible parts of speech (its ambiguity class). Cutting et al. state it plainly: they consult a manually-constructed lexicon, and note exhaustive lexicons of this sort are expensive, if not impossible, to produce. Kupiec's parameter-saving equivalence classes are derived from a 30,000-word dictionary, and the rule-based lineage (Greene and Rubin 1971; the 1963 coding system) needs hand-authored word lists and suffix tests. Morphology is used only as a fallback for words missing from the manual lexicon. This is a Resource Bottleneck: such dictionaries cost heavily to build and maintain, are language- and tagset-specific, and do not exist for most languages or specialized genres, so deployment to new languages and domains stays expensive. The sources hint the signal lives in raw text (learnable suffix statistics, strong distributional context, a few frequent words dominating occurrences) but none combines these to eliminate the manual lexicon entirely.

**Method.** Build a lexicon-free HMM tagger whose per-word ambiguity classes are induced from raw text, so retargeting needs only unlabeled text plus a tiny anchor seed. (1) Morphological signature model: put all word types in a letter-trie (as Cutting et al. build suffix tables) but automatically discover productive word-final and orthographic signatures (capitalization, hyphenation, digits) by frequency and partitioning power, yielding a probabilistic prior over candidate tags. (2) Distributional induction: represent each word type by its distribution over the most frequent context words, cluster on left/right context vectors to form induced equivalence classes, the unsupervised analogue of Kupiec's dictionary-derived classes. Combine 1 and 2 to assign each word an induced ambiguity class, replacing lexicon look-up. (3) Train the HMM in the Cutting/Kupiec/Jelinek regime: states are tags, observations are induced ambiguity classes, first-order transitions, Baum-Welch on unlabeled text, deleted-interpolation smoothing, Viterbi decoding; frequent words keep individual observation parameters while the rest share their class, preserving parameter economy. (4) Minimal anchor seeding: fix HMM state identifiability (states defined only up to permutation) with a few dozen seed words, closed-class function words plus clear open-class exemplars, that pin induced states to readable tags and constrain initialization; this is far cheaper than a 30k-50k-entry dictionary. Evaluation: on Brown, discard the lexicon, train on raw text, and compare accuracy to gold tags and a full-lexicon baseline to quantify the cost of removing the dictionary; sweep retained-lexicon size (0, top-100, top-1000 words) to chart accuracy vs. manual effort; sweep seed size for minimal supervision; simulate low-resource deployment by hiding Brown's lexicon and, if available, applying the same pipeline to the referenced French text to test language independence. Success: approach ~96% full-lexicon accuracy using only raw text plus a tiny seed.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** These POS-tagging works are limited by a resource bottleneck: costly hand-tagged corpora. Reported accuracies are incomparable across differing corpora, tagsets, and lexicons, so the best allocation of a fixed annotation budget is unknown.

**Method.** Build an empirical benchmark that holds corpus and tagset fixed and sweeps annotation resources to map accuracy versus cost, producing trade-off curves for tagged-corpus size, lexicon coverage, unlabeled-text volume, and tagset granularity.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 14 tool calls

**Motivation.** Progress in part-of-speech tagging in this literature is gated by two expensive resources, and every method trades one costly resource for another rather than eliminating the bottleneck. (1) Hand-tagged corpora: Church's stochastic parts program relies on the tagged Brown corpus, which TAGGIT could only disambiguate to 77%, leaving years of manual correction (Greene & Rubin 1971; Francis & Kucera 1982); Derouault & Merialdo's bootstrap still needs iterative manual tagging. (2) Hand-built lexicons/dictionaries: Kupiec's and Cutting et al.'s HMM taggers remove the tagged corpus via Baum-Welch, but only by assuming a lexicon that supplies each word's "ambiguity class" (its set of permissible tags). Ambiguity classes are what make these models vocabulary-independent and estimable from moderate text (e.g., 129 classes covering a 30,000-word dictionary), yet the classes and the word-to-class map are simply read off a pre-existing 25,000-75,000-entry dictionary — exactly the large-dictionary cost Klein & Simmons (1963) tried to avoid with hand-written suffix/context rules (another manual-engineering cost). Moreover, the field lacks any principled account of HOW MANY ambiguity classes/parameters are actually needed and HOW MUCH raw text suffices for reliable estimation; the "vocabulary independence" and parameter-reduction claims (also motivating the state-chain refinements of Kupiec 1989) are asserted empirically but never derived. This leaves retargeting to new domains, tagsets, and languages ad hoc and resource-heavy, because it presupposes a curated lexicon and offers no formal stopping rule or data-budget estimate.

**Method.** Contribute a formal framework that removes both the tagged corpus and the hand-built lexicon by deriving how to induce the ambiguity-class lexicon itself from raw text, together with bounds that quantify the resulting resource savings.

(1) Two-level generative model. Formalize tagging as an HMM whose hidden states are categories with transitions P(c_i|c_{i-1}), but whose observations are ambiguity classes rather than words: a deterministic lexicon map L sends word w to a class kappa(w) ⊆ tags, and emission P(kappa|c) is supported only where c ∈ kappa. Show the emission parameter count is N·m (N tags, m classes), independent of vocabulary size v — turning the informal "vocabulary independence" claim into a definition.

(2) Estimation-error bound. Derive that the KL/variance error of the maximum-likelihood (Baum-Welch) estimate scales as O(N·m / T) in T training tokens, versus O(N·v / T) for word-level emission models. This proves and quantifies why moderate unlabeled text suffices, and yields a data-budget formula T(epsilon) needed to reach a target error epsilon.

(3) MDL objective for lexicon induction. Introduce a minimum-description-length objective to induce the class inventory AND the word->class map jointly from raw text with no dictionary and no tags: DL = L_model + L_data, where L_model encodes the class inventory plus each word's class assignment (compressed with Klein & Simmons-style suffix/morphology codes so that regular endings cheaply predict class), and L_data = -log P(text | HMM). Derive the granularity trade-off: merging words into coarser classes lowers L_model and estimation variance (via the bound) but raises L_data by discarding distributional distinctions; the optimum minimizes total DL. Present a greedy agglomerative derivation — start with each word as its own class, propose merges from distributional context statistics and shared suffixes, and accept a merge iff it reduces DL — with a proof that each accepted merge monotonically decreases the description length.

(4) Principled stopping rule. Combine the bound with the MDL criterion to derive a rule linking the MDL-optimal number of classes m* to expected tagging error, giving a self-terminating induction that needs no external dictionary.

(5) Feasibility/validation. Implement on the Brown corpus with standard Baum-Welch + Viterbi decoding: measure induced m vs. accuracy, the empirical accuracy-vs-T curve against the predicted T(epsilon), and retargeting cost to a second domain/tagset — demonstrating tagging built from raw text alone, closing the lexicon and tagged-corpus bottlenecks with a formal, transferable account of the resources required.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior taggers are limited by human supervision cost. The minimal-resource HMM tagger (Cutting et al. 1992) still needs a manually built lexicon and hand-specified initialization hints, and Baum-Welch can hit poor local optima. No work allocates a small annotation budget to the highest-impact decisions, so retargeting to a new corpus, tagset, or language remains costly.

**Method.** Actively bootstrap the ambiguity-class HMM tagger under a fixed annotation budget, framed as resource-allocation search. Run cheap multi-restart Baum-Welch to expose unstable decisions, score each candidate decision by corpus coverage, cross-restart Viterbi disagreement, and posterior entropy, and query a human only for the top-ranked items. Convert answers into targeted parameter constraints, select the most consistent restart, retrain with constrained Baum-Welch, and iterate until marginal error reduction per query is small. Apply the same screening to build the lexicon minimally via suffix-proposed classes. Evaluate accuracy versus annotation count on Brown against blind Baum-Welch, random querying, hand hints, and full supervision.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** POS tagging is the first step in most corpus-analysis pipelines, so the cost of building and retargeting a tagger gates downstream progress. These works show a steadily receding but never eliminated resource bottleneck. Rule-based coders (Greene & Rubin's TAGGIT 1971; Klein & Simmons 1963) avoid a big dictionary only by hand-authoring context-frame rules and suffix tables per language. Supervised statistical taggers (Church 1988; DeRose 1988; Garside/Leech/Sampson 1987) need large hand-tagged corpora; the Brown corpus took years of manual correction, redone per tagset/domain/language. The HMM line (Jelinek 1985; Derouault & Merialdo 1986; Kupiec 1989; Cutting/Kupiec/Pedersen/Sibun 1992) is prized for removing the tagged corpus via Baum-Welch, but leaves standing a different expensive resource: a hand-built lexicon enumerating each word's candidate tags (ambiguity classes), plus language-specific hand-built suffix tables for unknown words. For a new language, low-resource setting, or specialized domain (medical, legal), a broad dictionary is absent or omits exactly the domain-specific words that matter. The lexicon is thus the dominant remaining bottleneck, and no method here operates from raw text alone: every statistical tagger presupposes an external resource telling it which tags each word may take. This leaves clear room to push the HMM approach into the strictly weaker-assumption, raw-text-only regime.

**Method.** Propose a lexicon-free, rule-free HMM tagger that induces its whole observation model (ambiguity classes and morphological back-off) from unlabeled text, relaxing the pre-existing-lexicon assumption of Cutting/Kupiec/Jelinek and extending statistical tagging to new languages, domains, and noisy corpora. Pipeline: (1) Context anchors: rank word types by frequency in raw text; the top-K types (dominated by closed-class function words) become distributional landmarks identified with no dictionary. (2) Distributional profiling: represent each word type by co-occurrence counts with anchor words in the immediately preceding/following positions, a data-driven version of Klein and Simmons' hand-written context frames. (3) Induced ambiguity classes: cluster the profiles into latent categories with soft/overlapping assignment, so an ambiguous word (Church's tag = noun/verb) gets the set of categories whose signatures it matches; this reproduces Kupiec's ambiguity-class abstraction (a few hundred classes over 50k words) but derived from raw text. (4) Data-driven morphology: instead of hand-built suffix tables, mine character n-gram affixes and score each by mutual information with induced categories, giving a probabilistic affix model that assigns candidate classes to rare or unseen words from spelling alone. (5) HMM estimation: states equal induced categories and observations equal induced ambiguity classes; initialize from clustering statistics, refine with Baum-Welch, decode with Viterbi, reusing Cutting et al.'s efficient linear-time machinery over a self-induced alphabet. (6) Optional lightweight naming: align induced states to standard POS labels using a tiny seed of a few unambiguous exemplar words per category, far cheaper than a full lexicon or tagged corpus. Evaluation: (a) On English, compare the induced model against the same HMM given the Brown-derived lexicon to quantify the accuracy cost of dropping the lexicon; report cluster purity vs gold tags and accuracy split by known/unknown words. (b) Simulate a new language/domain by withholding the dictionary and training on specialized corpora with poor dictionary coverage. (c) Stress-test on noisy/ungrammatical input such as titles, tables, and misspellings. Why it addresses the gap: by inducing both ambiguity classes and affix back-off from raw text, it removes the last hand-built resource in HMM tagging, letting an accurate tagger be bootstrapped for any language or domain from unlabeled text. It is a direct scope-relaxation of Cutting/Kupiec: same inference engine, strictly weaker input assumptions, broader applicability to low-resource and noisy settings.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** The advance in this literature was reducing resources. Jelinek (1985) and Kupiec (1989) train HMM taggers with Baum-Welch so no pre-tagged corpus is needed, and Kupiec's ambiguity (equivalence) classes make the model nearly vocabulary-independent. Cutting et al. (1992) summarize the state of the art: only a lexicon and unlabeled text are required, at over 96 percent accuracy. This exposes the remaining bottleneck, the lexicon. Cutting et al. concede that exhaustive lexicons are expensive, if not impossible, to produce, and Church (1988) reports that most errors are attributable to defects in the lexicon.

The bottleneck also causes a silent failure mode these systems ignore. In every tagger here, a known word's lexicon ambiguity class hard-constrains which tags the search may assign. If that class is incomplete or wrong (a valid tag omitted, or a spurious tag added, common for capitalized words, domain jargon, and rare senses), the correct tag is unreachable and the word is silently mis-tagged. Existing robustness work targets only unknown words: Cutting et al. and Kupiec (1992) guess classes from suffixes and back off to open-class defaults, while known-word entries are trusted. Brill (1989) acquires disambiguation rules but needs tagged text and a correct lexicon. So retargeting to a new domain or language forces expensive hand-curation of a defect-free lexicon, the very resource these methods cannot cheaply supply, and its defects become unflagged systematic errors.

**Method.** We propose a self-auditing training loop that lets an HMM tagger tolerate and repair an incomplete or noisy lexicon from unlabeled text alone, removing the need for an exhaustive lexicon and turning silent failures into detectable ones.

Step 1, cheap seed: start from a minimal lexicon (small verified core plus suffix-guessed ambiguity classes, as in Cutting and Kupiec), with words grouped into Kupiec-style equivalence classes so repairs generalize.

Step 2, dual-model estimation: train two HMMs on the same unlabeled corpus with Baum-Welch. A constrained model restricts each word to its lexicon ambiguity class. An escape model additionally lets any word emit any open-class tag with small smoothed probability. Both are linear-time and need no annotation.

Step 3, defect detection via posterior disagreement: from forward-backward posteriors, for each token compute the escape model's posterior mass on tags outside the word's ambiguity class, plus the constrained model's per-token likelihood and entropy. Pool this across all occurrences of a word type and its equivalence class. Strong repeated escape mass on an excluded tag flags a likely omission; a listed tag that never attracts mass flags a likely spurious entry.

Step 4, confidence-gated repair: expand or prune ambiguity classes only when pooled evidence exceeds a threshold combining posterior magnitude, occurrence count, and a suffix or morphology prior; re-estimate; iterate to convergence.

Step 5, calibrated abstaining output: expose the constrained-versus-escape posterior gap as per-token confidence; when the constrained model is confidently overruled, mark or abstain rather than force an unreliable tag, giving downstream components a trust signal.

Evaluation, feasible with these papers' resources: on the Brown corpus, systematically ablate the lexicon (drop true tags, inject spurious ones, delete rare words) and measure recovery of altered entries, end-to-end accuracy versus a fixed-lexicon HMM as lexicon quality degrades, calibration and error-detection rate of the abstention signal, and cross-genre transfer (seed on one genre, audit on another). Success means accuracy that degrades gracefully and recovers most of the gap to a clean lexicon with a fraction of the manual lexicon effort, converting the lexicon bottleneck into an automatically robustified process.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Low-resource POS tagging is limited by two costly hand-built resources. Church (1988) and Hindle (1989) need a manually pre-tagged corpus. Cutting (1992) and Kupiec (1989) drop that via Baum-Welch training on raw text but still require a lexicon listing permitted tags per word; Kupiec's equivalence classes still presuppose a 30000-word dictionary. Building this dictionary per new domain or language is the deployment friction. Klein and Simmons (1963) assigned word class without a large dictionary using affix, orthographic, and context tests at about 90 percent accuracy, but as a deterministic hand-built rule engine with no statistical disambiguation. These two literatures have never been merged, leaving open a tagger needing neither a tagged corpus nor a dictionary.

**Method.** Merge morphological coding (Klein and Simmons 1963) with unsupervised equivalence-class HMM tagging (Kupiec 1989; Cutting 1992). Stage 1: use affix, capitalization, and orthographic tests to map every token to an ambiguity set of permitted tags; tokens with identical signatures form equivalence classes, removing the dictionary. Stage 2: estimate HMM transition and emission probabilities by forward-backward training on raw text seeded from these classes, with augmented state chains for phrase context and frequent function words handled individually. Stage 3: tag raw text, treat high-confidence outputs as labels, and use Hindle-style rule acquisition to refine affix-to-class rules and fix systematic errors, then iterate. Evaluate against Cutting's dictionary tagger and the 1963 coder on standard corpora and a held-out language, measuring accuracy versus the size of hand-built resources consumed.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. Statistical POS taggers (DeRose 1988; Church 1988; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992) reach 95-96 percent accuracy only under narrow conditions. They depend on a manual lexicon listing each word's possible tags (Church notes most errors are lexicon defects and that rare capitalized words were discarded; Cutting et al. use a crude open-class default plus language-specific suffix rules for unknowns), and they assume clean, tokenized, in-vocabulary text from edited standard English (Brown Corpus). But the settings where tagging is most useful deliver uncertain input: phoneme-to-text and speech decoding yield lattices of competing word hypotheses (Merialdo), and OCR and technical corpora bring high out-of-vocabulary rates and domain shift (Klein and Simmons 1963 flagged scientific text). The lexicon-free portable approach (Klein and Simmons 1963) is only 90 percent and non-statistical; the input-uncertainty approach (Merialdo) targets transcription with a hand-built grammar, not portable tagging. No system is simultaneously lexicon-free and domain-adapting while consuming uncertain input and jointly resolving word identity and tag. A tagger that works only on clean, in-domain, in-vocabulary text cannot serve the speech, OCR, and specialized-corpus pipelines it is meant to support.

**Method.** Build a deployable artifact: a self-configuring, lattice-aware POS tagging engine plus a point-it-at-your-corpus workflow. (1) Self-building lexicon: from raw untagged domain text, induce ambiguity classes by combining a corpus-learned morphological affix trie (generalizing Klein and Simmons suffix rules) with distributional word-equivalence-class clustering (Kupiec), so unknown and rare words are handled natively rather than by open-class fallback. (2) Unsupervised adaptation: train an equivalence-class HMM via Baum-Welch on the target corpus, with deleted-interpolation smoothing and selective higher-order state chains, so pointing the tool at a new genre reconfigures lexicon and model automatically. (3) Lattice input: accept a word lattice from an ASR, phoneme, or OCR decoder (Merialdo confluent nodes) and run joint Viterbi over word-and-tag pairs, the HMM doubling as a language model that reranks word hypotheses while tagging, replacing the brittle transcribe-then-tag pipeline. (4) Human-in-the-loop runtime: resolve unknowns via the affix trie, emit per-token confidence, flag low-confidence spans for optional correction, retrain incrementally; ship as a linear-time trie-backed front end for speech and OCR. Evaluate via domain-shift tagging (train Brown, test scientific text) versus a lexicon-dependent HMM, joint word-and-tag accuracy on simulated noisy lattices versus a sequential pipeline, and ablation over zero, seed, and full lexicons.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch] — claude-opus-4-8, 23 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior stochastic taggers (Church 1988, DeRose 1988, Jelinek 1985, Kupiec 1989, and the Practical Part-of-Speech Tagger 1992) reach about 96 percent under narrow, poorly transferable conditions. They fix each word's ambiguity class via a manually built dictionary that enumerates a word's possible categories, and the HMM only disambiguates within that fixed set. Exhaustive lexicons are expensive if not impossible to build, and Kupiec's equivalence classes, though called vocabulary-independent, still need the dictionary to know a word's possible categories to place it in a class. Novel words fall back on an English-specific suffix table and a default open-class ambiguity class, which are language- and genre-bound patches. Systems also train and test on one homogeneous corpus, yet OOV rates and category distributions shift across genres and domains, so a word's realistic ambiguity class is genre-dependent, not static, and nothing re-estimates what categories a word can take when the target text differs from the training genre. This is a scope mismatch: methods tuned to a fixed dictionary and matched genre degrade where unrestricted text is most unrestricted, leaving open a tagger whose lexical and ambiguity structure adapts to the target domain from unlabeled text, without a new hand-built lexicon or English morphology.
ignore this parameter

**Method.** Treat ambiguity-class membership as a latent variable induced and re-estimated per target domain from raw text, coupled with unsupervised HMM adaptation. Build distributional signatures instead of a dictionary: for each word type including OOV words, form a signature from co-occurrence with a small stable set of high-frequency anchor closed-class words in left and right positions, replacing both the genre-matched dictionary and the English suffix guesser with a language- and genre-independent mechanism. Induce data-driven ambiguity classes by clustering the signatures, giving every word a soft membership, generalizing Kupiec's fixed equivalence classes into classes learned from the target text. Fold induced classes into the HMM emission model as probability of induced-class given tag, initialize from a source-genre model or seeded start, then run Baum-Welch on the untagged target corpus so transition and emission parameters adapt, extending the no-tagged-corpus training of Jelinek, Kupiec, and the practical tagger into a cross-genre adaptation step. Anchor interpretability by tying a few induced classes to canonical tags via a small closed-class seed lexicon. Evaluate by training on one genre and adapting and testing on a different genre, reporting overall and OOV-word accuracy against a static-lexicon HMM baseline and a suffix-guessing baseline, plus a cross-lingual test on a non-English corpus where suffix heuristics fail but distributional anchors work. Success means closing most of the cross-genre and OOV accuracy gap using only unlabeled target-domain text and no new hand-built lexicon.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical POS taggers of this era (Klein and Simmons 1963; Church 1988; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992; Hindle 1989; Jelinek 1985) claim to handle unrestricted text and be vocabulary-independent and robust, reporting 90-96 percent accuracy. But those numbers come from narrow conditions. They train and test on one edited corpus, overwhelmingly the Tagged Brown Corpus, so genre and domain shift is never tested. They silently assume a hand-built lexicon of each word's possible tags. They exclude unknown words from the score (Kupiec reports 95.6 percent ignoring unknown words) even though Church notes a large Zipfian OOV tail. And incommensurable tagsets (Brown ~87, Fidditch 46) make cross-study numbers non-comparable. This is a scope mismatch: methods are characterized in-domain, lexicon-complete, and on known words, while their advertised purpose is arbitrary running text. No systematic measurement exists of how accuracy degrades with genre distance, OOV rate, lexicon incompleteness, training size, and tagset granularity, nor of which ingredients (dictionary lookup, suffix morphology, equivalence classes, state chains, acquired rules) actually transfer.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map: a controlled cross-condition benchmark and error-decomposition study of statistical POS tagging under realistic conditions. Reimplement representative taggers spanning the paradigm space: a trigram Markov tagger with lexicon (Church 1988), an unsupervised HMM with lexicon (Cutting et al. 1992; Jelinek 1985), an equivalence-class HMM with state chains (Kupiec 1989), a dictionary-free suffix coder (Klein and Simmons 1963), and acquired rules (Hindle 1989). Partition tagged corpora into genre pools and define a tagset-normalization mapping onto shared coarse and fine tagsets so all methods are scored on identical labels. Then sweep the axes: train/test genre match versus mismatch (genre distance measured by vocabulary and tag-bigram divergence), lexicon coverage from full to none (a controlled OOV-rate sweep), training size, and tagset granularity. Report accuracy that includes unknown words as the primary metric, plus separate known/unknown/ambiguous accuracies, and decompose errors into unknown-word versus contextual errors. Deliverables: response surfaces of accuracy versus OOV rate and genre distance; a ranking of which architectural ingredients transfer out of domain; and a recipe for choosing a paradigm given a target genre, OOV rate, and available resources. This directly measures the out-of-domain, high-OOV, lexicon-poor performance the original single-number in-domain claims conceal.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. Resource-light statistical taggers here (Kupiec's phrase-dependent HMM, Cutting et al.'s Practical POS Tagger, DeRose, Church, Jelinek's Markov source model) rely on two narrow assumptions. (1) Equivalence-class emissions: words sharing a tag-set are treated identically, replacing P(word|category) with P(class|category); Kupiec covers 30,000 words with ~129 classes but concedes dog is usually a noun and see usually a verb yet both collapse into noun-or-verb. (2) Stationarity: one global HMM is assumed adequate for unrestricted text, though the works target new genres and hold out mixed-genre text. Errors concentrate where these break: skewed words in weak contexts and genre-shifted usage. No work formally says when class-merging is safe, how to pick lexicon granularity between the cheap class model and the data-hungry lexicalized model, or how to handle genre beyond retraining. The central knob, how coarsely to group words, lacks explanatory foundation.

**Method.** A formal-derivation contribution, trainable from untagged text. (1) Formalization: cast a tagger as an HMM whose emissions are set by a vocabulary partition, so P(word|category) factors into a within-class term times P(class|category); Kupiec/Cutting is the ambiguity-class endpoint and DeRose/Church the identity endpoint of a partition lattice, making granularity explicit. A taxonomy separates three disambiguation signals: intra-class lexical bias, local transitions, and global register. (2) Error-bound theorem: via a Pinsker-type inequality, bound the excess misclassification risk of a partition versus full lexicalization by a token-averaged square-root KL divergence between word-conditioned and class-conditioned tag posteriors, gated by the local-context margin; corollary, merging is lossless when within-class words are context-exchangeable or context disambiguates, and loss scales with lexical skew times context ambiguity. From the bound derive a Minimum Description Length objective that promotes a word to its own emission distribution only when likelihood gain exceeds parameter cost, giving a data-driven middle ground still estimable by Baum-Welch and a principled unknown-word default to the coarsest covering class. (3) Latent-register extension: a document-level mixture-of-HMMs with derived EM forward-backward updates and a corollary that register conditioning shrinks the gating term and tightens the bound. Validate on the genre-labeled Brown corpus: errors localize where skew times ambiguity is high; the MDL partition beats both endpoints at equal parameter budget; the register mixture cuts cross-genre error versus a stationary HMM.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical POS taggers of the late 1980s/early 1990s reached ~96% accuracy with first-order (bigram) HMMs over word equivalence/ambiguity classes trained on unlabeled text (Church 1988; Kupiec, "A Practical Part-of-Speech Tagger" 1992) or by statistical optimization / Viterbi decoding (DeRose 1988). First-order context, however, misses important selective dependencies — e.g., disambiguating adjective-or-noun after a determiner ("The period of..."), or subject/verb number agreement across an intervening prepositional phrase. Kupiec's augmented HMM (1989) captures these by adding "state chains" that model higher-order conditioning ONLY where needed, avoiding the parameter explosion of a uniform trigram model.

The scope mismatch: the structure of those state chains is hand-designed from manual error analysis and linguistic intuition on a single corpus and tagset. This assumption is narrow and poorly transferable. Uniform higher-order models, at the other extreme, spend parameters everywhere (n^3 transitions), overfitting sparse contexts and forcing ad hoc smoothing. Yet the same literature argues (Kupiec 1992, sec. 5.3) that a tagger must be "fast and easy to target... to new genres and languages, and to experiment with different tagsets." There is currently no principled, automatic way to decide WHERE to spend limited model capacity, and no explicit link between how much higher-order structure to add and how much (often sparse) training data is available. Every new domain, tagset, or language requires an expert to redo the error analysis and hand-build the network — leaving both portability and accuracy on the table.

**Method.** Replace manual state-chain design with an automatic, budget-constrained topology SEARCH that grows a selectively higher-order equivalence-class HMM from data. This is an optimization/resource-allocation contribution: adaptively allocate a fixed parameter budget to the contexts where extra capacity most reduces error.

(1) Base model. Start from the first-order, fully-connected equivalence-class HMM that requires only a lexicon plus unlabeled text and is trained with Baum-Welch (as in Kupiec 1989/1992). Equivalence classes keep it vocabulary-independent so new words need no retraining.

(2) Candidate expansions. Define a single primitive operation — a "context split" — that refines the model exactly like a hand-built state chain: take a state (a category used in some context) and split it by conditioning on a specific preceding category or equivalence-class context, introducing the extra states that uniquely model that second/third-order context (e.g., determiner-noun-X vs determiner-adjective-X). The set of all such splits over the current topology defines a large search space. Parameters of tied/duplicated states are pooled (state tying) as in the original augmented model.

(3) Benefit-per-parameter scoring. Score each candidate by estimated benefit divided by parameters added: benefit = gain in held-out data likelihood, or reduction in expected tagging error measured from forward–backward posteriors on a small held-out set (which may be self-labeled by the current model, or a modest amount of tagged text as used for rule acquisition in Hindle 1989). Crucially, scoring uses only a few localized EM re-estimation steps around the affected states rather than full retraining, making the search tractable.

(4) Adaptive/greedy search with pruning. Iterate: score all candidate expansions against the current model; add the top-k with highest benefit/cost; re-estimate the whole model with Baum-Welch; then prune (merge back) any expansion whose realized gain falls below a threshold. Continue until the parameter budget is exhausted or marginal gain < epsilon. The budget itself is chosen by cross-validation and scaled to the amount of training data, so capacity is added only where data can reliably estimate it — directly coupling model order to data sparsity. Uniform trigram (spend everywhere) and fixed hand-crafted chains (spend once, by hand) are the two degenerate endpoints this search interpolates between.

(5) Evaluation and transfer. (a) On the original corpus, match or beat the hand-crafted augmented HMM with fewer parameters. (b) Retarget the identical procedure — no manual analysis — across Brown genre subcorpora, to a coarser/finer tagset (via the lexicon-to-tagset mapping), and to a second language, showing it automatically rediscovers effective structure. (c) Ablate the budget to chart the accuracy/parameter/data-efficiency tradeoff. (d) Inspect discovered expansions to confirm they recover known linguistically meaningful contexts (determiner–noun–X; subject–verb agreement across a PP) plus novel ones, demonstrating the search substitutes for expert error analysis. This turns a narrow, non-transferable hand-engineering step into a portable, data-driven optimization that allocates model complexity where it pays off.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The statistical taggers here (Church 1988; Jelinek 1985; Derouault and Merialdo 1986; Kupiec 1989; Cutting et al. 1992) claim robustness on unrestricted text and near vocabulary-independence, but share one narrow boundary condition: the HMM emission side is anchored to a manually constructed lexicon that lists, for each word, its admissible tags (its ambiguity class). Kupiec's 129 equivalence classes still presuppose a 30,000-word dictionary mapping every word to a class; Church trains word-level probabilities from the tagged Brown corpus. The ambiguity-class trick makes the model independent of vocabulary size, not of the existence of a curated lexicon. This breaks exactly where these systems claim to serve: new domains and genres, other languages lacking such a lexicon, and open text full of out-of-vocabulary tokens. The state of the art punts here; Cutting et al. concede an unrecognized word gets a default ambiguity class containing all open-class categories, failing on the hard words where errors concentrate. Reported accuracies near 96 percent are measured in a favorable in-lexicon, in-domain regime and often ignoring unknown words (Kupiec 1989), overstating real performance. Rule-based alternatives (TAGGIT; Hindle 1989) depend on hand-crafted English morphotactics or a pre-tagged corpus plus an existing parser. The gap: no method builds the ambiguity-class structure the HMM depends on from evidence rather than a pre-existing dictionary, so none truly functions under an incomplete or missing lexicon.

**Method.** Relax the complete-lexicon assumption by inducing ambiguity classes and emission structure from raw untagged target-domain text, keeping only a small cross-domain-stable seed. First, seed with the closed-class inventory (determiners, prepositions, pronouns, auxiliaries, conjunctions, punctuation): a few hundred largely unambiguous entries, stable across domains and reusable across related languages, far weaker than a full content-word dictionary. These anchor the HMM state space (the tagset) and act as reliable context markers. Second, for every other word (frequent, rare, or unseen), compute a distributional context signature from unlabeled counts over neighboring seed and high-frequency anchor items, and cluster words by signature similarity to induce data-driven ambiguity classes that replace dictionary lookup; map each cluster to a soft distribution over tags by aligning its contexts to the seeded closed-class contexts. Third, for rare and OOV words combine the sparse signature with morphological features (suffix and prefix tries, per the 1963 CGC and Kupiec suffix cues) to yield a soft, evidence-weighted ambiguity class instead of the all-open-class default. Fourth, plug these soft classes into a first-order HMM and re-estimate transition and emission parameters with Baum-Welch on the target text itself, self-adapting per corpus with no hand lexicon or tagged data. Evaluate against the relaxed scope: incomplete-lexicon curves that progressively delete dictionary entries and compare to dictionary-dependent baselines while reporting accuracy including unknown words; cross-domain transfer adapting on a genre far from Brown versus a Brown-trained fixed-lexicon tagger; and a low-resource language setting using only a closed-class seed plus raw text. Success means matching in-lexicon accuracy in the favorable regime while sharply cutting OOV and domain-specific errors and degrading gracefully as the lexicon shrinks.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The statistical POS-tagging paradigm in these works reports high accuracy only under narrow, homogeneous conditions. Cutting et al. (1992), building on Church (1988) and Kupiec (1989), reach 96% by training a Baum-Welch HMM on half of Brown and tagging the other half; Klein and Simmons (1963) report over 90% on scientific text; the Markov-source line (1985) and phoneme-to-text work (1986) assume a stationary source; Hindle (1989) acquires rules from tagged text tuned to one domain. Each assumes a single in-domain corpus, a lexicon whose ambiguity classes are complete and correct, unlabeled training text from the same distribution as the target, and a forced single-tag decision per token. This is a scope mismatch with the goal of tagging unrestricted text, which is heterogeneous across genres, domains, and noise, and contains vocabulary the lexicon never anticipated. When lexicon ambiguity classes are wrong or the target genre differs from training, aggregate accuracy hides failure concentrated on unknown words, rare ambiguity classes, and out-of-domain constructions. Robust here means only that the model assigns non-zero probability to any token sequence, not robustness to distribution shift or lexical error, and not any calibrated signal of which tags are unreliable. The systems emit their worst errors silently, exactly where a downstream parser most needs a warning.

**Method.** Build a confidence-aware, self-adaptive HMM tagger that reduces brittleness to lexical incompleteness and genre shift and quantifies its own uncertainty, using the same resources these papers assume (a lexicon, unlabeled text, a small tagged sample for evaluation). Four robustification components. (1) Redundant evidence fusion: instead of trusting the lexicon ambiguity class alone, combine individually weak sources (the lexicon class, morphological suffix signatures extending Klein and Simmons 1963 suffix tests and Kupiec suffix prediction, and contextual tag n-gram statistics) via smoothed linear interpolation as advocated in the Markov-source framework, so a single wrong lexicon entry cannot force an error. (2) Lexicon-error-tolerant emission model: replace the hard constraint that a word may take only its listed tags with a soft prior that gives listed tags high prior mass but leaves a small leak probability for other open-class tags conditioned on the word suffix class, letting Baum-Welch recover valid tags the lexicon omits and down-weight spurious ones. (3) Unsupervised target-domain self-adaptation: for each new document or genre, run extra Baum-Welch iterations on the target text itself, initialized from the source-trained model, adapting emission and transition parameters to the target distribution, a cross-genre step the in-domain train/test protocols never perform. (4) Calibrated uncertainty and selective abstention: use per-token forward-backward posterior marginals as a confidence score, calibrate it, and when confidence is low output a ranked ambiguity set or defer to a downstream parser (as in Hindle 1989) instead of a forced single tag, turning silent errors into flagged low-confidence decisions. Evaluate beyond aggregate accuracy with robustness diagnostics: cross-genre transfer (train on one Brown genre, test on another); systematically corrupted or incomplete lexicons (randomly delete or add ambiguity-class entries); error concentration on unknown and rare classes; and confidence calibration plus the accuracy-versus-abstention trade-off. Success is smaller degradation under shift and lexicon corruption plus well-calibrated confidence that retains high accuracy on kept tokens, addressing the gap between in-domain benchmark accuracy and reliable tagging of genuinely unrestricted, noisy, out-of-vocabulary text.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two mature but separate strands attack lexical-category disambiguation, and each rests on narrow, poorly transferable assumptions (a scope mismatch). Statistical/Markov taggers (Church 1988; Jelinek 1985; the 1986 phoneme-to-text model; Kupiec 1989; the 1992 practical tagger) assume all relevant context is captured by a fixed short window of adjacent category transitions, that emission depends only on the current category, and that one genre corpus (Brown) represents unrestricted text. These assumptions fail exactly where the models fail: Kupiec's own error analysis ("to" preposition vs. infinitive marker; "The period of..." adjective vs. noun) shows residual errors are phrase-level and long-distance, precisely what the local Markov assumption excludes. His fix, hand-designed state chains from linguistic intuition, does not scale or transfer. Rule/parser-based methods (Klein and Simmons 1963; Greene and Rubin 1971; Hindle 1989) see phrase structure and long-distance context but are deterministic, tied to one hand-built grammar, and corpus/parser-bound. Hindle argues the real question is whether the needed linguistic information can be acquired from text, noting stochastic taggers succeed without reference to syntactic structure. The gap: no framework unifies these so that structural/long-distance constraints are acquired automatically, added only where the Markov assumption empirically breaks, and kept inside one graded, vocabulary- and genre-robust probabilistic model.

**Method.** Build a self-refining tagger that unifies unsupervised HMM tagging with automatically acquired symbolic phrase-structural rules, using the HMM's own systematic errors as the bridge between the two literatures. Step 1: train a Kupiec/Cutting-style HMM with word equivalence classes via Baum-Welch on untagged text, handling unknown words with Klein and Simmons-style suffix/morphology cues to keep vocabulary independence. Step 2: locate where the Markov assumption breaks by flagging low-margin Viterbi decisions and systematic disagreements on a small held-out sample, clustering them into recurrent error patterns, automating the error catalog Kupiec built by hand. Step 3: for each cluster, adapt Hindle's rule-acquisition procedure to induce a symbolic context template grounded in shallow phrase structure from Church's noun-phrase parser (phrase boundaries, distance to head, function-word anchors), so rules encode the long-distance/phrasal context the local model misses. Step 4, the synthesis: compile each acquired rule not as a hard override but as an automatically generated state-chain augmentation that unfolds the offending HMM state along the rule's context, then re-estimate by Baum-Welch, so symbolic phrase constraints decide where extra conditioning is warranted while probabilities decide how strongly, and a rule takes effect only if data support a distinct usage. Step 5: iterate train, error-mine, acquire, compile, retrain until residual systematic errors plateau, yielding a compact data-driven set of phrase contexts rather than uniformly higher-order models. Evaluation targets the scope mismatch: cross-genre transfer (train on one corpus, test on another) versus fixed-order HMMs and parser-bound rules; ablations isolating gains from structural augmentations; head-to-head comparison of automatically acquired state chains against Kupiec's hand-built ones and Hindle's deterministic rules on shared errors; and the phoneme-to-text task as a downstream test of the unified statistical-plus-grammatical model. Success: recover parser-level phrase accuracy while keeping the resource-light, corpus-portable, graded character of HMM taggers.

---

## Finite-time Analysis of the Multiarmed Bandit Problem

*seed paper `finite-time-analysis-of-the-multiarmed-bandit-problem-2002`, 12 sources, 64 generated ideas*

### Human idea (ground truth)

**Motivation.** The multiarmed bandit problem is a canonical instance of the exploration/exploitation dilemma, central to reinforcement learning and adaptive control. Classical work established that regret must grow at least logarithmically in the number of plays and produced policies matching this bound, but only asymptotically and only for restricted parametric families of reward distributions. Moreover, the optimal upper-confidence-index policies require computations that depend on the entire history of rewards from each arm, making them hard to compute. There is a gap for policies that (a) are simple and computationally efficient, (b) achieve logarithmic regret uniformly over all finite horizons rather than merely in the limit, and (c) work for arbitrary reward distributions with bounded support without prior knowledge. Closing this gap matters because practical learning systems operate under finite budgets and unknown, general reward distributions.

**Method.** We propose a family of simple allocation strategies with provable finite-time logarithmic regret bounds. The central idea is to attach to each arm an index equal to its current empirical mean reward plus a confidence-width term derived from Chernoff-Hoeffding concentration bounds, and to always play the arm with the largest index; because the confidence term shrinks as an arm is sampled more, this automatically balances exploration and exploitation. We instantiate this as a deterministic upper-confidence-bound rule (UCB1) using a confidence radius scaling like sqrt(2 ln n / (times played)), and prove, via decomposing the expected number of plays of each suboptimal arm and bounding the probability that its index exceeds the optimal arm's, that expected regret is bounded by a sum of logarithmic terms scaling with 1/gap for every finite horizon and any bounded-support reward distribution. We further develop an epoch-based variant (UCB2) that plays a chosen arm for geometrically growing block lengths to push the leading regret constant arbitrarily close to the information-theoretic optimum, and analyze a decreasing-exploration epsilon-greedy variant whose exploration probability decays as 1/n to obtain logarithmic instantaneous regret. We also specialize the index construction to normally distributed rewards with unknown mean and variance by using the sample variance to size the confidence interval. Finally, we validate the policies empirically on Bernoulli bandit instances of varying difficulty and dimension.

### LLM ideas

#### [method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The sources form a lineage of regime-specific optimal allocation policies. Lai & Robbins (1985) prove the universal O(log n) regret lower bound with a KL constant and give matching UCB rules only for specific families. Agrawal (1995) builds sample-mean UCB indices optimal for one-parameter exponential families. Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) give dual-problem indices for parametric populations with unknown nuisance parameters. Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) note that the achievable rate and correct strategy change with the bandit postulates (known family vs. bounded support vs. only finite moments), while the Lai-Robbins bound applies across all. Ishikida & Varaiya (1994) and the Gittins allocation-indices book address discounted/average-reward and arm-acquiring settings with index rules that are optimal but hard to compute; Q-learning for bandit problems (1995) and Sutton's RL introduction (1998) show Gittins-type indices can be learned by reinforcement learning. The unexamined premise across these works is that the deployer already knows which regime holds and pre-commits to the matching policy. In practice this is unknown: misspecification either silently forfeits a policy's regret guarantee or is needlessly conservative. No source detects the operative regime online, deploys the tightest valid asymptotically-efficient index, and reports a continuously valid optimality guarantee. This matters for the clinical-trial and online-allocation applications these papers invoke, where guarantee validity is what a deployer needs.

**Method.** Build a deployable adaptive-allocation engine that unifies the regime-specific optimal policies of the source literature behind one interface, selects among them from data, and emits a live regret certificate. Five components. (1) Problem-spec and streaming interface: the user declares objective (finite/infinite horizon; average vs. discounted reward) and any hard prior knowledge; rewards stream per arm, and new arms can enroll over time (arm-acquiring bandits, per Ishikida-Varaiya). (2) Regime monitor (novel core): per arm it maintains empirical moments and CDF and runs sequential goodness-of-fit and moment-tail tests to certify membership in a nested lattice of classes: A known one-parameter exponential family, B bounded support of unknown shape, C only finite p-th moment, D discounted-Bayesian; each certification carries a confidence level from the large-deviations machinery Agrawal uses. (3) Index synthesizer: for the tightest certified class it instantiates the matching efficient index: KL and sample-mean UCB (Lai-Robbins, Agrawal) for A; Burnetas-Katehakis dual-problem indices for parametric-with-nuisance and finite-support; Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric confidence-bound indices with their guaranteed rates for B and C; and for class D, Gittins indices computed by a built-in Q-learning and dynamic-programming module (Duff, Watkins per Sutton), making the Gittins-optimal policy deployable without a closed form. (4) Hot-swap meta-controller: as certification tightens or breaks it switches to the tightest valid index and defaults conservatively to the nonparametric index whenever a stronger assumption loses certification, keeping the deployed policy always sound; it uses the average-reward index extension of Ishikida-Varaiya for the streaming case. (5) Regret-certificate tracker: it continuously outputs the finite-time regret bound valid under current certification and the Lai-Robbins KL lower bound, exposing the live optimality gap, and logs counterfactual guarantee-void events where a pre-committed fixed policy would have lost its guarantee. Evaluation: a simulation harness sweeping arms across all four regimes (including regime shifts and heavy tails) plus a clinical-trial-style replay, comparing the platform against each fixed policy committed a priori, measuring regret, mistake counts, and the fraction of runs in which each fixed policy's stated guarantee was actually valid versus the platform's always-valid certificate. The deliverable is the open engine itself.

#### [unsteered (blank)] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior works give an asymptotic-only theory of the stochastic bandit. Lai and Robbins (1985) proved the log-n regret lower bound and built KL-based upper-confidence index policies; Agrawal (1995) made the indices sample-mean-based; Burnetas-Katehakis (1996) generalized optimal adaptive policies. Gaps: guarantees hold only as n grows so nothing is known at finite horizons; indices need the reward family (exponential families, or a distribution-specific large-deviation KL contraction); and the RL sources use epsilon-greedy heuristics with no regret theory. Room remains for a finite-time, distribution-free, trivially computable policy plus the first finite-time analysis of epsilon-greedy.

**Method.** Study index policies for rewards bounded in the unit interval whose exploration bonus comes from Hoeffding-type concentration, not distribution-specific rate functions. UCB1 plays the arm maximizing empirical mean plus square root of (2 ln n divided by play count). Prove a finite-time regret bound by bounding suboptimal-arm plays: split each play into optimal-arm underestimate, suboptimal-arm overestimate, and under-sampling; bound the first two with Hoeffding and a union bound over counts whose tails sum to a constant, yielding expected plays near eight ln n over the squared gap plus a constant, hence regret near the sum of eight ln n over gaps, uniform in n and independent of KL constants. Add refinements: an empirical-variance Bernstein pad, and an epoch variant whose constant approaches the Lai-Robbins optimum. Prove epsilon-greedy with per-step epsilon decaying like one over n attains log-n finite-time regret. Validate empirically versus Agrawal and Gittins indices on Bernoulli and normal arms.

#### [method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior works give bandit policies with asymptotic guarantees but no unified finite-time empirical comparison. Lai-Robbins 1985, Agrawal 1995, Burnetas-Katehakis 1996, and Yakowitz-Lowe 1991 prove log-n regret under different assumptions; Gittins 1989, Ishikida-Varaiya 1994, and Berry-Fristedt 1985 prove discounted-index optimality; Q-learning 1995 and Sutton-Barto 1998 give heuristics. Gaps: when the log-linear regret regime begins and whether empirical slopes match theoretical KL constants is unknown; policies are siloed by criterion and assumptions with no apples-to-apples comparison; robustness of parametric indices under misspecification versus nonparametric policies is unmeasured.

**Method.** Build a Bandit Policy Atlas benchmark comparing the Lai-Robbins KL index, Agrawal sample-mean index, Burnetas-Katehakis dual index, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric index, Gittins discounted index, and an epsilon-greedy or Q-learning baseline over a factorial grid of distribution family, reward gap, arm count, and horizon. Four diagnostics: asymptotic-onset and KL-constant recovery via regret-versus-log-n fits; a misspecification-robustness matrix running parametric indices on mismatched families against the nonparametric policy; a cross-criterion map testing each policy under both regret and discounted reward; and a tuning-sensitivity sweep. Deliver phase diagrams of which policy dominates each region plus tables of onset horizons and recovered constants, turning asymptotic theory into finite-time empirical guidance.

#### [method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** All of the regret analyses in this corpus are *asymptotic*. Lai & Robbins (1985) prove the celebrated liminf_n R_n/log n ≥ Σ (μ*−μ_j)/I(θ_j,θ*) lower bound and construct index rules that meet it only "as n→∞"; moreover their indices require the entire reward history and are tailored to specific parametric families (normal, Bernoulli, Poisson, exponential). Agrawal (1995) simplifies these to sample-mean-based upper-confidence-bound (UCB) indices, but the guarantee is still purely asymptotic (Theorem 2.2 bounds limsup_n E[T_n(j)]/log n), is proved via large-deviations lemmas, requires tuning an infinite triangular array {a_ni}, and its optimal constant depends on the unknown Kullback–Leibler number (hence on distributional knowledge). Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) give asymptotically optimal but family-specific "dual" indices; Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) give nonparametric but again asymptotic rates; Ishikida & Varaiya (1994) and the Gittins-index line address discounted/average-reward optimality, not finite-horizon regret.

This leaves a concrete gap. No result here bounds the expected regret of a *single, simple, distribution-free* policy *for every finite n*, with explicit numerical constants. This matters because (i) asymptotic statements of the form limsup R_n/log n ≤ c hide lower-order terms and burn-in periods that can be arbitrarily large at any horizon a practitioner actually faces; (ii) the achieved constants require knowing the reward family or a KL number, which defeats the purpose of "learning"; and (iii) the sequences {a_ni} and history-dependent indices are cumbersome to implement. The corpus itself gives the two ingredients needed to close the gap but never combines them: a matching Ω(log n) lower bound (Lai–Robbins), and the observation (Yakowitz–Lowe, Agrawal) that under a boundedness/moment postulate one can control the number of suboptimal pulls via tail inequalities.

**Method.** Contribution type: a formal derivation — a non-asymptotic regret theorem for a minimal index policy, plus a conceptual taxonomy that separates asymptotic from uniform (finite-time) optimality.

1. Formal model and conceptual distinction. Fix K arms with rewards in [0,1] (bounded postulate, as in Yakowitz–Lowe). Let Δ_j = μ*−μ_j and T_j(n) the pulls of arm j. Define three nested notions of optimality and organize the prior work into this taxonomy: (a) *consistency* (n^{-1}ES_n→μ*, Lai–Robbins eq. 1.12); (b) *asymptotic order optimality* (limsup R_n/log n < ∞, Agrawal/Burnetas–Katehakis/Yakowitz); (c) *uniform (anytime) finite-time optimality*: there exist explicit constants such that R_n ≤ f(n) for EVERY n≥1. The claim is that (c) is strictly stronger and has never been established in this corpus; the derivation targets (c).

2. The policy. UCB-index rule: play each arm once; thereafter at round n pull argmax_j [ x̄_j + √(2 ln n / T_j(n)) ]. The index uses only the sample mean (answering Agrawal's simplification goal) and a *deterministic, family-independent* confidence radius, eliminating the tuned array {a_ni} and the history-dependent, KL-specific indices of Lai–Robbins.

3. Key lemma (derivation). Using only the Chernoff–Hoeffding inequality for bounded variables, P(|x̄_{j,s}−μ_j| ≥ √(2 ln n / s)) ≤ n^{-4}, derive that a suboptimal arm j is pulled at round n only if one of three events holds: the optimal arm's index under-estimates μ*, arm j's mean over-estimates μ_j, or arm j has been pulled fewer than ℓ_j = ⌈8 ln n / Δ_j²⌉ times. Bounding the first two by a summable tail (Σ_t t·t^{-4}) gives the deterministic-threshold argument.

4. Main theorem (finite-time bound). E[T_j(n)] ≤ 8 ln n / Δ_j² + 1 + π²/3, hence for every finite n,
   R_n ≤ Σ_{j:Δ_j>0} ( 8 ln n / Δ_j ) + (1 + π²/3) Σ_j Δ_j.
This holds uniformly in n with fully explicit constants and no distributional knowledge beyond boundedness.

5. Matching and tightening. (a) Compare with the Lai–Robbins bound: the derived bound is O(log n) in n, matching their lower-bound *order* at all horizons, with a distribution-free 1/Δ_j constant that upper-bounds the KL-based constant via Pinsker's inequality — making explicit the price of family-independence. (b) Derive a worst-case (distribution-free over Δ) corollary R_n = O(√(K n log n)) by optimizing over the Δ_j. (c) Generalize the confidence radius to √(α ln n / T_j(n)) and derive the exploration–exploitation trade-off: prove the bound holds for any α>1, with the constant 8 replaced by 2α/Δ_j and the additive term by 1 + 1/(α−1)·(something), exposing how the leading constant degrades as α→1 and quantifying robustness.

6. Feasibility. All steps are elementary (Hoeffding + union bound + geometric/ζ(2) series summation) and self-contained given the corpus; the policy is trivially implementable and can be validated empirically against Agrawal's and Lai–Robbins' rules to show smaller finite-horizon regret and absence of burn-in, confirming the asymptotic-vs-finite-time distinction the theorem formalizes.

#### [method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** This corpus proves O(log n) regret is optimal for the bandit, with a Kullback-Leibler constant (Lai & Robbins 1985), and gives attaining index policies: Agrawal's (1995) sample-mean upper-confidence indices with a log inflation width, and Burnetas & Katehakis's (1996) KL-dual indices. Gittins indices (1989; Ishikida & Varaiya 1994) are exact only for discounted Bayesian problems with known priors; exact finite-horizon DP (Berry & Fristedt 1985) is intractable, and Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) note Bayes-optimal solutions are hard to compute. Three gaps: (1) optimality is asymptotic and horizon-agnostic, so the inflation width and constants are untuned for a finite known horizon T where finite-sample regret is very sensitive to them; (2) Burnetas-Katehakis indices need a KL-projection optimization per arm per step; (3) no source treats index/exploration-schedule design as an optimization or search problem tuned to horizon and instance distribution, though Holland's adaptive search and Q-learning-for-bandits show policies can be discovered by search. This leaves room to keep KL-efficiency while minimizing finite-horizon regret and cutting computation.

**Method.** Cast horizon-aware index-policy design as offline search anchored on KL-optimal structure, deployed with amortized look-ahead allocation. (1) Parametric family: index I_a = mean_a + c(theta)*phi(N_a, t, T, KLgap_a; theta), constrained so that as T grows phi reduces to the Lai-Robbins/Agrawal/Burnetas-Katehakis asymptotic width (preserving asymptotic efficiency), while for finite T it damps exploration as t approaches T and shifts budget to small-gap arms. (2) Offline meta-optimization: over a training distribution of instances (varied gaps, arm counts, horizons; Bernoulli, exponential-family, nonparametric finite-support), use derivative-free search (genetic algorithm per Holland, or CMA-ES/Bayesian optimization) to find theta minimizing Monte-Carlo finite-horizon regret, made efficient by racing/successive-halving screening and common-random-number variance reduction, with a penalty forbidding regret above a fixed multiple of the Lai-Robbins bound. (3) Amortized computation: precompute the Burnetas-Katehakis KL-dual index offline as an interpolated function of (mean, count) so per-step use is a lookup; each step also solve a small convex subproblem allocating the T-t remaining pulls to minimize an upper-confidence regret bound from plug-in KL estimates, using its marginal values to scale the index near the horizon end, then play the max-index arm. (4) Evaluate against UCB/Agrawal, Burnetas-Katehakis, and Gittins across horizons and arm counts, checking recovery of KL-optimal constants as T grows plus lower finite-horizon regret and cheaper per-step cost.

#### [method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The classical route to O(log n) regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits relies fundamentally on light-tailed reward assumptions and on the *sample mean* as the summary statistic. Lai & Robbins ("Asymptotically efficient adaptive allocation rules") and Burnetas & Katehakis ("Optimal adaptive policies") build indices from upper confidence bounds whose optimal constants come from Kullback–Leibler / large-deviation rate functions; Burnetas–Katehakis's key assumption (A2) explicitly requires estimators whose deviations are controlled "as the mean of i.i.d. random variables with finite moment generating function in a neighborhood of zero," verified via large deviations. Agrawal's "Sample mean based index policies with O(log n) regret" makes the dependence on the sample mean explicit and states that "all subsequent results ... are based entirely on ... large deviations," using the cumulant generating function c_p(θ)=log∫e^{θx}p(dx) being finite and steep — i.e., a finite MGF, hence essentially light (sub-exponential) tails; its worked examples are one-parameter exponential families (Gaussian, Bernoulli, Poisson).

Yakowitz & Lowe ("Nonparametric bandit methods") pinpoint exactly where this breaks. When only finite moments are assumed (heavy tails), a sample-mean rule can only guarantee a *polynomial* deviation bound P(d,n)=C/n^α (Chebyshev/Fuk–Nagaev), which forces their test-time schedule to grow as x(n)=O(n^{1/(1+α)}); only in the bounded-arm (exponential-tail) case do they recover x(n)=O(log n). Crucially, they prove this is not an artifact of loose analysis: for the sample mean one "cannot improve on the Chebyshev bound" uniformly over variance-1 distributions. Thus, under realistic heavy-tailed rewards (finance, queueing/network delays, insurance losses), the entire asymptotically-efficient O(log n) theory collapses to polynomial regret, and no index policy in these works attains logarithmic regret. The gap is that the bottleneck is the *estimator*, not the bandit principle — yet every one of these works hard-wires the sample mean into the index.

**Method.** Relax the light-tail assumption underpinning O(log n) index policies by decoupling the bandit index from the sample mean and rebuilding it around a *robust* mean estimator that concentrates exponentially even under only a finite (1+ε)-moment condition. Concretely:

1. Robust index construction. For each arm a with T_a(t) observations, replace the sample mean m_a in the UCB index with a robust location estimate μ̂_a: (i) a median-of-means estimator (split observations into k≈⌈c·log t⌉ blocks, take the median of block means), and/or (ii) an adaptively-truncated empirical mean with truncation level b_t chosen to trade bias against variance under the assumed moment. Define the index g_a(t)=μ̂_a + r_a(t), and play argmax_a g_a(t), preserving the simplicity of Agrawal-style sample-based indices.

2. Confidence width from robust concentration, not from the MGF. Choose the inflation r_a(t) using the exponential deviation guarantee of the robust estimator: under only finite variance σ², median-of-means satisfies P(|μ̂_a−μ_a|>d) ≤ exp(−c·T_a·d²/σ²), i.e., precisely the Yakowitz–Lowe "form (3.1b)" exp(−τn) that they could previously obtain only for *bounded* arms. Under a weaker (1+ε)-moment assumption, use the truncated estimator's bias-corrected tail bound (via Fuk–Nagaev) to obtain an analogous near-exponential bound. This lets r_a(t) ≈ C·σ̂_a·sqrt(log t / T_a) with an empirically estimated robust scale σ̂_a.

3. Regret analysis by substitution into the existing frameworks. Because the robust estimator restores an exponential P(d,n), plug it into (a) the Yakowitz–Lowe test-time recursion x(n)=2ΣP(d,x(j)) — whose solution is O(log n) exactly when P is exponential — and (b) the Burnetas–Katehakis assumption (A2), which only requires Σ P(|θ̂−θ|>ε)=o(log n): the robust estimator makes (A2) hold for heavy-tailed nonparametric families without a finite MGF. This yields a Σ_a O(σ_a²·log n / Δ_a) regret bound for arms with only finite variance, and a graceful (1+ε)-moment version, extending asymptotically-efficient O(log n) allocation from light-tailed exponential families to heavy-tailed arms.

4. Matching lower bound and a "robust divergence" constant. Mirror Lai–Robbins/Burnetas–Katehakis by deriving a class-restricted lower bound for heavy-tailed families and identifying the leading constant as a robust analogue of the KL number (a variance/moment-based information quantity that plays the role Agrawal's large-deviation rate function played in the light-tailed case), characterizing when the robust index is asymptotically optimal.

5. Weaker-knowledge and broader-scope extensions. (i) Unknown scale: estimate σ̂_a robustly online. (ii) Unknown moment order ε: calibrate the truncation level by a Lepski-type rule so the policy adapts to the heaviest admissible tail. (iii) Infinite-arm reservoirs: extend Yakowitz–Lowe's "infinitude of arms" section, where robust estimation now permits pulling from heavy-tailed arm populations while retaining rate guarantees.
