Feasibility: the method reuses the exact index-policy machinery and proof templates of these sources; only the estimator and its concentration input change, and simulations against heavy-tailed reward distributions (e.g., Pareto, Student-t) can directly demonstrate the transition from the polynomial n^{1/(1+α)} regret of sample-mean rules to the logarithmic regret of the robust index.

#### [method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 26 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works are classical bandit theory built on one primitive: the sample mean plus a confidence bonus. Lai-Robbins (1985), Agrawal (1995), Burnetas-Katehakis (1996), and Yakowitz-Lowe (1991) all prove logarithmic regret only under light tails (an MGF or bounded arms), a known reward range, and clean i.i.d. data. Gap: these sample-mean indices are brittle. With heavy tails (finite variance, no MGF) the large-deviation proofs fail, and a single adversarial or error outlier per arm can distort the index and lock onto a bad arm; scale must also be known. Real clinical trials and adaptive experiments have outliers, heavy tails, and misspecified scale, yet no listed work degrades gracefully instead of catastrophically.

**Method.** Robustify the index policy by replacing the sample mean with a robust estimator and using robust confidence widths, keeping logarithmic regret under only finite variance while tolerating an eta fraction of corrupted rewards per arm. For each arm compute a median-of-means or trimmed-mean estimate, then play the arm with the largest value of that estimate plus a bonus. Derive the bonus from median-of-means concentration, which depends on variance alone (handling heavy tails without an MGF), plus a term proportional to eta times a robust scale so it provably brackets the true mean under contamination. A scale-free variant estimates scale online from the interquartile range of bucket means, removing the known-range assumption. Prove finite-time regret equal to the classical logarithmic term plus a controlled linear-in-eta contamination floor, with a matching lower bound that the floor is unavoidable, recovering classical constants as eta goes to zero. Evaluate against sample-mean UCB and Yakowitz-Lowe under heavy tails, adversarial outliers, and misspecified ranges.

#### [method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 29 tool calls

**Motivation.** The sources describe three disconnected views of one object, a per-arm index from that arm's own history. The Bayesian Gittins tradition (Berry and Fristedt 1985; allocation-indices review; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994) is exactly optimal, decomposing N arms into N one-dimensional indices, where an arm's index is the retirement pension making one indifferent between continuing and quitting; but it needs a known prior and fixed discount, is hard to compute, and lacks regret bounds. The frequentist tradition (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991) uses Kullback-Leibler upper confidence bounds achieving optimal logarithmic regret from the sample mean alone, but is undiscounted and never linked to the retirement value. The RL tradition (Q-learning for bandit problems 1995; Sutton and Barto; Holland) learns indices on-line by stochastic approximation, only for the discounted case and without regret guarantees. No source unifies these three justifications of the same quantity.

**Method.** Propose a calibrated confidence index unifying the three views. Define each arm's index as the retirement pension at which continuing equals quitting (Ishikida and Varaiya), computed under a data-driven posterior and effective discount beta; this equals the Gittins index for fixed prior and discount. Prove that as beta approaches one, with concentration scaled so the exploration budget equals log n, this index converges to the Lai-Robbins and Agrawal Kullback-Leibler upper confidence bound, showing the retirement fixed point and the confidence supremum are the same variational problem, first for exponential families then nonparametrically. Estimate it on-line by a Q-learning restart recursion using only the sample mean and play count. Anneal beta with each arm's play count so the index shifts from a Gittins exploration bonus to the log-n confidence width; play the largest index. Using Agrawal large-deviation lemmas and Ishikida-Varaiya convexity, prove optimal logarithmic regret while matching Gittins in the discounted regime, and validate that it recovers tabulated Bernoulli Gittins indices and confidence-bound regret across parametric and nonparametric arms.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works form two historically separate streams rarely operationalized in one tool. A frequentist regret stream builds upper-confidence / large-deviation index policies for the undiscounted horizon: Lai & Robbins' asymptotically efficient rules, Agrawal's sample-mean O(log n) indices from Kullback-Leibler large deviations, Burnetas & Katehakis' dual-problem indices, and Yakowitz & Lowe's nonparametric strategies, all targeting KL-calibrated logarithmic regret with little or no prior. A Bayesian discounted stream builds Gittins dynamic-allocation indices (Gittins, Berry & Fristedt, Ishikida & Varaiya), exactly optimal for geometrically discounted reward via per-arm retirement problems, but needing a prior, heavy dynamic programming, and tied to the discounted (not regret) criterion. A reinforcement-learning stream (Sutton, Watkins' Q-learning for bandit problems, Holland) shows Gittins-type indices can be learned model-free by solving the per-arm stopping problem, but gives no finite-time regret guarantee. These literatures are disconnected on three axes: objective (regret vs. discounted vs. average reward, only partly bridged asymptotically by Ishikida & Varaiya), assumptions (KL/parametric vs. Bayesian prior vs. model-free), and machinery (confidence bounds vs. dynamic programming vs. temporal-difference learning). No artifact lets a practitioner take an arbitrary, possibly nonparametric arm and obtain one index that is exploration-aware like Gittins, model-free like Q-learning, and provably logarithmic-regret like Lai-Robbins. The bridge opportunity is to unify these streams in one deployable engine and expose that the confidence inflation of frequentist indices and the value-of-information of Gittins indices are two views of the same exploration bonus.

**Method.** Build BRIDGE-INDEX, a modular open-source index-policy synthesis engine whose central contribution is the artifact plus a new hybrid Bootstrapped Gittins-UCB (BGU) index. Layer (a) nonparametric belief: per arm keep an empirical reward distribution and a Bayesian-bootstrap/Dirichlet posterior over it, needing no parametric family, generalizing Yakowitz-Lowe with Pollard's empirical-process tools and replacing the parametric priors Gittins methods require. Layer (b) index computation: per arm solve the one-armed retirement stopping problem for a Gittins-type index model-free via a lightweight Q-learning/value-iteration routine (Watkins, Sutton) run on bootstrap rollouts of the posterior rather than a known kernel. Layer (c) regret calibration: combine the discounted Gittins-type index with a large-deviation/KL confidence term (Agrawal, Burnetas-Katehakis), annealing the effective discount toward 1 with horizon so the index reduces to a KL upper-confidence bound in the undiscounted limit, converting a discounted-optimal quantity into an O(log n)-regret one and unifying value-of-information and confidence-inflation views of exploration. Layer (d) harness: reimplement Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, Burnetas-Katehakis, Yakowitz-Lowe, pure Bayesian Gittins, and plain Q-learning as baselines so any arm model runs under all policies through one interface. Deliverables: (i) a theorem that annealed BGU inherits Agrawal-style O(log n) KL-optimal regret while approximating the Gittins index at fixed discount, using Ishikida-Varaiya's discounted-to-average-reward asymptotics as the crossing point; (ii) experiments over exponential-family, heavy-tailed, and nonparametric bounded arms comparing regret, discounted return, and compute, showing one learned index tracks Gittins early and Lai-Robbins late; (iii) a released deployment workflow turning "give an arm sampler, get a calibrated policy" into one call. This makes the three communities interoperable inside one calibrated, model-free, nonparametric, regret-guaranteed system.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two bandit literatures are disconnected. The statistics/large-deviations tradition (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991) proves index policies attain order log n regret with a Kullback-Leibler-optimal constant, but only asymptotically and often needing the reward-distribution family. The reinforcement-learning tradition (Sutton and Barto; Duff's Q-learning for bandits; Gittins indices; Holland) treats bandits as the exploration testbed but uses simple distribution-free heuristics such as epsilon-greedy and softmax, or discounted Bayesian Gittins indices, with no undiscounted finite-horizon regret guarantee. A practitioner with a fixed finite budget needs a guarantee valid at every n, no distributional knowledge, and constant per-step cost. Neither stream delivers this: no source gives a finite-time, distribution-free logarithmic regret bound for a simple sample-mean index policy, nor a finite-time account of when RL heuristics are near-optimal. Bridging the two yields provably good, practical exploration.

**Method.** Transplant the confidence-bound principle of Lai-Robbins and Agrawal into a finite-time, distribution-free algorithm. Define UCB1: for rewards in the unit interval, at step n play the arm maximizing its sample mean plus the square root of two times ln n divided by its pull count. Prove a bound uniform in n: expected regret at most eight times the sum over suboptimal arms of ln n over the gap, plus a constant times the sum of gaps, using only Chernoff-Hoeffding tails, requiring no distribution family and constant time per step. Add a variance-aware UCB-Tuned variant using empirical variance in the radius. Then give epsilon-greedy with a decreasing schedule of order arms over squared-gap over n a matching finite-time logarithmic regret proof, bridging RL heuristics to the statistics theory. Validate empirically against epsilon-greedy, softmax, and Gittins/Q-learning baselines on Bernoulli and bounded instances, reporting finite-horizon regret distributions.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The corpus reveals three mature but disconnected literatures on the same problem of sequentially allocating pulls among stochastic arms. Frequentist asymptotic index policies (Lai & Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas & Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz & Lowe 1991) optimize regret and give an instance-dependent Kullback-Leibler lower bound and UCB rules attaining it, but almost entirely asymptotically. Bayesian/Gittins-index work (Berry & Fristedt 1985; Gittins 1989; Ishikida & Varaiya 1994) proves index optimality under discounted reward, a different objective. RL/AI heuristics (Sutton & Barto 1998; Q-learning for bandits 1995; Holland 1992) use epsilon-greedy, softmax, Q-learning, and evolutionary search without guarantees. The bridge opportunity: these streams use incompatible objectives, assumptions, and evaluation practices, so no one has used Lai & Robbins's exact computable KL lower bound as a common yardstick to compare a Gittins index, a Q-learning learner, a genetic searcher, and nonparametric UCB rules on one empirical map. This matters because the theory is asymptotic while practitioners work in finite time: we do not know at which horizons the O(log n) constants govern behavior, how far each family sits from the lower bound in finite time, or how they trade off under misspecification and tuning.

**Method.** Empirical Mapping: build an open benchmark and diagnostic suite placing all three traditions on one theory-anchored scale. A common harness reimplements frequentist indices (Lai-Robbins UCB, Agrawal sample-mean, Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric), Bayesian/Gittins indices computed by dynamic programming at several discount factors, and RL/AI heuristics (epsilon-greedy, softmax, Q-learning for bandits, a genetic-algorithm searcher), each with an identical choose-arm/observe-reward API. A controlled instance grid varies distribution family (Bernoulli, Poisson, exponential, Gaussian, bounded nonparametric, heavy-tailed), gaps, arm count K, variance, and horizon n over several orders of magnitude, plus a misspecification axis where parametric policies face violated assumptions. Theory-anchored metrics compute the Lai-Robbins lower bound via the exact KL number and report each policy's finite-time regret as a normalized regret-to-lower-bound ratio, the empirical regret slope in log n, the crossover horizon where log-linear behavior begins, and discounted-reward performance, so all objectives are commensurable by scoring every policy on both regret and discounted criteria. Diagnostics map heuristics' sensitivity to free parameters and parametric indices' robustness under misspecification, attaching confidence bands across many replicates. Synthesis produces phase diagrams showing which family dominates as a function of instance features and regressions relating normalized regret to those features, releasing the benchmark, trajectory datasets, and phase-diagram atlas. This gives the disconnected communities a shared computable yardstick and common finite-time evaluation, revealing where asymptotically optimal indices, Bayesian Gittins rules, and RL/evolutionary heuristics actually win or lose.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the provided literature the identical stochastic multi-armed bandit problem is analyzed by two mature but largely disconnected "index" theories, plus a third learning-based community, each with its own optimality criterion.

(A) Bayesian dynamic-allocation-index theory: the Gittins–Jones dynamic allocation index / break-even value A(F,A) developed in Berry & Fristedt's "Bandit problems" (1985) and Gittins' "Multi-armed bandit allocation indices" (1989). For geometric discounting this decouples k arms into k one-armed problems and is exactly optimal for discounted Bayes reward. Duff's "Q-learning for bandit problems" (1995), together with the RL program of Sutton (1998) and Holland's adaptive systems (1992), shows these indices can be *learned online* via temporal-difference/Q-learning. But this stream's optimality notion (discounted Bayes reward) says nothing about finite-horizon regret, the discount factor β is an exogenous modeling constant, and the online RL exploration is heuristic with no regret guarantee.

(B) Frequentist adaptive-allocation theory: the Lai–Robbins (1985) asymptotic lower bound Σ(μ*−μ_j)/I(θ_j,θ*), Agrawal's (1995) sample-mean upper-confidence index, Burnetas–Katehakis (1996) dual-KL indices attaining nm*−M(θ)log n, and Yakowitz–Lowe (1991) nonparametric rates. This stream gives exact asymptotic regret optimality through KL/large-deviation *confidence* bonuses, but has no notion of prior, discounting, value function, or online value learning; its indices come from concentration, not from optimal lookahead.

Both streams compute the same object — index = sample mean + an uncertainty bonus — yet no formal derivation links the Gittins "prospective" bonus (value of information under posterior lookahead, driven by β) to the KL "confidence" bonus (driven by allowed horizon n). The bridge opportunity: connect the Bayesian/RL value-learning community with the statistical regret community so that (i) online Q-learning agents inherit provable Lai–Robbins/Burnetas–Katehakis regret optimality, and (ii) the discount factor — currently an arbitrary constant — is given a principled, regret-optimal, horizon-dependent meaning.

**Method.** Contribution is a formal derivation unifying the two index theories and their optimality criteria, with a learnable corollary for the RL community.

1. Taxonomy / conceptual distinction. Formalize any arm index as I_a = μ̂_a + Bonus_a, and distinguish two bonus generators: (i) a *prospective* bonus B_G(state; β) = the Gittins break-even excess over the posterior mean, defined by the discounted one-armed stopping problem of Berry–Fristedt/Gittins; (ii) a *confidence* bonus B_C(state; n) = the KL inflation radius of Burnetas–Katehakis/Agrawal. This isolates what each community actually optimizes and exposes them as two parameterizations (β vs. n) of one quantity.

2. Correspondence theorem. For single-parameter exponential-family arms with conjugate priors, derive the vanishing-discount (β→1) asymptotic expansion of B_G via a Laplace/large-deviations analysis of the stopping problem that defines the Gittins index (reusing the large-deviation lemmas Agrawal already employs). Prove that under the horizon-tied annealing schedule 1−β_n ∝ 1/log n, the leading-order Gittins exploration radius coincides, up to o(1), with the KL confidence radius that yields the Lai–Robbins constant. This is the formal bridge: annealed-discount Gittins index ≈ KL-UCB index.

3. Consequence theorem ("vanishing-discount regret"). Show that the policy playing the annealed-discount Gittins index attains regret nμ*−ES_n = M(θ)log n + o(log n), matching the Lai–Robbins lower bound and the Burnetas–Katehakis constant M(θ). This gives a single objective bridging discounted Bayes optimality and frequentist regret optimality, and endows β with a regret-optimal interpretation (β as an inverse-log-horizon exploration temperature).

4. Learnable corollary. Because Duff-style Q-learning already estimates per-arm one-armed-bandit values, show that a temporal-difference update with the derived discount schedule β_n and posterior-based optimistic initialization *provably* realizes the KL-optimal index — converting heuristic RL exploration into asymptotically efficient exploration. Prove a necessity result: mis-scaled discounts (fixed β, or 1−β decaying too fast/slow) yield linear regret or a suboptimal constant, characterizing the admissible schedules.

5. Scope and verification. Verify the exponential-family assumptions for Bernoulli, normal, and exponential arms; use Agrawal's contraction principle and Yakowitz–Lowe finite-moment/bounded-support conditions to extend the correspondence to nonparametric bounded arms. Complement the proofs with simulations confirming that annealed-discount Q-learning tracks KL-UCB regret and that the predicted β_n schedule is tight, and quantify the finite-time bias term.

This addresses the gap by supplying the missing derivation that makes the Bayesian/RL index and the frequentist confidence index one theory, giving RL agents regret guarantees and giving discounting a regret-based justification.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two research communities have, for decades, circled the same core question — how to allocate a scarce budget of trials among competing options whose payoffs are unknown — yet they have never been properly connected.

(1) The regret-optimal bandit community. Lai & Robbins (1985) established the O(log n) regret lower bound and asymptotically efficient allocation rules; Agrawal (1995) reduced these to simple sample-mean upper-confidence-bound (UCB) indices with the same O(log n) regret; Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) derived asymptotically optimal indices as solutions to dual problems, including for nonparametric discrete distributions; Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) gave nonparametric index rules with guaranteed convergence rates; and the Gittins-index line (Gittins 1989; Ishikida & Varaiya 1994; Berry & Fristedt 1985) proved index optimality for discounted and arm-acquiring bandits. This literature produces finite-time, provable guarantees on how many trials are "wasted" on inferior options.

(2) The evolutionary-search / genetic-algorithm community. Holland's schema analysis (Adaptation in Natural and Artificial Systems, 1992) explicitly justified the genetic algorithm as an implicit solution to a multi-armed bandit: competing schemata (hyperplane partitions of the search space) are "arms," and fitness-proportional selection is argued to allocate reproductive trials near-optimally via an exponentially-increasing rule derived from a two-armed-bandit heuristic. Sutton (1998/1992) confirms Holland's genetic algorithms and classifier systems are historically part of the same reinforcement-learning family that later produced Q-learning for bandits.

The bridge opportunity: Holland's bandit justification predates and never adopted the regret-optimal index machinery. Fitness-proportional selection is the exponential-allocation heuristic that Lai–Robbins-style analysis shows is generally NOT log-regret optimal, so canonical evolutionary search carries no finite-time guarantee on evaluations spent on inferior regions. Conversely, the bandit-index literature never took up Holland's structured, hierarchical, arm-generating search space as an application — its arms are typically a small fixed flat set. Neither community connected schema refinement (which continually creates new candidate partitions) to the arm-acquiring bandit theory of Whittle/Berry-Fristedt/Ishikida-Varaiya. Closing this gap matters because black-box combinatorial optimization is dominated by evaluation cost, and a principled, regret-bounded allocation of evaluations across schemata would give evolutionary search the sample-efficiency guarantees it has lacked, while giving bandit theory a rich, high-dimensional, arm-acquiring testbed.

**Method.** Proposed method: Index-Guided Hyperplane Search (IGHS) — replace the exponential fitness-proportional allocation at the heart of Holland's schema theory with provably efficient bandit index policies, and treat schema refinement as an arm-acquiring bandit. This is an optimization/search contribution: the algorithm is an adaptive resource-allocation scheme for the finite budget of fitness evaluations.

1. Schemata as bandit arms. Following Holland, partition the search space by hyperplanes: at any node, a set of competing schemata (e.g., fixing a coordinate/factor to each of its values, conditioned on already-fixed coordinates) are the "arms." Pulling an arm = drawing one candidate consistent with that schema and evaluating its (bounded, unknown-distribution) fitness. This directly instantiates the informal bandit that Holland only argued heuristically.

2. Regret-optimal allocation instead of proportional selection. Allocate each successive evaluation to the schema with the highest index rather than in proportion to observed fitness. Use Agrawal's sample-mean UCB index for the bounded/nonparametric case and the Burnetas–Katehakis dual index (and Yakowitz–Lowe nonparametric index) where finer distributional structure is available. By Lai–Robbins/Agrawal this guarantees that evaluations spent on inferior schemata at each node grow only as O(log n), yielding the finite-time sample-efficiency bound canonical GAs lack.

3. Refinement as arm acquisition. When a schema's index dominates, refine it by fixing an additional coordinate, spawning a new, finer set of competing sub-schemata — i.e., new arms appear over time. Crossover/mutation likewise propose genuinely novel schemata. Model the whole process as an arm-acquiring/superprocess bandit (Whittle 1981 via Berry–Fristedt; Ishikida–Varaiya 1994), and use the nested-index / retirement-pension optimality results to decide the principled trade-off between exploiting refined arms and acquiring newly generated ones (the retirement value sets an explicit exploration budget for novelty).

4. Handling non-stationarity. Because conditional fitness distributions shift as partitions are refined and the population concentrates, track them with the discounted Gittins index formulation (Ishikida–Varaiya's discounted/average-reward extensions) or windowed sample means, keeping indices valid under drift.

5. Analysis and evaluation. (a) Theory: compose per-node O(log n) regret across the refinement hierarchy to prove a bound on total evaluations wasted on suboptimal schema paths, and contrast it analytically with the exponential-allocation regret of fitness-proportional selection — making precise where Holland's heuristic is suboptimal. Use Pollard-style empirical-process / large-deviation tools (as Agrawal does) for the nonparametric case. (b) Experiments: on standard combinatorial black-box benchmarks (e.g., deceptive/trap functions, NK-landscapes, max-sat), compare IGHS against canonical fitness-proportional GA, a flat UCB bandit over full solutions, and Gittins-index baselines, measuring fitness reached per evaluation budget and empirical regret. Ablate the three ingredients (index vs proportional allocation; arm-acquisition rule; drift tracking) to isolate the contribution of each bridged idea.

This addresses the gap by importing finite-time regret optimality into evolutionary search and, reciprocally, supplying the bandit-index literature with a concrete, hierarchical, arm-acquiring optimization problem, unifying Holland's schema theory with Lai–Robbins/Agrawal/Burnetas–Katehakis/Gittins index policies.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two disconnected bandit literatures appear in these sources. The Bayesian discounted tradition (Gittins 1989; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994; Berry and Fristedt 1985) gives the Gittins index, an exact optimal calibration of an arm's learning value, but only under a known prior, a conjugate exponential family, and discount below one, and it is costly. The frequentist regret and UCB tradition (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991) builds prior-free indices from sample means and KL confidence bounds achieving optimal order log n regret, but only asymptotically, undiscounted, with a heuristic finite-time exploration bonus. Burnetas and Katehakis note their indices came from Bayes dynamic programming then simplified, and Duff's Q-learning for Bandit Problems (1995) with the RL tradition (Sutton 1998; Holland) shows Gittins indices can be learned model-free. Yet nobody has connected Gittins-optimal calibration to the KL-confidence machinery, nor unified the discounted and undiscounted regimes. Practitioners must choose between a computable heuristic UCB bonus and an optimally calibrated but model-bound Gittins index, with no interpolation between impatient discounted and patient long-horizon objectives.

**Method.** We propose a Nonparametric Optimistic Gittins Index making Gittins-style calibration work under prior-free UCB assumptions. The Gittins index is the retirement value at which one is indifferent between continuing an arm and taking a fixed reward forever under discount gamma, classically solved against a conjugate posterior. We relax this three ways. First, replace the conjugate posterior with the empirical distribution F-hat, a nonparametric belief in the Yakowitz-Lowe finite-moment setting, plus a KL confidence ball built with Agrawal and Burnetas-Katehakis machinery, and compute the index optimistically as the maximum over distributions in that ball of the discounted retirement value; the confidence set supplies exploration and the stopping value supplies calibration. Second, treat gamma as an explicit knob: gamma below one gives discounted-optimal behavior while gamma toward one with ball width scaled per Lai-Robbins recovers average-reward behavior targeting the optimal log n constant, so one policy spans both regimes. Third, compute the index model-free by treating the stopping subproblem as an arm-local belief MDP with transitions from bootstrap resampling of F-hat, solved by extending Duff's Q-learning, applicable to bounded and finite-moment arms including the nonparametric and unknown-variance cases Burnetas-Katehakis left open. We validate by proving order log n regret with the KL-optimal constant as gamma approaches one and recovery of the exact Gittins index in the conjugate limit, characterizing the gamma versus ball-width trade-off, and benchmarking against UCB, Burnetas-Katehakis indices, exact Gittins, and Q-learning indices on Bernoulli, heavy-tailed, and discrete-support arms.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works form three largely disconnected research streams that all revolve around the *index* as the organizing object of bandit decision-making, yet almost never talk to each other:

(1) Frequentist regret minimization. Lai & Robbins (1985) derive the O(log n) regret lower bound with a constant fixed by the Kullback–Leibler number; Agrawal (1995) and Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) build upper-confidence-bound (UCB) index policies that attain it, and Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) push toward nonparametric arms. The optimality of these indices, however, is tied to *correct specification* of the reward-distribution family (e.g., one-parameter exponential families). Agrawal explicitly notes that his sample-mean indices, obtained via a large-deviations "contraction principle," recover the optimal KL constant only for exponential families and are otherwise merely a lower proxy — i.e., robustness to misspecification is bought by sacrificing efficiency.

(2) Bayesian dynamic allocation. Gittins (1989), Berry & Fristedt (1985), and Ishikida & Varaiya (1994) show the Gittins index is exactly optimal for the discounted criterion. But this optimality presumes a *correct prior* and is computationally heavy; it is brittle when the prior or the discount model is wrong, and it is disconnected from the regret/KL machinery of stream (1).

(3) Learning-by-simulation. Q-learning for bandits (1995), Sutton & Barto (1998), and Holland (1992) offer stochastic-approximation and evolutionary ways to *compute* indices from experience, but with no statistical optimality or robustness guarantees, and no link to the sharp KL bounds of streams (1)–(2).

The bridge opportunity: no work connects the large-deviations/KL calibration of the frequentist community, the index-optimality of the Bayesian community, and the online-estimation machinery of the RL community into a single index that is *provably robust to misspecification* of both the reward family (UCB's weak point) and the prior/discount model (Gittins' weak point). This matters because in practice the reward family is unknown and priors are guesses; both classical index policies can fail badly precisely where the other might succeed, and neither degrades gracefully.

**Method.** Propose a **Distributionally Robust Index (DRI)** that unifies and robustifies UCB and Gittins indices, computed online by stochastic approximation.

Core construction. For each arm a with n_a observed rewards and empirical distribution F̂_a, build an *ambiguity set* U_a(t) = { G : D(G ‖ F̂_a) ≤ ρ(n_a, t) } around the empirical law (a KL or Wasserstein ball), taking the empirical/nonparametric law as center in the spirit of Yakowitz & Lowe rather than assuming a parametric family. Define the arm's index as an *optimistic-robust* value:
  I_a(t) = sup_{G ∈ U_a(t)} V(G),
where V(G) is the criterion-specific value of an arm whose reward law is G. This single template specializes across the disconnected literatures:
  - Regret criterion: V(G) = mean(G) → the sup is solved by convex duality using exactly Agrawal's large-deviations rate function, recovering KL-UCB but *without* needing to know the reward family.
  - Discounted criterion: V(G) = Gittins index of an arm with law G → a "robust Gittins index" that replaces reliance on a single prior with a worst-case set.

Calibrating the radius (the robustification knob). Set ρ(n_a, t) ≈ (log t)/n_a so the ball shrinks at the large-deviations rate. This is the key device that *closes Agrawal's efficiency gap*: because the ambiguity set is sized by the KL rate function, the robust index stays tight enough to match the Lai–Robbins constant asymptotically while remaining valid for any distribution in a broad nonparametric class. The radius directly trades robustness against efficiency, giving a graceful-degradation guarantee absent from both classical policies.

Online computation (bridging the RL stream). Solve the inner worst-case problem online via a Q-learning / stochastic-approximation recursion (Watkins; Sutton & Barto): for the discounted case, run a restart-in-state value recursion for the Gittins index nested with an adversarial-perturbation step that reweights sampled rewards to the worst case within U_a via the dual variables. For high-dimensional or infinite-arm settings (Yakowitz's infinite-population extension), use a Holland-style population/genetic search over the ambiguity set as a heuristic inner solver.

Guarantees and evaluation. (i) Prove a finite-time regret upper bound matching the Lai–Robbins/Agrawal lower bound uniformly over a nonparametric family, using Agrawal's large-deviations lemmas. (ii) Prove a *robustness* theorem: bounded regret/value degradation as a function of the true misspecification gap, whereas KL-UCB under a wrong family and Bayesian Gittins under a wrong prior have unbounded degradation. (iii) Show the exact reductions to KL-UCB and to the classic Gittins index in the well-specified limits, demonstrating the unification. (iv) Empirically compare against KL-UCB, sample-mean UCB, and Gittins on correctly specified, misspecified-family, heavy-tailed, and misspecified-prior instances to show DRI is the only policy robust across all regimes.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 31 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two mature but disconnected bandit literatures each produced index policies (a scalar index per arm from its own data, play the largest). A Bayesian/discounted stream (Gittins allocation indices in multi-armed-bandit-allocation-indices-1989; bandit-problems-1985, which catalogues it as the break-even value of a known arm; multi-armed-bandit-problem-revisited-1994) casts the index as a retirement/break-even value, the sup over stopping times of a discounted-reward ratio; it is optimal for discounted reward, needs a prior, and is learnable by RL (q-learning-for-bandit-problems-1995). A frequentist/regret stream (Lai-Robbins asymptotically-efficient-adaptive-allocation-rules-1985, O(log n) bound with a KL constant; Agrawal sample-mean-based-index-policies, inflating the sample mean via a large-deviations contraction of KL; Burnetas-Katehakis optimal-adaptive-policies, indices as the dual, the largest mean in a KL confidence ball; Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric-bandit-methods-1991) targets undiscounted regret with no prior and separate machinery. Bridge opportunity: both indices are one object, a sup over a plausibility set of a value functional, yet no work connects them, explains why the UCB exploration bonus is the frequentist analogue of the Gittins option value, or gives one online prior-free algorithm recovering each as a limit. RL (reinforcement-learning-an-introduction-1998) treats the bandit as its base case but its action-values encode neither index.

**Method.** Propose a single Calibrated Confidence Index (CCI) unifying the Gittins break-even value, the KL-UCB/Burnetas-Katehakis dual index, and Q-learning. Common template: I(arm) equals the sup over a plausibility set P of a value operator V evaluated against a known retirement reward lambda, taking the indifference lambda between continuing and retiring; this generalizes the Gittins break-even value and the Burnetas-Katehakis dual (largest mean over a KL-constrained set), which equals Agrawal sample-mean inflation. Two bridging knobs: a discount/effective-horizon beta and the plausibility set P, a KL/large-deviations ball around the empirical distribution with radius shrinking like (log n)/N_arm, using Agrawal contraction lemmas and Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric estimators so no parametric family is required. Discounted limit (beta below 1, P a concentrating credible set): CCI reduces to the Gittins index, optimality via Ishikida-Varaiya envelope arguments. Regret limit (beta toward 1, (log n)/N radius): the sup over P collapses to the KL-UCB/Lai-Robbins index, revealing the exploration bonus as the option value of the belief set upper tail, with O(log n) regret from Agrawal Theorem 2.2 and Lai-Robbins. One algorithm: generalize Gittins-via-Q-learning (q-learning-for-bandit-problems-1995) by keeping per-arm sufficient statistics as state and running a restart-style temporal-difference update (reinforcement-learning-an-introduction-1998) that estimates V against candidate lambda using the empirical plausibility set instead of a fixed prior, giving a prior-free, model-free, nonparametric index tuned continuously from discounted-Gittins to regret-optimal-UCB by one schedule. Validation: prove TD convergence via convergence-of-stochastic-processes-1984 and mathematical-statistics-1962, O(log n) regret with the Lai-Robbins KL constant undiscounted, exact Gittins recovery discounted, and compare CCI against Gittins, KL-UCB/Burnetas-Katehakis, and Q-learning on parametric and nonparametric arm families to show one algorithm spanning both silos and exposing the discounted-vs-regret tradeoff as a continuous dial.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 23 turns / 30 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior works give bandit policies each proven asymptotically optimal but under incomparable assumptions: Lai-Robbins (1985) O(log n) KL lower bound and UCB rules; Agrawal (1995) sample-mean index policies; Burnetas-Katehakis (1996) dual-index policies for discrete finite support; Gittins and Ishikida-Varaiya Bayesian discounted indices; Yakowitz-Lowe (1991) nonparametric strategies. Evidence gap: these guarantees are asymptotic under incomparable assumptions, and the field lacks a standardized, reproducible way to observe and measure finite-horizon behavior, how tight realized regret constants are versus the Lai-Robbins KL bound, when the asymptotic regime begins, and which policy dominates in which regime. Existing evidence is fragmentary; Yakowitz-Lowe report only a few two-armed runs to 2000 decisions and others are validated only theoretically. Practitioners cannot audit whether asymptotic optimality yields practical performance, compare policies on common ground, or locate failure under misspecification.

**Method.** Build an open, reproducible bandit evidence testbed as the central artifact, with four parts. One, a unified policy library behind one history-in arm-out interface: Lai-Robbins UCB, Agrawal sample-mean index, Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index, Gittins index, Q-learning, and Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric policies, each declaring its assumed reward class. Two, a distribution generator suite spanning the theories regimes: exponential families such as Bernoulli, Poisson, Gaussian, bounded support, discrete finite support, and heavy-tailed finite-moment, parameterized by gap, arm count, and horizon with fixed seeds. Three, instrumentation and diagnostics per configuration cell: finite-time regret curves, suboptimal-pull counts, a constant-tightness ratio of empirical regret to the KL-based Lai-Robbins bound times log n, an asymptotic-onset horizon, a misspecification-robustness score from running policies on assumption-violating arms, and compute cost. Four, an append-only evidence ledger producing dominance maps, tightness curves, and failure flags, extensible to new policies. Validation reproduces each source original claims, then compares all policies on shared environments to quantify finite-horizon gaps, constant tightness, cross-regime dominance, and robustness.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 23 turns / 32 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classical bandit papers prove many asymptotically efficient index policies with log n regret constants tied to KL numbers, but under disjoint assumptions and with no common way to measure and compare their finite-horizon behavior.

**Method.** Build a finite-horizon benchmark simulating diverse arm families and running all these policies, reporting efficiency ratios, floor-gap ratios versus the Lai-Robbins bound, log-linearity onset horizons, and per-arm regret attribution with confidence bands.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** The bandit works here form a chain of asymptotic optimality theorems, each under different assumptions and each yielding a different index rule. Lai and Robbins (1985) give the O(log n) lower bound with a KL constant and policies for exponential families. Agrawal (1995) gives simpler sample-mean UCB indices that match the KL-optimal constant only for one-parameter exponential families, otherwise using a provably suboptimal contraction constant. Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) give dual-index policies optimal for discrete finite-support families but leave normal-with-unknown-variance open. Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) give nonparametric rules with only some computational experience. Gittins-index theory (1989 book; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994) targets a Bayesian discounted objective, and Q-learning-for-bandits (1995) yet another framework. The evidence gap: the field lacks a common yardstick to observe and compare what these policies actually do at finite horizons. No one systematically measures how far realized finite-horizon regret sits from the KL lower bound, the horizon at which the asymptotic log-slope engages, how much the contraction constant loses versus the KL-optimal one, how each policy degrades when assumptions are violated, and compute cost and regret variance. The Berry-Fristedt bibliography confirms existing simulations are fragmentary, mostly two-arm Bernoulli, never unified across policy families or distributions. Without this evidence one cannot audit which guarantee translates into finite-sample performance.

**Method.** Build a unified, reproducible benchmark and diagnostic suite comparing the index policies from these sources on common ground. Policy library from a shared interface: Lai-Robbins KL-UCB, Agrawal sample-mean UCB with both KL-optimal and contraction constants, Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric index, the Gittins index with uniform and geometric-discount approximations, Q-learning-for-bandits, plus baselines (equal allocation, greedy, Thompson-style randomization). Environment grid: distribution class (Bernoulli, Gaussian known and unknown variance, exponential, bounded finite-support discrete, heavy-tailed finite-moment), arm count from 2 to many, gaps from well-separated to near-tied, horizons across several decades of n, many seeds each. Diagnostics per policy-environment cell: mean cumulative regret with confidence bands; an efficiency ratio equal to realized regret over the Lai-Robbins KL lower-bound curve; a burn-in horizon where the empirical log-n slope first matches the asymptotic constant; a contraction-loss metric differencing sample-mean versus KL-optimal constants to test Agrawal's suboptimality empirically; a misspecification-robustness score from running assumption-specific policies out of assumption (exponential-family UCB on heavy-tailed arms, Burnetas-Katehakis on normal unknown variance); regret variance and upper-quantile risk; per-step compute. Pattern analysis aggregating into empirical maps of dominance regions, heatmaps of efficiency ratio and burn-in horizon versus gap size and arm count, and frequentist-UCB versus Bayesian-Gittins comparison when objectives align, testing hypotheses such as contraction cost appearing only outside exponential families. Deliver an open, extensible benchmark with fixed seeds, the KL-lower-bound reference, and standardized diagnostics so new policies can be audited against the same map. This converts isolated asymptotic theorems into accumulated, comparable finite-sample evidence, quantifying the theory-practice gap and localizing where each guarantee holds or breaks.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Every policy in this corpus is certified by the same kind of guarantee: an asymptotic constant times log n. Lai and Robbins (1985) prove the floor with KL number I(theta_j,theta*); Agrawal (1995) gives sample-mean index policies whose constant is a contraction of KL, admittedly suboptimal; Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) obtain the exact optimal constant M(theta)=sum(mu*-mu_a)/K_a(theta) via a minimum-discrimination index; Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) give nonparametric rates; Gittins-index results target discounted optimality. This is an evidence gap: these constants live in the limit and require the unknown theta, so from a single finite run one cannot observe how far a policy is from the information floor or whether the asymptotic regime is reached; the policies use incommensurable yardsticks so there is no like-for-like way to audit, compare, or diagnose wasted exploration; and no accepted decomposition attributes accrued regret to distinguishable causes. The field proves asymptotic optimality but cannot measure finite-time efficiency from data, which matters for the clinical-trial and machine-learning settings these papers motivate.

**Method.** A Formal Derivation: a unified regret-accounting model, an efficiency functional, a decomposition theorem, and an observable auditor with concentration guarantees, built from objects in these sources (KL number I, Agrawal contracted KL, Burnetas-Katehakis K_a, large-deviation change-of-measure). (1) For any uniformly-fast index policy with inflation function g, decompose E[T_j(n)] into an information floor log n / I(theta_j,theta*), a slack term of extra pulls from the gap between the policy confidence inflation and the true KL ball, and an o(log n) transient; this yields a four-way taxonomy: information floor, confidence slack, misspecification, transient. (2) Using change-of-measure and large-deviation lemmas of Lai-Robbins and Agrawal, define the effective discrimination rate I_g induced by the index and the efficiency ratio rho_j = I_g / K_j(theta) in (0,1]; derive R_n = sum over suboptimal arms of (mu*-mu_j) log n /(rho_j K_j(theta)) + o(log n), and prove rho_j=1 recovers the Burnetas-Katehakis optimum, Agrawal contraction yields explicit rho_j<1, and nonparametric conservatism is a bounded rho-loss, placing all policies on one yardstick. (3) Since I and K need unknown theta, derive a plug-in floor estimator from each arm empirical KL or empirical likelihood and an observed regret proxy; via self-normalized concentration grounded in the convergence-of-stochastic-processes and mathematical-statistics sources, prove finite-time confidence bands for the online efficiency ratio and rho_j, plus a diagnostic stopping test flagging with controlled error whether the run entered the log-n regime and whether measured slack exceeds the minimum. (4) Instantiate on Bernoulli, exponential-family, and finite-support arms to get closed-form rho_j for Lai-Robbins UCB, Agrawal index, and Burnetas-Katehakis dual index, and simulate to confirm coverage. This converts asymptotic theta-dependent constants into a single efficiency scale rho, a causal decomposition of regret, and an online calibrated auditor for observing, benchmarking, and comparing finite-time exploration efficiency of any index policy from its own trajectory.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works form a lineage of bandit policies that are each proven optimal, but almost entirely in *asymptotic* or *idealized* terms. Lai & Robbins (1985) and Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) establish that regret grows like M(θ)·log n with a leading constant fixed by a Kullback–Leibler number (or by a dual-problem index); Agrawal (1995) derives a simpler sample-mean UCB whose constant is obtained from the optimal one via a "contraction," and is optimal only for one-parameter exponential families; Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) give guaranteed nonparametric convergence *rates* and only "share some computational experience"; the Gittins/Bayesian line (Berry & Fristedt, Ishikida & Varaiya, Gittins) proves index optimality under discounting; Q-learning/RL is validated mostly by isolated empirical demonstrations. 

The result is a clear **evidence gap**: there is no systematic way to *observe, measure, benchmark, and compare* how these policies actually behave at the finite horizons practitioners face, nor to *audit* when the celebrated asymptotic constants (KL numbers, contraction constants, dual-index M(θ)) genuinely govern performance versus when they are misleading. Key questions have no reproducible answer: At what horizon does each policy's regret enter the log-linear regime predicted by theory? What is the empirical ratio of realized regret to the theoretical constant across gaps, variances, arm counts, and distribution families? How large is the finite-time penalty of Agrawal's "contraction" relative to KL-optimal policies, and where do policies cross over? How robust is each policy when its distributional assumptions are misspecified (e.g., nonparametric vs. exponential-family arms)? This matters because policy choice is currently guided by asymptotic constants that may not predict finite-time behavior, and because the field has accumulated theory far faster than comparable, structured empirical evidence.

**Method.** Build an adaptive evidence-accumulation framework that recasts *comparing and auditing* these bandit policies as an optimization/search problem over the space of problem instances, and that spends simulation budget efficiently using the same confidence-bound allocation ideas the policies embody.

(1) Instance space and policy panel. Parameterize bandit instances by a vector z = (distribution family, arm means/gaps Δ, variances, number of arms, horizon n). Fix a panel of policies from the corpus: Lai–Robbins KL-UCB, Agrawal sample-mean index, Burnetas–Katehakis dual-index, Yakowitz–Lowe nonparametric, a Gittins-index rule, and Q-learning.

(2) Diagnostic objectives. For a target policy compute an audit score such as the ratio of Monte-Carlo finite-time regret R_n(z) to the theoretical prediction M(θ)·log n, and the empirical horizon at which R_n enters the log-linear regime. For a pair of policies compute the finite-time regret *separation*. These become objective functions over z.

(3) Adversarial/active instance search. Rather than a fixed grid, use black-box search (evolutionary search in the spirit of Holland's genetic algorithms, warm-started by Bayesian optimization) to *maximize* these objectives—i.e., automatically discover the instances where theory-vs-practice mismatch is largest, where a "contraction" penalty is worst, and where crossovers between policies occur. This turns benchmarking into a search for maximally informative evidence instead of arbitrary test cases.

(4) Efficient budget allocation (meta-bandit). Each objective evaluation is a noisy Monte-Carlo estimate, so treat the choice of which (instance, policy) cell to simulate next as itself a sequential resource-allocation problem: apply UCB-style upper-confidence-bound screening and racing/successive-elimination to concentrate simulation budget on cells whose ranking or audit verdict is still uncertain, and prune cells with settled outcomes. This yields provable-style sample efficiency (reusing the very KL/UCB machinery under study) and lets the framework scale across many horizons and families.

(5) Outputs. Produce a reproducible "evidence map": empirical-to-theoretical constant ratios per policy and family, finite-time crossover frontiers between policies, horizon thresholds where asymptotics take hold, and misspecification-robustness curves. 

This directly addresses the evidence gap—supplying the missing observational, benchmarking, and auditing layer—while the contribution is fundamentally an optimization/search-and-allocation strategy for discovering and certifying that evidence efficiently.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 25 turns / 35 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit theory offers index policies each proven asymptotically optimal with regret growing like a constant M times log n tied to a Kullback-Leibler large-deviation rate: Lai-Robbins for exponential families, Agrawal for sample-mean UCB, Burnetas-Katehakis for nonparametric dual-problem indices, Yakowitz-Lowe for nonparametric bounded or finite-moment arms, and Gittins-type index rules. Two evidence gaps remain. First, no shared instrument exists to observe or compare these policies: the field cannot tell whether M is attained in finite time, which policy dominates in which regime, or how much regret is over-exploration versus brittle estimation. Second, every guarantee rests on one assumption, that rewards are independent and identically distributed within an arm with a tail condition giving an exponential large-deviation bound on the sample mean. Real rewards are heavy-tailed, corrupted, weakly dependent, or drifting, and there is no evidence about whether these guarantees survive such violations nor any instrument to measure the degradation.

**Method.** Part A relaxes scope. The only property the log-n proofs use is that the probability the index underestimates the true mean decays faster than one over n. Sample means satisfy this only for independent, light-tailed, clean data. We keep the index structure of a robust location estimate plus an exploration bonus but replace the sample mean with robust estimators such as truncated, Catoni, or median-of-means estimators whose exponential tail bounds survive heavy tails, a fixed contamination fraction, and weak dependence. We re-derive the underestimation bound per regime, recovering logarithmic regret with an explicit degraded constant depending on contamination, tail index, and mixing rate, giving a robustified reference policy under weaker assumptions. Part B is an evidence framework instrumenting classical and robustified policies on a stress-test battery sweeping tail index, contamination, mixing, and drift. It reports each policy realized regret constant against its theoretical constant, a regret inflation factor per violation locating each policy breakdown boundary, and a decomposition attributing excess regret to estimator brittleness versus exploration. Everything is computed by Monte Carlo using closed-form indices from the sources, so it is feasible.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classic bandit index policies prove O(log n) regret only under specific distributional assumptions, with constants tied to the KL number of the assumed family. There is no shared way to measure, benchmark, or audit how they behave when assumptions are violated by heavy tails, contamination, or near-tied means.

**Method.** Build a robust index policy using median-of-means estimators and distribution-free empirical-Bernstein confidence widths, plus a benchmark suite that stress-tests classic policies across misspecification conditions and reports regret-inflation and exploration-calibration diagnostics.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit research is fragmented across Bayesian Gittins-index, frequentist KL-regret UCB, RL Q-learning, and evolutionary paradigms, each with its own objective and metrics. The evidence gap: no common instrument exists to observe, measure, and benchmark how much exploration each policy actually performs relative to the information-theoretic minimum.

**Method.** Build a unifying exploration-accounting benchmark that decomposes every policy into an exploitation term plus an exploration bonus, scores them under discounted, regret, and average-reward objectives on shared testbeds, and audits their exploration against the Lai and Robbins KL lower bound to compute over-exploration ratios and regret attribution.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works converge on a fact none explains. Two opposed derivations both yield index-plus-bonus allocation rules that behave near-optimally: the Bayesian discounted dynamic-programming route to the Gittins index (Berry and Fristedt; Ishikida and Varaiya; the Q-learning-for-bandits route that learns Gittins indices via RL, with Sutton and Barto), and the frequentist large-deviation route to upper-confidence indices with O(log n) regret whose constant is the Kullback-Leibler number (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe), using the limit tools of Cramer and Pollard. Yet no work gives a unified mechanistic account of why this happens or why the policies diverge. Open why-questions: why does a KL number govern the regret constant, and what is the exploration bonus really made of (a confidence radius, a value of information, or an implicit discount)? Why do Agrawal's sample-mean indices, admittedly attaining a suboptimal contracted constant, still perform comparably, i.e. when does the constant matter? Why do Gittins and confidence indices sometimes make identical choices and sometimes not? Why is finite-time behavior often far from the asymptotic log n theory? Each paper supplies an asymptotic constant or optimality proof but no causal decomposition of the finite-time exploration decisions that generate regret. This matters because practitioners choose among these rules with no mechanistic understanding of what drives performance in the regimes they actually run.

**Method.** Build a reproducible software platform, a Bandit Index Observatory, whose central contribution is an instrumented artifact producing the missing causal account. (1) A common generator instantiates arm environments matching the shared assumptions of all source policies: exponential families (Lai-Robbins), general sample-mean settings (Agrawal), nonparametric finite-support and bounded families (Burnetas-Katehakis, Yakowitz-Lowe), and matched Bayesian priors and discounts for the Gittins setting. (2) For every history state the platform computes each policy index and rewrites it canonically as mean term plus exploration term; Gittins indices are computed by dynamic programming and also learned by Q-learning, so the learned bonus can be compared directly with the analytic KL bonuses, each mapped to an implied confidence level and an implied value of information to test whether they encode the same quantity. (3) Every pull is logged and classified with a mechanistic taxonomy (arm-confusion events where empirical KL falls below the bonus, over-exploration, under-exploration, estimation error), and accumulated regret is additively attributed to these mechanisms. (4) The platform estimates the empirical large-deviation exponent of suboptimal-arm elimination and compares it to the Lai-Robbins optimal constant, Agrawal's contracted constant, and the Burnetas-Katehakis dual constant, quantifying when the suboptimal constant bites. (5) A controlled intervention suite sweeps discount factor, KL geometry, tail heaviness, arm count, and horizon to output a phase diagram of which structural factor drives agreement or divergence among the policies and which mechanism dominates finite-time regret. Deliverables: the open platform plus an evidence-backed causal account answering the why-questions, validated by the platform's own interventions rather than asymptotics alone.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 6 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** An "explanation gap" sits at the center of the classical bandit results collected here. Lai & Robbins (1985) established that the minimal achievable regret grows as (sum over suboptimal arms) of (mu*-mu_j)/KL(j,*) * log n, and Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) generalized the optimal constant to broad (including nonparametric discrete) families via dual/index constructions. Agrawal (1995) then showed that the practically attractive class of policies whose indices depend on the data ONLY through the sample mean (the UCB lineage) still attain O(log n) regret, but with a constant obtained by a large-deviations "contraction" of the Kullback-Leibler number onto the mean. Crucially, Agrawal reports two facts without a mechanistic explanation: (i) the sample-mean constant is exactly optimal for one-parameter exponential families but strictly suboptimal "in general," and (ii) if one instead indexes on the full empirical measure, the optimal constant is recovered "modulo continuity conditions." Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) similarly note that only moment/boundedness assumptions are needed for rate-optimality in nonparametric settings.

What is missing across all of these works is a causal account of WHY and BY HOW MUCH restricting an index to the sample mean costs asymptotic optimality, WHICH distributional features drive that loss, and WHY it vanishes precisely for exponential families. This matters because sample-mean indices are the ones actually deployed, yet the field lacks (a) a quantitative theory of the excess-regret constant as a functional of reward-distribution shape, (b) an explanation of the empirically observed robustness of sample-mean UCB at practical horizons despite asymptotic suboptimality, and (c) a principled statement of the *minimal* information beyond the mean needed to close the gap. The provided sources give all the analytical ingredients (KL/large-deviation rate functions, the contraction principle, the empirical-measure optimum, Pollard-style empirical-process tools, and moment-estimation results) but stop at existence/optimality claims rather than an explanatory decomposition.

**Method.** Goal: produce a mechanistic, quantitative theory of the suboptimality of sample-mean-based index policies and use it to explain when/why the loss appears and how minimally to remove it.

1) Formalize the gap. Define per-arm excess regret constant Delta_j(F) = C_mean(F) - C_opt(F), where C_opt uses the full-distribution rate I(F_j, F*) (Lai-Robbins / Burnetas-Katehakis) and C_mean uses Agrawal's mean-contracted rate J(x)=inf{KL(nu,mu): E_nu=x}. Because both constants have the form (mu*-mu_j)/rate, express Delta_j via the wedge between the mean-constrained I-projection and the true alternative distribution. Prove the equivalence: Delta_j(F)=0 iff the true competing distribution IS the mean-constrained I-projection at the boundary mean — which characterizes exactly the exponential-family case and yields a crisp "optimality boundary" in distribution space.

2) Explanatory decomposition. Perturb F around an exponential family and Taylor-expand Delta in interpretable shape quantities (higher cumulants: skewness, excess kurtosis, and proximity/geometry of the support edge relative to mu*). This produces closed-form predictions of gap magnitude and sign as a function of distributional features, turning "suboptimal in general" into concrete statements like "loss grows with the mismatch between the tail toward mu* and its exponential tilt."

3) Minimal sufficient exploration statistics. Construct a hierarchy of index policies indexed by how much of the empirical distribution they use: mean only -> mean+variance -> first m empirical moments -> full empirical measure (recovering Burnetas-Katehakis). Using the contraction machinery, characterize how fast Delta shrinks as m grows and identify, per distribution family, the smallest set of functionals that drives Delta below a target — the "minimal sufficient statistic for optimal exploration." Provide upper-confidence-bound constructions for these functionals (Agrawal explicitly notes his UCBs extend to additive functionals beyond the mean; combine with moment concentration and Pollard empirical-process tools for validity).

4) Explain finite-time robustness. Decompose finite-horizon regret into the asymptotic-constant term times log n plus lower-order terms, and quantify the horizon n* beyond which the asymptotic gap dominates the estimation-variance terms. This yields an explanatory account of why sample-mean UCB looks near-optimal at practical horizons even when asymptotically suboptimal (the constant gap is masked until n* is large).

5) Empirical validation. Simulate multi-armed bandits across controlled families (Bernoulli, truncated/bounded Gaussian, skewed mixtures, bounded heavy-tail) spanning the predicted feature axes. Measure realized regret and fit empirical constants for (a) sample-mean UCB, (b) the moment-augmented hierarchy, and (c) empirical-measure/Burnetas-Katehakis indices. Test the theory's three predictions: the optimality-boundary characterization, the shape-driven magnitude of Delta, and the n* crossover. Confirm that the identified minimal statistic recovers the optimal constant while remaining computationally simple.

This directly closes the explanation gap: instead of merely asserting that sample-mean policies are suboptimal except for exponential families, it explains why (mean-constrained vs. full I-projection), predicts how much loss occurs from measurable distributional features, explains their finite-time success, and pinpoints the least additional information needed to restore optimality.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works form a fragmented theory of bandit index policies, each proving asymptotic optimality yet leaving unexplained WHY these provably equivalent policies behave differently before the asymptotic regime. Lai and Robbins (1985) give the O(log n) regret lower bound with a leading constant set by a Kullback-Leibler (KL) number and matching UCB policies for exponential families. Agrawal (1995) builds simpler sample-mean UCB indices achieving O(log n) regret, but notes his constant is optimal ONLY for one-parameter exponential families and otherwise derived via a contraction principle, generically loose by an unknown amount. Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) define the truly optimal nonparametric constant M(theta) via dual problems, and Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) give nonparametric strategies with different rates. In parallel, Gittins-index theory (the 1989 book; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994) gives Bayesian-optimal indices for the discounted criterion, and Q-learning and RL (1995; Sutton and Barto 1998) offer another mechanism. Three phenomena go unexplained. Constant gap: nobody has quantified how much regret Agrawal's contraction costs versus the Burnetas-Katehakis optimal constant, nor which measurable features (distance from an exponential family, tail heaviness, variance) drive that loss. Rate-behavior mismatch: all guarantees are asymptotic with hidden burn-in terms, so the finite-horizon ordering of policies and the crossover horizon at which asymptotic KL ordering emerges is opaque. Criterion mismatch: it is unclear when and why a Bayesian-optimal Gittins index is beaten by frequentist UCB indices under undiscounted regret. The literature supplies rates and constants but no mechanistic account connecting them to observed finite-time regret, which matters because practitioners must choose among these policies at finite horizons where the theory is silent.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map, a bandit regret atlas, that measures and causally attributes the finite-time behavior of the index-policy families and turns their theoretical constants into a diagnostic account. Step one, a unified policy library: reimplement under one interface Lai-Robbins exponential-family UCB (KL-optimal constant), Agrawal sample-mean UCB (contraction constant), Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index policy (optimal nonparametric M(theta)), Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric strategy, the Gittins discounted index, and Q-learning for bandits; each exposes its per-arm index and confidence width at every step so internal behavior is observable. Step two, a designed instance grid: vary the levers the theory implicates, including number of arms, gap structure (single dominant versus near-tied arms), reward law (Bernoulli, Gaussian across variances, finite-support discrete, heavy-tailed and bounded non-exponential-family laws) parameterized by an explicit distance from a one-parameter exponential family, and horizon length from pre-asymptotic to deep-asymptotic; for each cell compute the theoretical leading constant each policy targets and the KL-based optimal floor. Step three, regret-decomposition diagnostics: split cumulative regret into unavoidable exploration predicted by the KL floor, excess exploration from over-wide confidence indices (the constant gap), burn-in cost, and under-exploration or mis-identification cost, tracking per-arm suboptimal pull counts against the floor over time. Step four, causal attribution: treat the measured constant gap and crossover horizon as responses and regress them on instance features using sensitivity analysis and controlled interventions (hold gap fixed while sweeping exponential-family distance) to test hypotheses, centrally that Agrawal's contraction loss grows monotonically with distance from an exponential family and vanishes exactly for exponential-family laws, and that Gittins undiscounted-regret suboptimality is governed by the mismatch between its effective discount and the horizon. Step five, deliverables: phase diagrams over instance space showing which policy dominates where, empirical calibration of realized versus theoretical leading constants quantifying the contraction penalty as a function of measurable features, and a map of crossover horizons explaining when asymptotic KL ordering takes over. This mapping fills the explanation gap by converting the corpus's asymptotic constants into a measured, mechanistic account of why asymptotically-equivalent bandit policies diverge in finite time and which features cause it; it is feasible via simulation and grounded entirely in the provided works.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 5 turns / 12 tool calls

**Motivation.** The prior work forms a coherent lineage on the stochastic multi-armed bandit, and it converges on one striking empirical/theoretical regularity: good index policies incur regret that grows like log n. Lai & Robbins (1985) prove that log n is the unavoidable rate and identify a Kullback–Leibler constant as the governing quantity, but only as a liminf over n→∞. Agrawal (1995) shows sample-mean-only index policies attain O(log n) "asymptotically," with a constant obtained from the KL-optimal one by a "contraction" principle. Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) again give an n m*(θ) − M(θ)log n + o(log n) expansion valid only as n→∞, and Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) prove asymptotic optimality with "guaranteed convergence rates" but no explicit horizon-dependent statement.

This leaves a genuine EXPLANATION GAP. The literature tells us the limiting rate and the limiting constant, but does not explain *why* the log n law emerges, *what mechanism* produces it, or *how* regret actually behaves at the finite horizons at which bandits are run. Concretely, four things are unexplained: (i) the asymptotic constants (KL numbers, M(θ)) are only meaningful in the limit and can be arbitrarily loose or even vacuous for small/moderate n, so they do not account for early behavior; (ii) it is unclear how much of the incurred regret is the irreducible "learning cost" versus transient over-exploration, and what causal quantity trades the two off; (iii) it is not explained why replacing full distributional knowledge with the sample mean plus a confidence correction is *sufficient*, i.e., what property of the estimator carries the argument; (iv) the required amount of exploration as an explicit function of the horizon n and the per-arm play count is never isolated. Because these are asymptotic-only accounts, they cannot answer the mechanistic question "why does this work, and work at time n?"—which is exactly what a practitioner or theorist needs to trust and tune such policies.

**Method.** Contribution type: a formal, non-asymptotic explanatory derivation that replaces the asymptotic "why" with a finite-time causal account.

1. Conceptual decomposition (taxonomy). Formalize regret as R_n = Σ_{j:Δ_j>0} Δ_j · E[T_j(n)], where Δ_j = μ* − μ_j is the suboptimality gap and T_j(n) the play count. This isolates the single object that must be explained: E[T_j(n)], the expected number of pulls of each suboptimal arm. Introduce a conceptual distinction between (a) the *confidence-failure* events (the estimator being far from its mean) and (b) the *decision* events (a suboptimal index exceeding the optimal one), and argue every suboptimal pull is attributable to one of these.

2. Objective / policy class. Define a distribution-free index of the form I_j = X̄_j + r(n, T_j), i.e., sample mean plus an explicit exploration radius, generalizing the sample-mean index policies of Agrawal and the bounded-arm setting of Yakowitz–Lowe. Treat r(·) as an unknown function to be *derived*, not assumed.

3. Formal derivation (the mechanism). Using only a concentration/large-deviation inequality for bounded rewards (Chernoff–Hoeffding, in the spirit of the "few key lemmas from large deviations" Agrawal invokes), derive the smallest radius r(n,s) = c·sqrt(ln n / s) that makes two things simultaneously true: the confidence-failure probability, summed over the horizon via a union bound, is O(1) (finite, horizon-independent); and once a suboptimal arm j has been pulled more than ~ (ln n)/Δ_j² times, its index provably drops below the optimal arm's index with high probability. Prove the central theorem: for all n (not merely n→∞),
   E[T_j(n)] ≤ (8 ln n)/Δ_j² + (1 + π²/3),
yielding R_n ≤ Σ_{j:Δ_j>0} (8 ln n)/Δ_j² + O(Σ Δ_j). The proof mechanistically explains the log n law: the ln n numerator is exactly the confidence width needed to survive a union bound over n rounds, and the 1/Δ_j² is the sample size at which concentration separates arm j from the optimum—so log n regret is the *price of maintaining valid confidence over a horizon of length n*, not an asymptotic accident.

4. Reconciliation with the KL account. Show how this distribution-free constant relates to the Lai–Robbins/Burnetas–Katehakis KL constant: prove that for bounded rewards Pinsker-type inequalities lower-bound the KL number by Δ²/2, explaining Agrawal's "contraction" gap as the difference between an estimator-agnostic variance proxy and the distribution-specific KL rate, and delimiting exactly when the two coincide. Provide a gap-regime taxonomy (large-gap "identification" regime vs. small-gap Δ ≈ sqrt(1/n) "pooling" regime) and derive a complementary distribution-free bound R_n = O(sqrt(n · K · ln n)) uniform over gaps, explaining why the instance-dependent log n constant can appear misleading when gaps shrink with n.

5. Validation. The theorem is self-contained and checkable; additionally, simulate the derived policy against the asymptotically-optimal KL-index policies of the cited works across horizons, empirically confirming that the finite-time bound tracks realized regret where the asymptotic constants do not, thereby demonstrating that the derivation supplies the missing causal/mechanistic explanation of the log n phenomenon at finite time.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit theory shows O(log n) regret with a leading constant set by Kullback-Leibler information (Lai and Robbins 1985), yet different index rules realize different constants on the same instance. Agrawal (1995) proves sample-mean-only indices are optimal only for one-parameter exponential families and otherwise attain a strictly suboptimal contracted constant, while Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) recover the true nonparametric constant via dual KL-minimization over the full empirical distribution, and Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) give still other constants. What is missing is an explanatory account of why and by how much discarding information beyond the sample mean costs efficiency, and which distributional features drive this contraction gap. Practitioners default to mean-based UCB for simplicity with no principled way to know when it silently sacrifices constant-factor reward. Prior works fix the endpoints but never characterize the mechanism, magnitude, or determinants of the gap.

**Method.** Cast index design as optimization and search over a parametrized family of statistic-to-index maps, used both to explain and to close the contraction gap. Define a family indexed by phi that selects which empirical functionals feed the index, from sample mean alone, to mean plus variance, to quantiles, up to a full empirical-KL projection onto a screened set of reference measures, plus an inflation schedule; Agrawal mean-only UCB and Burnetas-Katehakis dual-KL indices are the endpoints. Offline explanatory search over a designed grid of distribution families labeled by interpretable features solves the Burnetas-Katehakis dual for the optimal constant and evaluates Agrawal contraction for the mean-only constant, computes their ratio, and uses variance-based sensitivity screening to attribute the gap to specific features, yielding a decomposition explaining why the loss varies. An online bandit-over-indices meta-search spends a vanishing exploration budget to estimate each arm's features, predicts its gap from the offline map, and adaptively picks the cheapest phi within epsilon of optimal, reverting to mean-UCB when the gap is negligible and escalating to dual-KL indices only where warranted, with the estimated dual-KL value as screening surrogate and an exploration schedule keeping meta-selection cost o(log n) so asymptotic optimality is preserved. Validate that the meta-policy tracks Burnetas-Katehakis regret where the gap is large and mean-UCB efficiency where it is small, and that observed per-instance loss is predicted by the feature decomposition.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit theory in these sources is complete only asymptotically. Lai and Robbins (1985) prove a log n lower bound with a Kullback-Leibler constant and matching index policies; Agrawal (1995) gives simpler sample-mean index policies reaching O(log n); Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) extend optimality to broader families; the nonparametric-methods paper (1991) confirms the log n barrier nonparametrically. Every result is of the form: for fixed theta, as n goes to infinity, characterizing the limiting slope of regret over log n and its constant, not the finite-horizon behavior that governs real applications like clinical trials with a finite patient stream.
