This is an explanation gap: the literature states the asymptotic rate but not why the exploration term forces logarithmic regret at every finite n, how regret decomposes across arms, gaps and horizon at practical sample sizes, or why the simple sample-mean policies lose the optimal constant. It matters because an asymptotically optimal constant can be misleading early, and the optimal policies need strong structure: a known parametric family and the whole observation history or large-deviation index computations. No existing account explains why regret is controlled under weak, realistic assumptions uniformly over the horizon.

**Method.** Build a finite-time, distribution-free explanatory theory of index-policy regret that makes the prior asymptotic results hold under weaker assumptions and uniformly over all n. Relaxations: replace known parametric family with only bounded or sub-Gaussian rewards; replace limits at infinity with bounds valid at every finite n; replace history and KL-based indices with a policy storing only each arm running sample mean and play count. Analyze the policy playing the arm that maximizes its sample mean plus a confidence radius equal to the square root of alpha times ln n over that arm play count, the radius derived from Hoeffding or Chernoff concentration rather than the true KL number. The core is an explanatory decomposition of expected pulls of a suboptimal arm into a deterministic exploration threshold of order alpha ln n over the squared gap, after which the arm inflated mean provably falls below the optimal arm index, plus a summed tail-probability term for concentration failures, each bounded by explicit computable constants, yielding regret at most a sum over suboptimal arms of a constant times ln n over the gap plus a constant. This answers the why by exhibiting the concentration-driven separation threshold producing log n growth at every n, showing how arm count and gaps enter, and comparing the Hoeffding radius to the KL rate to pinpoint where the sample-mean policy constant exceeds the Lai-Robbins optimum. Extend with a variance-adaptive Bernstein radius explaining when variance rather than range drives regret and a horizon-agnostic variant bounded uniformly in n, and validate by simulation across Bernoulli, bounded, and truncated heavy-tailed rewards, checking predicted versus observed per-arm pulls and regret curves and recovering the classical asymptotic constant as the limit.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classical stochastic bandit theory yields index/UCB policies with O(log n) regret whose constant is governed by a Kullback-Leibler / large-deviations rate (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996). But each construction's optimality is silently conditioned on a correctly specified reward model: Agrawal's sample-mean index attains the optimal constant only for one-parameter exponential families and otherwise uses a suboptimal contraction of the KL number; Burnetas and Katehakis require the functional form to be known and leave normal-with-unknown-variance open; Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) get distribution-free rates only under bounded or finite-moment assumptions and are rate-optimal only when bounded; Berry and Fristedt (1985) treat robustness only via Bayesian priors. This leaves an explanation gap: we have asymptotic guarantees but no mechanistic account of why and how much these asymptotically optimal policies degrade and vary in finite horizons under misspecification (heavy tails, wrong family, unknown variance, near-tied arms). Is failure driven by confidence inflation, by contraction bias, or by estimation variance? Because the regret constant is itself a functional of the assumed law, practitioners cannot tell when an index policy is trustworthy, which matters for the clinical-trial and adaptive-control applications these works target.

**Method.** A unified theory-plus-policy that explains and robustifies index policies against distributional misspecification. First, an explanatory decomposition: using Agrawal's large-deviations lemmas, decompose finite-horizon regret of any KL-index policy into three attributable terms, namely estimation variance, confidence-inflation (the a_n schedule), and a specification-bias term from the contraction that maps the true law onto the assumed family's rate function. Derive how the bias term scales with divergence between the true law and the assumed family, giving a causal account of when regret blows up (predicting near-tied arms and heavy tails amplify bias while inflation dominates early), and derive an online per-arm diagnostic that estimates the bias term to flag untrustworthy indices. Second, robustification: replace the family-dependent index with a distributionally robust index, defined as the worst-case plausible mean over an ambiguity set of laws consistent with the sample (an empirical-measure KL ball intersected with Yakowitz-Lowe moment and support constraints), solved via the Burnetas-Katehakis dual machinery but without committing to a single family; using the empirical measure targets the optimal constant Agrawal notes is recoverable modulo continuity and directly covers the normal-unknown-variance case left open. Third, guarantees: prove that when the truth lies in the shrinking ambiguity set the robust index retains O(log n) regret and drives the specification-bias term to zero, and derive an explicit price-of-robustness bound in which excess regret increases with the ambiguity radius, turning unexplained finite-time variability into a tunable quantity. Fourth, calibration and validation: give a data-driven rule for the ambiguity radius that balances the estimation and bias terms, then validate on simulations across exponential-family, heavy-tailed, bounded, and unknown-variance normal arms, checking the diagnostic predicts where classical indices fail and the robust index removes those failures at bounded cost.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Explanation gap: The sources offer three distinct constructions that all yield efficient index policies for sequential allocation, but no account explains why they coincide or why their efficiency varies. Bayesian: Gittins' allocation index is optimal under discounting (Berry and Fristedt 1985; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994, extending to average reward). Frequentist: Lai and Robbins (1985) tie regret to the Kullback-Leibler number, attained by KL upper-confidence indices (Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991). RL: Q-learning and optimistic value estimation solve the same problem by stochastic approximation (Sutton and Barto 1998; Q-learning for bandits 1995; Pollard 1984 tools), but explore heuristically. Each uses a different criterion and proof style, leaving open why a Bayesian retirement-value index, a frequentist KL width, and an RL optimistic estimate produce the same allocation; why Agrawal's sample-mean index attains the optimal Lai-Robbins constant for exponential families but a worse one via a KL contraction otherwise; and why RL exploration is efficient in some regimes and wasteful in others. With RL now deployed beyond settings where the tight KL theory holds, this missing mechanistic account matters.

**Method.** Build a unifying information-adjusted value framework writing every index as estimated mean plus exploration bonus, and show each bonus is, to leading order, a functional of the same KL rate at which an arm could plausibly be optimal. Adopt the Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis KL rate and Agrawal's rate function as reference, formalizing an arm's bonus after t plays as the mean-space inflation needed for its empirical law to be consistent at rate exp(-tI) with being best. Reduce the Bayesian index by taking the discounted Gittins index in retirement form, computing its average-reward limit via Ishikida-Varaiya's interchange-free time-change argument, and extracting its implicit bonus. Reduce the frequentist index by rewriting Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, and Burnetas-Katehakis indices as KL-inflation bonuses, identifying Agrawal's contraction as projection of the full KL rate onto the sample mean. Reduce the RL index by modeling Q-learning as stochastic approximation to an inflated fixed point and expressing its effective bonus via step-size and exploration schedule using Pollard and Watkins-type convergence. Prove a unification theorem that all bonuses share the form c times the information rate per play, with the regret constant monotone in c and in how faithfully the algorithm's statistic preserves the KL rate, with corollaries for when the Gittins average-reward limit hits the Lai-Robbins constant, the information-loss penalty of sample-mean indices, and which RL schedules attain versus miss optimality. Validate on the sources' testbeds by empirically measuring each algorithm's applied bonus against the predicted KL rate.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 31 turns / 38 tool calls

**Motivation.** 
Bandit index policies (Lai-Robbins 1985, Agrawal 1995, Burnetas-Katehakis 1996, Yakowitz-Lowe 1991) assume a correctly specified reward family, cumulant conditions, or bounded moments, and give only asymptotic guarantees. Under heavy tails, contamination, misspecification, unknown variance, or nonstationarity their confidence terms miscalibrate and regret can become unbounded, with no runtime detection of assumption failure and no reproducible brittleness measure, a reliability and safety risk for uses like allocating competing clinical treatments.


**Method.** 
Build BanditGuard, a deployment runtime plus reproducible brittleness benchmark that makes bandit index policies auditable, self-monitoring, and robust; the software artifact is the central contribution. It wraps any index policy (Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, Burnetas-Katehakis, Gittins, UCB); per-arm streaming monitors (tail-index and empirical cumulant estimators from Agrawal large deviations and Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametrics, a contamination detector, a change-point detector) emit calibration scores comparing the assumed confidence width to a data-driven empirical-Bernstein width; a controller keeps the native index when valid, else substitutes robust nonparametric bounds, windowed statistics under drift, and a conservative safe-exploration fallback capping per-round risk, with a governor logging certifiability; a benchmark injects controlled violations at tunable severities and reports regret distribution, calibration error, catastrophic-failure rate, and detection latency across seeds.


#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classic bandit index policies give only asymptotic logarithmic regret under strong tail assumptions, so they can silently lock onto a suboptimal arm at finite horizons and miscalibrate under misspecification, with no reliability certificate.

**Method.** Build a robust index policy using a truncated or median of means estimator with an empirical variance width, prove a non asymptotic high probability regret certificate via a time uniform martingale bound, and add an online self test that widens exploration when deviations exceed the assumed schedule to cap catastrophic lock in.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** The classic bandit index policies in these works give only asymptotic mean-regret guarantees under strong assumptions. Lai and Robbins require exponential families and a KL constant that blows up as arm gaps shrink. Agrawal is optimal only for exponential families and otherwise loses via a contraction. Burnetas and Katehakis need finite-support models and leave normal unknown variance open. Yakowitz and Lowe need finite moments or bounded rewards. Gittins needs known priors and discounting. In clinical trials, adaptive control, and online learning these assumptions are routinely violated. This is a failure and risk gap: no study characterizes how brittle these policies become under misspecification, how often they lock onto a bad arm, how heavy-tailed regret becomes, how miscalibrated their confidence bounds are, or how far finite-time behavior departs from the asymptotic promise. No empirical map tells a practitioner which policy is reliable in which regime.

**Method.** Build a reproducible stress-testing benchmark that empirically maps finite-time reliability and failure of these bandit policies. Reimplement each index rule plus epsilon-greedy and Q-learning baselines. Vary stress axes drawn from their assumptions: wrong reward family, heavier tails, shrinking gaps toward near ties, more arms, shorter horizons, and nonstationary drift. Measure beyond mean regret: the full regret distribution and upper quantiles, the chance of catastrophic lock-in, seed-to-seed variance for reproducibility, calibration of each confidence bound, and the ratio of observed regret to the policy own asymptotic prediction. Deliver heatmaps of safe versus failure regions, a brittleness profile per policy, a taxonomy of failure modes tied to each assumption, and guidance for choosing policies under model uncertainty.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** The entire corpus optimizes one criterion: the expectation of cumulative regret. Lai and Robbins (1985) give the O(log n) lower bound on E[R_n] via the KL number; Agrawal (1995) builds sample-mean index policies attaining O(log n) expected regret with large-deviation lemmas; Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) characterize uniformly-fast policies optimal in expected reward; the Gittins-index results concern expected discounted reward; Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) give asymptotically optimal mean suboptimal-selection rates. A search confirms none analyze the variance or tail of regret. This is a Failure/Risk gap. The motivating applications, sequential clinical trials and treatment allocation, are safety-critical, where a single realization determines harm, not a long-run average. Efficient index policies attain low mean regret precisely by occasionally over-committing to an inferior arm when rare large-deviation events inflate its confidence index, making realized regret heavy-tailed. Two policies indistinguishable in expected regret can differ enormously in worst-case behavior and reproducibility. Expected-regret optimality thus gives no reliability or safety guarantee, and the field lacks any formal object describing when and how badly an asymptotically efficient policy fails on a given trajectory.

**Method.** Develop a formal theory of distributional (risk) regret that upgrades the corpus mean-only guarantees to guarantees about the whole regret distribution. First, a taxonomy: treat regret R_n and per-arm suboptimal-pull counts N_i(n) as random variables and define graded reliability criteria, namely expected-regret efficiency (classical), tail reliability (control of the probability that R_n exceeds (1+delta)E[R_n], plus a CVaR functional), and robustness (control of Var(R_n)); formally distinguish mean-optimal from risk-optimal policies. Second, a risk lower bound impossibility theorem: extend the Lai-Robbins change-of-measure/KL argument from E[N_i(n)] to the law of N_i(n), proving any uniformly-fast consistent policy must have Var(N_i(n)) growing at least like log n and an unavoidable upper tail on N_i(n) beyond (1+delta) log n divided by the KL number I(theta_i, theta*), yielding a fundamental mean-tail trade-off so no policy is simultaneously expected-regret optimal and light-tailed. Third, a matching family and Pareto frontier: introduce a one-parameter family of sample-mean index policies generalizing Agrawal with an exploration-inflation function, and using Agrawal large-deviation lemmas derive simultaneous upper bounds on E[R_n] and on the probability that R_n exceeds x, giving an explicit achievable frontier between the expected-regret constant and a tail-risk exponent, with a risk-efficient constant that is a risk analogue of the KL constant expressed via the Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index formulation. Fourth, an operational objective: define J equal to E[R_n] plus lambda times a CVaR/tail penalty, derive the optimizing index in variational form, and prove the resulting policies lie on the frontier. Fifth, scope: verify regularity assumptions for one-parameter exponential families and bounded or finite-support nonparametric distributions, matching the settings of Agrawal, Burnetas-Katehakis, and Yakowitz-Lowe, so results plug into existing guarantees. The deliverable is lower-bound, matching-upper-bound, and Pareto-characterization theorems that convert expected-regret efficiency into certified reliability with a tunable safety-efficiency dial.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 26 turns / 35 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classic asymptotically-efficient bandit policies such as Lai-Robbins UCB, Agrawal sample-mean indices, Burnetas-Katehakis dual indices, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric rules, and Gittins indices all minimize expected regret. Two risk gaps remain. First is brittleness: their logarithmic-regret bounds require known exponential families, finite exponential moments, or bounded arms, so under misspecification or heavy tails the KL-based constants and confidence bounds are miscalibrated and regret can blow up, and being asymptotic they permit catastrophic single runs, a reliability and reproducibility problem for the motivating clinical-trials setting. Second is risk-neutrality: mean-based indices ignore the variance and tail of realized regret and even favor the higher-variance arm, and no source controls regret variance, regret tails, or per-run harm. Safety-critical allocation therefore needs policies simultaneously robust to misspecification and reliable and safe per run, which these works do not provide.

**Method.** Design a distributionally-robust, risk-sensitive index policy whose per-arm index solves an adaptive inner optimization over an ambiguity set, replacing the fixed upper-confidence-bound and generalizing the Burnetas-Katehakis and Agrawal index-by-duality view from the mean to a risk functional. For each arm, form its empirical distribution and an ambiguity ball of distributions within divergence rho of it, using KL or Wasserstein for heavy tails, and tune rho as a shrinking budget of order log-time over pull count via concentration so the ball just covers the truth, recovering logarithmic exploration without family assumptions. Set the index to the supremum of a risk functional such as conditional value at risk or mean minus a tail-variance penalty over the ball, giving optimism-driven exploration plus tail control, solved by convex duality for closed-form indices. Add a safety screen computing the pessimistic infimum of the risk functional over the ball and excluding or capping arms below a safety threshold. Allocate extra exploration to arms whose ambiguity width dominates the index gap, and anneal risk level and radius to trade regret growth against regret-tail control. Play the maximum-index safety-feasible arm. Prove logarithmic expected regret against the risk-adjusted best arm plus bounds on regret variance, regret tail, and safety violations uniform over the ambiguity set, and evaluate against Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, and Gittins baselines on heavy-tailed, misspecified, and clinical-trial simulations reporting mean regret, regret-tail quantiles, and worst-case per-run harm.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Failure/Risk gap. Every allocation rule in this corpus builds an arm's index from the sample mean of clean i.i.d. rewards, inflated by a KL or large-deviations radius: Lai and Robbins (1985) for exponential families, Agrawal (1995) reducing these to purely sample-mean indices, optimal adaptive sequential-allocation policies, and Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) relaxing to finite-moment arms but still centering on the empirical mean via Chebyshev and Fuk-Nagaev bounds. This is brittle and unsafe. The sample mean has zero breakdown point: one corrupted or heavy-tailed reward can arbitrarily inflate an arm's index, so a small fraction of faulty or adversarial feedback (transcription errors in the clinical trials these papers invoke, click fraud, malicious ratings, sensor faults) can drive the policy onto an inferior arm and silently destroy the O(log n) guarantee. Sharp constants also require knowing the reward family, so misspecification quietly voids guarantees. Yakowitz-Lowe still relies on the empirical mean and gives only loose asymptotic rates under heavy tails, and none of these works consider adversarial corruption; the only robustness in the corpus (Berry and Fristedt) is Bayesian robustness to prior misspecification. Reliability, security, and reproducibility of these classic index policies under noisy, corrupted, or misspecified rewards is thus an open failure gap in the safety-critical settings they motivate.

**Method.** Relax and extend scope: robust upper-confidence-bound index policies that keep the Lai-Robbins and Agrawal guarantees under weaker assumptions. First, weaken assumptions from bounded exponential-family rewards to only a finite (1+epsilon) moment per arm plus up to an eta-fraction of arbitrarily corrupted samples per arm. Second, replace the sample mean in Agrawal's index with a robust location estimator from that arm's own history (median-of-means or an alpha-trimmed mean), preserving the cheap per-arm index structure. Third, replace the KL and large-deviations inflation with a confidence radius derived for the robust estimator, extending Agrawal's large-deviations lemmas and Yakowitz-Lowe's Fuk-Nagaev and Bernstein machinery to the tail of median-of-means and trimmed means under contamination, and prove the inflated robust index remains a valid upper confidence bound so it plugs into Agrawal's general upper-bound theorem and the Lai-Robbins lower bound. Fourth, prove finite-time regret bounds: a robust O(log n) exploration term plus an additive bias term growing smoothly with contamination eta and tail-heaviness, recovering the classical O(log n) when eta is zero and rewards are light-tailed. Fifth, give a data-driven calibration of block count, trimming fraction, and inflation schedule so guarantees hold without knowing the reward family, addressing misspecification and reproducibility. Sixth, validate on clinical-trial-style allocation and machine or strategy selection with injected outliers, heavy-tailed rewards, and adversarial reward-poisoning, comparing regret and worst-case suboptimal-pull counts against the sample-mean UCB indices of Agrawal and Yakowitz-Lowe.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 22 turns / 33 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit index policies rely on empirical means, which are brittle to outliers, adversarial reward corruption, and heavy tails; Yakowitz-Lowe show logarithmic regret collapses to polynomial under only finite moments, and none of the works defend against contamination or misspecified tails.

**Method.** Swap the empirical mean in UCB index policies for a robust estimator such as median-of-means or a trimmed Catoni M-estimator, build a matching robust confidence radius scaled with log n, and prove logarithmic regret under heavy tails plus graceful linear-in-corruption degradation, with Lepski-type adaptation when the corruption level and moment order are unknown; validate against Agrawal UCB and Yakowitz-Lowe under Pareto rewards and reward poisoning.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 26 tool calls

**Motivation.** Foundational bandit works give only asymptotic or assumption-dependent guarantees that are unreliable at finite horizons. Lai-Robbins 1985, Agrawal 1995, and Burnetas-Katehakis 1996 achieve O(log n) regret only asymptotically with constants set by unknown KL numbers and assume a known parametric family, so performance cannot be certified at a fixed n and misspecification can break it. Gittins indices (Gittins 1989; Berry-Fristedt 1985; Varaiya 1994) need discounting and a correct prior and are brittle to prior misspecification. Yakowitz-Lowe 1991 gives only asymptotic nonparametric rates, and the Q-learning and RL strand (Sutton-Barto; Holland) only asymptotic convergence. None offers distribution-free finite-time high-probability regret control, so users cannot know how much to trust a policy at fixed n, on unknown distributions, or under misspecification.

**Method.** Build a unified confidence-bound bandit index that gives finite-time reliability certificates and bridges frequentist, Bayesian Gittins, nonparametric, and RL strands. The index is empirical mean plus a concentration padding (Hoeffding or empirical-Bernstein) instead of a KL term; this padding is both a distribution-free surrogate for the Lai-Robbins large-deviations inflation and a frequentist version of the Gittins exploration premium, matched up to constants by a confidence radius. Using Pollard 1984 empirical-process tools, prove a non-asymptotic KL-free O(log n) expected-regret bound for all finite n over a nonparametric class, a high-probability any-time bound on suboptimal pulls via time-uniform peeling, and asymptotic recovery of the Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis rate. Show parametric and Gittins indices go linear under misspecification while this index stays logarithmic. Validate on Yakowitz-Lowe Bernoulli testbeds and RL bandit environments, reporting certified bounds and full regret distributions against Gittins, Lai-Robbins, nonparametric, and Q-learning baselines.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** A puzzle sits at the heart of this literature on asymptotically efficient bandit index policies. Lai & Robbins ("Asymptotically efficient adaptive allocation rules") prove an O(log n) regret lower bound whose leading constant is the Kullback–Leibler number M(θ), and construct upper-confidence-bound (UCB) index policies that hit it — but only when the parametric family (essentially one-parameter exponential families) is known a priori. Three later works then send flatly contradictory signals about whether that optimal constant survives when the family is unknown:

(1) Agrawal ("Sample mean based index policies with O(log n) regret") builds a fully general index that uses only the sample mean, but proves it must pay a strict "contraction principle" penalty: its constant is a contraction of the KL number and is provably suboptimal except for one-parameter exponential families. Message: generality COSTS optimality.

(2) Burnetas & Katehakis ("Optimal adaptive policies for sequential allocation problems") define a distribution-agnostic "dual"/plug-in index (a KL-constrained optimization over the empirical reward measure) and prove it attains the exact optimal constant with NO parametric assumptions — for nonparametric discrete distributions with finite support. Message: generality is FREE.

(3) Yet Burnetas & Katehakis can only verify their sufficient conditions for finite-support discrete arms and explicitly leave the normal-unknown-variance case (the canonical continuous model) as an open problem; Yakowitz & Lowe's nonparametric methods likewise attain the optimal rate only for bounded arms. So the very case practitioners care about — continuous, unbounded, possibly heavy-tailed rewards — is exactly where the "generality is free" claim silently breaks.

These conflicting claims have never been reconciled empirically, because every one of these contributions is a pencil-and-paper asymptotic theorem with no shared implementation or benchmark; and the parallel model-free route (Q-learning for bandits, Sutton & Barto's RL framing, Gittins indices) optimizes a discounted criterion disconnected from the regret constant, so it cannot arbitrate the dispute. The gap that matters: is distribution-free attainment of the optimal KL constant a real phenomenon or a mirage that collapses on continuous/heavy-tailed rewards, and is the plug-in empirical index's failure on unbounded supports fundamental or a fixable coverage problem? No deployable artifact exists to answer this.

**Method.** Build OmniIndex, a self-calibrating, distribution-free bandit index engine plus a unified benchmark harness, as the central artifact. The system turns the scattered family-specific index constructions into one deployable pipeline and adds a data-driven safeguard aimed squarely at the continuous/unbounded case that breaks the theory.

Core engine (per arm, each round):
1. Maintain the empirical reward measure p̂_a from the samples observed so far.
2. Compute an "empirical rate-function UCB" by solving the Burnetas–Katehakis dual: index U_a = max over distributions q of mean(q) subject to KL(p̂_a ‖ q) ≤ (log t)/T_a(a) (t = time, T_a = pulls of arm a). This is a finite-dimensional convex program over the empirical support — directly implementable, no parametric family assumed.
3. Tail/support safeguard (the novel component addressing the open normal-unknown-variance failure): because on unbounded supports the empirical measure never "sees" the tail, the plug-in KL confidence set fails to cover the true distribution and the index loses its upper-confidence property. OmniIndex augments p̂_a with an adaptive "ghost mass" at an inflated support endpoint whose location/weight is set by an online-estimated tail/scale statistic, so the confidence set provably contains distributions with the estimated variability. The inflation is auto-tuned by a coverage monitor.
4. Coverage monitor / self-calibration: hold out a fraction of each arm's samples and empirically test how often the constructed index actually upper-bounds the held-out mean; drive the ghost-mass inflation and confidence radius with a controller to hit a target coverage (e.g., 1−1/t), giving a data-driven, family-free calibration in place of the parametric assumptions that the theory needed.
5. Robust fallback selection: run, in shadow mode, a bounded-arm sample-mean contraction index (Agrawal) and a parametric KL index (Lai–Robbins-style) when a family is confidently identified; an online model-selection layer picks among engine variants by a cross-validated regret proxy.

Theory-grounded justification hooks: use empirical-process/uniform-convergence tools (Pollard, "Convergence of stochastic processes") to argue the empirical rate function converges uniformly and to size the ghost mass; use likelihood-ratio/empirical-likelihood results (from mathematical statistics) to relate the KL-constrained program to valid confidence sets.

Benchmark harness (the second half of the artifact): a reproducible testbed instantiating each theoretical policy under one interface — parametric KL-UCB, sample-mean contraction UCB, empirical dual index, OmniIndex with safeguard, and a Q-learning/Gittins model-free baseline — evaluated across (a) finite-support discrete arms (where theory says the dual index is optimal), (b) exponential-family arms, (c) normal arms with unknown variance, and (d) heavy-tailed arms. We measure realized regret and, crucially, the empirical leading constant regret/log(n) versus the Lai–Robbins M(θ).

How this resolves the puzzle: the harness directly tests whether "generality is free" (Burnetas–Katehakis) or "generality costs optimality" (Agrawal) as reward distributions move from finite-support to continuous/heavy-tailed, isolating exactly where the plug-in index's optimal-constant claim degrades. OmniIndex's coverage-calibrated safeguard is the concrete mechanism for recovering upper-confidence validity — and thus near-optimal-constant regret — in the normal-unknown-variance and heavy-tailed regimes that the source theory leaves open, converting an unproven asymptotic dispute into a measured, deployable result.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** A paradox runs through this body of work on the infinite-horizon frequentist multi-armed bandit. Lai & Robbins (1985) proved that the *optimal* O(log n) regret constant is an information-theoretic quantity — the Kullback–Leibler number of the full reward distribution — suggesting that to be optimal a policy must, in effect, learn the whole distribution of each arm. Two later camps take opposite stances on this, and both fail in the regime practitioners care about most (unbounded rewards, unknown/heavy-tailed spread).

(1) The "simplicity" camp uses only the sample mean. Agrawal (1995) shows sample-mean UCB indices attain O(log n) but with a *contracted* KL constant that is provably optimal only for one-parameter exponential families and strictly suboptimal otherwise; Yakowitz & Lowe's (1991) sample-mean rule is rate-optimal only for bounded arms, and merely "rate-guaranteed" under finite moments. So robustness costs constant-factor optimality.

(2) The "full-distribution" camp achieves the optimal constant. Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) build indices from a dual KL-projection over the full model and reach the optimal constant — but only for finite-support discrete or known-variance families, and they explicitly leave the *Gaussian-with-unknown-variance* case as an open problem (one assumption unverified). Their method needs the model class and a heavier optimization.

Here is the contradiction: theory says optimality requires the whole distribution, yet the whole-distribution method is exactly the one that cannot handle unbounded, unknown-variance rewards, while the robust sample-mean method that *can* run there is provably suboptimal. Meanwhile the reinforcement-learning view (Sutton 1992; "Q-learning for bandit problems", 1995) treats a bandit as a degenerate MDP and explores with sample-mean value estimates plus epsilon-greedy/optimism — inheriting the suboptimal-constant problem with no principled fix. So neither "use only the mean" nor "use the whole distribution" is right, and the gap is widest precisely where it matters.

**Method.** Propose a *moment-budgeted empirical-KL index policy* that interpolates continuously between the sample-mean and full-distribution extremes, with the budget chosen adaptively from data.

Core index. For arm a with T_a observations, replace Burnetas–Katehakis's full-distribution KL projection with a *moment-constrained* empirical-likelihood projection. Define the index as the largest candidate mean μ such that there exists a distribution that (i) matches the arm's empirical estimates of the first r moments to within data-driven tolerances and (ii) lies within an empirical KL/likelihood ball of the observed sample. By convex duality this reduces to a low-dimensional optimization over only r+1 Lagrange multipliers (one per retained moment), so computation scales with the moment budget r, not with support size or a parametric family. At r=1 this provably collapses to Agrawal's sample-mean/contraction index (recovering its robustness and O(log n) guarantee under a single moment condition, à la Yakowitz–Lowe); as r grows toward the full empirical distribution it recovers the Burnetas–Katehakis optimal KL constant. The optimal Lai–Robbins constant is thus attained whenever the retained moments span the directions that determine the true KL divergence.

Adaptive moment budget. Run r online: start at r=1 and increment r only when a concentration test certifies that the r-th empirical moment is estimated reliably enough that adding its constraint tightens the confidence bound by more than its estimation error. This yields graceful degradation — the policy never pays for moments the data cannot support — and turns "how much of the distribution do you need?" into a self-tuned, per-arm decision rather than an a priori modeling choice.

Resolving the open case. For the Gaussian-unknown-variance / general unbounded case that Burnetas–Katehakis leave open and that sample-mean methods handle only suboptimally, instantiate r=2 (mean + variance) with a *self-normalized* deviation bound so the confidence width uses the estimated variance rather than a known bound. We conjecture and will prove this two-moment index attains the correct KL constant for the Gaussian family and a near-optimal constant for sub-Gaussian/finite-variance arms, closing the open regime.

Analysis and evaluation. (a) Prove an O(log n) regret upper bound whose constant is the r-moment "partially contracted" KL, and show it converges to the Lai–Robbins bound as the adaptive budget grows, quantifying the exact optimality gap as a function of retained moments. (b) Show r=1 reproduces Agrawal's constant and r=2 resolves the Gaussian-unknown-variance case. (c) Bridge to RL: drop the index in place of optimistic Q-values in the bandit Q-learning/epsilon-greedy loop to give that framework its first optimal-constant exploration rule. (d) Empirically compare against sample-mean UCB (Agrawal), full-distribution KL indices (Burnetas–Katehakis), and epsilon-greedy Q-learning on bounded, Gaussian-unknown-variance, and heavy-tailed arms, measuring both regret and per-step compute to demonstrate the simplicity–optimality trade-off is largely illusory once the moment budget is chosen adaptively.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 21 turns / 32 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit theory offers several index policies each proven optimal but in incompatible senses, a puzzle for deployment. The Gittins index is optimal for the Bayesian discounted objective but not in finite horizon (Ishikida-Varaiya). Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis are optimal for undiscounted regret, matching an asymptotic O(log n) bound with a KL constant M. Agrawal's simpler sample-mean index gets the same rate but a sub-optimal constant. Each is optimal for a different objective, only asymptotically, via a constant silent about finite time. So the optimal-constant policy can have worse finite-time regret than a sub-optimal one, and a policy optimal for one criterion can be poor for another. No source maps where these asymptotic orderings actually take hold or the cost of using the wrong optimal policy.

**Method.** Build an empirical atlas linking asymptotic constants to finite-time behavior. On a controlled instance grid (Bernoulli, exponential-family, bounded nonparametric, heavy-tailed arms; varied arm count, gaps, horizon, discount), run Gittins, Lai-Robbins KL-UCB, Agrawal's index, Burnetas-Katehakis, a Yakowitz-Lowe rule, and Q-learning, computing each theoretical constant M. Measure regret trajectories plus two diagnostics: constant fidelity (regret / (M log n)) and crossover horizon (when the constant-optimal policy overtakes the simpler one). Evaluate all policies under discounted, undiscounted, and average-reward objectives to quantify the wrong-objective penalty. Produce regime diagrams of which policy wins in finite time and regress crossover horizon on instance features (gap, arms, KL curvature, tails) to predict when asymptotic optimality matters.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 26 tool calls

**Motivation.** A contradiction runs through the classical bandit literature. Lai and Robbins (1985) showed the unavoidable O(log n) regret constant is governed by the KL number I(theta_a, theta_star), and Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) gave its exact optimal form M equals sum over arms of Delta_a divided by K_a, where K_a is the infimum of I(theta_a, theta_prime) over alternatives whose mean exceeds mu_star, achieved only by indices solving full-distribution KL dual problems. Yet Agrawal (1995) showed that index policies depending on an arm's history only through its scalar sample mean already achieve O(log n) regret, attaining the exact optimal constant for one-parameter exponential families while being strictly suboptimal otherwise, via a contraction of KL, namely I1_p(x) equals the infimum of KL(nu, p) over distributions nu whose mean equals x. This is a paradox: KL is the fundamental quantity, and a single scalar provably suffices for optimality in one regime but provably wastes exploration in another. The field treats this as a dichotomy: simple but generally suboptimal (Agrawal; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991, optimal rate only for bounded arms) versus optimal but full-distribution (Lai-Robbins; Burnetas-Katehakis). Missing is an explanatory account of what the mean discards, why exponential families are the precise boundary of sufficiency, and which minimal statistic restores optimality. Closing this converts an all-or-nothing choice into a tunable, quantified tradeoff between simplicity and regret optimality.

**Method.** Introduce a formal moment-sufficiency hierarchy unifying Agrawal, Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis as endpoints of one continuum, deriving the exact price of a low-dimensional statistic. Ontology: for a K-dimensional statistic Phi equals (phi_1, ..., phi_K), for example the first K moments, define the class Pi_K of index policies summarizing each arm only through the empirical Phi; Agrawal is Pi_1 with Phi being the identity, and full-distribution policies are Pi_infinity. Central object: the level-K KL contraction IK_p(t), defined as the infimum of KL(nu, p) over distributions nu with expected Phi equal to t; by Sanov large deviations this is the sharpest confidence rate constructible from Phi, and its minimizer is the maximum-entropy exponential tilt matching t, giving a finite convex dual over K plus one multipliers. Effective divergence: DK_a, the infimum of KL(nu, p_a) over alternatives whose mean, seen through Phi, exceeds mu_star; prove the best attainable constant in Pi_K is M_K equals sum over arms of Delta_a divided by DK_a, matching Burnetas-Katehakis with K_a replaced by DK_a. Monotonicity theorem: adding moments adds equality constraints, shrinking the feasible set and raising the infimum KL, so M_1 is at least M_2 is at least M_infinity equal to M_LR, and the gap M_K minus M_infinity, namely sum over arms of Delta_a times (one over DK_a minus one over Dinf_a), is the explicit price of moment truncation. Sufficiency-boundary theorem: M_K equals M_infinity if and only if the information projection onto the set where expected Phi equals t stays inside the true family for all critical alternatives, equivalently if and only if the family together with its critical alternatives is a K-parameter exponential family whose sufficient statistic is spanned by Phi; this explains Agrawal's paradox as the K equals 1 case. Constructive corollary MOMENT-UCB: track empirical Phi and set each index to the supremum of the mean over distributions nu whose contraction IK evaluated at expected Phi is at most (log n) divided by N_a, computed via the K plus one dimensional exponential-family dual, generalizing Agrawal's inflated mean and Burnetas-Katehakis's dual indices. Prove a matching upper bound, regret at most M_K log n plus o(log n), via Agrawal-style large-deviations lemmas, plus a Pi_K-restricted lower bound establishing M_K as the class optimum. The result is a taxonomy of policies indexed by statistic dimension, a theorem locating exponential families as the exact sufficiency frontier, and a tunable knob trading estimation cost for provable regret.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 28 turns / 38 tool calls

**Motivation.** Burnetas-Katehakis attain the optimal Lai-Robbins regret constant with an index that solves a KL-ball optimization over an arm's empirical distribution, yet their proof needs finite known support; Agrawal's general sample-mean index only matches the constant for exponential families, and Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric methods are rate-optimal but constant-suboptimal. The paradox: the empirical distribution seems to hold the information the optimal index needs, yet dropping distributional knowledge forfeits the optimal constant. Can that gap be closed for unknown, continuous, or heavy-tailed rewards?

**Method.** Build a self-calibrating distributionally-robust index policy. For each arm, adaptively bin the observed reward range with resolution growing in play count, form a smoothed empirical distribution, and set the index to the optimum of maximizing mean reward over distributions within a divergence ball of radius beta times log-n over play-count, padding the grid beyond the observed max for unseen tails; solve incrementally by stochastic approximation. A meta-layer treats beta, the divergence family, and the binning schedule as configurations searched online via successive halving that reallocates plays to the best realized reward. A large-deviations diagnostic detects under-resolved support and triggers targeted exploration and refinement. Benchmark regret constants against Agrawal, Burnetas-Katehakis, and Yakowitz-Lowe, targeting recovery of the optimal constant without knowing the reward family.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** A contradiction runs through this bandit literature. Lai-Robbins showed the optimal log-n regret constant is a KL quantity attainable only when the reward distribution family is known. Burnetas-Katehakis generalized this optimal constant but their index still needs the family, and they cannot even verify their condition for normal arms with unknown variance. Agrawal dropped the need for the family with a sample-mean index but attains the optimal constant only for exponential families, otherwise paying a strictly larger contraction constant. Yakowitz-Lowe are fully nonparametric under finite moments but reach only the optimal rate for bounded arms. So the optimal constant is provably achievable yet every method either needs parametric knowledge we lack or forfeits optimality, and a benign continuous family defeats the optimal machinery. This matters because the KL bound is meant to price ignorance exactly, but no policy pays that price when the family is unknown and continuous.

**Method.** Attain the optimal Burnetas-Katehakis constant from the empirical distribution alone: index each arm by the largest mean among distributions supported on its observed reward values whose KL divergence from its empirical distribution is at most log-n over the number of pulls, then pull the arm with the largest index. This keeps Agrawal's data-only simplicity but replaces his contraction of the KL number with the exact nonparametric KL constraint that defines the optimal constant. Analyze it via empirical Sanov-type large deviations and empirical-process tools rather than parametric large-deviation lemmas, proving the index is an asymptotic upper confidence bound and that suboptimal pulls grow at the optimal KL rate, which resolves the normal-unknown-variance case left open by Burnetas-Katehakis. Establish guarantees along a ladder of weaker assumptions: bounded support, sub-exponential arms via adaptive truncation with bias correction, and heavy-tailed finite-moment arms via winsorized empirical-KL constraints. Provide a matching lower bound beyond known families, asymptotic efficiency within uniformly-fast policies, and simulations where the empirical index tracks the optimal constant while sample-mean and parametric indices do not.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Lai and Robbins (1985) proved the O(log n) regret bound with a Kullback-Leibler constant and gave index policies attaining it; Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) sharpened this to uniformly-fast optimality; Agrawal (1995) gave a simpler version depending only on the sample mean inflated by a large-deviation amount. The contradiction: these policies are sold as optimal and general, yet their guarantees rest on fragility. Agrawal's proof relies entirely on large-deviation lemmas needing a steep cumulant generating function, i.e. an exponentially light tail or exponential family, while Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis require the exact parametric family to be known. The design that hits the optimal constant, a sample-mean index tuned to a light-tailed family, is exactly what makes it brittle: a few corrupted observations or a heavy-tailed arm (Pareto, Student-t, common in finance, latency, clinical response) can inflate or deflate the index arbitrarily and turn O(log n) into near-linear regret. Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) relax the parametric assumption but attain the optimal rate only for bounded arms and ignore contamination. Thus asymptotic efficiency and robustness appear to trade off, and no policy here is at once distribution-agnostic, robust to heavy tails and outliers, and O(log n) with a near-optimal constant. This matters because in clinical allocation occasional corrupted or extreme outcomes are the norm, making a provably optimal but easily derailed policy untrustworthy.

**Method.** Build a Robust large-deviation UCB index policy that decouples the estimator from the confidence inflation. Replace the sample mean in Agrawal's index with a bounded-influence robust estimator (median-of-means, trimmed mean, or Catoni M-estimator) per arm, and re-derive the index inflation from concentration bounds valid under only a finite 1-plus-epsilon moment, replacing Agrawal's steep-cumulant bound with sub-Gaussian-type robust deviation bounds, so the index stays a valid upper confidence bound even without a moment generating function. Parameterize by a per-arm corruption fraction epsilon and prove a regret bound that degrades continuously in epsilon and the tail index rather than breaking. Show the leading constant recovers the Lai-Robbins and Agrawal optimal constant in the light-tailed uncontaminated limit. Because the tail index and epsilon are unknown in practice, add an online diagnostic comparing sample mean to robust estimate and monitoring empirical tail behavior to self-tune the number of blocks or trimming level, removing the need to specify the family. Prove O(log n) regret and the epsilon-degradation bound using empirical-process tools in the spirit of Pollard (1984), and compare empirically against Agrawal, Lai-Robbins, and Yakowitz-Lowe across clean, heavy-tailed, and contaminated regimes.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** The bandit literature has produced two families of "provably optimal" index policies that, paradoxically, disagree with each other. On the Bayesian / dynamic-programming side, the Gittins allocation index (Multi-armed bandit allocation indices; Multi-armed bandit problem revisited) is exactly optimal for the discounted-reward objective by decomposing an N-dimensional problem into N one-dimensional retirement problems, and Ishikida & Varaiya surprisingly show the same index rule is also asymptotically optimal under the average-reward criterion; moreover it can be computed model-free by reinforcement learning (Q-learning for bandit problems). On the frequentist side, Lai & Robbins (Asymptotically efficient adaptive allocation rules), Agrawal (Sample-mean index policies), and Burnetas & Katehakis (Optimal adaptive policies for sequential allocation) build indices from Kullback-Leibler / large-deviation confidence bounds that are asymptotically efficient for *regret* with the optimal KL constant.

Here is the contradiction. Both are called "optimal index rules," yet they are derived from entirely unrelated principles (optimal-stopping calibration vs. KL confidence sets), they prescribe different indices, and neither optimality notion implies the other: the Bayes-optimal Gittins index carries no guarantee on the Lai-Robbins regret constant and discards nothing but also proves nothing about learning loss, while the regret-optimal UCB index ignores prior information and says nothing about discounted optimality. Worse, a second paradox lives *inside* the frequentist camp: Agrawal's simple sample-mean index attains O(log n) regret but, in general (beyond one-parameter exponential families), only with a *suboptimal* constant obtained through a KL "contraction," whereas Burnetas & Katehakis recover the *optimal* constant only by solving a distribution-specific KL dual with extra assumptions and heavier computation. So simplicity, generality, and constant-optimality cannot currently be had together, and Bayesian versus frequentist optimality point in different directions. Why should two "optimal" indices disagree, and can a single object explain and reconcile them? This matters because practitioners must otherwise choose between prior-exploiting Bayesian optimality and distribution-free regret guarantees, and because the gap leaves no simple, general, model-free rule that is simultaneously regret-constant-optimal.

**Method.** Introduce a single unifying object — a distributionally-robust Gittins index defined on empirical KL-confidence balls and learned online by reinforcement learning — and show that both the Bayesian Gittins index and the frequentist KL-optimal UCB index are special limits of it.

(1) Reconciling construction. For each arm, set up a per-arm "calibration" restart MDP exactly in the Gittins style: the state is the arm's empirical reward distribution (nonparametric, finite-support/finite-moment, following the Yakowitz-Lowe and Burnetas-Katehakis settings), and the index is the retirement/fair-charge value that makes pulling indifferent to retiring. The one change from classical Gittins is that the continuation value is evaluated not under a fixed Bayesian posterior but *optimistically over a KL / large-deviation confidence set* of plausible reward distributions of radius r_t = log t / T_a(t) around the empirical distribution. Concretely the index is the retirement value of an optimal-stopping problem whose per-step reward is sup{ mean(q) : KL(empirical || q) <= r_t } — i.e., the Burnetas-Katehakis optimistic mean is embedded as the instantaneous continuation reward of a Gittins retirement problem.

(2) Unification theorem (resolving the puzzle). Prove that this robust index reduces to (i) the classical Bayesian Gittins index when the confidence set collapses to a trusted posterior (radius -> 0 with prior weight), and (ii) the Burnetas-Katehakis KL-dual index — hence the Lai-Robbins optimal regret constant, in the *general* nonparametric case, not only exponential families — when the radius is calibrated to log t and the discount factor is driven to 1 (the average-reward limit for which Ishikida & Varaiya already proved Gittins-index optimality). This exhibits the two literatures as two limits (confidence radius and discounting) of one index, explaining precisely *why* the classical indices differ and removing Agrawal's contraction loss by using the exact KL ball rather than its contraction.

(3) Model-free computation. Extend Duff-style Q-learning for bandits from posteriors to empirical KL-balls: learn the retirement value of the calibration MDP by temporal-difference/Q-learning on the empirical-distribution state, so the index is obtained without solving a family-specific dual and without knowing the distribution class. This gives a rule that is simultaneously simple (index type, sample/empirical-distribution based), general (nonparametric), and constant-optimal — the combination the current tradeoff forbids.

(4) Analysis, assembling the four toolkits. Prove asymptotic efficiency (regret matching the Lai-Robbins/Burnetas-Katehakis lower-bound constant) and the reduction results by combining: Agrawal's large-deviation lemmas to control the KL ball; the interchange/retirement-index optimality arguments (Multi-armed bandit problem revisited) for the stopping problem; Q-learning convergence (Watkins, via the RL introduction) for the learned index; and empirical-process uniform-convergence tools (Convergence of stochastic processes) to control empirical KL over the confidence ball. 

(5) Validation. Empirically compare the learned robust Gittins index against UCB/Lai-Robbins indices, Burnetas-Katehakis dual indices, and the exact discounted Gittins index across Bernoulli, exponential-family, and non-exponential nonparametric arms, verifying (a) it tracks the discounted-optimal policy at small radius/high discount and (b) attains the optimal regret slope and constant at the log-t radius, thereby demonstrating the reconciliation in practice.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** These papers establish asymptotically optimal index policies for bandits (Lai-Robbins KL rules, Agrawal sample-mean UCB, Burnetas-Katehakis dual-optimization indices, Gittins DP indices, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric), but deployment is blocked by resource costs, not theory. Optimal indices are expensive per decision (Gittins DP, Burnetas-Katehakis optimization; Yakowitz-Lowe call Bayes solutions exceedingly difficult to compute), while Agrawal's cheaper sample-mean policies lose the optimal constant unless the reward family is known. Tight KL-optimal constants need the family known a priori; nonparametric fallbacks are robust but converge slower, wasting scarce expensive samples that dominate clinical trials, materials and drug screening, and online experimentation. Modern settings run millions of concurrent bandits or low-power edge hardware where per-step DP or optimization and per-arm posterior storage are infeasible. No source offers a deployable artifact amortizing index computation, handling unknown families without wasting samples, and porting to large-scale or constrained hardware.

**Method.** Build IndexServe, a deployable adaptive-allocation engine. (1) Offline Index Compiler: since these policies act on low-dimensional sufficient statistics (play count, sample mean, optional variance or CDF summaries), precompute index values on a discretized grid once for a library of policies (Gittins DP, Burnetas-Katehakis, Agrawal UCB, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric), stored as compact interpolatable lookup tables, moving prohibitive computation offline. (2) Runtime Index Server: at each decision, look up and interpolate an arm's index from its sufficient statistics in constant time and memory, giving optimal-quality indices at the cost of simple policies, enabling millions of concurrent bandits and edge deployment. (3) Online Family Router: a per-arm goodness-of-fit monitor scores parametric families against a nonparametric fallback, serving a confidence-weighted blend that starts robust and nonparametric and shifts to the tighter parametric index as evidence accrues, with a Q-learning-for-bandits meta-controller choosing among tables to minimize realized regret. (4) Deployment workflow: a report-outcome and get-next-arm API, one-time domain calibration running the compiler, and a benchmark harness comparing sample efficiency, latency, and memory against per-step recomputation and fixed single-policy baselines. This decouples expensive computation, paid once offline, from constant-time serving, preserves sample efficiency by adaptively routing between nonparametric and parametric indices, and ports the theory to massive scale and constrained hardware. It is feasible via sufficient-statistic state reduction, grid interpolation, and standard goodness-of-fit tests, requiring no new theory.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The applications motivating this corpus (sequential clinical trials and allocation of scarce, costly treatments/experiments; Lai and Robbins 1985, Yakowitz and Lowe 1991, Berry and Fristedt 1985) are resource-bottleneck problems: every arm pull spends an expensive, strictly limited resource. Yet nearly all policies here (Lai and Robbins 1985, Agrawal 1995, Burnetas and Katehakis 1996) are built for the non-Bayesian infinite-horizon regime, minimizing asymptotic O(log n) regret with confidence-bound inflation calibrated to elapsed time. These guarantees are vacuous when the total budget N is small and fixed: the log-n inflation keeps exploring as if the horizon were unbounded, wasting scarce pulls and never cashing in learning as the budget runs out; and the optimal exploration constant needs the KL number / full distribution family, which tiny samples cannot supply. The only horizon-aware alternatives (finite-horizon DP and Gittins indices; Berry and Fristedt 1985, Ishikida and Varaiya 1994) are Bayesian and computationally prohibitive, a compute/prior-elicitation bottleneck Agrawal 1995 also flags for history-dependent UCBs. So no policy is simultaneously budget-aware, distribution-free/sample-mean-based, and cheap enough for the scarce-sample regime that motivates the field.

**Method.** Propose a Budget-Aware Sample-mean Index (BASI) policy for a finite-budget bandit with known small total pulls N. At step t with remaining budget R=N-t, each arm's index equals its empirical mean plus an inflation g(n_j,R), where g uses Agrawal 1995's large-deviation / empirical-likelihood upper-confidence construction (sample-mean-only, computed as a cheap one-dimensional dual in the spirit of Burnetas and Katehakis 1996). The key change: replace the time-based allowance (about log n) with one tied to remaining budget R, so exploration is large when much horizon remains and decays to zero as R approaches 0, automatically shifting from exploration to exploitation as the resource is spent. Steps: (1) prove a value-of-remaining-budget result showing the optimal exploration term must vanish as R approaches 0, motivating a schedule like log R or information-gain-per-remaining-pull instead of log elapsed time; (2) keep per-step cost linear in the number of arms, with no dynamic programming or priors; (3) support bounded/nonparametric arms per Yakowitz and Lowe 1991; (4) add a batched/delayed-feedback variant that spends the budget in K batches and recomputes indices only K times, cutting compute and deployment friction. Analysis: derive non-asymptotic finite-N regret bounds via Agrawal's large-deviation lemmas, identifying small-N and many-arm regimes where budget-aware inflation provably beats the finite-N restriction of log-n policies while staying within a bounded factor of the Gittins/DP optimum at far lower cost. Validation: simulate small-N clinical-trial-like settings (Bernoulli and bounded arms, many arms relative to budget), reporting simple and cumulative regret plus compute/wall-clock cost against Lai-Robbins, Agrawal UCB, Burnetas-Katehakis, and exact Gittins/DP.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** The corpus produced several distinct families of asymptotically optimal bandit allocation policies: KL/UCB rules with the Lai-Robbins O(log n) constant (Lai and Robbins 1985), sample-mean-only index policies trading a tighter constant for cheaper computation (Agrawal 1995), dual-index policies for parametric and finite-support populations (Burnetas and Katehakis 1996), nonparametric bounded-arm methods (Yakowitz and Lowe 1991), discounted Gittins index rules needing per-arm dynamic programming (Gittins 1989; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994), and Q-learning approaches (1995; Sutton and Barto 1998). A recurring subtext is resource cost: authors justify contributions by computational simplicity, easily computable indices, or the expense of Gittins indices. Yet the success metric is an asymptotic regret constant tied to a Kullback-Leibler number. This creates a resource-bottleneck gap. A practitioner deploying a bandit under a budget faces two scarce resources the theory ignores: finite samples/plays, since horizons are short while guarantees are asymptotic, so the KL-optimal constant may never dominate before the experiment ends; and compute/time per decision, since Gittins DP, parametric index inversion, and nonparametric estimators differ by orders of magnitude in per-decision cost and memory. No source characterizes when a KL-constant-optimal but expensive policy actually beats a cheap sample-mean policy at practical horizons, how the ordering changes under model misspecification, or where the compute-versus-regret trade-off lands. The theory ranks policies at infinity and unit cost; deployment needs ranking under finite samples and finite compute.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map: a reproducible benchmark and diagnostic study measuring finite-sample regret jointly with computational cost across the corpus policy families, distilled into budget-indexed decision maps. Implement one representative per family behind a common interface so per-decision compute is measured equally: Lai-Robbins KL-index, Agrawal sample-mean UCB, Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric bounded-arm rule, discounted Gittins index via calibration DP, and a Q-learning bandit learner. Sweep a factorial of instances: reward families (Bernoulli, exponential-family, finite-support discrete, heavy-tailed/finite-moment cases that stress parametric assumptions), arm count K, gap structure, and horizon n, plus a misspecification axis where parametric policies get the wrong family. Per policy-instance cell, record cumulative regret and suboptimal-pull counts, wall-clock and elementary-operation cost per decision plus memory, and variance/tail behavior across replications, yielding a regret-versus-compute Pareto frontier per instance. From these derive crossover horizons n-star where the KL-constant-optimal policy overtakes cheaper competitors; compute-normalized regret under a fixed per-decision budget revealing where an asymptotically suboptimal cheap policy wins; robustness maps under misspecification and heavy tails; and scaling of per-decision cost in K to locate where Gittins/parametric approaches become infeasible. Release the harness plus a decision map that, given a resource profile (horizon, compute/memory budget, model confidence), recommends the family on the empirical Pareto frontier and flags misspecification risk.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 28 turns / 35 tool calls

**Motivation.** Optimal bandit policies (Lai-Robbins 1985, Agrawal 1995, Burnetas-Katehakis 1996, Gittins 1989, Yakowitz-Lowe 1991) attain the order log n regret rate only asymptotically and require reward-family knowledge, per-index optimization or dynamic programming, and tuning. These costs block use when samples are scarce, horizons short, and arms many. Missing is a distribution-free, tuning-free, finite-time guaranteed rule.

**Method.** Derive a UCB index using only bounded rewards: mean plus sqrt(c ln t / count). Prove via Chernoff-Hoeffding a finite-time bound, expected suboptimal pulls at most C ln n / gap^2, giving regret at most sum of C ln n / gap uniformly in n, plus a distribution-free O(sqrt(K n ln n)) bound; recover the KL-optimal constant as a limit. Add a resource-cost taxonomy (information, computation, horizon) and arm-elimination for scalability.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 23 turns / 30 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classic bandit works such as Lai-Robbins, Agrawal UCB, Burnetas-Katehakis, the Gittins index, and Yakowitz-Lowe minimize regret in the number of pulls under unit cost. None address a fixed experimental budget with heterogeneous unknown per-pull costs and parallel batches, the real bottleneck in screening applications like clinical trials and materials or drug candidate search, where the optimal indices are also expensive to compute (Gittins needs dynamic programming; Burnetas-Katehakis solve a dual each step). Progress in these applications is limited by scarce, costly, slow experiments, so counting pulls is the wrong resource metric.

**Method.** Reformulate adaptive allocation as a budget-constrained search maximizing discovered reward per unit cost. Maintain a cost-normalized index per arm: a large-deviations upper confidence bound on the mean (using only sample mean and pull count, so updates are constant time and avoid Gittins or dual dynamic programming) divided by an online estimate of that arm's unknown per-pull cost, with confidence inflation set via a Burnetas-Katehakis-style contraction dual in a cost-normalized Kullback-Leibler sense. Each round, choose the parallel batch by solving a budgeted knapsack that allocates spend to maximize aggregate marginal information (uncertainty reduction about the best arm) per unit cost, subject to remaining budget and batch size, solved greedily by spending on the highest information-per-cost arm until constraints bind. Adapt the Lagrange multiplier pricing the budget online by search so projected spend exactly exhausts budget B at the horizon, shifting from exploration to exploitation as budget depletes. Prove a logarithmic-in-budget cost-normalized regret bound with a cost-weighted Kullback-Leibler constant, using Agrawal large-deviations lemmas and the Lai-Robbins lower bound, plus a matching lower bound. Evaluate on screening tasks with heterogeneous unknown costs and batches, comparing cost-normalized regret and best-candidate-found-within-budget against unit-cost UCB, Gittins, and Burnetas-Katehakis baselines under equal spend, and report compute saved by the sample-mean indices.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** There is a hard trade-off in this bandit literature between the sample cost of a policy (expensive suboptimal pulls) and its compute/knowledge cost. Lai and Robbins (1985) and Agrawal (1995) attain the optimal O(log n) regret constant only by assuming a known reward family (one-parameter exponential families), unavailable in noisy real deployments. Agrawal's sample-mean index is cheap but outside exponential families its constant is only a suboptimal contraction of the KL number, wasting scarce costly experiments. Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) recover the optimal nonparametric constant but restrict to finite-support populations and solve a KL dual optimization for every arm at every epoch, a compute bottleneck growing with arms and horizon. Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) are nonparametric under finite-moment conditions but still solve auxiliary equations and lack the optimal constant. So no policy is at once sample-optimal, cheap to run at scale, and valid under weak realistic assumptions (unknown distributions, noisy or delayed or batched costly feedback). Because each pull is a scarce costly experiment and compute is itself constrained, this joint resource bottleneck, not asymptotic regret order, limits practical use.

**Method.** Build an amortized empirical-KL index policy that makes the optimal Burnetas-Katehakis and Lai-Robbins guarantee hold under weaker assumptions and far less compute. First, a distribution-free index: keep each arm's empirical reward measure and define its index as the largest mean reachable within an empirical-KL ball of radius (log n)/T_a(n) around that measure, computed against the raw empirical distribution rather than a known family or finite support, using Agrawal's large-deviation lemmas plus empirical-process concentration to cover general bounded and truncated light-tailed rewards. Second, amortized approximate computation: replace the exact per-epoch dual solve with an incremental warm-started convex solver that maintains certified upper and lower bounds on the true index, recomputing an arm only lazily when its pull count crosses a doubling threshold or its empirical measure shifts beyond tolerance epsilon_n, reusing cached bounds otherwise, turning per-epoch per-arm exact solves into a bounded amortized number of cheap updates. Third, an optimality-preserving schedule: shrink epsilon_n on a log-n schedule and always play the certified upper bound, and prove this keeps the upper-confidence-bound property so suboptimal arms are pulled only about M(theta) log n times and the optimal constant survives both empirical estimation and approximation, yielding an explicit compute versus sample-cost frontier. Fourth, extended scope: lazy refresh naturally handles batched and delayed feedback, heterogeneous per-arm pull costs under a fixed budget via cost-weighted regret with KL balls scaled by cost, and large or streaming arm sets. Validate with theory giving cost-weighted O(M(theta) log n) regret under nonparametric, delayed, budgeted conditions and with simulations against exact Burnetas-Katehakis and Agrawal's contraction index, measuring regret and number of optimizations to show matched sample-optimality at a fraction of the compute.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bandit index policies minimize costly wasted pulls in scarce-sample sequential experiments (clinical trials, expensive A/B tests, materials discovery), where the binding resource bottleneck is the number of expensive observations spent on suboptimal arms. But the asymptotically efficient policies achieve O(log n) regret only under a correctly specified distribution family: Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis calibrate their optimal constant and dual indices to a known family, and Agrawal's sample-mean UCB attains the optimal KL constant only for one-parameter exponential families (else a suboptimal contraction constant); nonparametric methods relax assumptions at a rate penalty. Under misspecification, heavy tails, or the small-sample regime that dominates when pulls are expensive, these KL-based confidence bounds miscalibrate, causing over-exploration that burns scarce samples or lock-on to a wrong arm giving catastrophic regret. No existing policy is simultaneously sample-frugal and robust to misspecification, so practitioners with costly experiments and uncertain distributional knowledge cannot trust these guarantees where failure costs most.

**Method.** We propose a Distributionally-Robust Upper-Confidence Index (DR-UCI) that keeps O(log n) frugality while hardening against misspecification and finite-sample brittleness. Each arm's index is the worst-case optimistic mean over an ambiguity set: distributions within a divergence ball (KL or Wasserstein radius rho_a) of the empirical distribution, intersected with weak nonparametric constraints (bounded support or a finite-moment bound). This index is a valid upper confidence bound simultaneously over all candidate distributions, so no single family must be correct. It is computed as a one-dimensional convex dual, generalizing the Burnetas-Katehakis dual-index construction and reusing Agrawal's large-deviations lemmas to calibrate confidence inflation, keeping per-step cost near standard UCB. The reliability mechanism is an adaptive radius: rho_a is driven by a running sequential goodness-of-fit test against a nominal model, shrinking toward zero to recover the Lai-Robbins/Burnetas-Katehakis optimal constant and full frugality when the model fits, and inflating to prevent premature lock-on when misspecification or tail anomalies appear, so robustness is paid for only when needed. Analysis via large deviations proves regret of the form M_robust log n + o(log n) matching the optimal constant under correct specification with rho_a to 0, and staying bounded for any distribution in the ambiguity set, converting catastrophic worst-case regret into controlled bounded loss, plus a bound on extra sample cost from the adaptive radius. We benchmark against Lai-Robbins, Agrawal UCB, and Burnetas-Katehakis under correct specification, adversarial misspecification, and heavy-tailed or contaminated rewards, reporting regret and wasted suboptimal pulls at small horizons; success is near-optimal sample cost when correct and dramatically reduced worst-case wasted pulls under misspecification.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works split into two disconnected literatures for the same allocation problem. The Bayesian/Gittins-index line (Gittins 1989; Berry and Fristedt 1985; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994) gives the exactly optimal rule for the discounted infinite-horizon bandit but at heavy resource cost: the index solves a dynamic-programming retirement problem per arm, presumes an exponential-family model with a conjugate prior (explicit formulas only for Normal, Bernoulli, exponential), and extensions add more computation. The RL alternative, Q-learning for bandit problems (Duff 1995) in the Sutton tradition (RL 1998), replaces DP with simulation, trading analytic burden for heavy sample and compute cost plus offline precomputation. Both scale poorly with many arms and are hard to deploy in real time or under unknown reward distributions. The frequentist regret line (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991) yields cheap, distribution-general indices (sample-mean based, nonparametric), but calibrated only to asymptotic O(log n) regret and decoupled from any discount factor or horizon, with no near-optimality guarantee for the discounted objective. The bottleneck is concrete: a discount- and horizon-aware near-optimal index today requires expensive DP or simulation and parametric priors, blocking deployment at scale, in real time, and on arbitrary distributions. No source offers a single index as cheap and model-free as the UCB/KL indices yet as horizon-aware as Gittins.

**Method.** Unify the two index literatures by showing both are instances of one Kullback-Leibler variational (KL-ball) program, then re-derive the Gittins index as a discount-calibrated large-deviation confidence bound computable in closed form from sample data. Step 1, common form: write both index families as the supremum of mean m(q) subject to KL(p_hat,q) at most c over an empirical neighborhood of the observed reward distribution, exactly the inf-KL critical-parameter objects of Burnetas-Katehakis and the large-deviation bounds of Agrawal. The regret line uses budget c=log(t)/T_i(t); the claim is that the Gittins index is the same program with a budget c(beta,T_i) fixed by discount factor beta. Step 2, calibration bridge: derive c(beta,T_i) by equating the exploration value of expanding the KL ball to the Gittins retirement value; anchor it so that as beta approaches 1 it reproduces the Lai-Robbins/Agrawal log-form, and for moderate beta it matches the tabulated small-sample Gittins bonus for Normal and Bernoulli, fitting the one-dimensional function to interpolate. Step 3, model-free surrogate: since the index uses KL projections of the empirical distribution rather than a conjugate posterior, instantiate it nonparametrically as in Burnetas-Katehakis and Yakowitz-Lowe, yielding an O(1)-per-arm root-finding computation needing no DP tables or simulation. Step 4, guarantees: prove that in the undiscounted limit the surrogate inherits O(log n) regret via Agrawal's lemmas and the KL-dual machinery, and that under discounting the gap to exact Gittins is bounded by the calibration error, using empirical-process convergence (Pollard 1984) and Cramer large deviations (Cramer 1962).
