Step 5, validation: benchmark cost and joint discounted-plus-regret performance against exact Gittins, Q-learning indices (Duff 1995), and KL-UCB on Bernoulli, exponential, and heavy-tailed arms, stress-testing many-arm and arm-acquiring settings from Ishikida and Varaiya. This yields a cheap, distribution-agnostic, closed-form surrogate for the Gittins index, removing the compute, prior, and deployment bottleneck while retaining discounted near-optimality and asymptotic regret optimality.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. The sources are the classical backbone of optimal sequential allocation, each proving log-n regret or index-optimality only under narrow assumptions that rarely co-hold in real deployments. Lai and Robbins (1985) need a known one-parameter family with KL-regularity; Agrawal (1995) is optimal only for one-parameter exponential families; Burnetas and Katehakis (1996) reach nonparametric finite-support laws yet leave open the normal-unknown-variance case; Yakowitz and Lowe (1991) require known moment or support bounds; the Gittins line (Gittins 1989; Ishikida and Varaiya 1994; Q-learning-for-bandits 1995) assumes stationary Markovian arms with immediate single-pull feedback. A practitioner must pick the right policy before data arrive, supply constants they do not know, and accept immediate unbatched stationary feedback. Real reward streams are batched, delayed, drifting, of unknown variance and heavy-tailed, and the regime is unknown a priori, so the proven optimality does not transfer. No source can detect the regime online, self-calibrate constants from data, or switch policies safely under drift.

**Method.** Build an assumption-adaptive sequential-allocation engine as a deployable software system plus a calibration protocol and a benchmark. It has a policy library with a common index-oracle interface implementing the Lai and Robbins KL-UCB index, the Agrawal sample-mean index, the Burnetas and Katehakis dual-problem index, the Yakowitz and Lowe nonparametric strategy, and a Gittins or Q-learning index; each policy declares its required assumptions and constants. An online diagnostics module runs per arm, using sequential nonparametric tests to estimate support and tail behavior, which moments exist, whether variance is known or estimated, and stationarity via change-point detection, producing a live per-arm regime certificate. A self-calibration layer estimates needed constants from accumulating data, including plug-in variance and confidence widths for the normal-unknown-variance case left open by Burnetas and Katehakis, with conservative inflation for finite-sample validity. A meta-controller maps each certificate to the policy whose guarantee currently holds, blends across heterogeneous arms, and on a detected change-point discounts statistics and re-selects the matching policy. A feedback adapter wraps index computation with sliding-window and discounted statistics and a batched or delayed reward buffer so single-pull rules stay valid under batched and delayed allocation. A shadow-deployment harness runs all policies as counterfactual shadows beside the live controller, logging estimated regret with safety guardrails. The benchmark spans each source's assumed regime plus nonstationary and batched variants. Evaluation shows fixed policies win only inside their assumed regime while the engine tracks the per-regime best policy and self-calibrates. All component policies come from the sources and the diagnostics are standard tests, so the artifact is feasible.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope mismatch in the existing regret-optimal bandit literature. The seminal optimal policies — Lai & Robbins (1985), Agrawal's sample-mean index policies (1995), and Burnetas & Katehakis / Burnetas' optimal adaptive policies (1996) — all deliver their celebrated O(log n) regret guarantees only in an *asymptotic, infinite-horizon* regime (statements of the form "as n → ∞"), and their optimal constants are expressed through Kullback-Leibler numbers that presuppose knowledge of a narrow parametric family (one-parameter exponential families, or a known functional form of the density). Agrawal explicitly derives the confidence inflation from a "contraction" of the KL number, and Burnetas-Katehakis compute indices as solutions to KL dual problems; both leave the normal/unknown-variance and fully nonparametric cases only partially resolved. The Gittins-index line (Berry & Fristedt 1985; Ishikida & Varaiya 1994) is worse for this purpose: it requires geometric discounting and known priors and yields no distribution-free finite-time regret statement at all. The one genuinely distribution-free line, Yakowitz & Lowe's nonparametric methods (1991), pays for its generality with a looser O((log n)^2) rate and again only asymptotic, rate-based guarantees.

This is a scope mismatch that matters. A practitioner running a bandit for a *fixed, finite* number of plays with *no* parametric knowledge (clinical trials, adaptive routing, on-line assignment) is handed guarantees whose leading constants and o(log n) remainders are only realized in a limit they never reach, and whose confidence radii are literally uncomputable without the true reward family. At the horizons people actually care about, an asymptotic KL-optimal bound provides no operational number, and the only distribution-free alternative is provably a log-factor worse. What is missing is a single policy that is (i) simple and sample-statistic-based like Agrawal's, (ii) fully distribution-free like Yakowitz-Lowe, yet (iii) carries an *explicit finite-time (uniform-in-n) logarithmic* regret bound whose constant still adapts to the hardness (variance) of each arm — bridging the gap between loose distribution-free rates and tight-but-parametric asymptotics.

**Method.** Design and analyze a distribution-free, variance-adaptive upper-confidence index policy with explicit non-asymptotic regret bounds.

Setup: K arms with rewards bounded in [0,1] (or, more generally, with finite variance), unknown distributions, finite but arbitrary horizon. No parametric family is assumed.

Policy (cheap, sample-statistic-based, in the spirit of Agrawal's sample-mean indices): maintain for each arm j only the running count n_j, empirical mean μ̂_j, and empirical variance V̂_j. At each step t play the arm maximizing the index
  B_j(t) = μ̂_j + sqrt( 2 V̂_j ln t / n_j ) + c · ln t / n_j,
an *empirical-Bernstein* confidence inflation. The additive ln t / n_j term handles the concentration of the empirical variance itself; the middle term shrinks the confidence radius on low-variance arms. This differs from Lai-Robbins/Agrawal (which inflate via a KL number requiring the reward family) and from Yakowitz-Lowe (which use a fixed moment/Chebyshev radius and only reach (log n)^2): the radius here is computed purely from observed samples yet is variance-aware.

Finite-time analysis: prove that for *every* horizon n (not just n → ∞), the expected number of pulls of a suboptimal arm j with gap Δ_j = μ* − μ_j is bounded by
  E[T_j(n)] ≤ ( 8 V_j / Δ_j^2 + C ) ln n + (small constant),
so total regret ≤ Σ_{j:Δ_j>0} (8 V_j/Δ_j + C Δ_j) ln n + O(1). The proof combines (a) an empirical-Bernstein deviation inequality (the Bernstein-type inequalities already invoked by Yakowitz-Lowe) with a union bound over the ≤ n possible sample sizes, and (b) the standard index-policy argument that a suboptimal pull requires either the optimal arm's lower confidence bound to fail or the suboptimal arm's radius to still exceed Δ_j. This yields a bound that is (i) uniform in n, (ii) fully distribution-free, and (iii) whose constant is governed by the *true variance* V_j, so for low-noise arms it approaches the variance-dependent (KL-type) optimal constant of Lai-Robbins without ever knowing the family — directly closing the scope-mismatch gap.

Extensions and validation: (1) a "tuned" variant that plugs an upper confidence bound on the variance into the radius, and a version for finite-variance unbounded rewards via truncation, addressing the normal-unknown-variance case Burnetas-Katehakis left open. (2) Derive a matching finite-time lower bound of the same variance-scaled form to show the constant is not improvable distribution-free. (3) Empirically compare, at realistic finite horizons (n = 10^2–10^5), against Agrawal's KL sample-mean policy, the Burnetas-Katehakis index, and Yakowitz-Lowe, on Bernoulli, low-variance, and heavy-tailed arms, demonstrating that the asymptotic policies' advantage does not materialize (and their indices are uncomputable without the true family) at the horizons practitioners use, whereas the proposed policy has a computable, horizon-valid guarantee and adapts automatically to per-arm variance.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. The prior works all establish O(log n) regret for bandits but only under narrow, poorly transferable boundary conditions. Lai & Robbins (1985) prove optimality only as n goes to infinity and only for known single-parameter exponential families, with constants equal to Kullback-Leibler numbers that require the true distribution. Agrawal (1995) uses sample-mean indices but still an asymptotic O(log n) with large-deviation constants. Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) relax the parametric form yet remain an n-to-infinity limit and leave a normal-variance assumption open. Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) give nonparametric asymptotic rates, and the Gittins line (allocation-indices review; Ishikida & Varaiya 1994) optimizes infinite-horizon discounted/average reward via costly dynamic-programming indices. None address the practical regime: finite, often short horizons; unknown or only-bounded distributions; many arms; and no knowledge of the KL constants governing the bounds. There is no account of when the asymptotic log-n regime begins, how much regret accrues before it, how fragile the constants are under misspecification, or how these index families compare head-to-head at realistic budgets. The theory's boundary conditions mismatch practice, leaving finite-budget deployment without guidance.

**Method.** Empirical mapping study: a systematic simulation benchmark and diagnostic suite charting the finite-time behavior of the corpus policies and where their asymptotic guarantees hold or break. Testbed: bandit instances varying horizon n (tens to millions), number of arms K, gap structure (one large gap, uniformly small gaps, clustered near-optimal arms), reward family (Bernoulli, normal with unknown variance, finite-support discrete, heavy-tailed with finite moments), plus misspecification where a policy is fed the wrong family. Policy panel on equal footing: Lai-Robbins KL upper-confidence indices, Agrawal sample-mean indices, Burnetas-Katehakis dual indices, Yakowitz-Lowe nonparametric strategies, Gittins discounted indices, and epsilon-greedy/Q-learning baselines. Diagnostics from many Monte Carlo runs: empirical regret curves with fitted growth coefficient versus the KL lower bound; a crossover horizon where observed regret first tracks the log-n slope; sensitivity to tuning and misspecification; distribution-free worst-case regret over each family. Deliverables: phase-diagram heatmaps over (n, K, gap, family) showing which policy dominates in each region and where asymptotic theory is predictive versus misleading, quantifying the pre-asymptotic penalty of KL-based constants. Synthesis: use the patterns to isolate the minimal ingredients giving regret uniform over the whole horizon and robust to unknown distributions, namely a simple sample-mean-plus-confidence-radius index using only bounded support, and empirically validate it attains near-optimal regret where parametric/asymptotic policies falter. This yields a reproducible empirical map of finite-time, misspecification-robust performance to guide policy selection for a given budget.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope mismatch in the objective. Every asymptotically-optimal bandit theory in the corpus defines the best arm, and hence regret, only through expected reward and the largest mean. Lai-Robbins (1985) prove liminf R_n/log n >= sum (mu*-mu_j)/I(theta_j,theta*) using KL distinguishability of MEANS; Agrawal (1995) builds sample-mean index policies whose optimal constant is a large-deviations contraction of that KL number; Burnetas-Katehakis (1996) show the optimal index is the dual K(j)=inf KL(P_j||Q) subject to mean(Q)>=mu*; Yakowitz-Lowe (1991) and Gittins-index work (Ishikida-Varaiya 1994) likewise optimize expected reward. This risk-neutral, mean-only ranking is a narrow boundary condition for the very applications cited (clinical trials, adaptive control, finance), where variance and tail risk change which arm is preferable and decision-makers are risk-averse. The mismatch is structural: the KL machinery is silent once ranking depends on higher moments; tellingly, Burnetas-Katehakis explicitly leave the Gaussian unknown-mean-AND-variance case as an open problem. Thus there is no known fundamental regret limit and no provably efficient policy when best is defined by a risk-adjusted criterion.

**Method.** A Formal Derivation generalizing the mean-only theory to an arbitrary law-invariant risk functional, recovering classical results as the risk-neutral boundary. (1) Model: arm j has unknown P_j; fix a law-invariant risk functional rho (mean-variance mu-c*sigma^2, entropic/exponential utility, spectral/CVaR as instances). Define rho*=max_j rho(P_j) and rho-regret R(n)=sum over t of (rho* minus rho of the chosen arm). Define consistent (uniformly-fast) policies relative to rho. (2) Fundamental limit (theorem plus proof): define the constrained information K_rho(j)=inf KL(P_j||Q) subject to rho(Q)>=rho* with Q in the model class, the generalization of the Burnetas-Katehakis dual index with the mean constraint replaced by rho. Via a change-of-measure and Wald-likelihood argument (the Lai-Robbins Theorem-1 template) prove: for any consistent policy, liminf R(n)/log n >= sum over suboptimal j of (rho*-rho_j)/K_rho(j). (3) Matching policy (theorem plus proof): a risk-adjusted upper confidence index, index_j = sup of rho(Q) over Q in a KL-ball of radius about log n / N_j(t) around arm j empirical law. Using Agrawal's large-deviations contraction lemmas applied to rho, prove these are valid upper confidence bounds for rho and that the index policy attains the lower bound, establishing efficiency and tightness of K_rho. (4) Regularity and taxonomy: identify conditions on rho (monotonicity under stochastic dominance, quasi-convexity, continuity in the KL topology) that make K_rho positive and finite; classify risk functionals by which admit O(log n) risk-regret. (5) Boundary case and open problem: show rho=mean collapses K_rho to the Lai-Robbins constant, positioning classical theory as the risk-neutral edge of a functional family; derive closed-form K_rho and indices for mean-variance and entropic utility, and an explicit constrained-KL for the Gaussian unknown mean and variance case, resolving the Burnetas-Katehakis open assumption within the risk-aware formulation; characterize how risk-aversion reshapes which arm is hard to distinguish and thus the regret constants. Feasibility: KL lower bounds, contraction/large-deviations lemmas, and constrained-KL dual indices all appear in the corpus; the contribution is the unifying derivation for general rho, the efficiency proof, and the special-case computations.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The classic bandit works here share narrow boundary conditions that mismatch real use. Optimality is asymptotic: Lai and Robbins (1985), Agrawal (1995), Burnetas and Katehakis (1996), Yakowitz and Lowe (1991), and Ishikida and Varaiya (1994) prove O(log n) or index-rule optimality only as n grows, giving no prescription for a fixed finite horizon, which the Bandit problems (1985) monograph shows behaves quite differently. The exploration width also depends on distribution constants assumed known a priori: Lai-Robbins and Burnetas-Katehakis assume a known exponential family and use the exact Kullback-Leibler number; Agrawal's UCB conditions require large-deviation rate constants; Yakowitz-Lowe require a hypothesized moment or boundedness constant and admit one must start at a sensible point since rates depend on the hypothesized bandit postulates. Misspecification is costly and asymmetric: over-stating the rate causes chronic over-exploration, while under-stating risks locking onto a suboptimal arm and linear regret. Rewards are also assumed stationary and i.i.d. The gap: no principled way to choose the exploration configuration when the reward family, its rate or moment constant, and even the horizon are unknown, which is exactly where finite-sample performance is decided.

**Method.** Treat the exploration tuning of an index policy as something to be optimized and searched online rather than fixed a priori, yielding a self-tuning, horizon-aware bandit needing no known family or rate constant. First, define a parameterized index family: UCB-style indices g(theta) that nest the Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, and Yakowitz forms, where theta has an inflation coefficient c, an assumed rate or moment constant rho, and a schedule shape s that shrinks the width with the number of pulls and with the remaining horizon; classic policies are special points. Second, make rho data-driven by estimating the local rate function online from each arm's rewards via empirical large-deviation and empirical-likelihood estimates, reusing Agrawal's large-deviation lemmas and the Yakowitz-Lowe moment and boundedness bounds, with uniform sample-path validity justified by empirical-process tools (Convergence of stochastic processes, 1984); this turns the fixed distribution constant into a self-calibrating width. Third, run a horizon-aware meta-search over theta: wrap the base policy in a policy-over-policies scheme that allocates the finite budget across screening candidates using racing and successive-halving on a slow timescale, comparing them by an online pseudo-regret proxy (a gap-weighted suboptimal-pull count from running estimates) and eliminating dominated configurations so budget concentrates on the best exploration level for the actual instance and horizon. Fourth, couple two timescales: the fast timescale plays arms and updates statistics while the slow timescale adjusts theta by gradient-free stochastic search on the proxy, framed as a small meta-bandit or stochastic-approximation problem in the reinforcement-learning and Q-learning-for-bandits spirit; because s reads the remaining horizon, exploration tapers near the deadline, and drift in estimated rates or means triggers reallocation, giving robustness to mild non-stationarity. Finally, benchmark against fixed-constant Lai-Robbins, Agrawal UCB, and Yakowitz-Lowe under correctly specified families, misspecified constants, and short or drifting horizons, targeting recovery of the correct KL-optimal O(log n) rate when the family is known, a bounded competitive ratio versus the best fixed theta in hindsight for finite n, and avoidance of the linear-regret failure under under-specified constants. This addresses the scope mismatch by discovering, via online optimization and screening, exactly the constants and finite-horizon schedule that prior work assumes away.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The classic bandit theory in this reference set (Lai and Robbins 1985; Agrawal 1995; Burnetas and Katehakis 1996; Yakowitz and Lowe 1991; the Gittins-index tradition) rests on assumptions far narrower than the settings where bandits are actually deployed. Three interlocking scope mismatches recur. First, asymptotic-only guarantees: all optimality statements are limits as n grows. Lai and Robbins give a liminf lower bound on regret divided by log n of at least the sum of the gap over the Kullback-Leibler number; Burnetas and Katehakis give value equal to n times the best mean minus M(theta) log n plus lower-order terms. None bounds regret at a finite horizon, nor says from which n the log n regime holds or how large the hidden constants are. Berry and Fristedt note finite-time performance (Witten 1973) was only treated heuristically. Second, strong distributional knowledge: Lai and Robbins and Burnetas and Katehakis require a known parametric family, typically one-parameter exponential families, with family-specific confidence bounds. Even Agrawal 1995, whose stated aim was to simplify to sample-mean indices, still inflates the sample mean by a function built from a contraction of the Kullback-Leibler number, so the padding still encodes the reward family and its large-deviation rate, and the constant is generally not optimal. Third, computational and memory burden with clean-model assumptions: Gittins indices and early Lai-Robbins bounds need the full history per arm and/or auxiliary dual problems, and assume clean i.i.d. models with little guidance for noisy, heavy-tailed, or merely-bounded rewards. Together these leave a gap: no simple, distribution-free index policy has an explicit non-asymptotic regret guarantee holding uniformly for every finite horizon under only a boundedness assumption. This matters because real deployments run for finite, often modest, horizons, rarely know the reward family, and need a trivially computable policy that is provably good after any number of pulls.

**Method.** Relax the scope of classical index theory on all three axes at once: replace family-specific, asymptotically-justified confidence bounds with purely empirical padding, and prove a finite-horizon, distribution-free regret bound uniform in n. Setting with weaker assumptions: rewards are i.i.d. per arm with unknown distributions assumed only to have bounded support in the unit interval; no parametric family, KL rate, or exponential-family structure is assumed. This strictly generalizes Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, and Burnetas-Katehakis while keeping Agrawal's sample-mean-index philosophy. Policy with a simpler index: keep two statistics per arm, the running sample mean and the pull count. At each step play the arm maximizing the sample mean plus a confidence radius equal to the square root of two times log n divided by the pull count. This radius is distribution-free, derived from a Hoeffding tail bound rather than from a contraction of the KL number, so it needs no family knowledge and uses only the sample mean. Finite-time analysis in a new regime: the core step bounds, for each suboptimal arm, the expected number of pulls at every finite n. Via a union-of-tail-events decomposition plus Hoeffding's inequality, replacing the asymptotic large-deviation lemmas that Agrawal invokes, show that the expected pulls of a suboptimal arm are at most eight times log n divided by the squared gap, plus a small constant of one plus pi squared over three. This yields an explicit regret bound equal to eight times the sum over suboptimal arms of log n over the gap, plus a constant term, valid for all n at least one, converting the field's limit statements into a uniform guarantee with fully specified constants. Consistency with the lower bound: show order-optimality, matching the log n growth of the Lai-Robbins lower bound, losing nothing asymptotically while gaining non-asymptotic control. Recover a sharper leading constant with refinements: first, an empirical-Bernstein padding that adds a term using the observed sample variance, adapting the radius to low-variance arms and shrinking the constant toward the variance-dependent optimum; second, an epoch or eviction variant that pulls arms in geometrically growing blocks to cut the log n factor at small additive cost. Robustness extensions to broader, noisier conditions: because the proof relies only on concentration, extend to heavy-tailed rewards with finite moments of order one plus epsilon via a truncated or median-of-means estimator with a matching tail bound, and to mild non-stationarity via sliding-window sample means, giving finite-time bounds under drift. Evaluation: compare against baselines in these sources, namely the Gittins index, Agrawal's KL-contraction sample-mean index, and epsilon-greedy and certainty-equivalence-with-forcing, across Bernoulli, bounded-continuous, and heavy-tailed arms over short, medium, and long horizons, reporting realized regret with confidence bands against the proven envelope. Success criteria: regret stays below the explicit bound for every n; at small and medium horizons the distribution-free policy is competitive with family-tuned indices despite using no distributional knowledge; and the variance-adaptive variant closes most of the gap to family-optimal constants. This delivers what prior works do not: a trivially computable, assumption-light index policy with a guarantee meaningful at finite time.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** The classical stochastic multi-armed bandit results in this corpus all achieve their O(log n) regret only under narrow tail/cleanliness assumptions that rarely hold in the "machine learning" and adaptive-control settings they are motivated by. Lai & Robbins (1985) and Ishikida & Varaiya (1994) require rewards from specific families (normal, Bernoulli, Poisson, double-exponential, or single-parameter exponential families) and their proofs "depend critically on properties of tail behavior." Agrawal (1995) generalizes to simple sample-mean index policies, but the optimal constant is a Kullback–Leibler / large-deviations quantity that is only attained for exponential/Gaussian families, and the index radius is calibrated by a variance-type rate function. Burnetas & Katehakis (1996) still assume a known functional form of the density. Even the most assumption-light work, Yakowitz & Lowe (1991) "Nonparametric bandit methods," ultimately needs bounded arms or finite (1+α)-moments and rates that hold only over "bandit families whose sample means uniformly satisfy a Bernstein-type inequality."

Two coupled scope mismatches follow. (1) Every one of these policies estimates each arm's value with the raw sample mean and derives its index/upper-confidence radius from concentration of that sample mean. The sample mean is the least robust location estimator: under heavy tails (finite variance but no exponential moments) its confidence radius degrades from log to polynomial rates (exactly the O(n^{1/(1+α)}) penalty Yakowitz–Lowe pay), and a single large observation destroys concentration. (2) All works assume perfectly i.i.d., uncorrupted rewards, whereas real reward streams (clinical outcomes, sensor-fed assembly lines, online systems) contain occasional outliers, recording errors, or adversarial/anomalous pulls. Under even a vanishing fraction of such corruption, a sample-mean index policy can be driven to either inflate a truly inferior arm's index or suppress the optimal arm's index, converting the celebrated O(log n) regret into linear regret. The gap: no policy in this literature is designed to retain logarithmic regret when the reward distributions are heavy-tailed and a bounded fraction of observations is corrupted. Because these methods underpin a "larger class of stochastic adaptive control problems," this brittleness matters wherever bandits are actually deployed.

**Method.** Robustify the sample-mean index-policy paradigm itself, keeping its structure (per-arm index = robust location estimate + confidence radius; play the largest index) but replacing every place the sample mean and its light-tail concentration appear.

(1) Broadened operating regime. Model each arm's rewards as coming from a "gross-error" mixture: with probability 1−η an i.i.d. draw from an unknown distribution assumed only to have a finite (1+α)-moment (α>0, heavy tails allowed, no boundedness/exponential-family), and with probability up to η an arbitrarily/adversarially corrupted value. This strictly contains the bounded / exponential-family / finite-moment scopes of Lai–Robbins, Agrawal, and Yakowitz–Lowe while adding contamination they never consider.

(2) Robust index construction. For each arm, from its T_j(n) pulls compute a robust location estimator instead of the mean: median-of-means (partition pulls into k blocks, take block means, take the median) or a Catoni/trimmed M-estimator. Pair it with a robust confidence radius derived from deviation inequalities for that estimator that require only the finite (1+α)-moment and account for the corruption budget η — i.e., a "robust upper confidence bound" that replaces Agrawal's variance-rate function J and Yakowitz–Lowe's Bernstein bound. The block count k is tied to the target failure probability (∝ log n) so the radius shrinks at the fast rate even under heavy tails, and the median aggregation absorbs up to a constant fraction of corrupted blocks so a bounded number of outliers cannot move the index.

(3) Assumption-light self-calibration. Because α, the moment bound, and η are unknown, set the confidence radius from an online robust scale estimate per arm (e.g., interquartile range of block means) and grow the number of blocks with a doubling schedule, so the policy is nonparametric in the spirit of Yakowitz–Lowe but does not rely on the sample mean. Optionally add a lightweight per-arm contamination flag: compare the robust estimator against the trimmed mean and, on large disagreement, widen that arm's radius, hedging against undetected corruption.

(4) Analysis and validation. Prove an O(log n) regret upper bound whose leading constant is a robust divergence/gap-to-scale ratio (a corruption- and heavy-tail-tolerant analogue of the Kullback–Leibler constant of Lai–Robbins/Agrawal), and complement it with a lower-bound/impossibility argument showing that any sample-mean index policy (Agrawal-style UCB, Yakowitz–Lowe test-time rule) incurs polynomial or linear regret under the same heavy-tailed/η-contaminated regime. Empirically, on Gaussian arms the method should match the classical constants (recovering their results as a special case), while on Pareto/Student-t and outlier-injected arms it should preserve logarithmic regret where the sample-mean policies collapse. This directly closes the scope mismatch by trading the brittle sample-mean estimator and light-tail assumptions for robust estimation with guarantees under weak-moment, contaminated conditions.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 24 turns / 32 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior bandit guarantees rely on narrow assumptions. Lai-Robbins (1985), Agrawal (1995), Burnetas-Katehakis (1996), and Yakowitz-Lowe (1991) give only asymptotic, n-to-infinity log-n optimality, with no control for a fixed small number of plays. The first three also require a known parametric reward family with Kullback-Leibler or dual-optimization constants, failing under unknown or misspecified families. The Gittins line (Gittins 1989; Ishikida-Varaiya 1994; Berry-Fristedt 1985) needs full Bayesian priors, geometric discounting, and expensive per-state indices. No single result gives a trivially computable policy whose regret guarantee holds at every finite n, needs no reward-family knowledge, and still attains log-n growth, though target applications live in exactly that finite, distribution-agnostic regime.

**Method.** Unify three strands into one bounded-reward index policy. Take the upper-confidence idea of Lai-Robbins and Agrawal and the large-deviation tools they and Yakowitz-Lowe use: each arm index equals its empirical mean plus a bonus sqrt of c times ln n over its play count, the bonus from a finite-sample Hoeffding or Chernoff bound rather than a Kullback-Leibler constant. Store only mean and count per arm and pick the largest index, realizing the Gittins own-state index in constant time. Prove a non-asymptotic regret bound for every horizon n and any bounded distribution, of order the sum over suboptimal arms of ln n over the gap, by pairing a deterministic bonus-below-gap argument with a tail union bound, replacing the asymptotic guarantees of Lai-Robbins, Agrawal, and Burnetas-Katehakis. Add a variance-adaptive Bernstein bonus using empirical variance and show it interpolates toward the arm-specific large-deviation constant, reconciling the distribution-free constant with the asymptotically optimal one. Relate the bonus to the Gittins index of Berry-Fristedt and Ishikida-Varaiya as a prior-free learning premium, mapping finite-horizon discounting to finite-n regret. Validate on Bernoulli, truncated-normal, and bounded heavy-tailed arms including the finite-support families of Burnetas-Katehakis and Yakowitz-Lowe, comparing the unified index, its variance-adaptive form, Gittins indices, and epsilon-greedy across horizons. The result is one simple memory-light policy that is simultaneously finite-time, distribution-free, and log-n optimal.

## Least Squares Support Vector Machine Classifiers

*seed paper `least-squares-support-vector-machine-classifiers-1999`, 12 sources, 64 generated ideas*

### Human idea (ground truth)

**Motivation.** Standard support vector machine classifiers require solving a quadratic programming problem arising from inequality constraints, which is computationally expensive, and their kernel-based formulation is otherwise attractive for avoiding the local minima that plague gradient-trained neural networks. A reformulation that preserves the kernel-mapping and structural-risk benefits of SVMs while reducing the training problem to something computationally cheap and free of local minima would make these classifiers far more practical for complex nonlinear classification tasks.

**Method.** We propose reformulating the two-class SVM classifier using equality constraints instead of inequality constraints and adopting a least-squares (sum of squared errors) penalty on the constraint violations rather than the classical hinge-type slack penalty. Specifically, we require that each mapped training point satisfy y_k[w^T phi(x_k) + b] = 1 - e_k with an error variable e_k, and minimize (1/2)w^T w + (gamma/2) sum of e_k^2. Constructing the Lagrangian and applying the optimality (Kuhn-Tucker) conditions yields, after eliminating the primal weight vector and error variables via the kernel trick (applying Mercer's condition so that phi(x_k)^T phi(x_l) becomes a kernel function such as an RBF), a single linear system in the bias and the Lagrange multipliers. Solving this linear set of equations directly produces the classifier, replacing quadratic programming with linear algebra. A consequence of the equality-constrained least-squares formulation is that support values become proportional to the per-point errors, yielding a full support-value spectrum rather than a sparse set. Kernel parameters can be selected via the VC-dimension bound. We will demonstrate the approach on a linearly separable illustrative case to visualize the support-value behavior and on the two-spiral benchmark using an RBF kernel to show generalization performance.

### LLM ideas

#### [method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** The sources contain two grounded but disconnected traditions. The Circular Backpropagation (CBP) network (Ridella et al., 1997) unifies representations: appending one extra input equal to the squared norm of x lets an MLP unit act as either a surface-based hyperplane or a prototype-based hypersphere with an interpretable center, radius, and gain, decided by optimization. It is cheap (one extra weight), hardware-friendly (obtained by augmenting the training set), interpretable, and has Cover-capacity and VC-dimension bounds. But it is trained by backpropagation (local minima, no margin-based capacity control) and only for classification. The support-vector tradition (Vapnik's Statistical Learning Theory and support-vector function estimation; Schölkopf et al.'s SVM-vs-RBF comparison; Saunders et al.'s dual-variable ridge regression) provides what CBP lacks: convex training, explicit Structural Risk Minimization capacity control, automatic center/weight selection, and a unified view of classification, regression, and operator equations, but via implicit kernels, dense support-vector expansions, heavier inference, and poor interpretability. The comparison paper's successful hybrid (SV-selected centers plus backprop weights) shows the traditions are complementary, yet no artifact fuses CBP's explicit, low-dimensional, interpretable, deployable circular augmentation with convex SRM-controlled support-vector or dual-ridge training, nor extends circular representations to regression. This forces a choice between interpretable-but-locally-optimal CBP and accurate-but-opaque heavyweight kernel machines.

**Method.** Build CircML, a training-and-deployment system whose central artifact is a Circular Support Vector Machine (CSVM) pipeline that treats the CBP augmentation as an explicit feature map fed to convex learners. Module 1, Augmentation: map x to z equal to the concatenation of x and the squared norm of x, adding one dimension so any linear convex learner becomes a hypersphere or prototype learner. Module 2, Convex trainer with a shared interface: fit z with a max-margin SVM or dual-variable ridge regression for global optima, supporting classification, regression, and operator-equation targets. To exceed single-sphere expressivity, offer two composition modes: a circular kernel combining the explicit augmented inner product with a Gaussian kernel, and a two-stage mode where a Gaussian-kernel SVM selects a few support vectors as candidate centers that seed a compact bank of circular units re-fit convexly, giving an SRM-guided interpretable analogue of the hybrid system that succeeded on USPS. Module 3, SRM model selection: use the CBP-derived Cover and VC capacity expressions as explicit capacity estimates to choose the number of units and regularization by minimizing a risk bound. Module 4, Rule extraction: invert learned weights into circular parameters (center, radius, gain), emitting readable prototype and boundary rules and flagging collapsed-gain surface units versus prototype units. Module 5, Compact deployment: prune to the fewest units meeting the SRM bound and export a network needing only one extra input, targeting MLP-style hardware. Evaluate on the USPS digits benchmark against MLP, clustering-RBF, and Gaussian-kernel SVM, plus regression tasks, measuring test error, retained units and support vectors, inference cost, and rule fidelity. This delivers CBP's cheap, interpretable, deployable circular representation under the support-vector tradition's convex optimization and capacity control, generalized beyond classification.

#### [unsteered (blank)] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** Three strands of the provided literature developed largely in isolation, and their intersection exposes a concrete gap.

(1) Statistical Learning Theory and the Support Vector method (Vapnik, "Nature of Statistical Learning Theory" 1995; "Support Vector Method of Function Estimation" 1998) give convex, sparse, margin-maximizing classifiers whose capacity is controlled data-dependently by the radius–margin VC bound (Theorem 5.1: h ≤ min([R²A²], n)+1, with R the radius of the sphere enclosing the mapped data and A a bound on ‖w‖). But the feature map / kernel is chosen a priori; there is no principled, learned decision about whether a "surface-based" (hyperplane) or "prototype-based" (spherical/RBF) decision rule is appropriate, and expressive kernels (e.g., Gaussian) inject very large (effectively infinite-dimensional) capacity.

(2) The SVM-vs-RBF study (Schölkopf et al. 1997) shows on USPS digits that an SVM with a Gaussian kernel beats classical RBF networks and even a hybrid, and that the SV method automatically places prototype "centers." Yet it still requires a fixed rich kernel and yields many support vectors; it does not address which representational paradigm the data actually warrant, nor how to obtain that choice cheaply.

(3) Circular Backpropagation (CBP) networks (Ridella et al. 1997) elegantly unify the surface-based and prototype-based paradigms by a single, minimal input augmentation x → (x, ‖x‖²): a linear unit in this augmented space realizes hyperspheres, and the model exhibits "paradigm plasticity" — the coefficient on ‖x‖² decides, during learning, whether the unit behaves like a hyperplane or a prototype. The authors even analyze the model's VC dimension and Cover capacity. However, CBP is trained by non-convex backpropagation performing empirical risk minimization, so the promised VC/capacity advantage is never exploited during learning, and paradigm selection is left to an opaque, local optimizer.

(4) Kernel ridge regression in dual variables (Saunders et al. 1998) provides a closed-form, regularized dual solution but is non-sparse and says nothing about paradigm selection or margin-based capacity control.

No prior work connects CBP's minimal, interpretable circular augmentation with SVM-style, radius–margin capacity control and sparsity. This matters because CBP identifies the single cheapest feature that spans the gap between hyperplane and RBF classifiers, and SLT provides exactly the tool (a computable radius–margin bound) needed to choose along that spectrum in a principled, convex way — a synthesis none of these works performs.

**Method.** Propose the Circular Support Vector Machine (C-SVM): a maximum-margin classifier built on CBP's explicit finite feature map, together with a theoretically grounded, convex replacement for CBP's "paradigm plasticity."

1. Feature map and model. Map inputs by φ_β(x) = (x, β·‖x‖²), the CBP circular augmentation with a single scalar paradigm/scale parameter β ≥ 0. Learn a soft-margin separator sign(w·φ_β(x) + b) in this (d+1)-dimensional space. Equivalently this is an SVM with the explicit "circular kernel" k_β(x,x') = x·x' + β²‖x‖²‖x'‖², whose decision surfaces are hyperspheres. β = 0 collapses the machine to a linear (surface-based) SVM; large β emphasizes the prototype/spherical (RBF-like) direction. Because φ_β is finite and explicit, training, prediction, and geometry are all cheap and interpretable.

2. Convex, data-dependent paradigm selection via the radius–margin bound. Because the map is explicit, the radius R_β of the smallest sphere enclosing {φ_β(x_i)} and the margin (‖w_β‖) are directly computable in closed/low-cost form for any β. Use Vapnik's Theorem 5.1 to form the capacity proxy R_β²·‖w_β‖² and select β (and the regularization constant C) by minimizing this radius–margin bound over a 1-D grid / gradient search — each inner solve being a standard convex SVM QP. This turns CBP's non-convex, optimizer-dependent paradigm plasticity into a convex, SRM-justified model-selection procedure: the data themselves determine, through the tightest generalization bound, whether a hyperplane or a hypersphere (and everything between) is warranted. Crucially, unlike the Gaussian kernel, the circular map adds only one dimension, so the enclosing radius and effective capacity increase minimally — potentially yielding tighter bounds and far fewer support vectors than Gaussian-kernel SVMs for problems whose class structure is (approximately) spherical.

3. A fast closed-form variant. Provide a least-squares/dual-ridge counterpart: solve kernel ridge regression in dual variables (Saunders et al.) with the circular kernel k_β, giving a closed-form (K + λI)⁻¹y solution and enabling the same radius–margin-based selection of β and λ without a QP. This trades the QP's sparsity for O-closed-form speed, letting us empirically characterize the sparsity-vs-scalability tradeoff of the two learners on the same circular representation.

4. Evaluation. Reproduce the USPS handwritten-digit protocol of the SVM-vs-RBF study. Compare C-SVM (QP and dual-ridge variants) against (i) linear SVM, (ii) Gaussian-kernel SVM, (iii) classical RBF networks, and (iv) backprop-trained CBP/MLP, on: test error; number of support vectors / free parameters; the selected β (does capacity-minimization recover a surface-based vs prototype-based rule matching the data?); and the correlation between the computed radius–margin bound and observed test error (does the bound track generalization and thus validate automatic paradigm selection?). Ablate β selection (fixed vs bound-minimizing vs cross-validated) to isolate the value of SLT-driven paradigm plasticity.

This directly fills the gap: it imports SVM/SLT capacity control and sparsity into CBP's minimal, interpretable circular representation, and it makes CBP's paradigm plasticity convex and theoretically grounded via the radius–margin bound, while offering a closed-form dual-ridge alternative for scale.

#### [method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 25 turns / 33 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works split learning machines into prototype-based (Gaussian-kernel SVM, RBF networks, dual-variable kernel ridge regression) and surface-based (perceptron, MLP) paradigms, each controlling capacity with a different measure used in isolation: the SV function-estimation work uses a margin-radius VC bound for model selection; the circular backpropagation paper derives VC dimension and Cover capacity for a model unifying both paradigms through one circular coefficient; the SVM-vs-RBF study shows SV auto-selecting RBF centers and beating clustering-based RBFs but only on one dataset. No study empirically maps how these theory-derived capacity measures relate to actual generalization across the family, nor diagnoses when prototype- versus surface-based representation wins as a function of data geometry. This matters because capacity control is statistical learning theory's central promise yet its bounds are loose, paradigm choice is made by fiat, and the reliability of VC-bound structural risk minimization versus cross-validation is uncharted. Circular backpropagation uniquely enables a controlled sweep of the paradigm spectrum while SV and ridge tools supply computable capacity estimates.

**Method.** Benchmark study. Use circular backpropagation whose circular coefficient smoothly interpolates surface-based to prototype-based behavior, with Gaussian-kernel SVM, k-means RBF, the SV-centers/backprop hybrid, and dual-variable kernel ridge regression as anchors. Evaluate across a grid of synthetic datasets varying dimensionality, sample size, label noise, and class geometry, plus real handwritten digits. For each model-dataset cell, compute the source-defined capacity estimates (margin-radius VC bound and SV model-selection functional, circular-BP VC dimension and Cover capacity, support-vector or prototype counts, effective ridge regularization) together with measured train and test error and the generalization gap. Produce three empirical maps: a bound-tightness map regressing generalization gap on each capacity estimate to reveal where bounds track reality or invert; a paradigm phase diagram over data-property axes giving the optimal circular coefficient and the winning machine; and a model-selection reliability map comparing VC-bound structural risk minimization against cross-validation. Deliver a reproducible protocol plus these maps as diagnostics for which capacity handle to trust, when to prefer each paradigm, and when VC-bound selection can replace cross-validation.

#### [method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two research lineages in these sources describe the same geometric fact from opposite ends but never meet formally. (1) Ridella et al.'s Circular Backpropagation (CBP) network augments a multilayer perceptron with a single extra input equal to the sum of squared inputs (‖x‖²), turning hyperplane discriminants into hyperspherical ones. This gives the network "paradigm plasticity": a single learned coefficient continuously interpolates between the surface-based paradigm (open half-space boundary) and the prototype-based paradigm (bounded ball around a center/radius, like RBF). But CBP is trained by plain backpropagation (empirical risk minimization); its capacity control is only a worst-case hidden-unit / VC-dimension count, and the plasticity coefficient is fit with no principled regularization or margin. (2) Vapnik's Support Vector method and Schölkopf et al.'s SVM-vs-RBF study obtain capacity control through Structural Risk Minimization: for canonical hyperplanes in a feature space, Theorem 5.1 bounds the VC dimension by min(⌈R²A²⌉, n)+1 (R = radius of the smallest enclosing sphere of the mapped data, A = weight-norm ≈ 1/margin), and Schölkopf shows an SV-RBF that automatically selects centers/weights beats clustering+backprop RBF on USPS. Yet in the SVM framework the "paradigm" (linear vs. localized-prototype) is fixed a priori by the kernel choice; the Gaussian kernel is infinite-dimensional and its center/radius geometry is only implicit, and there is no explicit, minimal-dimensional, interpretable lift that lets the paradigm itself be selected by SRM. The gap: no formal object connects CBP's interpretable, learnable surface↔prototype plasticity to margin-based statistical learning theory, and no theorem quantifies how the plasticity coefficient trades capacity (R) against margin. Closing this gap matters because it would (a) explain when the extra ‖x‖² feature actually helps, (b) replace CBP's uncontrolled backprop fitting of the plasticity term with a principled SRM rule, and (c) give SVMs a cheap, finite-dimensional, geometrically interpretable route between linear and prototype classifiers — exactly the regime (few high-dimensional samples) Vapnik emphasizes.

**Method.** Develop a formal, margin-theoretic unification of the surface-based and prototype-based paradigms via an explicit one-parameter "spherical lift," and derive from it an SRM principle for paradigm selection.

1. Formal model (the circular lift and its kernel). Define φ_λ(x) = (x, (λ/2)‖x‖²) ∈ R^{n+1}, giving the kernel K_λ(x,x') = ⟨x,x'⟩ + (λ²/4)‖x‖²‖x'‖². Prove K_λ is positive definite (sum of the linear kernel and a rank-structured product-of-norms kernel), hence usable in any dual SVM/dual-ridge solver. A linear classifier ⟨v,φ_λ(x)⟩+b with v=(w,β) has input-space boundary c‖x‖²+w·x+b=0 with c=λβ/2.

2. Equivalence theorem (conceptual distinction, ERM vs SRM). Prove that the decision function of a max-margin SVM using K_λ is exactly a CBP circular unit, and give the explicit bijection between the SVM dual solution and CBP's "circular parameters": center = −w/(2c), radius² = ‖w‖²/(4c²) − b/c, gain = c. Establish the conceptual distinction geometrically: c=0 ⇒ unbounded (surface) decision region; c≠0 ⇒ bounded ball (prototype) region. This positions CBP-backprop as the empirical-risk instance and the proposed "Support Vector Circular Machine" (SVCM) as the maximal-margin/SRM completion of the same model family.

3. Invariance corollary (when plasticity matters). Prove that if data are norm-normalized (‖x‖ constant) the quadratic term is constant and φ_λ collapses to the linear model, so surface and prototype paradigms coincide; therefore the extra representational capacity is governed precisely by the spread of squared norms. Formalize a scalar "plasticity budget" tied to Var(‖x‖²), predicting exactly when the augmentation can and cannot help.

4. Capacity derivation and the SRM paradigm-selection rule. Note that φ_λ maps to R^{n+1}, so the unconstrained VC dimension is n+2 for every λ — the augmentation cannot cause dimensional blow-up (unlike full quadratic/polynomial lifts CBP warns about). Then apply Vapnik's radius-margin bound in feature space: h_λ ≤ min(⌈R_λ² A_λ²⌉, n+1)+1, where R_λ is the radius of the smallest sphere enclosing {φ_λ(x_i)} (computable by the enclosing-sphere QP given in the SV-function-estimation source) and A_λ = 1/γ_λ with γ_λ the SVM margin. Derive the one-parameter structure S_λ and the SRM objective λ* = argmin_λ R_λ²/γ_λ². Analyze this objective's behavior in λ (limits λ→0 recovering the linear SVM bound; growth of R_λ vs. gain in γ_λ) and give an envelope-theorem expression for its derivative so λ* can be found by a cheap 1-D outer search around repeated SVM solves.

5. Regression extension. Instantiate the same lift in dual (kernel) ridge regression to obtain a spherical/circular regressor, with the identical R_λ²/γ_λ²-style bound guiding selection of λ, connecting the classification and function-estimation views.

6. Validation plan. Empirically compare CBP-backprop, linear SVM, Gaussian-kernel SVM, and the derived SVCM (with SRM-selected λ) on the USPS-style and UCI datasets used in the source papers, measuring test error, number of support vectors/centers, and the tightness of the derived R_λ²/γ_λ² bound as a predictor of the empirically best λ. Success criteria: (a) the derived λ* tracks the empirically optimal paradigm; (b) SVCM matches or beats CBP where norm-spread is high and reduces to the linear SVM where it is low; (c) interpretable center/radius parameters are recovered via the equivalence theorem.

#### [method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** SVMs solve a convex QP that minimizes a test-error bound, but the outer hyperparameters (Gaussian width gamma, C, and epsilon for regression) are not set by the QP and are still chosen by heuristics or costly cross-validation grids (Scholkopf et al.; Vapnik). The theory offers cheap surrogates rarely used for search: the support-vector-count bound and a closed-form leave-one-out kernel bound from one trained model (Zhang). This leaves room to replace grid search with a principled, efficient search driven by these bounds and the smooth solution path.

**Method.** Propose SAAHS, a search framework selecting theta=(C, gamma, epsilon, kernel) by adaptively minimizing a theory surrogate. Surrogate: train the QP once per theta and compute S from that solve, the support-vector fraction plus the closed-form leave-one-out kernel bound, with an epsilon-insensitive analogue for regression, so each evaluation is one solve not k-fold CV. Adaptive search: model S over a log-scaled box with a response surface and pick the next theta by expected improvement. Efficiency: warm-start each QP from the nearest solved model along the C and gamma path and screen non-support-vectors using factorization updates, so re-solves are cheap. Multi-fidelity: rank candidates by cheap S, then run true CV only on top survivors and recalibrate. Evaluate on digit and benchmark tasks versus grid CV and fixed-width heuristics, targeting equal accuracy at far fewer solves.

#### [method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 12 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two lineages appear in these sources. (1) The Circular Backpropagation (CBP) network of Ridella et al. shows that augmenting the input of a linear unit with a single extra feature — the squared norm ||x||² — lets one unit represent both hyperplane (surface-based) and hyperspherical/prototype (RBF-like) decision boundaries, "unifying" the two paradigms while raising the VC dimension only minimally (from d+1 to d+2). (2) The statistical-learning/SVM lineage (Vapnik; SV method of function estimation; ridge regression in dual variables; SVM-vs-RBF comparison) provides margin/SRM-based capacity control and dual (kernel) training that extend cleanly to regression and to automatic center selection.

Each lineage leaves a concrete gap that the other could fill. CBP is (a) restricted to classification, (b) trained by non-convex backpropagation, so its central promise — "paradigm plasticity," i.e. choosing where to sit between hyperplane and prototype — is left to gradient descent with local minima and no capacity guarantee; (c) applied only to the raw input space with one fixed quadratic term, and never given a principled model-selection rule despite its clean VC-dimension analysis. Conversely, the SVM comparison paper shows Gaussian-kernel SVMs win but require choosing a width and correspond to an essentially unbounded VC dimension; there is no interpretable, finely graded family of function classes whose capacity grows in small, provable increments. This matters precisely in noisy, small-sample regimes, where an over-powerful kernel overfits and where one wants the smallest capacity increase over a linear model that still captures spherical/cluster structure. No source combines CBP's minimal, interpretable capacity increment with convex SV training, regression, and SRM-based selection.

**Method.** Relax and broaden CBP by recasting its circular augmentation as a "circular kernel" inside the convex support-vector / dual-ridge framework, turning it from a single non-convex classifier into a general, noise-tolerant estimator with explicit capacity control.

1) Circular feature map and kernel. Define the augmentation φ_λ(x) = (x, √λ·||x||²) and the induced kernel k_λ(x,x') = ⟨x,x'⟩ + λ·||x||²||x'||², with a scalar λ≥0 that continuously interpolates between the pure linear machine (λ=0, VC = d+1) and the spherical machine (λ>0, VC = d+2). Because the augmentation is just one extra coordinate, the resulting SVM/ridge solution is a hyperplane in feature space, i.e. a hypersphere/quadric of the restricted CBP form, but now obtained by convex optimization with a global optimum — removing CBP's local-minima dependence.

2) Broaden the loss/task. Instantiate the circular kernel in (a) the maximal-margin SVM for classification, (b) the ε-insensitive SV method for regression, and (c) dual ridge regression. This extends CBP — defined only for classification via backprop — to noisy regression and to the robust, margin-based setting, so the "paradigm plasticity" survives under label noise and outliers.

3) SRM ladder with provable increments. Replace CBP's gradient-driven paradigm choice with Structural Risk Minimization over a nested structure. Build the structure two ways: along λ (linear ⊂ circular), and greedily by adding spherical features anchored at candidate centers, each addition provably increasing VC dimension by one. Select λ, the margin/regularization, and the number of spheres by minimizing a VC-dimension-based generalization bound (or cross-validation as a practical surrogate). This gives an interpretable capacity ladder with minimal increments, in contrast to the width-tuned, high-capacity Gaussian kernel.

4) Kernelized generalization (weaker-assumption scope). Generalize the augmentation to any base kernel k by defining k̃(x,x') = k(x,x') + λ·k(x,x)·k(x',x'), i.e. augment an arbitrary RKHS with its own norm feature. This adds "spherical" capability to any kernel machine at the cost of only +1 VC dimension, letting practitioners take a proven linear or low-degree model and grant it minimal prototype-style capacity without moving to an unbounded-VC kernel.

5) Evaluation. Compare circular-kernel SVM/ridge against (i) CBP trained by backprop, (ii) plain MLP, (iii) classical RBF with clustered centers, and (iv) Gaussian-kernel SVM, on the USPS digit task from the comparison paper plus controlled synthetic classification/regression benchmarks with injected label noise and small sample sizes. Key hypotheses: the circular kernel matches or beats CBP and RBF while giving convex training and, in high-noise/small-sample regimes, generalizes better than the Gaussian-kernel SVM at far lower capacity; the SRM ladder recovers the correct surface-vs-prototype regime automatically and yields interpretable center/radius parameters via the CBP weight-to-circular-parameter transformation. This directly addresses the gap: CBP's minimal, interpretable capacity increment now operates under weaker assumptions (convex, noisy, regression, arbitrary base kernels) with principled SRM capacity control.

#### [method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The support vector framework unifies classification, RBF, and regression under Structural Risk Minimization (Vapnik 1998, 1995), and dual-variable ridge regression (Saunders 1998) and the SVM-vs-RBF comparison (1997) show kernel methods generalize well. But all fix the loss shape and its parameters in advance, coupled to an assumed noise model. Vapnik states this: squared loss (dual ridge) is optimal only for Gaussian noise; the epsilon-insensitive linear loss is Huber's minimax choice under noise uncertainty; Huber's smooth loss is robust only for a contamination mixture whose proportion, and hence its transition point, must be known in advance. In practice noise is unknown, often heavy-tailed, heteroscedastic, or contaminated by outliers and mislabels. Under such misspecification these methods are brittle: squared loss gives outliers unbounded quadratic influence and no sparsity; even the epsilon-insensitive loss grows without bound in the residual, so a few outliers dominate the fit and corrupt the support vector set; and a single global epsilon mis-sizes the tube under heteroscedastic noise. None of the provided works give a data-driven way to adapt the loss and epsilon to unknown noise while keeping capacity control and sparsity. That is the gap.

**Method.** Propose Robust Adaptive-Loss Support Vector Regression, a robustification of the SV and kernel-ridge family with three coupled parts. First, a bounded redescending insensitive loss that keeps an epsilon dead zone for sparsity, transitions Huber-like for moderate residuals, and saturates beyond a cutoff so gross outliers get near-zero influence, a regime none of the three losses Vapnik lists provides; its shape parameters interpolate between quadratic, epsilon-insensitive, Huber, and redescending. Second, data-driven locally adaptive epsilon and cutoff, set as multiples of a robust noise scale (median absolute deviation) estimated in kernel-weighted local neighborhoods, removing the homoscedastic global epsilon and the requirement of a known contamination proportion. Third, iteratively reweighted quadratic programming: because the loss is non-convex, solve a sequence of standard box-constrained SV dual QPs (the fast solver Vapnik describes), where per-example weights from the linearized loss drive tail outliers to near-zero weight; initialize from an L1 or epsilon-insensitive fit to avoid poor optima. Capacity stays controlled by the norm of w under SRM, and the regularization constant plus loss-shape and scale multipliers are chosen by cross-validation against the SRM risk bound, retaining sparsity and generalization guarantees. Evaluate robustness explicitly with the paper set baselines: regression under Gaussian, heavy-tailed, asymmetric, heteroscedastic, and outlier-contaminated noise, measuring error, empirical breakdown point, support vector set stability, and sparsity, versus dual ridge, fixed-epsilon SVR, and fixed-Huber SVR; USPS digit classification with injected label noise versus fixed-loss SVM and RBF; and an ablation isolating each component. Success means matching the best fixed-loss method when its noise assumption holds while sharply reducing degradation and preventing support vector corruption under contaminated, heavy-tailed, or heteroscedastic noise.

#### [method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** CBP networks (Ridella 1997) unify surface-based and prototype-based classifiers via a squared-norm augmented input and a plasticity coefficient, but train by backprop with no capacity control. SVM and kernel methods (Vapnik; dual ridge regression) give margin-based SRM and RBF prototypes but pick the surface-vs-prototype kernel a priori. No work bridges the CBP learnable continuum with kernel SRM.

**Method.** Show the CBP squared-norm augmentation is exactly a Mercer kernel equal to the inner product plus the product of squared norms. Define a one-parameter kernel homotopy from linear through circular to Gaussian RBF generalizing CBP plasticity. Solve in the SVM and dual-ridge framework so prototypes and weights follow from margin maximization. Select the homotopy parameter by minimizing the radius-margin VC bound. Map solutions back to CBP geometry and to VC and Cover capacity analyses. Validate on USPS digits versus linear SVM, RBF SVM, CBP-backprop, and RBF-backprop.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 21 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** The Circular Backpropagation (CBP) network augments a perceptron with one extra input equal to the sum of squared inputs, giving paradigm plasticity between a hyperplane (MLP) and a prototype sphere (RBF), trainable by backprop and endowed with Cover-capacity and VC-dimension theorems, but fit by empirical-risk backprop with no margin and only heuristic capacity control. Separately, statistical learning theory (Vapnik SRM, Gaussian-kernel SVMs, dual-variable ridge regression) gives convex training, margin-based SRM, guaranteed-risk bounds, and top USPS accuracy, but needs a fixed hand-chosen kernel, learns no features, and is opaque. These communities are disconnected. Yet the sum-of-squares augmentation of a circular unit is exactly the feature map that turns a hyperplane into a sphere, so CBP is latently an adaptive kernel machine. No prior work connects CBP paradigm-plastic feature maps to SVM and dual-ridge margin machinery, nor uses CBP VC and Cover formulas inside SRM for model selection. Bridging them yields a learner feature-adaptive and interpretable like a neural net yet margin-regularized with principled capacity control like an SVM, addressing fixed kernels and opacity for SVMs and unprincipled capacity for CBP.

**Method.** Build PlasticKernel, a toolkit whose central contribution is the artifact: a paradigm-plastic SVM fusing CBP feature learning with dual margin-based learning. It has a bank of J learnable circular units, each computing a CBP stimulus that interpolates between a hyperplane and a prototype sphere via a learned radial coefficient; their activations form a trainable feature map whose Gram matrix is an adaptive kernel. The output layer is trained by a convex dual solver, either a soft-margin SVM quadratic program or dual-variable ridge regression. Training alternates freezing circular units to solve the convex dual for outer weights, then taking gradient steps on circular-unit prototypes, radii, and gains to widen the margin. Model complexity is chosen by inserting CBP Cover-capacity and VC-dimension formulas into Vapnik guaranteed-risk bounds and minimizing them. An interpretability read-out labels each unit surface or prototype and reports centers, radii, and support vectors. It is evaluated on USPS digits against classical clustered RBF, Gaussian-kernel SVM, the SV-centers-plus-backprop hybrid, and plain CBP or MLP, with ablations on fixed versus adaptive kernel, backprop versus dual solver, and cross-validation versus VC-bound selection.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Bridge opportunity between two late-1990s literatures that both invoke Vapnik's theory but never connect their machinery. Stream A: the neural circular-unit / Circular Backpropagation (CBP) work (Ridella et al. 1997), which augments the MLP with one extra feature, the squared norm of x, so each unit represents a hypersphere. This yields an explicit, low-dimensional, interpretable, differentiable feature map unifying surface-based (hyperplane) and prototype-based (RBF/spherical) paradigms, with derived VC-dimension and Cover-capacity formulas. But CBP is trained by ordinary backprop, with capacity controlled only crudely by the number of hidden units; its capacity results are post-hoc analysis, not a training objective, and there is no margin or SRM criterion. Stream B: the kernel/SVM/kernel-ridge community (Vapnik 1995/1998; Scholkopf-Burges-Vapnik RBF-vs-SVM; Saunders dual ridge regression), offering convex, dual-variable, margin-maximizing fits that minimize a VC bound (SRM), but with feature geometry fixed a priori: the kernel shape is chosen in advance and RBF-SVM centers are restricted to training points. Gap: CBP has adaptive, interpretable spherical geometry but a weak, non-convex training principle; SVM/kernel-ridge have a principled convex SRM fit but frozen geometry with data-point centers. No work marries CBP's trainable circular feature map with kernel methods' convex, VC-bound-driven estimation, even though CBP already supplies the exact VC/Cover-capacity terms an SRM objective needs. Bridging them could yield classifiers with few adaptive, interpretable spherical prototypes fit under convex capacity control with real generalization guarantees.

**Method.** Adaptive Circular Kernel Machine (ACKM): use CBP circular units as a trainable feature map, fit with kernel-ridge/SVM convex machinery, and select capacity via CBP's own formulas. Step 1, explicit circular kernel: the augmentation z(x) built from 1, x, and the squared norm of x is a finite-dimensional feature space where every linear separator is a hypersphere or hyperplane; define K_circ as the inner product of z(x) and z(y). Dual-variable ridge regression on K_circ gives a convex, closed-form single-sphere classifier as the minimal bridge instance. Step 2, bank of learnable prototypes: K circular units with learnable centers, radii, and gains produce features phi_k(x). Unlike RBF-SVM, centers are free continuous parameters not tied to training points, typically far fewer than support vectors, each interpretable as a sphere or, when the quadratic coefficient vanishes, a hyperplane (CBP paradigm plasticity). Step 3, convex inner fit with SRM: given features, solve the read-out layer by dual-variable ridge regression or a margin-maximizing quadratic program (globally solvable); set the regularization strength and K by Structural Risk Minimization, plugging CBP's VC-dimension and Cover-capacity expressions in as the explicit complexity term, turning CBP's post-hoc analysis into active model selection the kernel literature lacks for this geometry. Step 4, alternating optimization: first freeze circular parameters and solve the convex weight problem in closed form or QP, then backpropagate the resulting margin/ridge loss into centers, radii, and gains via gradient descent, combining CBP representation learning with SVM/kernel-ridge estimation. Step 5, evaluation: reproduce the RBF-vs-SVM setup on USPS digits and standard benchmarks, comparing ACKM to classical RBF (k-means plus backprop), RBF-SVM, the SVM/backprop hybrid, and plain CBP. Metrics: accuracy; number of prototypes versus support vectors (compactness); interpretability of learned spheres; and how tightly the SRM/VC bound predicts test error, testing whether importing CBP's capacity formulas yields useful model selection.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two communities in these works pursue the same goal—flexible decision surfaces with controlled capacity—but stay disconnected. The neural-network tradition (Circular Backpropagation, Ridella et al. 1997; with Bishop 1995, Haykin 1994, Zurada 1992, Cherkassky 1998) augments a perceptron with one ||x||^2 term whose learnable coefficient continuously interpolates between a hyperplane (surface-based) and a sphere/prototype (RBF), trained by backprop and analyzed via VC dimension and Cover capacity. The statistical-learning/kernel tradition (Vapnik 1995/1998; kernel ridge regression, Saunders 1998; SVM-vs-RBF, Schölkopf 1997) achieves flexibility through implicit Mercer kernels and controls capacity via margin/SRM, with sparsity as support-vector count. The unexploited bridge: circular backprop's ||x||^2 augmentation is the minimal explicit feature map producing exactly the spherical geometry a Gaussian kernel produces implicitly. Both claim to unify polynomial/NN/RBF families, but along different axes and with incompatible capacity vocabularies (VC/Cover vs margin/SRM). Existing comparisons are narrow—Schölkopf compares three RBF variants on one dataset by accuracy alone; circular backprop gives theory plus small tests. No work connects explicit low-order augmentation to implicit kernels, translates the two capacity notions into comparable measurements, or tells practitioners when a cheap explicit augmentation matches an expensive kernel. This matters for obtaining kernel-like nonlinearity at perceptron-like cost and for giving both communities a shared empirical language.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map (a phase diagram) that places NN and kernel/SLT machines on one grid and measures them with common diagnostics beyond accuracy. Axis A, representation richness from explicit to implicit: linear perceptron; circular-backprop perceptron (one ||x||^2 term); explicit low-order polynomial augmentation; RBF net with k-means centers; Gaussian-kernel SVM; dual kernel ridge regression. Axis B, capacity-control principle: weight decay/early stopping, margin/SRM, Tikhonov ridge; where possible fit the same representation under each principle to separate representation effects from control effects. Diagnostics per machine-by-dataset cell: (1) test error and sample-size learning curves; (2) comparable capacity measures—empirical VC/Cover estimates via random-label memorization curves, margin distributions, and count of active units/support vectors/effective degrees of freedom; (3) decision-surface geometry—local curvature, number of boundary components, and fraction of boundary that is locally spherical, testing whether ||x||^2 augmentation reproduces the Gaussian kernel's geometry; (4) hyperparameter sensitivity surfaces. Protocol: use the USPS digit database (anchoring to Schölkopf) plus controlled synthetic 2-D problems with known boundaries for exact geometry metrics; include the SV-centers-plus-backprop-weights hybrid as a bridging control point; implement dual solves and stable capacity/margin computations using standard linear algebra (Cholesky/QR) and optimization routines from the corpus. Analyses addressing the gap: (a) overlay explicit-augmentation and kernel machines in a shared (capacity, geometry, error) space to find the regime—dimension, sample size, curvature—where the one-parameter circular augmentation matches Gaussian-kernel SVM at far smaller model size; (b) factor representation versus capacity control by fitting identical features under weight decay, margin, and ridge, quantifying how much SVM advantage is the kernel versus the SRM principle; (c) release the map, diagnostic code, and shared capacity/geometry metrics as a reusable comparative study translating VC/Cover and margin/SRM into the same measurable quantities, concretely linking the two literatures.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 25 turns / 32 tool calls

**Motivation.** CBP's squared-norm augmentation and Vapnik's enclosing-sphere radius in the SVM radius-margin bound are the same circular object, but the neural-network and statistical-learning literatures never connect them; bridging gives CBP capacity control and SVMs an exact VC characterization.

**Method.** Define the circular kernel k(x,y)=inner(x,y)+lambda*norm(x)^2*norm(y)^2, prove a linear separator in it is a hypersphere and that Vapnik's enclosing sphere is linear there, then derive an exact-VC (n+2) SRM bound over lambda solved by dual kernel ridge regression.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** A bridge opportunity connects two isolated streams. Circular Backpropagation (Ridella et al., 1997) shows appending a norm-squared feature lets a linear/MLP machine slide continuously between surface-based (hyperplane/polynomial) and prototype-based (RBF) decision surfaces via one scalar paradigm-plasticity coefficient, but is trained by non-convex backprop with no capacity control. The kernel/SLT stream (Vapnik 1998 SV method; Scholkopf et al. 1997 SVM-vs-RBF; Saunders et al. 1998 dual ridge regression) gives convex training and radius-margin VC capacity control, but fixes the kernel family a priori. Since the RBF kernel is built from the same norm-squared term underlying CBP plasticity, the two are naturally linkable yet never linked. Gap: no method lets data choose the surface-to-prototype operating point while retaining convex training and VC-bound capacity control. This matters because kernel and hyperparameter choice is the dominant practical bottleneck.

**Method.** Build a searchable kernel continuum and pick the operating point by optimizing a VC bound. Step 1: define a low-dimensional kernel family K_theta interpolating from a dot-product/polynomial kernel (theta small, hyperplane paradigm) to a Gaussian RBF kernel (theta large, prototype paradigm), inspired by CBP norm-squared augmentation; theta is a continuous plasticity knob inside a positive-definite kernel. Step 2: for fixed theta and regularization C, solve convexly in dual variables via kernel ridge regression or the SV method, giving a global optimum per candidate representation. Step 3 (optimization contribution): select models by minimizing the radius-margin bound (radius-squared times weight-norm-squared, both kernel-computable) rather than blind cross-validation; search over theta and C with coarse screening then gradient/coordinate refinement, using Cholesky low-rank updates and warm starts so re-solving as theta shifts is cheap. Step 4: allow class/region-specific theta and support-vector-focused active search. Step 5: evaluate on USPS digits and UCI sets against fixed polynomial SVM, fixed RBF SVM, CBP, and tuned kernel ridge regression, reporting accuracy, selected theta, bound-vs-test-error correlation, and cost. This delivers one machine that reproduces CBP automatic paradigm selection, trains convexly, and chooses its representation by minimizing a VC bound, bridging the neural-network and kernel-SRM literatures through efficient optimization over a unifying kernel continuum.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two communities have independently attacked the same problem — building flexible classifiers that can behave either as surface-based (hyperplane) or prototype-based (spherical/RBF) machines — but their key results have never been connected.

(1) The neural-network / mapping-network community (Ridella, Rovetta & Zunino's Circular Backpropagation, and the broader MLP/RBF literature) discovered a remarkably cheap unification: augmenting a perceptron's input with the single extra feature ||x||² gives the "circular unit," a model with "paradigm plasticity" — a single, backprop-trainable network whose learned parameters decide, per unit, whether to act as a hyperplane (surface paradigm) or as a hypersphere/prototype (prototype paradigm). Its virtues are interpretability, minimal parameter increase, and reuse of standard MLP training/hardware. Its weakness: it is trained by empirical risk minimization (plain backprop).
