In practice noise magnitude is unknown and varies across the input space. A wrong global ε silently biases the fit; there is no mechanism for input-dependent noise.

2) Sensitivity to outliers / contamination. Kernel/dual ridge uses a squared loss, which the same texts note is the ML estimator under an assumed Gaussian noise density and is not robust — Vapnik explicitly recalls Huber's critique that one rarely knows the true noise density, and the statistical-learning-theory source stresses that ML is "not a robust rare-event estimator." A few contaminated labels can dominate the squared-loss solution.

3) Unreliable uncertainty. The statistical-learning-theory source states plainly that "an SVM classifier tends to give unreliable information on the confidence of its prediction," yet such confidence "can be crucial." Neither SVR nor dual-ridge outputs calibrated, input-dependent predictive uncertainty.

Thus the gap is a failure/risk gap: these estimators assume a known, homoscedastic, outlier-free noise model, and when that assumption breaks (the common case) they degrade silently, without robustness guarantees or trustworthy uncertainty. The listed works leave this open because each fixes its loss/noise assumption a priori (SVR: one global ε; ridge: Gaussian squared loss) and selects hyperparameters by procedures (clustering, cross-validation) that presume clean structure.

**Method.** Proposal: a unified, noise-adaptive robust kernel regression framework that relaxes the fixed-ε, homoscedastic, outlier-free assumptions of SVR and dual-ridge regression to the broader regime of unknown, input-dependent (heteroscedastic), heavy-tailed and contaminated noise, while adding calibrated uncertainty — all reusing the existing dual (kernel) machinery.

(1) A locally parametrized loss family. Generalize Vapnik's three losses (linear-insensitive, quadratic-insensitive, Huber) into one loss whose insensitivity width ε(x) and transition scale σ(x) vary with the input, and which is quadratic (statistically efficient) near clean regions and linear/redescending (robust) beyond a data-driven residual quantile. Setting ε(x)≡ε recovers SVR; setting ε=0 with a quadratic core recovers dual-ridge. This is a strict scope extension of the prior loss functions.

(2) An alternating estimation scheme (Huber-minimax relaxation). Instead of assuming one known noise density, alternate: (a) given current per-point scales, solve a weighted dual problem — either the SVR QP with per-point C_i and ε_i, or the closed-form dual-ridge system with per-point weights — for the regression function f; (b) given residuals r_i = y_i − f(x_i), re-estimate the local noise scale σ(x) and insensitivity ε(x) by smoothing |r_i| with a second "noise kernel" (a separate RBF), and downweight points whose |r_i| exceeds a robust quantile (redescending influence), giving a finite breakdown point that squared-loss ridge lacks. Iterate to convergence (an IRLS-style fixed point).

(3) Capacity control by SRM, not clean CV. Because adaptivity can inflate complexity, tie the effective regularization and the noise-kernel bandwidth to the VC/structural-risk bound emphasized in the statistical-learning-theory sources, selecting them to minimize a robust upper bound on risk rather than a validation error that itself is corrupted by outliers.

(4) Calibrated predictive intervals. Emit prediction intervals directly from the estimated local scale σ(x), converting point estimators into uncertainty-aware ones and directly addressing the noted "unreliable confidence" limitation.

Evaluation: on regression benchmarks and the USPS-style digit data used in the SVM-vs-RBF study, inject (i) heteroscedastic noise, (ii) heavy-tailed noise, and (iii) label contamination/outliers at varying rates, and compare against plain SVR (fixed ε, C) and dual-ridge on accuracy, breakdown behavior as contamination grows, and interval calibration (coverage vs. nominal). Ablate the local-vs-global ε and the outlier reweighting.

How it addresses the gap: it makes the prior kernel estimators operate under weaker, more realistic assumptions — unknown and spatially varying noise with outliers — while preserving their dual/kernel solvability and SRM capacity control, and it supplies the robustness (bounded outlier influence) and reliability (calibrated, input-dependent uncertainty) that SVR and dual-ridge lack.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 20 turns / 33 tool calls

**Motivation.** Kernel and neural machines in these works are accurate but risky. They are brittle to hyperparameters (Gaussian width, regularization, epsilon are tuned ad hoc, hurting reliability and reproducibility), noise-sensitive (quadratic and hinge losses are outlier-prone, robust losses treated in isolation, least-squares poor for rare events), and silently over-confident (point predictions with no calibrated uncertainty or abstention; the support-vector set's robustness is only empirical). The sources supply the ingredients but never combine them into a certifiably reliable machine.

**Method.** A Risk-Bounded Robust Kernel Machine: (1) robust reweighted dual training with a bounded Huber-ized epsilon-insensitive loss, flagging and down-weighting points that saturate alpha=C as outliers; (2) reproducible capacity-aware selection of width, regularization, epsilon by minimizing an SRM/VC penalty plus the closed-form leave-one-out kernel bound, giving certified risk; (3) cross-paradigm agreement for calibrated uncertainty using an ensemble of prototype-based RBF-SVM and surface-based polynomial/CBP-MLP with a common-support-vector diagnostic; (4) selective prediction that abstains when certified risk exceeds a threshold. Evaluate on USPS digits with injected noise, measuring accuracy-vs-noise, hyperparameter sensitivity, calibration, and risk-coverage against SVM, RBF, kernel ridge, and CBP baselines.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** Failure/Risk gap in per-instance reliability. The kernel and neural machines in these works (SVMs with Gaussian kernels, RBF classifiers, dual kernel ridge regression, the SV method of function estimation, and MLP/circular-backpropagation networks) all emit point predictions with no trustworthy per-input reliability, causing brittleness: confidently wrong outputs on out-of-distribution or ambiguous inputs, and ad hoc abstention. The SVM-vs-RBF paper takes reject decisions from the maximal output before the sgn function (margin magnitude only), ignoring whether the input is supported by training data. Statistical Learning Theory's SRM yields only global, worst-case, distribution-free confidence intervals dominated by the worst function, too loose to certify a single prediction; kernel ridge regression and RBF regressors give no uncertainty. Two complementary reliability signals exist across these literatures but are never combined: a surface-based signal (margin |f(x)|) and a prototype-based signal (proximity to and density of training data and support vectors). A large-margin point far from all data is dangerously overconfident under margin-only scoring, while a point near data but near the boundary is genuinely ambiguous. No source reconciles these into a calibrated, theoretically grounded per-instance reliability estimate with a principled reject option.

**Method.** Build a unified per-instance reliability estimator bridging three strands in the sources: Vapnik's local risk minimization with vicinity functions K(x,x0;beta) and its joint SRM control of empirical risk, capacity and locality; the surface-based margin of kernel/SV machines (SVM, SVR, kernel ridge); and the prototype-based representation of the circular-backpropagation generalized neural unit, where the SVM feature-space hyperplane and the RBF prototype network are limiting cases and the circular coefficient interpolates between them. Steps: (1) express any machine (SVM, SVR, kernel ridge, RBF, circular-BP MLP) in the single generalized-unit formalism so one kernel defines both f(x) and geometry; (2) for a test point x compute a surface term from margin |f(x)| and a support term as a vicinity-weighted density of training/support vectors around x, using the model's own kernel as the vicinity function; (3) instantiate Vapnik's local risk inequality to obtain a per-instance guaranteed-risk bound conditional on x, namely vicinity-weighted local empirical risk plus a local capacity term, minimized jointly over empirical risk, VC term and locality beta, replacing the loose global SRM interval; (4) combine surface and support terms through this local bound into a calibrated reliability score R(x) and derive a reject threshold that provably controls conditional risk on accepted inputs, calibrating beta and the combination on held-out data and exploiting the closed-form leave-one-out of dual kernel ridge regression for efficiency; (5) apply uniformly to classification and regression. Evaluate on the USPS digit database used by the comparison paper and regression benchmarks, measuring calibration (reliability diagrams), OOD/outlier rejection and risk-coverage curves, against margin-only, density/prototype-only and global-VC-bound baselines it unifies. The synthesis makes the two reliability mechanisms mutually corrective (an input is trusted only when both far from the decision surface and well supported by data), grounded in a per-instance version of SRM rather than an untrustworthy worst-case global bound.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle/contradiction. Two lines in this corpus target the same hypothesis class (spherical/RBF-type decision surfaces) and both claim to beat classical RBF networks, yet via opposite principles. Ridella et al.'s circular backpropagation (CBP, 1997) adds exactly ONE feature, the squared norm of x, so a unit realizes a hypersphere; VC dimension rises only from n+1 to n+2, and this minimal increment of dimensionality is argued 'in many senses optimal' for generalization, trained cheaply by backprop. Vapnik's SVMs (1995/1998; Scholkopf et al. 1997) instead embrace unbounded, even infinite-dimensional kernel spaces, with a bound (expected number of support vectors over sample size) declared independent of the dimensionality of the space, governed only by margin, solved by QP with dense expansions. So the field simultaneously prescribes 'minimize added dimensionality' and 'dimensionality is irrelevant, only margin matters.' Dual ridge regression (Saunders et al. 1998) is fully dense while SV function estimation is sparse only via epsilon-insensitivity, crediting yet other mechanisms. These claims have never been reconciled under a common hypothesis class and training procedure. Which actually governs generalization of RBF-type classifiers: parsimony of an explicit feature map, or margin in a rich implicit space? This matters because it decides whether to ship small interpretable gradient-trained models or large kernel expansions.

**Method.** Build Circular Support Vector Networks (CSVN): one training framework/toolkit fusing CBP and SVM that also disentangles dimensionality from margin. (1) Explicit low-rank circular feature map phi(x) formed by concatenating x with its squared norm (the CBP augmentation). A linear separator here is exactly a hypersphere, equal to one CBP unit and a Gaussian-RBF-equivalent prototype (Ridella et al. prove this equivalence): deliberately the minimal, finite counterpart of the infinite Gaussian kernel. (2) Margin training by gradient descent, not QP: train circular units under the soft-margin objective (hinge loss plus norm regularization), Vapnik's capacity criterion, but optimized by backprop over phi(x); this injects SVM margin control into CBP's cheap training. (3) Constructive, capacity-bounded growth: start with one circular unit, iteratively add units covering margin-violating regions (each an interpretable center-plus-radius prototype); at each step compute BOTH the CBP VC/Cover-capacity estimate and the margin/support-vector bound, using them jointly as a stopping rule, yielding sparse RBF-like classifiers that are gradient-trainable and margin-regularized. (4) Unified toolkit/workflow: classical RBF, Gaussian-kernel SVM, dual ridge regression, SV regression, and CBP all become configurations over the same explicit circular feature map, selectable by loss (hinge, epsilon-insensitive, squared) and expansion size. Resolution of the gap: holding the hypothesis class (spherical) and optimizer (gradient margin) fixed while sweeping the number of explicit circular units/features, CSVN measures directly whether test error tracks added explicit dimensionality (CBP's claim) or only the achieved margin (SVM's claim), which is impossible when methods differ in both class and optimizer. Evaluate on the USPS digits used by Scholkopf et al. plus standard benchmarks against Gaussian-kernel SVM, classical RBF, and plain CBP on accuracy, model size (prototypes vs support vectors), and training cost. Beyond adjudicating the puzzle, the artifact delivers sparse, interpretable, gradient-trained RBF classifiers with statistical-learning-theory model selection, avoiding both QP scaling and CBP's lack of principled capacity control.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** A puzzle sits at the intersection of two 1997–1998 results that were never connected.

(1) Schölkopf et al. ("Comparing SVMs with Gaussian kernels to RBF classifiers") show that on USPS digits a support-vector machine beats a backprop/k-means-trained RBF network. Their explanation is structural risk minimization (SRM): the SVM maximizes margin and thereby controls the *capacity* of the function class, whereas backprop only minimizes empirical error. The SVM literature (Vapnik; "The support vector method of function estimation") states the SRM principle sharply: generalization depends on the VC dimension of the chosen function set, "regardless of the dimensionality of the space." Yet the very kernels used to realize SRM in practice — Gaussian and full degree-2 polynomial — embed the data in infinite- or O(d^2)-dimensional feature spaces and produce solutions supported on many support vectors.

(2) Ridella et al. ("Circular backpropagation networks") show the opposite extreme: adding a *single* fixed feature, the squared norm ||x||^2, upgrades a linear perceptron to a full hypersphere/quadric classifier that unifies surface-based (hyperplane) and prototype-based (RBF-like) representations. Crucially, this "circular unit" has VC dimension only d+2 versus d+1 for a plane — a near-minimal capacity increase for genuine nonlinear power, which the authors argue is "in many senses optimal."

The contradiction: the compact, provably low-VC-dimension representation that SRM theory says should generalize best is trained by Ridella et al. with plain backpropagation — pure empirical risk minimization, the exact procedure Schölkopf et al. show to be inferior and which is also prone to local minima. Meanwhile SRM is deployed through generic high/infinite-dimensional kernels that discard precisely the low-dimensional, low-VC structure that theory prizes. In short: the field applies margin control to the wrong (huge) feature space and applies the right (minimal) feature space with the wrong (ERM) training rule. Nobody married CBP's minimal circular feature to SVM's margin-based capacity control, so it is unknown whether SVM's practical advantage comes from big feature spaces or merely from margin plus the *right minimal* nonlinear feature.

**Method.** Propose the Support-Vector Circular Machine (SVCM): apply structural risk minimization directly in the compact circular feature space, resolving the ERM/SRM mismatch.

1. Circular kernel. Use the explicit map phi(x) = (x, beta*||x||^2), of dimension d+1, corresponding to the Mercer-valid "circular kernel" k(x,x') = <x,x'> + beta^2 (||x||^2)(||x'||^2) (a sum of PSD kernels). This is the CBP augmentation, but now used as an SVM kernel rather than an extra backprop input.

2. Max-margin circular classifier. Solve the soft-margin SVM quadratic program in this space. The solution is a maximum-margin hyperquadric; using Ridella et al.'s weight-to-circular-parameter map (their Table I) it reads out directly as a center, radius, and gain. Because the QP is convex, this yields a unique global optimum, removing the local-minima weakness of backprop-trained CBP while importing Vapnik's margin/SRM generalization guarantees onto a function class whose VC dimension is bounded by ~d+2.

3. Automatic paradigm selection with a guarantee. The weight the margin optimizer places on the ||x||^2 coordinate governs the representation: near zero recovers a max-margin hyperplane (surface-based), nonzero gives a sphere (prototype-based). Thus CBP's "paradigm plasticity" is now driven by margin-based capacity control instead of by unsupervised backprop dynamics.

4. Doubly-controlled capacity bound. Derive a generalization bound that takes the minimum of (a) the combinatorial VC bound using dim = d+2 from the CBP analysis and (b) Vapnik's margin bound. Use this bound to set C and beta, testing the hypothesis that the circular kernel attains SVM-level accuracy at a fraction of the effective capacity of Gaussian/degree-2 kernels.

5. Expressiveness ladder via SRM. Since one quadric is limited, define a nested family S_m of sums of m max-margin circular units and select m by the SRM bound (not validation error alone). Construct S_m concretely as a two-stage analogue of Schölkopf's "hybrid": use the support vectors of the circular-kernel SVM as candidate sphere centers, fit a max-margin circular unit around each, and combine them with a max-margin linear output layer — a fully margin-trained CBP network.

6. Regression variant. Instantiate the circular kernel inside epsilon-insensitive SV regression and dual (kernel) ridge regression, connecting to the function-estimation and dual-ridge sources, to test whether isotropic-quadratic features suffice for smooth function estimation.

7. Experiments. Replicate Schölkopf et al.'s USPS protocol and the CBP benchmark datasets, comparing SVCM against (i) Gaussian-kernel SVM, (ii) full degree-2 polynomial SVM, (iii) backprop-trained CBP, and (iv) RBF+k-means, measuring test error, number of support vectors / free parameters, and training stability. The decisive test of the puzzle: if SVCM matches Gaussian-SVM accuracy while using a d+1-dimensional feature space and dramatically lower capacity, and simultaneously beats backprop-CBP, then the SVM advantage is attributable to margin plus the right minimal feature — not to high dimensionality — reconciling the two conflicting results.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** A contradiction runs through these sources about *what actually causes good generalization* in nonlinear classifiers, and no study orthogonalizes the competing explanations.

(1) Vapnik's statistical learning theory (The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory; Statistical Learning Theory; The Support Vector Method of Function Estimation) says generalization is bounded by empirical risk plus a confidence term that depends only on capacity (VC dimension / margin / weight-vector norm). This prescription is deliberately architecture-agnostic: capacity control (structural risk minimization) — not the choice of representation — should drive test performance.

(2) Yet Schölkopf et al. ("Comparing SVMs with Gaussian kernels to RBF classifiers") observe that merely swapping the *representation* — choosing RBF centers by the SV algorithm versus k-means — lowers test error even when weights are trained classically, i.e., "solely due to the lower value of the empirical risk; the confidence term... did not change." Their full SV machine changes both the representation (which points become prototypes) and the capacity-control regime (margin/QP) at once.

(3) In apparent tension, Ridella et al.'s circular backpropagation (CBP) network claims to be "in many senses optimal" and to beat MLPs and Gaussian RBF networks purely by changing the *representational paradigm* (its "paradigm plasticity" interpolates between surface-based hyperplanes and prototype-based hyperspheres), while being trained by plain backpropagation with no explicit capacity control — and it justifies this via VC-dimension and Cover-capacity arguments.

So two camps explain the same phenomenon (better generalization) with different causes: one credits capacity control / margin (SLT, SVM, ridge regression in dual variables), the other credits representational paradigm (CBP, SV-vs-k-means center choice). Every existing comparison confounds these two axes — models differ simultaneously in *how they represent* the decision boundary and in *how they control capacity*. The risk-bound decomposition itself shows the empirical-risk and confidence terms can move independently, yet no work has mapped their interaction. Until representation and capacity control are varied orthogonally on a common substrate, we cannot say whether generalization obeys the architecture-agnostic SLT prescription or whether representational form has an effect even at matched capacity. This matters because practitioners choosing between RBF, SVM, and CBP-style models are given conflicting guidance about which knob to turn.

**Method.** Build a factorial diagnostic benchmark that orthogonalizes two axes on a single shared substrate, then decompose the variance in generalization to see which axis (and their interaction) drives it.

Common substrate. Exploit the CBP insight that a "circular" (prototype/spherical) classifier is just a *linear* classifier on an augmented feature map: phi_gamma(x) = [x, gamma*||x||^2]. This turns the representational paradigm into a single continuous dial that does not change the underlying data:
- Axis A (Representation paradigm), gamma from 0 to large: gamma=0 gives the pure surface-based hyperplane (MLP-like); increasing gamma continuously introduces the hyperspherical/prototype behavior (RBF-like). Extend the prototype extreme with explicit Gaussian-RBF feature maps so the axis spans surface -> circular -> full prototype, exactly the paradigms the CBP paper unifies and the SVM/RBF paper contrasts.
- Axis B (Capacity-control regime / learning principle) applied to the *same* representation: (i) plain empirical risk minimization by backpropagation (no capacity control), (ii) explicit L2 / ridge regularization solved in dual variables (kernel ridge regression), and (iii) margin-based structural risk minimization via the SVM quadratic program. These three are precisely the learning principles spanned by the source papers.

Measurements per grid cell. On multiple datasets — the USPS handwritten-digit database and the synthetic 2D ball/circle problem used by Schölkopf et al., plus additional standard benchmarks — record: train and test error; the generalization gap; and several *effective-capacity* diagnostics drawn directly from the sources: (a) the Cover-capacity fraction of shatterable dichotomies and VC-dimension estimates (CBP paper), (b) the achieved margin distribution and the norm of the weight vector that the SVM paper links to the confidence term, and (c) the number of active prototypes / support vectors.

Empirical map and analysis. Produce a 2D map (representation dial x capacity-control regime) of generalization and of each capacity diagnostic. Then run the controlled comparisons the literature never did:
1. Hold effective capacity fixed (match VC/margin/weight-norm across cells by tuning regularization strength) and vary representation gamma — does test error still change? A positive result confirms the CBP claim that paradigm matters beyond capacity; a null result vindicates the architecture-agnostic SLT prescription.
2. Hold representation fixed and vary the capacity-control regime — quantify how much test error tracks the confidence term as predicted by the risk bound.
3. Variance decomposition (ANOVA) attributing generalization variance to Axis A, Axis B, and their interaction at matched capacity.

This directly resolves the confound: because representation is a feature-map dial and capacity control is an independently chosen learning principle, the study isolates whether the SV machine's advantage over RBF and CBP's advantage over MLP stem from representation, from capacity control, or from an interaction (e.g., prototype representations may benefit disproportionately from margin control). All components are implementable with the tools in the sources (backprop, dual ridge regression, SVM QP; VC/Cover-capacity and margin measurements), making the map feasible and its conclusions grounded in, and adjudicating between, the provided works.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two of the provided works give conflicting explanations for *why* a "radial" or "circular" lift of the inputs improves classification, even though both operate on essentially the same object.

(1) Ridella–Rovetta–Zunino's Circular Backpropagation (CBP) network augments a perceptron with a single extra input equal to the sum of squared inputs, ρ(x)=||x||². They prove (Corollary 1) that this raises the Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension only from n+1 to n+2 while jumping from hyperplane to full hypersphere/prototype separators, and argue that generalization improves "for free" because the dimension-counting capacity increment is minimal. Crucially, the resulting machine is trained by plain backpropagation with *no* explicit capacity control, and CBP claims formal equivalence to Gaussian RBF networks.

(2) Vapnik's statistical-learning-theory works (and the SVM-vs-RBF comparison paper) tell an opposite story: for canonical hyperplanes over data of radius R, the VC dimension is bounded by h ≤ min([R²·A²], n)+1, i.e. by the radius/margin ratio R²/M², *independent* of the feature-space dimension. Under this view dimension counting is irrelevant; what one must actively do is maximize the margin (SRM), which is exactly why margin-trained SVMs with Gaussian kernels beat backprop-trained RBF/hybrid networks on USPS.

Here is the puzzle. CBP's radial lift Φ(x)=(x,||x||²) is precisely a rank-1 slice of the degree-2 polynomial feature map — the paraboloid embedding that also underlies kernel machines and dual-variable ridge regression. So the very same construction is credited by one literature to *keeping VC dimension low* and by the other to *being dimension-irrelevant but margin-governed*. Neither is reconciled: CBP never maximizes a margin, and it is silent on the fact that appending ||x||² inflates the data radius (R↦√(R²+R⁴)), which by the radius–margin bound could *worsen* generalization unless a large spherical margin is gained. Conversely, SVM practice never exploits the interpretable single-center/single-radius structure that CBP shows the same lift possesses. This leaves an unresolved, testable question: under margin theory, when does adding the circular feature actually help, and how should such a machine be trained?

**Method.** A formal-derivation contribution that unifies the two accounts and yields an algorithm.

1. Ontology / conceptual distinction. Formalize the "isotropic radial lift" Φ(x)=(x,||x||²)∈ℝ^{n+1} and its induced kernel k(x,z)=x·z+||x||²·||z||². Prove that linear separators in Φ-space coincide exactly with the class of hyperspheres-plus-hyperplanes realized by a CBP circular unit (re-deriving CBP's center/radius/gain parametrization as the coordinates of a canonical hyperplane in Φ-space). This places CBP and SVM on the *same* feature map but at different corners of a 2-axis taxonomy: (feature-lift dimensionality) × (capacity-control principle: dimension-counting SRM vs. margin SRM).

2. Two bounds, one class. State Theorem A (dimension view): VC dim of the circular class = n+2 (recovering CBP). State Theorem B (margin view): apply Vapnik's canonical-hyperplane bound in Φ-space to get h ≤ min([R_Φ²·A²], n+2)+1, and derive R_Φ explicitly from the original data radius, R_Φ² = R² + R⁴ (after standard scaling γ). This shows the two bounds are not contradictory but are the worst-case and data-dependent faces of the same class.

3. The reconciling inequality (explanatory formulation). Compare the linear perceptron's bound R²/M² with the circular machine's (R²+R⁴)/M_c², where M and M_c are the achievable margins. Derive the closed-form condition under which augmenting with ||x||² lowers the generalization bound: it helps iff the spherical margin gain satisfies M_c/M > √(1+R²). This makes precise the "free lunch" claimed by CBP — it holds only when the data admit a genuinely large-margin spherical (prototype) separation; otherwise the radius inflation R⁴ dominates and the feature hurts. This resolves the puzzle and is directly falsifiable.

4. Algorithm derived from the bound: the Support Vector Circular Machine (SVCM). Instead of CBP's backprop, minimize ½||w||² in Φ-space subject to margin constraints (equivalently solve the SVM dual with kernel k above), with a normalization scale γ chosen to minimize the derived radius–margin bound R_Φ²·||w||². A soft/regression variant follows immediately by using the dual-variable ridge-regression formulation for the ε-insensitive/squared loss. The solution retains CBP's interpretability (each unit reads off as an explicit center and radius) while inheriting SVM-grade, margin-based capacity control. A multi-center version sums such units, bridging to Gaussian RBF as the limiting case CBP identified.

5. Validation. On USPS digits (the benchmark of the SVM-vs-RBF paper), compare (a) backprop-trained CBP, (b) SVCM, and (c) Gaussian-kernel SVM, measuring test error against the two predicted bounds, and empirically test the reconciling inequality by scanning problems from clearly spherical to clearly linear structure. This confirms which capacity principle governs the circular lift and demonstrates that margin-optimal training of the single circular feature closes the gap between interpretable CBP networks and kernel SVMs.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Vapnik's SV theory promises validation-free, dimension-free model selection via the radius-margin capacity term (eq. 3.37 of "The support vector method of function estimation"), computable from training data alone; dual ridge regression and Zhang's leave-one-out analysis add an exact cheap LOO error equal in expectation to generalization error. So tuning kernel width and C should be principled and cheap. Yet in "Comparing SVMs with Gaussian kernels to RBF classifiers," top USPS accuracy needs hand/grid-tuned width and C plus k-means centers, and the capacity bound is used only to rank a few fixed models, never to search; "Circular backpropagation" derives capacity results but tunes by backprop. The paradox: a computable objective meant to automate tuning is unused while grid search and validation splits dominate. Cause: the bound is treated as a per-model number, not a function of hyperparameters, and the cheap-LOO machine (dense ridge) is not the deployed sparse SVM.

**Method.** Recast model selection as continuous optimization over theta = (Gaussian bandwidths, optionally per-feature, and C). (1) Make the radius-margin bound R(theta) a smooth objective; its two quadratic programs (SVM dual for weight norm, enclosing-sphere program eqs. 3.32-3.36 for radius) have locally stationary multipliers, so by the envelope theorem dR/dtheta reduces to analytic kernel-matrix derivatives over active support vectors, enabling quasi-Newton search. (2) Use dual ridge regression's closed-form exact LOO as a cheap dense surrogate for coarse multistart global search. (3) Two-stage adaptive search: Stage A minimizes ridge LOO from several starts; Stage B refines theta on the deployed sparse SVM via R(theta) gradients, bridging cheap-dense and sparse-expensive machines. (4) Efficiency via Cholesky factorizations with rank-one updates and warm-started path-following QP re-solves. (5) Per-dimension bandwidths make R(theta) minimization perform validation-free feature scaling. Evaluate on USPS-style digits and 2-D benchmarks against grid-search+validation SVMs and k-means RBF, measuring accuracy, model fits, and cost.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two 1997–98 strands of this literature give conflicting practical guidance about how to obtain good nonlinear classifiers, even though they rest on the same statistical-learning-theory foundation.

(1) The kernel/SVM strand (Vapnik's "Nature of Statistical Learning Theory", "Statistical Learning Theory", "The Support Vector Method of Function Estimation", the dual ridge-regression paper, and the empirical "Comparing SVMs with Gaussian kernels to RBF classifiers") argues that generalization is governed by capacity, not dimensionality: the VC dimension of the optimal hyperplane is bounded by (R/margin)^2, independent of the feature-space dimension. This licenses mapping data into implicit, even infinite-dimensional feature spaces and controlling capacity purely by maximizing the margin. Empirically the Gaussian-kernel SVM gives the best accuracy on USPS digits. But the winning machine is a fixed kernel chosen a priori, is implicitly infinite-dimensional, expands into O(N) support vectors (heavy and slow at test time), and is essentially uninterpretable.

(2) The Circular Backpropagation (CBP) strand shows the opposite: adding a single explicit feature — the isotropic quadratic term ||x||^2 — to a plain perceptron already unifies the surface-based (hyperplane) and prototype-based (circular/RBF) paradigms, is trainable by ordinary backpropagation with only one extra weight per unit, yields interpretable "circular parameters" (center, radius, gain), and empirically beats both the MLP and the Gaussian RBF network.

Here is the puzzle. If, as the theory insists, only capacity (margin) matters and dimensionality is irrelevant, then (a) why does the richly-lifted implicit kernel machine beat the minimal explicit augmentation, and (b) why pay for infinite-dimensional kernels and O(N) support vectors when one explicit quadratic feature already captures the needed nonlinearity? Each strand optimizes exactly one half of the picture — SVM has principled margin/SRM capacity control but an opaque, fixed, high-cost implicit representation; CBP has a cheap, explicit, interpretable, adaptive representation but is trained by backprop with no margin maximization, no capacity control, and vulnerability to local minima. No work combines margin-based capacity control with an explicit, minimal, interpretable feature augmentation, leaving open whether the dimension-independent margin bounds can be harvested in an explicit low-dimensional space.

**Method.** Relax the SV method's core assumption — that capacity control must operate over a single, fixed, implicit (often infinite-dimensional) kernel feature space — and instead make maximum-margin structural risk minimization operate over an explicit, incrementally grown, low-dimensional and interpretable feature hierarchy built from the circular-backpropagation augmentation.

1. Explicit nested feature hierarchy. Define a sequence of explicit maps of increasing dimension: level 0 = raw input x (pure surface-based perceptron); level 1 = the CBP augmentation φ1(x) = (x, ||x||^2), which makes a linear separator a hypersphere; higher levels add a small, ordered set of explicit prototype/quadratic features (per-coordinate squares, then a few anisotropic or product terms selected greedily). These spaces are explicitly nested S0 ⊂ S1 ⊂ ... , making them a genuine SRM structure with known finite dimension at each level.

2. Margin optimization in the primal explicit space. In each explicit space, replace CBP's backprop with maximum-margin / regularized fitting: solve the linear SVM in the primal (or the dual-variable ridge-regression solution) directly on the augmented features. This transfers SVM's principled capacity control and convex (local-minimum-free) optimization onto CBP's cheap, interpretable representation, while producing a single compact weight vector rather than O(N) support vectors — fast test time and no kernel matrix.

3. SRM-based, data-adaptive level selection. Because each level's dimension and the achieved margin R/ρ are explicitly computable, use the VC/margin bound h ≤ (R/ρ)^2 (dimension-independent, per the SV theory) together with the level's structural index to pick the hierarchy element minimizing the guaranteed risk. This relaxes the need to guess a kernel a priori: capacity is controlled jointly by an adaptively chosen explicit feature dimension and the margin, rather than by a fixed implicit space.

4. Greedy margin-guided feature growth. To extend scope to problems needing richer nonlinearity, add features one at a time, each iteration inserting the candidate explicit feature (a new prototype/quadratic term, initialized from mis-margin points) that most increases the margin, stopping when the SRM bound stops improving. This yields a data-driven explicit feature-construction procedure that interpolates between the minimal circular model and a full quadratic map.

5. Regression and noisy regimes. Using the SV function-estimation / dual ridge-regression formulation, apply the same explicit-hierarchy SRM to ε-insensitive regression, and evaluate specifically in regimes where kernel SVM is impractical — large N, streaming/online updates, strict test-time budgets, and demands for interpretable decision regions (readable centers/radii).

Evaluation: on USPS digits (the shared benchmark of the comparing paper) and additional noisy/low-data tasks, compare accuracy, number of stored parameters, test-time cost, and interpretability against (i) Gaussian-kernel SVM, (ii) RBF networks, and (iii) backprop-trained CBP. The central test of the puzzle is whether explicit low-dimensional circular feature spaces, trained to maximum margin and selected by SRM, recover most or all of the accuracy of implicit infinite-dimensional kernels while remaining compact and interpretable — thereby showing that the dimension-independent margin bounds can indeed be realized without lifting into implicit high-dimensional spaces.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle/contradiction in the prior work. Statistical learning theory casts kernel machines as both theoretically well-founded and empirically robust. Vapnik (Nature of Statistical Learning Theory, 1995; Support Vector Method of Function Estimation, 1998) argues via Structural Risk Minimization that the generalization bound depends on capacity/compression — essentially the fraction of support vectors, not input dimension — and observes (5.12.3) that "the set of support vectors is a robust characteristic of the data": different optimal SV machines reuse almost the same 3-5% of points. Scholkopf et al. (1997) show an SV machine with a Gaussian kernel beats a classical RBF network on USPS digits. Yet the same work exposes a contradiction. (1) The bound is minimized when support vectors are FEW, but the SV set is exactly the margin violators; under label noise every mislabeled point becomes a bounded support vector, inflating the SV count, worsening the very bound optimized, and pinning the decision surface to corrupted points — so the "robust characteristic" is what noise attacks. (2) Ridge regression in dual variables (Saunders et al., 1998) is the extreme: quadratic loss makes EVERY point a support vector, maximizing outlier sensitivity, though it is the same dual kernel machine. (3) Reported robustness is conditional on a well-chosen kernel/width/C, which SRM does not supply, and circular backpropagation (Ridella et al., 1997) shows the representation can slide continuously between surface-based and prototype-based regimes — so which kernel machine is a live, brittle choice. Thus the literature simultaneously claims the support-vector set is a stable intrinsic signature and that these machines are brittle to label noise and hyperparameters. Vapnik's stability observation is only descriptive; nobody turned it into an operational safeguard. This matters because kernel machines are deployed as off-the-shelf classifiers whose failures on noisy data are opaque.

**Method.** Robustification via support-vector-set stability: turn Vapnik's descriptive observation that the SV set is a robust characteristic into an explicit criterion making kernel machines robust to label noise and hyperparameter choice. Step 1, diversified ensemble of dual kernel machines: train the dual solution across a grid spanning kernel families/widths (Gaussian, polynomial, sigmoid, per Scholkopf et al.), regularization C, and the loss continuum from sparse hinge/epsilon-insensitive SVM to non-sparse dual ridge regression (Saunders et al.); the circular-backpropagation unification (Ridella et al.) gives a knob sliding between surface-based and prototype-based representations. Each model exposes, per point, its dual multiplier and KKT status (non-SV, margin SV, bounded violating SV). Step 2, two statistics: (i) SV-set stability S(theta), the Jaccard overlap of support-vector sets and prediction agreement between neighboring hyperparameters; (ii) per-point SV persistence p_i, the multiplier-weighted fraction of models in which point i is a bounded margin-violating support vector. Step 3, robust model selection: choose the hyperparameter region jointly maximizing accuracy and SV-set stability S(theta), operationalizing the robust-characteristic claim as a selection principle and targeting hyperparameter brittleness, since stable regions are where the data rather than the arbitrary kernel determines the support set. Step 4, robust noise handling: flag high-p_i points as suspected mislabels/outliers, since a point violating the margin under essentially every stable representation is unlikely genuine boundary structure; down-weight, trim, or relabel them and retrain a final trimmed machine. Removing noise-induced bounded SVs shrinks the SV fraction, restoring the low-capacity regime the SRM bound rewards and closing the loop between bound and observed robustness. Step 5, evaluation: on USPS digits with controlled injected label noise, compare against a best-tuned SVM and dual ridge regression on accuracy under noise, number of support vectors, decision-function stability under resampling, and precision/recall of noise flagging against known corrupted labels; success is recovering accuracy and SV sparsity that vanilla kernel machines lose under noise, with no oracle knowledge of corrupted labels. The ensemble's agreement about support vectors acts as a self-diagnostic distinguishing where the robust characteristic is genuine from where it is an artifact of noise or an unstable kernel.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** A sharp contradiction runs through these works about how much feature-space dimensionality a classifier really needs and how its capacity should be controlled. On one side, the Circular Backpropagation (CBP) work of Ridella et al. shows that augmenting a plain perceptron/MLP with a SINGLE extra input equal to the squared norm ||x||^2 gives it "paradigm plasticity" — it can represent both hyperplanes (surface-based) and hyperspheres (prototype-based). This minimal, explicit lift has a tiny, exactly-known capacity (circular-unit VC dimension n+2, Cover capacity merely doubled), is trainable by ordinary backpropagation, and is reported to match or beat Gaussian RBF networks — even solving a seven-point alternate-labels problem that a Gaussian RBF provably cannot. On the other side, the SVM-with-Gaussian-kernel study (Schölkopf et al.) and Vapnik's statistical-learning / support-vector-function-estimation chapters argue that dimensionality "does not matter": the Gaussian kernel maps into an infinite-dimensional feature space, capacity is governed by the margin (SRM), and this infinite-dimensional, many-support-vector machine achieves the best accuracy on USPS digits. 

Herein lies the paradox: if dimensionality is irrelevant and the margin is everything, why does the lowest-possible-dimensional circular lift, trained WITHOUT any margin control, rival an infinite-dimensional margin-maximized RBF-SVM? And if a minimal explicit quadratic lift is "in many senses optimal," why does the SVM literature insist on enormous feature spaces? The two literatures never met under a common protocol: they control capacity by two different, unreconciled mechanisms — explicit architectural VC dimension (CBP) versus data-dependent margin in a fixed rich space (SVM/kernel ridge) — and they confound representation with training objective (backprop vs. margin/Tikhonov regularization). Which mechanism actually drives generalization, and are the two "secretly" the same? Resolving this matters because it would tell practitioners whether interpretable, low-parameter, low-VC quadratic models with proper capacity control can replace opaque kernel machines, and it would unify neural, kernel, and regularization views that these sources currently treat as rivals.

**Method.** Synthesis/unification: place CBP, SVM-RBF, and dual-variable ridge regression on a single axis and train them under one objective, so representation capacity and margin/regularization capacity can be varied independently and reconciled.

(1) A continuum of feature maps. Reinterpret the CBP circular unit as the smallest member of an explicit polynomial family: phi_0(x)=[x] (linear perceptron), phi_1(x)=[x, ||x||^2] (the exact circular map, prototype+surface plasticity), phi_2(x)= x plus diagonal squares, ..., phi_full(x)= all degree-2 monomials (which is precisely the degree-2 polynomial kernel completion of the circular map), with the Gaussian kernel as the infinite-dimensional limit. This single axis makes CBP (minimal explicit lift) and SVM-RBF (infinite implicit lift) two endpoints of the SAME construction.

(2) A unified training objective. Replace CBP's plain backprop with a margin-maximizing / Tikhonov-regularized objective identical in form to the SVM and to ridge regression, i.e. minimize regularization term ||w||^2 plus a hinge/squared loss over the explicit features phi_k(x). Because low-order phi_k are finite and explicit, the same problem can be solved in the primal (interpretable circular parameters: center, radius, gain) OR in the dual. Use the dual-variable ridge-regression formulation to bridge the two: show each phi_k induces a rank-limited kernel K_k(x,x')=<phi_k(x),phi_k(x')> so that dual kernel ridge / SVM on K_k is mathematically the same machine as regularized least-squares/margin training on the explicit CBP features. This yields the "Margin-Regularized Circular Machine" (a support-vector version of the circular unit): RBF-like localized boundaries with a handful of interpretable parameters plus explicit SRM capacity control.

(3) A unified generalization bound. Combine CBP's exact VC/Cover capacity results with Vapnik's SRM/margin bound to write one risk bound for the whole family carrying BOTH a dimension term (VC of phi_k) and a margin term. Show analytically that CBP (small VC, no margin control) and SVM-RBF (huge VC, tight margin control) are two operating points minimizing the same bound differently, predicting an optimal intermediate: a low-order margin-regularized quadratic/circular machine.

(4) Decisive experiments that disentangle the confound. On the seven-point alternate-labels construction and on the USPS handwritten-digit benchmark used by the SVM-RBF paper, sweep the two capacity knobs independently: representation order k (dimension) and regularization strength (margin). This factorial design isolates whether the CBP-vs-SVM discrepancy is caused by dimensionality or by the training objective. Concretely, we test the hypothesis that CBP's success is due to its implicit low-VC representation while SVM's success is due to margin control, and that giving the minimal circular map explicit margin control closes the gap to infinite-dimensional RBF-SVM at a fraction of the parameters/support vectors. Reporting test error, VC/margin bound values, and support-vector/parameter counts across the family reconciles the conflicting evidence and delivers a single, interpretable, theoretically-grounded model spanning the neural, kernel, and ridge-regression literatures.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Kernel machines such as SVMs and dual kernel ridge regression are accurate but have an inference time resource bottleneck, because test cost and memory scale with retained training points. The SVM to RBF hybrid keeps one center per support vector, and k means RBF has no capacity guarantee, so deployment on constrained hardware lacks budget control. The Circular Backpropagation network is a fixed size backprop trainable model with known VC dimension and Cover capacity but has not been used to remove this cost.

**Method.** Build a compiler that converts a trained kernel machine into a fixed size Circular Backpropagation network. Train a teacher kernel machine and record its soft outputs and support vectors. Use the CBP capacity formulas to choose the smallest number of hidden units meeting a user resource budget, applying Structural Risk Minimization to the compact student. Initialize the circular units as prototypes by clustering the support vectors. Distill by backpropagation to match the teacher outputs. Output an accuracy versus footprint Pareto frontier and a deployable model, evaluated on USPS digits against the SV teacher, dense kernel ridge regression, the SV center RBF hybrid, and k means RBF.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Kernel and support-vector machines give top accuracy but their cost scales with data: the SV quadratic program and dual ridge regression need an n-by-n kernel, dual ridge is fully dense, and Vapnik's bound ties model size to the growing number of support vectors. This is a memory, training-time, and inference-latency bottleneck at scale. Circular backpropagation shows one extra input (squared norm of x) cheaply gives RBF-like surfaces in a primal model with explicit capacity bounds, but no listed work fuses this cheap augmentation with closed-form ridge regression and the SV sparsity/SRM principle to make model size and inference cost independent of n.

**Method.** Budgeted Circular Ridge Machines: an explicit compact feature map trained in the primal so cost never scales with n. Generalize the circular augmentation [x, sqnorm(x)] by adding B prototype features sqnorm(x-c_j), which expand to sqnorm(x)-2 c_j dot x + const, interpolating from one circular unit to a full RBF net with B as an explicit resource knob. Choose B centers cheaply by k-means or SV-guided margin selection. Train by closed-form primal ridge: w=(Phi'Phi+lambda I)^-1 Phi'y with feature dimension m=d+1+B much smaller than n, giving O(n m^2) time, O(m^2) memory, no n-by-n kernel, and O(m) constant-cost prediction. Use the primal/dual ridge equivalence to relate the map to full Gaussian-kernel ridge and set lambda; use circular-unit VC/Cover bounds plus the expected-#SV/n bound via structural risk minimization to pick the smallest adequate B. Evaluate on the USPS digit benchmark and SV regression tasks, plotting accuracy versus model size, memory, training time, and inference FLOPs against full RBF-SVM, the SV/backprop hybrid, and dense kernel ridge regression, testing whether accuracy is preserved while resources stay flat as n grows.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The learner families in this corpus (SVMs with Gaussian/polynomial/sigmoid kernels, RBF networks with k-means centers, dual-variable kernel ridge regression, and circular-backpropagation networks that unify surface- and prototype-based schemes) are all point-expansion models that reach comparable, excellent accuracy. The binding constraint is not accuracy but resource cost. Training is dominated by an O(l^2) kernel matrix plus QP or backprop; the comparing-SVMs paper notes the machines are comparable in classification speed and memory yet differences are noticeable in training, but never quantifies this. Deployment cost is fixed by the number of support vectors/prototypes (prediction FLOPs and memory), and the largest hidden cost is model selection: kernel width, C/ridge lambda, and number of units are chosen by exhaustive grid search with full retraining. The corpus already contains cheap single-fit signals plausibly tracking cost and accuracy (Vapnik's E[#SV]/l bound, the closed-form leave-one-out of the dual ridge solution, and the VC/Cover-capacity formulas for circular-backpropagation nets), yet no work maps these diagnostics to measured compute, memory, and deployment cost across the unified family. A practitioner under a training-time, memory, or latency budget therefore cannot pick a method or hyperparameters without brute-force retraining. This is the resource bottleneck left open.

**Method.** Build an empirical accuracy-versus-resource map of the unified expansion-based family and turn it into a budget-aware, diagnostic-guided selector. First, on a shared suite (USPS digits plus lower-dimensional classification/regression tasks) train a matched hyperparameter sweep of hard/soft-margin SVMs with Gaussian, polynomial and sigmoid kernels, dual/kernel ridge regression, classical RBF-with-k-means, the SV-centers-plus-backprop hybrid, and circular-backpropagation nets. Second, instrument each configuration with resource metrics (training time/FLOPs, peak kernel-matrix memory, model size equal to the number of support-vectors/prototypes which sets prediction cost, and cumulative selection retraining budget) and a held-out error metric. Third, for each single fit also compute cheap grid-free diagnostics: margin, Vapnik's E[#SV]/l bound, the dual-ridge closed-form leave-one-out error, and circular-BP VC-dimension/Cover-capacity. Fourth, empirically quantify how well each cheap diagnostic predicts measured error and each resource cost, and assemble per-family Pareto frontiers of accuracy versus training FLOPs, memory, and prediction cost. Fifth, deliver and validate a selector: given a compute/memory/latency budget, use the fitted diagnostic-to-outcome map to predict the frontier-optimal method and hyperparameters from a few single fits, then measure the model-selection compute saved versus full cross-validated grid search at matched accuracy and report where each family dominates the frontier.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The kernel machines across these works — SVMs for classification/regression, kernel ridge regression in dual variables, and Gaussian-kernel/RBF classifiers — share an unaddressed resource bottleneck: they build the n-by-n Gram matrix (O(n^2) memory, expensive QP) and output f(x)=sum_j alpha_j K(x,x_j)+b whose test-time cost and storage scale with the number of retained basis terms. The RBF-comparison paper notes SV training is an order of magnitude heavier, and this worsens with n. Critically, the amount of computation is uncontrolled: kernel ridge regression is fully dense (all n points appear at test time); SVM sparsity emerges implicitly from data and C, not settable in advance; RBF picks centers by k-means with no guarantee. Vapnik's bound E[error] <= E[#SV]/(n-1) ties sparsity to generalization but only describes it after the fact — it does not let a practitioner fix a compute/memory budget up front and know the resulting risk. No unified formal framework makes the representational budget an explicit, tunable design variable carrying a generalization certificate, which is exactly what deployment under fixed cost, memory, and latency requires.

**Method.** A formal-derivation framework for budget-constrained kernel expansions. (1) Conceptual distinction: separate the representation budget B (number of training points allowed in the expansion set S, |S|<=B, the sole driver of Gram-submatrix size, QP dimension, storage, and test-time cost) from statistical capacity (margin/coefficient norm, set independently). This taxonomy places existing methods as special cases: kernel ridge regression = B=n; SVM = B chosen implicitly by KKT; k-means-RBF = B fixed heuristically with no certificate. (2) Objective: minimize (1/n) sum_i phi(y_i f(x_i)) + lambda ||f||^2 over f(x)=sum_{j in S} alpha_j K(x,x_j)+b subject to |S|<=B, where phi is any convex surrogate (hinge, epsilon-insensitive, or squared loss); B is set by the user's compute/memory budget. (3) Theorem (budget compression bound): any solution is a compression scheme of size <=B, fully determined by the B chosen points. Generalizing Vapnik's leave-one-out argument behind E[#SV]/(n-1) — removing non-expansion points leaves the solution unchanged and each held-out error is charged to an expansion point — derive an SRM-style bound E[error] <= (B + capacity/margin term)/(n-B), monotone increasing in B. Fixing B a priori thus fixes an upper bound on expected test error, giving the missing design lever linking compute budget to accuracy; instantiate the capacity term using VC/Cover-capacity results from the circular-backpropagation unification. (4) Derived algorithm: since the bound is monotone in B, a forward-selection procedure adds one basis point at a time, each step picking the point most decreasing regularized empirical risk and re-solving only a small subproblem, replacing the O(n^2)-memory full QP with B small problems (about O(nB^2) time, O(nB) memory); every added point is a valid compression-set element, so the step-3 bound holds at each budget, yielding an anytime accuracy/cost curve with a certificate at every point. (5) Validation: on USPS digits and toy sets from the RBF-comparison and SLT works, check that the bound tracks test error as B varies, that at matched B the budgeted expansion beats k-means-RBF and full kernel ridge regression at a fraction of memory/test cost, and that it recovers SVM accuracy once B reaches the empirical support-vector count — confirming each classical method as a point on the derived budget curve.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 13 tool calls

**Motivation.** Resource bottleneck. The kernel/support-vector family that dominates these sources delivers strong, theoretically justified accuracy (Vapnik, "The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory"; "The Support Vector Method of Function Estimation"; Schölkopf et al. comparing SVMs to RBF classifiers), but all of the concrete estimators here pay a severe and coupled cost in compute, memory, and deployment:

1. SVM training is a quadratic program over the full n-point kernel matrix. The comparison paper explicitly notes the optimization "requires us to work with potentially large matrices," forcing chunked processing and special large-scale QP solvers — i.e., training does not scale gracefully in n.
2. Kernel ridge regression in dual variables (the "Ridge regression learning algorithm in dual variables" source) solves (K+λI)α=y, an O(n^3) dense linear system with an O(n^2) matrix, and — unlike the SVM — produces no sparsity: every training point is a basis center, so both memory and test-time cost grow with the full training set.
3. Test-time cost is a first-class bottleneck: the SVM decision rule evaluates the kernel between a test point and every support vector; the paper resorts to a post-hoc "reduced set" approximation to make prediction affordable.
4. The classical RBF alternative sidesteps QP cost by choosing centers with task-agnostic k-means clustering, but this spends the (fixed) center budget without regard to the classification/regression risk, so accuracy suffers.

Crucially, statistical learning theory already tells us the resource we should be economizing on is directly tied to generalization: Theorem 5.2 bounds expected test error by E[#support vectors]/(l−1). So a model that uses fewer, better-chosen basis points is simultaneously cheaper to train, cheaper to store, cheaper to evaluate, AND expected to generalize at least as well. Yet none of the provided methods actually optimizes the model under an explicit compute/memory budget: SVM sparsity is an uncontrolled by-product of the QP, dual ridge regression has no sparsity at all, and RBF center selection is decoupled from the task. This leaves open a resource-allocation gap: how to build a kernel model that hits a target accuracy at a chosen, controllable train-time, memory, and test-time cost.

**Method.** Budget-constrained, risk-guided greedy center selection for sparse kernel ridge regression (a "screen-and-allocate" optimization procedure). The base learner is dual-variable ridge regression, but the solution is restricted to an actively grown working set S of m ≪ n selected basis points (columns of K), turning the O(n^3) dense solve into a cheap reduced problem.

Core loop (active/adaptive design + resource allocation):
1. Reduced solve. For the current working set S, fit the Nyström-style reduced kernel ridge model using only columns K_S, solving the small m×m regularized normal system rather than the full n×n system. Cost per fit is O(m^2 n) instead of O(n^3).
2. Incremental updates (leverage "Matrix Computations" and "Practical Methods of Optimization" toolboxes). When a candidate point is added, update the factorization by a rank-one Cholesky/QR update instead of refactoring from scratch, so evaluating each candidate is cheap and the whole sweep stays near-linear in n.
3. Screening/acquisition. Rank remaining points by a cost-normalized marginal-gain criterion: the reduction in the regularized dual objective (equivalently the residual/leverage score, computable in closed form from the current factorization) per unit of added compute. This is the search/optimization contribution — a greedy submatrix (column-subset) selection that directly targets the objective, unlike task-agnostic k-means center placement.
4. Bound-aware stopping = compute allocation. Because #basis-points plays the role of #support-vectors in the generalization bound (Theorem 5.2), track a validation/PRESS-style leave-one-out estimate (obtainable in closed form for ridge regression) as m grows and stop when the marginal risk reduction per added FLOP/memory unit falls below a user-set threshold, or when the explicit budget (max m, max memory, max test-time kernel evaluations) is exhausted. This makes train cost, model size, and inference cost tunable dials rather than by-products.

Two efficiency accelerators grounded in the sources:
- Circular-backpropagation feature augmentation: for Gaussian/RBF-type kernels, append the squared-norm feature (the single extra input of the CBP model) so a linear-in-augmented-features model cheaply approximates hyperspherical "bumps," reducing the number of explicit kernel evaluations per candidate and per test point.
- Reduced-set-by-construction: since selection is greedy and budgeted, the resulting model is already a small expansion, eliminating the need for a separate post-hoc reduced-set approximation of the decision rule.

Evaluation. On the USPS handwritten-digit benchmark used by the comparison paper (and toy/regression tasks from the SV function-estimation chapter), compare against (a) full-QP SVM, (b) dense dual ridge regression, and (c) k-means-center RBF, plotting accuracy versus explicit budgets (training time, peak memory, test-time kernel evaluations). Success = matching SVM/kernel-ridge accuracy while using far fewer basis points and asymptotically lower train/memory/inference cost, with the achieved sparsity tracking the theoretical error bound. This addresses the resource bottleneck by turning kernel model construction into an explicit, budget-aware optimization over which few training points to spend compute on.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Resource-bottleneck gap. The sources show kernel machines grounded in statistical learning theory generalize excellently: the SV method minimizes an upper bound on test error (Vapnik works), and Scholkopf/Sung/Burges show a Gaussian-kernel SVM beats a k-means+backprop RBF net on USPS digits. But adoption is cost-limited, not accuracy-limited. SVM training solves a constrained QP; the comparison paper concedes it needs large matrices, chunked processing, and special algorithms for many support vectors, with O(n^2) memory and roughly O(n^3) time. The dual ridge regression paper gives a closed-form kernel predictor but inverts the full n-by-n kernel matrix and is fully dense, so all n points are stored and evaluated at test time. Circular backprop nets train cheaply but lack margin/VC capacity control. No method keeps the margin guarantees while running under tight compute/memory budgets in the large-n regime where exact QP and full inversion are infeasible.

**Method.** Budgeted Iteratively-Reweighted Ridge Support Vector learning: relax the SVM QP into a few cheap dual ridge-regression solves and extend dual ridge to be sparse, budgeted, and robust. (1) Approximate the hinge and epsilon-insensitive losses by iteratively reweighted quadratic losses; each iteration solves an SPD dual ridge system (K+lambda W^-1)alpha=y via conjugate gradient, reusing the dual-ridge machinery with weights that up-weight margin violators. (2) Replace the full kernel matrix with an incomplete-Cholesky/Nystrom low-rank factorization on a working set of m<<n basis points, cutting memory to O(nm) and time to O(nm^2). (3) Grow the budget greedily, adding the basis point that most reduces the SV/margin generalization bound and pruning points whose coefficients vanish, yielding a sparse predictor with far fewer than n basis vectors. (4) The same solver covers classification, regression, and Huber estimation as reweighting schedules and tolerates label noise. Evaluate on the USPS protocol plus a large/noisy regime, reporting accuracy, training time, memory, and basis-vector count versus exact QP-SVM, full dual ridge, and circular-backprop/RBF baselines as functions of m and n; target near-SVM accuracy and sparsity at a fraction of the cost where exact methods are infeasible, using the SRM bound as both selection criterion and generalization certificate.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Resource bottleneck. The strongest predictors here are kernel machines whose cost is dominated by scarce compute and memory. Kernel ridge regression in dual variables has a clean closed-form rule but requires inverting the n-by-n matrix (K+lambda*I): O(n^2) memory, O(n^3) time, and its solution is fully dense, so test cost scales with the entire training set. SVMs are sparse and enjoy the E[number of support vectors]/(l-1) generalization bound, but the support-vector count is set by the QP, uncontrollable in advance, and grows with noise (Vapnik prefers the epsilon-insensitive loss precisely because it uses fewer support vectors than Huber); training is a large QP and C, epsilon, and kernel-width are brittle. Classical RBF classifiers choose centers by unsupervised k-means, ignoring labels and underperforming SVMs on USPS digits. Circular backpropagation nets give RBF-like, capacity-bounded predictors at cheap MLP cost but, as a backprop heuristic, offer no explicit resource budget or robustness guarantee. In resource-limited deployment (embedded or real-time inference, large or streaming n, tight memory), none of these lets a practitioner fix a hard budget on model size and training cost while staying reliable. This matters because deployment cost is dominated by test-time kernel evaluations and training kernel-matrix memory, exactly what dense KRR and data-dependent SVM sparsity leave uncontrolled.

**Method.** Robustification. Build a Budgeted Robust Kernel Machine whose complexity is capped a priori by a user-set budget B of kernel centers, converting an uncontrolled resource cost into a fixed one while adding mechanisms that reduce known failure modes. First, capacity-guided robust center selection: rather than unsupervised clustering or an unbounded QP support set, greedily add centers up to B, each chosen to most reduce an SRM-style upper bound equal to robust empirical risk plus a capacity penalty, using the margin and capacity terms (R^2 times squared weight norm, and the number-of-expansion-terms bound) from statistical learning theory; this ties the compact model to a generalization guarantee and guards against overfitting. Second, robust loss: fit coefficients with a Huber or epsilon-insensitive loss so outliers and label noise are downweighted. Third, numerically stable reduced solve: with centers fixed, solve only a B-by-B ridge-regularized least-squares system via Cholesky or QR, removing the ill-conditioning brittleness of inverting the full (K+lambda*I) and cutting training to O(n*B^2) time, O(n*B) memory, and test cost to O(B). Fourth, subsample ensembling: average several budgeted predictors trained on bootstrap subsamples to reduce variance from greedy selection and yield a predictive-disagreement uncertainty estimate that flags unreliable inputs. Evaluation: on USPS digits and standard regression sets, plot accuracy versus resource budget (test-time kernel evaluations, training memory) against full KRR, Gaussian-kernel SVM, k-means RBF, and circular backpropagation, then stress-test robustness by injecting label noise and outliers and perturbing kernel-width and regularization. Success means matching SVM and KRR accuracy at an order-of-magnitude smaller fixed model size, with markedly smaller accuracy loss under noise and hyperparameter misspecification.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 19 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** Kernel machines grounded in statistical learning theory (Gaussian-kernel SVM, dual kernel ridge regression) give top accuracy but are resource-bound: SVM training solves a QP, dual ridge inverts an n-by-n kernel matrix, both dense so all points are retained, and tuning kernel width and C needs repeated re-solves; deployment stores many support vectors. Circular backpropagation shows that adding one explicit squared-norm coordinate turns hyperplanes into hyperspheres, unifying surface-based and prototype-based models cheaply with ordinary backprop, closed-form VC dimension and Cover capacity, and provable equivalence to Gaussian RBF networks. Thus RBF-like power is available through explicit low-dimensional features rather than costly implicit kernels. These literatures are unreconciled: circular backprop lacks margin and SRM selection, and SVM/kernel-ridge practice ignores cheap explicit features. No prior work offers a low-cost yet principled machine bridging them.

**Method.** Build a capacity-ordered feature ladder of explicit finite-dimensional maps generalizing the circular unit: level 0 is x, level 1 is x plus the squared norm (hyperspheres), higher levels add block-wise or randomized squared-norm and low-order product terms that per the circular-backprop proof approach a Gaussian-RBF machine, making linear SVM, RBF-SVM, and kernel ridge endpoints of one continuum. At each level of explicit dimension m, fit a regularized linear model (ridge closed-form or hinge margin) in the primal at cost linear in n via an m-by-m solve, avoiding the n-by-n kernel matrix and QP; when m exceeds n, use the Woodbury primal-dual identity (the same equivalence linking dual ridge to kernel ridge) to solve in the dual only when cheaper, yielding a compact weight vector instead of stored support vectors. Select level and regularization by analytic structural risk minimization using the closed-form VC dimension and Cover capacity per level, minimizing empirical risk plus the capacity term in one cheap sweep, replacing grid search over kernel width and C. Validate on USPS digits against Gaussian-kernel SVM, k-means RBF, and the hybrid, comparing accuracy, training time, peak memory, test-time model size and latency, and agreement of the SRM-selected model with the best cross-validated kernel model.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Corpus kernel/SVM results assume fixed a-priori kernels, batch offline training on small stationary benchmarks, and offline cross-validation, mismatching streaming, drifting, resource-bounded deployment.

**Method.** A self-tuning online kernel-ridge-regression engine that auto-selects kernel and regularization via a closed-form leave-one-out surrogate and keeps a budgeted basis-vector dictionary with rank-one updates.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap between two neighboring lines. Ridella et al. (1997) circular backpropagation (CBP) shows that augmenting input x with one isotropic quadratic feature (the squared norm of x) gives paradigm plasticity: a linear machine interpolates between a surface-based classifier (a hyperplane) and a prototype-based one (a hypersphere or RBF bump), chosen by the data. But this lived only in a non-convex backprop-trained MLP with no operational capacity control (its VC and Cover results are post-hoc), local minima, and narrow validation. Conversely, the support-vector and statistical-learning-theory line (Vapnik 1995/1998; Scholkopf et al. 1997) supplies the missing capacity control through margin maximization and the radius-margin bound, and automatically places RBF centers minimizing a bound on test error, but the paradigm is frozen a priori in the kernel choice, a Gaussian kernel injects a large capacity increment, and its flagship validation is one narrow benchmark (USPS digits). Kernel ridge regression in dual variables (1998) shares the fixed-kernel limitation. Thus plasticity exists only without capacity control and capacity control only without plasticity; no method makes the surface-versus-prototype paradigm itself an object of convex, capacity-controlled model selection, and none is validated across diverse regimes. This matters because the appropriate paradigm and amount of sphericalness are unknown a priori: Gaussian kernels overshoot capacity while linear kernels underfit.

**Method.** Propose the Support Vector Circular Machine (SVCM), moving CBP plasticity augmentation into the convex SRM framework and making the paradigm the object of structural risk minimization. One, a capacity ladder: augment x with a single extra coordinate equal to the square root of lambda times the squared norm of x (equivalently a circular kernel equal to the dot product of x and z plus lambda times the product of their squared norms) and train a standard soft-margin SVM. Small lambda recovers the maximal-margin hyperplane; larger lambda gives hyperspherical prototype surfaces, adding only one feature dimension, a small tunable increment between a linear SVM and a full quadratic or Gaussian SVM. Two, SRM over the paradigm continuum: index a nested structure by lambda and pick lambda minimizing the radius-margin bound (enclosing-sphere radius squared times squared weight norm), the radius from the small enclosing-sphere QP in the SV function-estimation chapter and the weight norm from the SVM dual. Convexity gives a unique global solution per lambda, so selection walks a well-defined capacity ladder without local minima, turning CBP post-hoc VC analysis into an operational criterion. Three, a localized extension adding several circular features at candidate centers greedily while the bound improves, yielding an explicit sequence from one hyperplane to a full Gaussian-RBF SVM. Four, a regression variant applying the same augmentation to SV regression and dual-variable ridge regression. Five, broad validation across CBP Gaussian-mixture and vowel tasks, USPS digits, and added UCI benchmarks, reporting accuracy, support-vector count, capacity, and selected lambda. Feasibility: augmentation is a one-line change to any SVM or kernel-ridge solver.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. SVM theory rests on two strong claims: (1) its generalization bounds are essentially dimension-independent — the support-vector-count bound E[P(error)] <= E[#SV]/(l-1) and the radius-margin functional R^2*|w|^2 (Eq. 3.37 in "The support vector method of function estimation"; Sec. 5.6/5.7 in "The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory"), said to depend "neither on the dimensionality of the space, nor on the norm of the coefficients"; and (2) minimizing these bounds soundly selects among models and kernels ("the model that minimizes the estimate (3.37) is selecting the best SV machine"). But the supporting evidence lives in a narrow regime: separable/near-separable pattern recognition, essentially one benchmark (USPS digits), one kernel family (Gaussian), classification only. The influential verdict that "the SV machine achieves the highest recognition accuracy" (Scholkopf et al. vs RBF classifiers) comes from that same single dataset and kernel. Competing machines cited by these works — dual-variable kernel ridge regression, classical k-means+backprop RBF nets, and circular-backpropagation nets with known VC/Cover-capacity properties — are each evaluated under different losses, assumptions, and datasets, leaving no common comparison ground. This is a scope mismatch: theory and practice were validated only at flattering boundary conditions (low Bayes error, one modality, one kernel), yet practitioners transfer these bounds and single-benchmark verdicts to noisy, high-overlap, varying-dimensional, and regression settings where validity is untested. It matters because the practical appeal of SVMs — automatic capacity control and bound-based kernel selection without cross-validation — hinges on whether the bounds track test error outside the demonstrated regime.

**Method.** Build an empirical-mapping benchmark that tests where SVM generalization bounds and single-dataset comparative verdicts actually transfer. (1) Regime grid: controlled synthetic data generators sweeping the conditions prior work held fixed — input dimensionality, training-set size l, Bayes error/class overlap (separable to heavy noise), intrinsic vs ambient dimensionality, and task type (classification and regression) — plus real USPS digits as an anchor.
