(2) Common-footing models: per configuration, with matched preprocessing and tuned hyperparameters, train an SVM under several kernel families (Gaussian, polynomial, sigmoid), dual-variable kernel ridge regression, a classical RBF net (k-means centers + pseudoinverse/backprop), and a circular-backpropagation net, reusing the QP/optimization and matrix routines in the sources. (3) Instrument the bounds: for each fitted SVM compute the radius-margin estimate R^2*|w|^2 via the smallest-enclosing-sphere QP (Eqs. 3.33-3.36), the support-vector-count ratio bound E[#SV]/(l-1), and the SRM/VC estimate, alongside true held-out test error and cross-validation error. (4) Diagnostics along each regime axis: bound tightness (bound/true-error ratio), rank correlation between each bound and true test error as kernel hyperparameters vary, and selection regret (excess test error from choosing kernel and width/degree by minimizing the bound vs by cross-validation), directly testing the claim that bound minimization selects the best machine; also map when SVM's accuracy edge over kernel ridge regression, RBF, and circular-BP actually appears. (5) Deliverable: an empirical validity map over regime space showing where bound-based capacity control and kernel selection are reliable, where they degrade (hypothesized: high Bayes error, small l, regression, non-Gaussian kernels), and where the reported SVM-vs-RBF ranking reverses — giving regime-specific guidance on when to trust the bounds versus fall back to cross-validation.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap in the noise assumptions of support-vector and kernel regression. The sources build function estimation on Huber's loss-to-noise correspondence: quadratic loss is best-unbiased for Gaussian noise, least-modulus is minimax for the worst symmetric density, and Vapnik's epsilon-insensitive loss matches a fixed-proportion contamination mixture with epsilon set by that proportion. Dual-variable ridge regression is the epsilon=0 quadratic member of that family. All assume noise is identically distributed across the input space (homoscedastic), so one global loss shape, one insensitivity radius epsilon, and one capacity constraint apply everywhere. Real problems have input-dependent noise: a single epsilon is too wide in low-noise regions (biasing the fit, absorbing informative points, destroying support-vector meaning) and too narrow in high-noise regions (fitting noise, spawning spurious support vectors). Cross-validating epsilon and C cannot fix a mis-specified functional form. No source derives the optimal loss or epsilon when the noise density varies with x, nor a bound for that regime, leaving room for a formal treatment.

**Method.** A Formal-Derivation contribution extending the Huber-Vapnik loss-to-noise correspondence from homoscedastic to heteroscedastic noise. (1) Taxonomy theorem: define a family L(r;e,s) interpolating the sources' losses (e=0,s=2 quadratic = dual-variable ridge; e=0,s=1 least modulus; e>0,s=1 epsilon-insensitive; Huber blend) and prove a mapping from noise density p to optimal (e*,s*), with e* the half-width of p's flat/contaminated core. (2) Heteroscedastic model: with input-dependent density p_x, apply the local minimax/ML argument to derive pointwise-optimal e*(x)=kappa*sigma(x) and shape s*(x), giving adaptive empirical risk (1/l)sum of |y_i-f(x_i)| in tube e(x_i) under the SRM constraint norm(w)<=c. (3) Generalization theorem: prove the adaptive-risk minimizer bounds the true expected epsilon-insensitive risk with the standard VC confidence term in h and l, and that this bound is strictly tighter than the fixed-epsilon SVM bound under non-degenerate heteroscedasticity, with excess risk quantified by the variance of sigma(x). (4) Boundary-case unification: show reductions to standard SV regression, kernel ridge regression, and least-modulus regression as degenerate corners. (5) Convex algorithm: derive the dual where per-point tubes e_i enter only the linear term, preserving the sparse kernel expansion w=sum(a_i-a_i*)Phi(x_i) and convexity; a two-stage plug-in estimates sigma(x_i) from k-nearest residual variance of a pilot fit, sets e_i=kappa*sigma(x_i), then solves the adaptive QP. Validate on heteroscedastic synthetic and real data against fixed-epsilon SVM and kernel ridge regression by test risk, support-vector counts, and tube calibration.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. The kernel/large-margin methods represented in these sources are all justified by generalization theory but committed, in practice, to a single, hand-fixed model structure. Vapnik's SV method and Structural Risk Minimization (the-support-vector-method-of-function-estimation-1998, the-nature-of-statistical-learning-theory-1995) index capacity by a single scalar chosen along a predetermined nested chain, and the "SV algorithm automatically determines centers/weights minimizing an upper bound" only *after* the kernel family, kernel width, and the regularization/C are fixed by the user. The empirical comparison (comparing-support-vector-machines-with-gaussian-kernels-to-r) uses one global Gaussian width for all centers and contrasts SV-selected centers with k-means centers, again holding the kernel scale fixed. Circular backpropagation (circular-backpropagation-networks-for-classification-1997) introduces a single global radial term that interpolates linear and spherical decision surfaces, but that interpolation is a fixed architectural choice, not something adapted per problem. Kernel ridge regression in the dual (ridge-regression-learning-algorithm-in-dual-variables-1998) and the leave-one-out risk bounds for kernel formulations (statistical-learning-theory-1998) give closed-form solutions and computable error bounds, yet these bounds are used *descriptively* to justify a fixed machine rather than *prescriptively* to choose its structure. The shared, unrealistic assumption is that one global kernel scale, one regularization level, and one geometry (linear vs. radial) suffice — a poor fit for heterogeneous, multi-scale, mixed-feature real data. Consequently hyperparameters are set by expensive grid search or clustering heuristics that neither scale nor exploit the very bounds that motivate the methods. This matters because model-selection choices dominate real accuracy, and the gap between "bounds that justify" and "bounds that select" leaves substantial room for a principled, adaptive alternative.

**Method.** Turn the generalization bound itself into the objective of an efficient adaptive search over a richer, heterogeneous model structure — replacing grid search / clustering with bound-driven optimization. (1) Parameterize the structure with continuous knobs: an anisotropic/multi-scale kernel K(x,x') = exp(-Σ_d θ_d (x_d - x'_d)^2) with per-feature (or per-group) scales θ_d, a convex-combination weight vector over a small dictionary of base kernels, a circular-backpropagation-style coefficient α that continuously blends linear and radial (||x||^2-augmented) geometry, and the regularization λ (equivalently C). (2) For fixed structure, solve the machine in the dual closed form (kernel ridge regression: coefficients = (K+λI)^{-1}y; or the SVM dual), which crucially lets the leave-one-out residuals be read off from the diagonal of (K+λI)^{-1} without refitting. (3) Objective: a smoothed leave-one-out generalization bound — the LOO squared-error/margin form for regression and the convex-loss LOO risk bound of statistical-learning-theory-1998 for classification — evaluated on the dual solution. (4) Optimize all continuous knobs {θ_d, kernel weights, α, λ} by gradient descent, computing analytic gradients through the dual solution using standard matrix-derivative identities for the inverse, with Cholesky/eigen updates and iterative refinement from matrix-computations-1989 for stability. (5) Search/efficiency layer to beat grid search and to scale: (a) warm-start the dual re-solves along a regularization path; (b) use low-rank / incomplete-Cholesky (Nyström) approximations of K so each LOO-bound and gradient evaluation is cheap; (c) an active, greedy forward-backward screening that adds the candidate center/support vector whose inclusion most reduces the bound and prunes those with negligible dual weight, yielding a sparse machine whose support set is chosen by the bound rather than by clustering; (d) block-coordinate alternation between continuous-hyperparameter steps and discrete support-set updates. (6) Evaluation: on USPS handwritten digits (matching comparing-support-vector-machines-with-gaussian-kernels-to-r) and on datasets with deliberately mixed feature scales, compare against grid-searched fixed-width SVM, k-means-center RBF, and circular backpropagation, measuring test error, number of support vectors/centers, and total training+selection cost. The method directly addresses the scope mismatch by (i) letting kernel scale, geometry, and regularization adapt per feature/scale instead of globally, and (ii) making the generalization bound the search criterion, converting SRM from a one-dimensional fixed chain into an adaptive, resource-aware optimization over the model's structure.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Support-vector regression (Vapnik, "The Support Vector Method of Function Estimation," 1998; "The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory," 1995) fits real-valued functions with an epsilon-insensitive loss using a SINGLE globally fixed tube width epsilon chosen a priori, justified via Huber's minimax theory that assumes ONE homogeneous symmetric noise density everywhere. The least-squares and kernel-ridge baselines (quadratic loss; "Ridge regression learning algorithm in dual variables," 1998; RBF baselines in "Comparing SVMs with Gaussian kernels to RBF classifiers," 1997) assume even more strongly additive zero-mean constant-variance (homoscedastic) noise. This is a narrow, unrealistic boundary condition: in real regression, signal-processing, and time-series data the noise scale varies across the input domain (heteroscedasticity). Under a single global epsilon the tube is simultaneously too narrow in noisy regions (fits noise, destroys sparsity, spawns spurious support vectors) and too wide in clean regions (discards accurate data, biases the fit). None of the provided sources treats input-dependent noise; epsilon and the loss remain global constants and model selection is just choosing that one number. This leaves a concrete opening to relax the homoscedastic fixed-tube assumption so the well-founded SV/kernel machinery still works under spatially varying noise.

**Method.** Extend SVR from a single global epsilon to a locally adaptive data-estimated tube epsilon(x), turning the Huber/Vapnik noise-matching argument from global to pointwise while preserving convexity, kernelization, and sparsity. Feasibility rests on existing structure: in the primal (Eq. 3.45-3.46) epsilon already appears per constraint, and in the dual (Eq. 3.51) only through the linear term minus the sum of epsilon times (a_i + a_i-star). Replacing scalar epsilon with per-example epsilon_i changes this term to a per-point weighting, leaving the box constraints 0 to C and the quadratic form unchanged, so it remains a convex QP of the same size solvable with the same optimizers and Mercer kernels. Iterative EM-like procedure: (1) fit a standard small-epsilon SVR or kernel-ridge model and compute residuals r_i; (2) estimate a smooth local noise scale sigma(x) by regressing residual magnitudes or robust MAD on x with a second kernel smoother, yielding a nonparametric heteroscedastic noise map with no parametric noise form assumed; (3) set epsilon_i proportional to sigma(x_i) and optionally the penalty C_i inversely proportional to sigma, giving wide sparse tubes where noisy and tight tubes where clean; (4) re-solve the modified convex QP, recompute residuals, and iterate to a fixed point. Extensions in the same convex frame: asymmetric tubes for local skew/bias, a locally adaptive Huber transition point c(x), and a heteroscedastic nu-SVR controlling the local out-of-tube fraction. Evaluate on synthetic functions with controlled input-varying variance (sinc/Doppler) and real regression/time-series tasks against global-epsilon SVR, kernel ridge regression, and RBF baselines, measuring predictive risk, sparsity, calibration of the recovered tube to true sigma(x), and robustness across noise regimes. Since the method reduces exactly to standard SVR when sigma(x) is constant, it strictly generalizes prior work: matching it in the homoscedastic regime and dominating under realistic heteroscedastic noise. The hypothesis class of norm-bounded kernel expansions is unchanged, so structural-risk-minimization capacity bounds transfer with epsilon replaced by its sample average, only the empirical-risk tube being localized.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior kernel function-estimation work relies on narrow noise assumptions. Vapnik's SV method of function estimation (1998) uses an epsilon-insensitive loss with a single global tube width epsilon and penalty C, and its loss-choice justification requires homoscedastic normal noise (quadratic loss), symmetric noise (least-modulus loss), or a normal+symmetric mixture with fixed known contamination (Huber loss). Real noise is heteroscedastic, asymmetric, heavy-tailed, and of unknown scale, so a global epsilon is too wide in clean regions and too narrow in noisy ones, the estimator breaks under high-leverage outliers, and its predictive intervals are miscalibrated. Companion works share this: the RBF/SVM comparison (Scholkopf 1997) fixes kernel width heuristically, and dual ridge regression (Saunders 1998) uses a global L2 loss. This scope mismatch degrades accuracy, robustness, and calibration.

**Method.** Adaptive-Tube Robust Support Vector Regression. (1) Fit dual SV regression or dual kernel ridge regression to get residuals via kernel evaluations. (2) Fit a second kernel model to residual magnitudes for a spatially varying noise scale sigma(x), and to signed residuals for a local asymmetry index, distribution-free with no symmetry assumption. (3) Refit with per-example insensitivity epsilon_i = kappa*sigma(x_i) so the tube follows local noise, plus separate upper/lower slack penalties scaled to local skew, retaining the linear Huber tail but with a locally learned transition point replacing the assumed contamination proportion; the dual QP keeps Vapnik's structure with index-dependent box constraints and stays convex. (4) Alternate steps 2-3 EM-style until stable, combining outlier down-weighting with tube shrinkage to reduce breakdown. (5) Calibrate kappa on held-out data for distribution-free predictive bands at target coverage, selecting kernel width and base penalty by structural risk minimization. Evaluate against fixed-epsilon SVR, kernel ridge, and RBF networks on synthetic heteroscedastic/asymmetric/heavy-tailed data with injected outliers and on real benchmarks, reporting error, support-vector count, breakdown, and interval coverage; extend to classification label noise via region- and class-dependent C.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two mature but disconnected literatures address nonlinear classification. Kernel/statistical-learning machines (Vapnik's Nature of Statistical Learning Theory and Support Vector Method of Function Estimation; Scholkopf et al.'s SVM-vs-RBF study; ridge-regression-in-dual-variables and regularization networks) give convex, margin-maximizing dual/kernel solutions with structural-risk-minimization (SRM) guarantees. Neural mapping networks, notably the Circular Backpropagation (CBP) network of Ridella et al., give a data-adaptive machine with paradigm plasticity that interpolates between a surface-based representation (hyperplane/MLP) and a prototype-based one (hypersphere/RBF), with VC-dimension and Cover-capacity analysis. Scope mismatch: SVM/kernel practice assumes the representational geometry is fixed a priori. The user commits to one kernel, hence one paradigm (linear/polynomial = surface, Gaussian = prototype), before seeing data, and SRM bounds hold only for that pre-chosen family. A wrong paradigm choice hurts accuracy and interpretability, and cross-validating unrelated kernels gives no principled complexity ordering. CBP adapts the paradigm but only via backpropagation, forfeiting convexity, the kernel/dual trick, margin maximization, and SRM guarantees. Neither literature offers a single machine that adapts the surface-vs-prototype paradigm from data while keeping kernel machines' convex, SRM-grounded guarantees.

**Method.** Unify the literatures by noting that CBP's paradigm plasticity is exactly a feature map, liftable into the SVM/kernel-ridge framework. CBP augments x with the squared norm, so a linear discriminant of the form w.x + b*sqnorm(x) + w0 gives hyperplanes when b vanishes and hyperspheres otherwise, an explicit Mercer feature map, i.e. a circular kernel. Step 1: define a one-parameter Mercer kernel family interpolating surface-to-prototype, K_beta(x,y) = (x.y + c) + beta*sqnorm(x)*sqnorm(y), connected at its upper end to the Gaussian RBF (the prototype extreme in the SVM-vs-RBF paper), yielding a nested ladder linear, circular, RBF; beta is the SVM analogue of CBP's circular coefficient. Step 2: train by convex optimization, solving the standard SVM quadratic program (and, for regression, ridge regression in dual variables) with K_beta, inheriting the global optimum, support-vector sparsity, and automatic center selection while realizing CBP's adaptive hyperplane/hypersphere surfaces. Step 3: reconcile the two capacity analyses by combining CBP's VC-dimension and Cover-capacity results with SVM margin bounds to show how beta and the margin jointly control capacity; since varying beta traces genuinely nested function classes, select beta and the regularization constant C by SRM (minimizing the guaranteed-risk bound) rather than blind kernel-shopping cross-validation, restoring generalization guarantees to the adaptive machine. Step 4: validate on USPS handwritten digits (used in the SVM-vs-RBF study) and synthetic surface- vs prototype-structured problems, comparing the unified circular SVM against linear/polynomial SVMs, Gaussian-kernel SVMs, and backprop-trained CBP, testing whether SRM-selected beta recovers the correct paradigm per dataset, matches or beats fixed-kernel SVMs, avoids CBP's local minima, and yields interpretable decision surfaces. This bridges neural mapping networks and kernel/SRM theory: kernel machines gain CBP's data-driven paradigm plasticity, and CBP gains convex optimization, dual/kernel representation, and SRM model selection, removing the fixed-paradigm assumption constraining prior kernel work.

---

## Neocognitron: A Self-Organizing Neural Network Model for a Mechanism of Visual Pattern Recognition

*seed paper `neocognitron-a-self-organizing-neural-network-model-for-a-me`, 12 sources, 64 generated ideas*

### Human idea (ground truth)

**Motivation.** Existing pattern recognition models, including perceptrons and the earlier cognitron, fail when stimulus patterns are shifted in position or distorted in shape—their responses change drastically with such transformations. Understanding how the brain achieves position- and deformation-invariant recognition, and building a machine with the same capability, would illuminate the neural mechanism of vision and enable robust artificial recognition. A model that learns to classify patterns by shape alone, tolerating shifts and distortions, without requiring category labels, is therefore needed.

**Method.** We propose a multilayered neural network organized as a cascade of modules, each inspired by the Hubel–Wiesel hierarchy and extended to stages beyond hypercomplex cells. Each module pairs a layer of feature-extracting S-cells (analogous to simple/lower-order hypercomplex cells) with a layer of C-cells (analogous to complex/higher-order hypercomplex cells). Cells are grouped into cell-planes in which all cells share identical, spatially shifted afferent synapses, so each plane detects one feature across all positions. S-cells use shunting inhibition to respond selectively to specific features; C-cells pool over a local group of S-cells of the same plane so they fire if any nearby S-cell is active, granting tolerance to positional shift. Stacking modules progressively enlarges receptive fields while shrinking cell density, so features become more complex and more position-invariant with depth, until the deepest layer contains single cells each responsive to a whole pattern regardless of position or size. Self-organization proceeds by unsupervised learning: patterns are presented repeatedly with no category information; from local 'S-columns' the most strongly responding S-cell is selected as a representative for each plane (at most one per plane, avoiding redundant feature detectors), and only its active afferent synapses are reinforced—along with the entire plane sharing the update. Repeated reinforcement drives each S-cell to compute a normalized inner-product similarity to its learned feature, with an inhibition parameter controlling the selectivity–tolerance tradeoff, yielding a network that autonomously discovers feature detectors and recognizes shifted and distorted patterns.

### LLM ideas

#### [method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 20 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior works form one lineage: activity-dependent ordered map formation (retinotectal plasticity), the simple-to-complex columnar architecture of visual cortex (Hubel and Wiesel), position-invariant grandmother-cell responses in inferotemporal cortex, and self-organizing hierarchies (Rosenblatt's four-layer perceptron; Fukushima's cognitron and neocognitron). All engineered systems are strictly feedforward and self-organize only isolated, pre-normalized patterns; the neocognitron needs one normalized pattern at a time and cannot segment superimposed patterns, complete occluded inputs, or attend within clutter. Yet Rosenblatt names selective attention and feedback from response units to association units as open problems and shows discrimination degrades under unit removal; inferotemporal recordings show task-dependent top-down modulation; retinotectal work shows descending connectivity forms and regenerates via activity-dependent competition. No source closes the feedforward hierarchy with a self-organized top-down pathway for attention, segmentation, and completion.

**Method.** Build NeoAttend, a simulated bidirectional neocognitron whose artifact is a closed feedforward-feedback loop. Ascending: neocognitron cascade of modifiable S-layers and shift-tolerant C-layers, self-organized by unsupervised competitive reinforcement to grandmother units. Descending: a mirror cascade from grandmother units back to input, initialized as the transpose of ascending weights and refined by the same activity-dependent Hebbian competition, grounded in retinotectal ordered-connection formation, giving a generative top-down template. Attention: each S-cell is gain-modulated by the co-located descending signal (inferotemporal modulation, Rosenblatt feedback); the winning grandmother unit projects its template down to enhance consistent cells and suppress others. A serial loop with inhibition-of-return isolates one object, suppresses its support, and re-runs to recognize superimposed or multiple patterns one at a time; the generative descending pathway completes occluded features. Evaluation: unsupervised training on a small character set, then tests on superimposed, occluded, noisy, and cluttered multi-character inputs, measuring accuracy and objects segmented against a feedforward-only baseline, plus lesion experiments removing S-cells to test graceful degradation and whether descending plasticity regenerates connectivity after damage.

#### [unsteered (blank)] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (Fukushima 1980) is the first hierarchical, shift-invariant, self-organizing pattern recognizer, grounded in the Hubel-Wiesel simple/complex cell hierarchy and cortical columns. It learns without a teacher by competitively selecting representative S-cells that are Hebbian-reinforced (from the cognitron). The paper reports two unresolved weaknesses: fragility (with ten digits, small parameter deviations or changes in presentation order critically hurt accuracy, and resembling patterns like X,Y,T,Z are hard to separate) and fixed capacity (cell-plane counts are hand-set and cap learnable patterns). Both stem from purely bottom-up unsupervised learning where every winning feature is reinforced equally regardless of task usefulness. The perceptron work (Block, Knight, Rosenblatt 1962) offers error-correction reinforcement, expectancy, and selective attention but lacks similarity and shift invariance. IT-cortex recordings (Gross 1972; the 1980 discrimination study) show top-of-hierarchy neurons are pattern-selective and modulated during active discrimination, implying task relevance shapes selectivity, which the neocognitron ignores. Retinotectal plasticity models (1974) show ordered connectivity self-organizes via activity-dependent competition with capacity regulation. No prior work combines the invariant hierarchy with a local reinforcement signal and capacity allocation for robust, task-adaptive self-organization.

**Method.** Add three local mechanisms to the neocognitron, preserving its S-cell/C-cell hierarchy and shift-invariant pooling, with no global backpropagation. One, reward-gated three-factor reinforcement: train as a discrimination task like the IT-cortex experiments, read last-layer C-cell responses, derive a scalar reward r for whether the winner matched the target, and scale each representative S-cell Hebbian update by r (pre times post times reward) so correct trials reinforce and errors depress the culprit representatives, an error-correction rule applied locally to the deep competitive layer. Two, activity-driven cell-plane allocation: detect S-planes where two categories persistently collide and, when errors persist, recruit a new small-weighted plane captured by the sub-feature that best separates them, an activity-gated growth rule inspired by retinotectal map formation, removing the hand-set plane count. Three, top-down expectancy gating: on ambiguous inputs, transiently boost selection of S-planes historically diagnostic for the competing categories. Evaluate by reproducing Fukushima's protocol on X,Y,T,Z and digits 0-9, measuring accuracy under shift and small distortion; robustness by varying inhibition efficacy, selectivity thresholds, and presentation order and comparing accuracy variance to the unsupervised baseline; and capacity by comparing recruited planes to the fixed count needed to match accuracy. Ablate each mechanism. Success is reduced parameter/order sensitivity, correct discrimination of resembling and many categories, and automatic plane counts, while retaining position-invariant, local, teacher-sparse learning.

#### [method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 22 turns / 29 tool calls

**Motivation.** Hubel and Wiesel described a graded cortical hierarchy of simple, complex, and hypercomplex cells with increasing invariance and feature complexity; Gross reported shape-selective, position- and size-invariant inferotemporal units. The perceptron, Cognitron, and Neocognitron were built to emulate this hierarchy but validated only qualitatively at the output layer, so no source measures whether their internal cells reproduce the graded quantitative signatures of the biological cells they claim to model. Closing this gap would show which architectural and learning choices produce brain-like tuning and where the analogy breaks.

**Method.** Build an in-silico electrophysiology benchmark and empirical map. Assemble a stimulus battery reconstructing canonical protocols: spots for receptive-field mapping, oriented bars swept over orientation, position, length, and width, and complex shapes at varied positions, sizes, and distortions. Self-organize a perceptron baseline, the Cognitron, and the Neocognitron on a shared corpus. Probe each internal unit as a recorded neuron, measuring receptive-field area, orientation bandwidth, position tolerance normalized by field size, size tolerance, distortion tolerance, selectivity, and feature complexity. Plot layer-by-layer metric trajectories, overlay values digitized from the source papers, classify each unit against the simple, complex, hypercomplex, and inferotemporal taxonomy, and report per-layer matching. Diagnose whether S-to-C alternation yields the position-tolerant jump, whether invariance grows gradually with depth, and where each model diverges from biology.

#### [method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across the provided works there is a single recurring architectural idea: visual pattern recognition is achieved by a hierarchy that alternates *selectivity-increasing* stages with *tolerance-increasing* stages. Hubel & Wiesel (1962) describe how a complex cell is built by pooling over a set of simple cells with a common orientation but staggered positions, so that "an edge of light would activate one or more of these simple cells wherever it fell within the complex field," yielding position tolerance. Fukushima's cognitron (1975) and neocognitron (1980) make this explicit and mechanistic: modifiable S-cells (simple/lower-hypercomplex) refine feature selectivity through an unsupervised "winner-take-all + reinforce" rule and an r.m.s.-type similarity measure, while fixed C-cells (complex/higher-hypercomplex) with connecting areas that "become larger and larger for the hinder modules" accumulate shift tolerance until, at the top, a single cell responds to a pattern "not affected by position at all" — the position- and size-invariant selective units Gross et al. (1972) and Sato et al. (1978) reported in inferotemporal cortex. Rosenblatt's perceptron (1962) is the baseline that lacks this cascade and is therefore "severely affected by the shift in position."

Every one of these papers *asserts* a trade-off but none *derives* it. Fukushima states only that the pooling area "should be determined with a compromise between the ability to differentiate similar patterns and the ability to tolerate the distortion of the pattern's shape"; Hubel & Wiesel argue verbally that opposing effects are "relatively weak"; the neocognitron's number of cell-planes, layer count, and receptive-field growth are all set by hand. What is missing is a formal account of (i) how tolerance accumulates through a cascade of local pooling operators, (ii) the precise sense in which buying tolerance must cost discrimination, and (iii) a principled rule linking architectural resources (layers, pooling radii, number of cell-planes) to the number of patterns that can be recognized at a given invariance level. Closing this gap matters because it would turn the neocognitron's hand-tuned design choices into derivable quantities and give the informal "grandmother-cell" hierarchy a rigorous foundation.

**Method.** Develop an **invariance–selectivity calculus** for hierarchical simple/complex (S/C) cascades — a formal-derivation contribution comprising an ontology, three theorems, and a design rule, validated on a neocognitron simulation.

1. Formal model and taxonomy. Represent the network as an alternating composition F = C_L∘S_L∘…∘C_1∘S_1 acting on input fields u:ℤ²→ℝ. Formalize the neocognitron's own equations: an S-operation as an r.m.s.-normalized template-matching map producing a feature response ϕ(similarity), and a C-operation as a local pooling (weighted-OR/max over connecting area D_l of radius ρ_l, with subsampling factor σ_l). Define a conceptual distinction — S-type operators are *resolution-refining, tolerance-neutral*; C-type operators are *tolerance-increasing, resolution-coarsening* — and make it precise by assigning each map a "feature resolution" r_l and a "tolerance radius" t_l as functionals of its kernels.

2. Theorem 1 (tolerance accumulation). For the transformation group of translations (and, to first order, bounded smooth displacement fields modeling small distortions), prove that the top-layer tolerance obeys the recursion t_L = σ_L·t_{L-1} + ρ_L, giving a closed form t_L = Σ_l ρ_l ∏_{k>l} σ_k in input coordinates, and a Lipschitz-style bound: for any shift s with |s| ≤ t_L, ‖F(u∘shift_s) − F(u)‖ ≤ Σ_l κ_l·(residual mismatch at layer l), where κ_l depends on the pooling kernel's decay. This derives, rather than assumes, the neocognitron's growing receptive fields and Hubel–Wiesel's "wherever it falls" property.

3. Theorem 2 (invariance–selectivity uncertainty relation). Show that a C-operation granting tolerance t necessarily maps any two inputs that differ only by displacements ≤ t at the pooled feature scale to responses within ε(t) of each other, so they become indiscriminable. Cast this as a conserved-budget inequality t_L · ρ_disc ≥ γ, where ρ_disc is the minimum feature-level separation still resolvable at the top and γ is a constant set by the pooling kernels — an "uncertainty principle" formalizing Fukushima's compromise: tolerance and discrimination cannot both be made arbitrarily fine.

4. Theorem 3 (learning consistency and capacity). Prove that the neocognitron's unsupervised update (its Eqs. 7–8: reinforce the winning "representative" S-cell in proportion to its input) is a stochastic ascent on the expected r.m.s. similarity between S-templates and their winning inputs; hence at fixed points the K_l templates of a layer are the centroids of a K_l-way clustering of the layer's feature distribution. Combine with Theorem 2 to obtain a covering-number capacity bound: to recognize M patterns invariantly at tolerance t_L, the number of cell-planes must satisfy K_l ≥ N(feature distribution; ρ_disc(t_L)), the covering number at the tolerance-limited resolution. This yields an explicit design rule mapping (desired invariance, number of patterns) → (required layers, pooling radii, cell-planes).

5. Validation. Re-implement the neocognitron (as Fukushima did on a digital computer), sweep pooling radii and cell-plane counts, and empirically trace the tolerance-vs-discrimination frontier, testing whether it saturates the predicted uncertainty bound and whether the covering-number rule predicts the minimal K_l needed for error-free recognition. Contrast against a Rosenblatt-style non-pooling perceptron to confirm it lies far inside the frontier, quantifying exactly what the S/C cascade buys.

This addresses the gap by converting the qualitative "selectivity vs. tolerance" intuition shared by Hubel–Wiesel, Fukushima, and the IT-cortex literature into an explicit bound, an objective the classic learning rule provably (approximately) optimizes, and a resource-to-performance derivation that the original works left as hand-set parameters.

#### [method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron self-organizes by winner-take-all reinforcement but is brittle: plane counts are hand-fixed (24 planes failed on ten patterns) and success depends critically on parameter values and presentation order. No prior work treats capacity, parameters, and exposure schedule as quantities to discover automatically.

**Method.** Recast self-organization as unsupervised search over capacity, parameters, and scheduling. Grow S-planes on demand when a feature is not matched above a novelty threshold, and merge/prune redundant planes. Tune the few per-layer parameters greedily bottom-up via coordinate search. Score configurations by a label-free objective of pattern separability plus jitter-invariance minus a plane-count penalty. Adaptively schedule which stimulus to present next by targeting the most ambiguous top-layer response. Validate on the paper's digit protocol against the fixed-24-plane baseline.

#### [method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 29 turns / 39 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (1980), extending the cognitron and Hubel and Wiesel's hierarchy, achieves unsupervised shift-invariant recognition but under strong assumptions it itself flags: pooling gives only translation invariance, not scale or rotation, unlike its target IT neurons (Gross et al. 1972) invariant to position and size; it self-organizes on single centered patterns on clean backgrounds, so clutter and noise corrupt representative-cell selection; and it is fragile, since small setting changes or a changed presentation schedule critically degrade learning. No source provides broad transform invariance via unsupervised learning nor robustness to clutter, noise and presentation order, yet orientation-column and weight-shared cell-plane ideas plus surround inhibition make an extension feasible.

**Method.** We extend the neocognitron under weaker assumptions while keeping learning without a teacher. First, extend C-cell pooling from translation to the full similarity group: each learned S-cell feature spawns a weight-tied family of rotated and rescaled copies, and C-cells blur over space plus orientation and scale, giving deep cells invariance to translation, scale, and rotation while only one canonical S-plane per feature is modifiable so cost stays fixed. Second, add a competitive front-end using surround inhibition as saliency-driven winner-take-most, so representative-cell selection is driven by one segmented figure at a time under clutter and noise. Third, replace hand-tuned thresholds and skillful presentation with a homeostatic rule where each cell-plane self-adjusts its threshold toward a target activation and dead planes are reallocated. Evaluate on cluttered, noisy, overlapping, translated, scaled and rotated scenes, measuring invariance, robustness, and sensitivity to setting and order perturbations versus the baseline neocognitron.

#### [method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron achieves invariant recognition but its unsupervised self-organization is brittle; Fukushima notes small parameter or presentation-order changes critically affect performance, leaving a reliability gap.

**Method.** Add homeostatic competition among cell-planes, adaptive vigilance-based recruitment of fresh planes, and epoch-averaged consensus reinforcement to the neocognitron's S-cell learning, then evaluate reliability across randomized orders and parameters.

#### [method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two foundational learning paradigms in this corpus were developed in parallel and never reconciled into a single principle. (1) Rosenblatt's series-coupled/cross-coupled perceptron (1962) learns categories through *supervised* error-correcting reinforcement rules with a teacher (and Rosenblatt explicitly flagged "selective attention mechanisms," feedback from response units, and new reinforcement rules as open problems), but its response is, as the neocognitron paper itself notes, "severely affected by the shift in position and/or by the distortion in shape" of inputs. (2) Fukushima's cognitron (1975) and neocognitron (1980) achieve shift- and distortion-invariant recognition by *unsupervised* self-organization of a Hubel–Wiesel S-cell/C-cell hierarchy, but they cannot exploit category labels when available, cannot be told which cell should stand for which pattern, and — as the paper concedes — are brittle: with ten patterns "a small deviation of the values of the parameters, or a small change of the way of pattern presentation, has critically influenced" recognition, and the grandmother-cell-to-category assignment is essentially arbitrary. 

Crucially, the biological sources indicate that these two mechanisms coexist rather than compete. Inferotemporal-cortex recordings show high-level, position/size-invariant feature cells whose responsiveness is modulated while the animal *performs a discrimination task* (the 1972 and 1980 IT studies), implying a top-down/behavioral teaching signal reaching invariant visual representations. And the retinotectal neuroplasticity models (Meyer & Sperry, 1974), which Fukushima invokes to justify self-organization, show that ordered, target-matched connection maps form through activity-dependent plasticity — a mechanism for making a mapping *stable and reproducible* rather than arbitrary. No model in this literature unifies supervised error-correction with unsupervised invariance-building, nor uses a top-down task signal to stabilize the category mapping. Closing this gap matters because it would explain how invariant perception and label-/task-driven learning arise from one learning rule, and would fix the neocognitron's core weaknesses (brittleness, uncontrolled category assignment, inability to use supervision) while giving the perceptron the invariance it lacks.

**Method.** Propose a single hierarchical learning framework — a "guided neocognitron" — whose plasticity rule provably interpolates between unsupervised self-organization (cognitron/neocognitron) and supervised error-correction (perceptron), bridged by a top-down teaching pathway grounded in IT-cortex task modulation and retinotectal topographic plasticity.

1. Shared architecture. Keep the neocognitron's bottom-up S-cell/C-cell cascade (Hubel–Wiesel hierarchy) as the invariance engine, and attach a perceptron-style decision/response layer (R-units) that reads the deepest C-layer. This physically joins the two literatures: invariant features flow up; category decisions are made by a trainable linear readout.

2. Unified plasticity rule. Reformulate the neocognitron's "representative S-cell" selection-and-reinforcement step as a gated update: Δw = (competitive Hebbian term, exactly the neocognitron's winner-take-all reinforcement) × g, where g is a top-down teaching gate broadcast from the R-units back down the hierarchy. When no label is present, g≡1 and the rule is identical to neocognitron self-organization. When a label is present and the decision is correct, g reinforces the representatives that fed the correct R-unit; when the decision is wrong, g suppresses reinforcement of representatives feeding the erroneous unit and promotes runner-up candidates — this is Rosenblatt's error-correcting reinforcement expressed inside the competitive selection step. Prove the two limits analytically: teacher-absent → pure neocognitron; single trainable layer, no invariance pooling → pure perceptron error-correction. Intermediate supervision fractions yield a principled semi-supervised regime that operationalizes Fukushima's own conjecture that familiar (teacher-shaped) and unfamiliar (self-organized) patterns are processed by the same substrate.

3. Stabilizing the mapping via retinotectal-style ordering. Make the top-down teaching connections (R-units → S-layers) themselves plastic under an activity-dependent, target-matching rule analogous to compressed/ordered retinotectal map formation. This forces the category-to-cell assignment to converge to a stable, reproducible topographic organization rather than the arbitrary, parameter-sensitive assignment of the original neocognitron, directly addressing its documented brittleness.

4. Experiments (all reproducible with the original simulation setups). Train on shifted/distorted/noisy character sets. Compare four models: pure perceptron, pure neocognitron, a naive cascade (neocognitron features + separately trained perceptron readout), and the fully integrated gated model. Metrics: (a) recognition accuracy under position shift and shape distortion (tests whether supervision preserves the neocognitron's invariance); (b) reproducibility/stability of the category assignment across random presentation orders and parameter perturbations (tests the retinotectal-ordering claim vs. neocognitron brittleness); (c) label efficiency as the supervised fraction is swept from 0→100% (tests the unification/interpolation claim); (d) a biological prediction: switching the teaching gate on ("discrimination mode") should sharpen deep-layer cell selectivity relative to the ungated mode, mirroring the modulation reported for IT neurons during discrimination tasks.

This is a synthesis contribution: it reconciles the supervised (perceptron) and unsupervised (cognitron/neocognitron) learning traditions under one gated rule, and integrates neurophysiological hierarchy (Hubel–Wiesel), task-dependent high-level modulation (IT cortex), and activity-dependent topographic map formation (retinotectal plasticity) into a single coherent mechanism.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Four evidence streams in these sources developed in isolation, and connecting them is a bridge opportunity. (1) Self-organizing recognizers: the cognitron/neocognitron build a feedforward S-cell/C-cell hierarchy mirroring Hubel and Wiesel's simple-to-complex cascade and ending in IT-cortex-like invariant cells, learned purely bottom-up without a teacher via winner-take-all reinforcement. (2) Teacher/reinforcement learning: Rosenblatt's perceptron (Principles of Neurodynamics; four-layer perceptron) formalizes reward-driven learning the neocognitron avoids. (3) Task-dependent physiology: Gross (1972) and Sato/Iwai (1980, IT units during discrimination) show IT responses are gated by the animal's task, which a fixed feedforward net cannot express. (4) Activity-dependent plasticity: retinotectal regeneration (Meyer and Sperry 1974), cited by Fukushima to justify self-organization, shows topographic maps re-tile after lesions. Gap: the neocognitron models ventral-stream anatomy but leaves its own stated open problems untouched (top-down completion of degraded patterns, task modulation, recovery after damage), precisely the phenomena in streams 2-4, which sit in its citation neighborhood yet were never unified with self-organizing hierarchies. Bridging them turns a static recognizer into a model of adaptive, behaviorally driven, self-repairing perception.

**Method.** Central contribution: a simulation platform, an Adaptive Neocognitron, extending Fukushima's cascade of modifiable S-layers and position-tolerant C-layers with three integrated subsystems, released as a reusable simulator with defined stimulus, task, and lesion protocols. (1) Top-down feedback: add reciprocal projections so an active higher C-cell primes the lower S-cells supporting it; inference runs settling iterations where top-down expectation boosts and lateral inhibition suppresses lower S-cell activity, giving attention and pattern-completion for occluded or degraded inputs. (2) Teacher-gated competitive learning: scale the reinforcement of the winning representative S-cell by a scalar reward from Rosenblatt's framework tied to whether the top-layer output matches the task target; with no reward it reduces exactly to the original unsupervised rule, spanning teacher-free self-organization and reinforcement learning and letting identical features be sharpened differently across tasks (an analogue of IT task modulation). (3) Activity-dependent regeneration: give S-planes a Sperry-style ownership rule where coverage of feature space is set by cumulative co-activation and competition, so surviving planes re-tile after lesions. Evaluation in digital simulation: (a) reproduce position/shape-invariant character recognition; (b) measure accuracy on noisy, occluded, partial stimuli with feedback on vs off to quantify completion; (c) train under two competing discrimination tasks and show tuning reorganizes with the rewarded task, comparing qualitatively to IT task-dependence; (d) lesion cell-planes and plot recovery of accuracy over continued exposure, comparing compressed re-tiling to Meyer and Sperry's retinotectal reprojection. Ablations isolate each subsystem and probe the neocognitron's flagged parameter sensitivity, making the bridging hypotheses testable in one runnable system.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two disconnected strands here hold complementary halves of a solution to learning invariant object representations. Strand A: Fukushima's neocognitron (1980), extending the cognitron (1975), builds an S-cell/C-cell cascade modeled on Hubel and Wiesel's (1962) simple/complex-cell hierarchy and reaching toward IT object cells (Gross, 1972). But it hard-wires C-cell invariance pooling to fixed spatial neighborhoods; its plastic S-cells self-organize only on instantaneous geometric similarity of static patterns. So it gets position invariance but only small tolerance to rotation/scale/deformation, and the paper admits that recognizing 10 patterns needed skillful presentation and delicate tuning: which features form an invariance class is imposed by the designer, not learned. Strand B: Block, Knight and Rosenblatt's perceptron analysis (1962) showed an unused principle: a network can autonomously learn transformation-equivalence classes from temporal contiguity (successive forms/positions of the same object, contiguous in time, get one response), plus cross-coupled feedback for selective attention and figure-ground. This machinery was never placed inside a deep self-organizing hierarchy. Two evidence streams bridge to biology: retinotectal plasticity models (1974), cited by Fukushima, show orderly wiring arises from correlated activity over time; IT recordings during discrimination (1980) show high-level responses are modulated by behavioral task. Joining Rosenblatt's temporal-contiguity-plus-feedback idea with Fukushima's Hubel-Wiesel hierarchy, grounded in activity-dependent plasticity and IT task modulation, directly attacks the neocognitron's core limitation: invariance imposed rather than learned.

**Method.** Build a neocognitron-style S/C hierarchy but make invariance pooling learnable from time and gated by top-down attention. First, a feature stage keeps S-cells that self-organize by winner-take-all Hebbian competition on the current input, yielding simple/hypercomplex-like detectors. Second, a temporal-trace pooling stage is the core novelty importing Rosenblatt's contiguity principle: replace fixed spatial C-cell pooling with C-cells whose afferents from S-cells are learned by a trace/persistence Hebbian rule, where each C-cell keeps a slowly decaying trace of its recent activity and a synapse strengthens when an active S-cell coincides with high postsynaptic trace. A smoothly transforming stimulus (translating, rotating, rescaling, deforming, changing viewpoint) activates a time-contiguous sequence of S-cells, which the rule binds to a common C-cell, thereby learning an invariance class instead of assuming a spatial one. Applied at every cascade level, low levels learn local geometric invariances and high levels learn IT-like object invariance, using the correlated-activity plasticity supported by the retinotectal models. Third, top-down cross-coupled attention combines Rosenblatt feedback with IT modulation: add feedback from higher layers giving multiplicative attentional gain to lower layers and gating the trace learning rate, and let a behavioral-relevance/reward signal bias which temporal groupings consolidate, aligning invariance classes with meaningful categories and aiding figure-ground, solving the neocognitron's arbitrary-category problem. Fourth, the protocol trains unsupervised on continuous transformation movies rather than static snapshots. Predictions: complex-like C-cells with position/phase invariance emerge though pooling was never wired in, matching Hubel and Wiesel; higher layers develop transformation-invariant object units resembling IT selectivity; reward-gated feedback reproduces task-dependent high-level modulation. Ablations remove the temporal trace (reverting to static geometric-similarity learning and losing non-positional invariance), remove attentional gating (invariance classes drift from behavioral categories), and vary the trace time-constant (predicting a speed/invariance trade-off). All components are simulable at the scale of the original neocognitron studies.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 20 turns / 33 tool calls

**Motivation.** The corpus links two disconnected literatures: self-organizing artificial networks (Rosenblatt's perceptron 1962; Fukushima's Cognitron 1975/1981 and Neocognitron 1979/1980), judged on recognition performance, and quantitative neurophysiology (Hubel and Wiesel's cortex studies 1962/1965/1977; inferotemporal recordings 1972 and 1980; retinotectal plasticity 1974). The Neocognitron borrows the Hubel-Wiesel simple/complex/hypercomplex hierarchy and posits IT grandmother cells only qualitatively; no one has measured whether its emergent units reproduce the quantitative signatures of real neurons such as orientation bandwidth, simple-to-complex position invariance, end-stopping, receptive-field growth, and IT shift-tolerant selectivity. Bridging the two would reveal which biological cell classes each model layer recapitulates and whether unsupervised self-organization versus the supervised perceptron buys biological realism.

**Method.** Build a benchmark that applies classic electrophysiology stimulus protocols to the internal units of reimplemented perceptron, Cognitron, and Neocognitron models, extracts a fixed panel of neuronal tuning metrics (orientation bandwidth, on-off segregation, position-invariance index, end-stopping index, receptive-field size, direction selectivity, selectivity, and shift tolerance), and compares them layer by layer to digitized biological measurements to produce a correspondence matrix mapping each model layer onto retinal, simple, complex, hypercomplex, and IT cell classes, plus a developmental diagnostic checking whether topographic feature maps emerge as in retinotectal data.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** A bridge opportunity connects four parallel literatures. Fukushima's cognitron/neocognitron builds a shift-invariant hierarchy by unsupervised winner-take-all Hebbian reinforcement, with plane-wide weight-sharing giving translation equivariance and C-cell pooling giving position invariance; Fukushima reports the outcome becomes brittle and parameter-sensitive as the pattern set grows, with no principled way to steer which features form. Rosenblatt's perceptron supplies the opposite ingredient, teacher-driven error-correction/reinforcement (plus open problems of selective attention and geometric constraints), but no built-in invariance. Inferotemporal physiology (Gross et al.; Sato et al., monkey performing discrimination) shows invariant selectivity at the top of the hierarchy that is modulated by the behavioral task, which Fukushima's task-blind bottom-up model cannot explain. Hubel and Wiesel supply the columnar architecture, and retinotectal chemoaffinity (Sperry) is invoked only informally to justify shared-weight topography. The gap: no formal account unifies unsupervised self-organization with task/reinforcement modulation while respecting the weight-sharing that grants shift invariance. Bridging them would explain where a task signal can enter a shift-invariant hierarchy without destroying invariance and predict why task modulation appears at the invariant IT stage rather than early stages.

**Method.** A formal derivation. (1) Unified objective: model the neocognitron with plane-shared S-cell weights (equivariant) and C-cell pooling (invariant), minimizing J = J_unsup + lambda J_task + mu J_topo, where J_unsup reproduces Fukushima's representative-cell Hebbian rule, J_task is a perceptron error-correction term on a read-out of the top C-layer, and J_topo is a Sperry chemoaffinity gradient-matching prior on cell-plane identity. (2) Gated-reinforcement rule: the task term enters only through a scalar, position-independent gain per plane/category that gates plane-wide reinforcement, so reward selects which planes strengthen, not which positions. (3) Theorem: the modulated update preserves exact translation equivariance of each S-plane, hence C-cell position invariance, if and only if the gain is constant across positions within a plane; a position-dependent teacher breaks weight-sharing and destroys invariance. Corollary: task signals may enter a shift-invariant hierarchy only at the pooled invariant level, predicting task modulation at the IT stage (Gross/Sato) but not early cortex. (4) Capacity bound: derive the number of cell-planes needed to separate C categories as a function of task-discriminability margin and feature overlap, explaining why Fukushima's 24 planes failed for ten digits and showing J_task lowers the requirement by allocating planes to discriminative features. (5) Validation: re-implement the neocognitron with all three terms; measure equivariance error under position-dependent versus position-independent gating, test the capacity bound on Fukushima's X/Y/T/Z and 0-9 tasks, and compare the layerwise task-modulation profile to the IT-versus-early-cortex physiology.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** Fukushima's neocognitron (1980) built a shift-invariant S-cell/C-cell feature hierarchy modeled on Hubel and Wiesel simple/complex cells and hypercolumns, via unsupervised competitive self-organization that reinforces representative winning S-cells per column. Its own discussion reports two brittle, unsolved bottlenecks: capacity is fixed a priori (24 cell-planes per layer, which the author could not raise and which was not sufficient for 10 digits), and success depended on hand-tuned parameters (selectivity threshold, inhibitory efficacy r_l) and skillfully presented patterns, with small parameter or ordering changes destroying recognition. Because it forgoes any teacher, it has no principled way to size or tune itself. This is a bridge opportunity across literatures never connected to the problem: Hubel and Wiesel functional architecture suggests detectors should just cover stimulus statistics; retinotectal neuroplasticity models describe activity-driven adaptive allocation and remapping of connections; inferotemporal recordings show high-level detectors emerge selectively for behaviorally discriminated patterns; and Rosenblatt's perceptron tradition supplies the corrective-reinforcement feedback the neocognitron omits. No prior work brings adaptive biological resource allocation plus a weak perceptron-style feedback signal to bear on the neocognitron's fixed-capacity, hand-tuning fragility.

**Method.** Wrap the neocognitron's self-organization in an optimization and search loop that adaptively allocates capacity and tunes parameters. First, vigilance-driven plane growth: replace fixed cell-planes with on-demand allocation. Per pattern, in each S-column find the best-matching representative response; if it falls below a per-layer vigilance threshold theta_v, spawn a new cell-plane seeded from that column, else reinforce the existing winner. Detector count per layer becomes an emergent search outcome, a computational analog of retinotectal and hypercolumn adaptive coverage. Second, weak top-down feedback: add a scalar discriminability objective at the top C-layer measuring collisions and fragmentation, echoing Rosenblatt corrective reinforcement applied only at the output. Third, coarse-to-fine parameter search: treat selectivity threshold, inhibitory efficacy r_l, and theta_v as a low-dimensional space optimized by hill-climbing or coordinate descent to maximize discriminability while penalizing total planes allocated, replacing brittle hand-tuning. Fourth, active presentation scheduling: oversample patterns currently causing colliding or ambiguous top responses. Evaluation: re-implement the 7-layer digit network and the 10-digit task it failed at 24 planes; report planes allocated versus classes learned, accuracy under parameter perturbation versus the original collapse, and invariance to presentation order. Ablate growth, feedback tuning, and curriculum separately.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 19 turns / 30 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron and cognitron learn a shift-invariant S-cell/C-cell hierarchy by learning without a teacher, forming categories only from geometric similarity via winner-take-all reinforcement of representative cells, with no behavioral relevance or top-down signal. Rosenblatt perceptron work develops the complementary reinforcement paradigm but lacks invariance. The physiology sources (Hubel and Wiesel hypercolumns, Gross inferotemporal grandmother-like neurons, and inferotemporal single units recorded during a monkey discrimination task) show recognition units are modulated by task and behavior, not geometry alone; retinotectal plasticity models supply activity-dependent competition. Gap: the neocognitron cites IT grandmother cells and retinotectal competition yet remains bottom-up, teacher-free, noise-fragile, and forms only geometry-aligned categories. No source links the competitive self-organizing hierarchy to reward and attention-modulated plasticity. Bridging them explains how an invariant hierarchy acquires behaviorally meaningful categories under noisy, sparsely-supervised conditions.

**Method.** Relax the neocognitron unsupervised, noise-free, geometry-only assumptions and extend it with sparse reinforcement and attention. Keep the S-layer and C-layer cascade for invariance. Add a three-factor plasticity rule gating representative-cell reinforcement by a global delayed reward signal from a discrimination task, so rewarded trials strengthen active representatives and unrewarded trials weaken them and recruit a fresh cell-plane, fusing reinforcement with competition as an analogue of outcome-gated retinotectal competition. Add top-down attentional gain from the grandmother layer biasing which S-columns win, modeling IT task-modulation and letting categories cut across geometric similarity. Train under noise and sparse reward on a task whose categories conflict with geometric clustering. Evaluate against pure neocognitron, plain perceptron, and a no-attention variant, measuring behaviorally-appropriate grandmother cells, robustness, sample efficiency, and invariance, comparing unit task-modulation to the IT data and hypercolumns.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Fukushima's neocognitron (1980) cites two robustness-related evidence streams as inspiration yet never operationalizes them, leaving a bridge opportunity. It justifies its unsupervised learning by pointing to retinotectal plasticity, noting that after removal of half the tectum the whole retina makes a compressed orderly projection onto the remaining half (Meyer and Sperry retinotectal review, 1974), and it motivates its top layer with inferotemporal-cortex neurons whose responses are scarcely affected by position or size (Gross et al., 1972; responsiveness of IT single units during discrimination, 1980). These document graceful degradation, self-repair after lesion, and robust invariance. In contrast, the engineering recognizers (Rosenblatt's perceptron, 1962; four-layer series-coupled perceptron, 1962; the cognitron) were brittle to shift and distortion; the neocognitron, built on Hubel and Wiesel's simple/complex hierarchy and hypercolumns (1962, 1977), fixes position invariance but the author reports new brittleness: it forbids redundancy (no two S-planes may detect the same feature), tolerates only small distortion, and a small deviation in parameters or presentation order critically degrades the self-organized network. The gap is that the biological robustness principles cited as inspiration are exactly the fault-tolerance the model lacks, because winner-take-all, redundancy-forbidding self-organization is fragile to lesions, noise, larger distortion, and training order. Connecting retinotectal-regeneration and IT-cortex robustness evidence to this architecture matters for trustworthy recognition and has never been done at the level of concrete mechanism.

**Method.** Build and evaluate a regenerative neocognitron that robustifies self-organizing hierarchical recognition by turning cited-but-unused biological principles into mechanisms, each targeting a documented failure mode. Mechanism one, redundant non-exclusive feature allocation, targets lesion brittleness: relax the rule forbidding two S-planes from encoding the same feature by allowing a controlled multiplicity per feature, softening representative selection so several strong S-cells in a column reinforce toward the same feature, giving overlapping distributed codes like IT-cortex population coding and a substrate for graceful degradation. Mechanism two, retinotectal-style self-repair via continual plasticity, targets damage and novelty: keep synapses modifiable after training and monitor activity so that when a C-plane or S-column goes silent under a simulated lesion, or an input weakly drives the top layer, reinforcement re-opens and surviving cells with nearby receptive fields re-select as representatives and re-tune to reconstitute the lost feature map, an analog of the compressed reprojection onto the remaining tectum. Mechanism three, ensemble and consolidation reinforcement, targets order and parameter sensitivity: replace hard winner-take-all with soft temperature-controlled reinforcement over the top candidates plus slow consolidation using running-average synaptic templates. Evaluation reuses the paper's digit and letter stimuli on a simulator. For lesion robustness, delete growing fractions of S-cells and planes and measure accuracy and recovery time with and without self-repair, expecting graceful degradation and recovery mirroring retinotectal regeneration. For noise and distortion tolerance, measure accuracy under pixel noise and larger geometric distortion, scale, and shift, testing whether redundancy widens the tolerated distortion band toward IT-like invariance. For training stability, run many randomized presentation orders and parameter perturbations and measure variance in final accuracy, testing whether ensemble and consolidation remove the reported critical sensitivity. For neuro-alignment, verify top-layer units keep single-pattern selectivity yet respond invariantly across position and size, and that the population sustains readout after partial lesion. Ablations isolate each mechanism, and success is judged comparatively against a faithful baseline neocognitron: equal or better clean accuracy plus substantially lower failure rates under lesions, noise, distortion, and randomized training.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** The perceptron tradition (Rosenblatt: reinforcement-driven, task-oriented, aiming at selective attention) and the neocognitron tradition (Fukushima: unsupervised self-organization of a position-invariant Hubel-Wiesel simple/complex-cell hierarchy that learns without a teacher and is purely feedforward) are disconnected learning literatures. Their cited neurophysiology is likewise split between Hubel-Wiesel feedforward receptive-field buildup and Gross's finding that inferotemporal neurons are modulated by the discrimination task, with retinotectal work framing plasticity as activity-dependent. No model bridges reinforcement/attention learning with an unsupervised invariant hierarchy, nor reconciles the feedforward-buildup and task-modulated evidence streams. Each tradition supplies what the other lacks: invariance versus behavioral steerability.

**Method.** Unify both traditions in one network governed by one plasticity law. Architecture: neocognitron S/C modules for invariance, capped by perceptron association/response stages, with top-down feedback from response back to cell-planes. Unified rule: synaptic change equals a Hebbian competitive term (neocognitron winner reinforcement) times two modulators, a reward signal and an attention gate. With neutral reward and no attention it reduces exactly to Fukushima unsupervised self-organization; with corrective reward at higher stages it reduces to Rosenblatt reinforcement, making learning with and without a teacher two regimes of one law. Feedback implements selective attention that biases which features are reinforced, so rewarded discriminations sculpt which invariant features and high-level categories form, modeling Gross task-modulated inferotemporal neurons; a retinotectal-inspired schedule stabilizes lower modules first while keeping upper modules plastic and reward-gated. Validation: on patterns varied in position, size, and shape, compare a pure neocognitron, a pure perceptron, and the unified model on shift-invariant discrimination accuracy, alignment of emergent categories with the rewarded task, and emergence of task-dependent modulation matching inferotemporal data.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Every biologically-inspired recognition model in this corpus is justified by an asserted correspondence to real visual neurons, yet that correspondence is never measured. Hubel and Wiesel (1962) built their simple/complex-cell taxonomy on precise, reproducible protocols: mapping on/off subregions with spots, sweeping slit orientation where a 5 to 10 degree change abolishes a simple cell's response, testing movement direction, and separating simple from complex cells by position dependence versus invariance. Gross et al. (1972) characterized inferotemporal grandmother cells by size- and position-invariant responses to complex shapes. Fukushima's neocognitron and cognitron, and Rosenblatt's perceptron, explicitly claim their internal units correspond to simple, complex, hypercomplex, and grandmother cells, and that invariance and receptive-field size grow up the hierarchy. But the evidence is anecdotal: the neocognitron paper validates only that each final-layer C-cell fires for one trained digit and that this last layer is roughly position and size invariant; it never applies the neurophysiologists' stimulus batteries and classification criteria to its internal S and C cells. The field has no standardized instrument to observe an artificial network's units the way an electrophysiologist observes neurons, quantify how closely they match measured biological distributions, compare models on a common biological yardstick, or accumulate such evidence across model generations. This matters because these models' scientific purpose is to be clues to the neural mechanism of recognition, so biological plausibility must become empirical rather than rhetorical.

**Method.** Build NeuroProbe, a model-agnostic virtual-electrophysiology benchmarking platform that audits any layered recognition network against the neurophysiology in this corpus. First, a standardized stimulus battery of parametric generators reproducing the source protocols in retinotopic coordinates: spots for on/off mapping; oriented slits, edges, and bars swept over position, fine orientation steps, and length for end-stopping; moving stimuli for direction selectivity; and Gross-style complex shapes such as triangles, hands, and silhouettes rendered at varied positions and sizes. Stimuli map onto each model's input array so the same battery drives perceptron, cognitron, and neocognitron. Second, a virtual-electrode harness: a uniform API that records each unit's activation during the battery, reconstructing receptive-field maps via systematic sweeps and reverse correlation, orientation and direction tuning curves, and position and size response profiles, layer by layer, mimicking a microelectrode penetration through the hierarchy. Third, a metric library operationalizing the neurophysiologists' own criteria: an orientation-selectivity index and tuning half-width calibrated to the 5 to 10 degree standard, on/off subregion segregation as a simple-cell criterion, summation versus within-receptive-field position invariance as the simple-versus-complex criterion, an end-stopping index, a direction-selectivity index, and size and position invariance indices as the grandmother criterion. It auto-classifies each artificial unit into the biological taxonomy and summarizes receptive-field-size and invariance gradients across depth. Fourth, a reference database and scorecard: digitize reported biological distributions such as cell-type proportions, invariance, and tuning as targets, and compute a correspondence score measuring divergence between a model's per-layer property distributions and the biological reference. Output an auditable per-model scorecard and leaderboard that tests specific claims, such as whether the neocognitron S-layer shows simple-cell on/off structure and whether C-cells show graded complex and hypercomplex invariance and receptive-field growth, diagnoses deviations, and enables cumulative comparison of models and future variants. To validate, run NeuroProbe on the self-organized neocognitron reproducing its five-digit training regime, on the cognitron predicting weaker invariance, and on a trained perceptron, showing it recovers known qualitative differences as reproducible quantitative scores and surfaces mismatches invisible to output-only evaluation, turning untested biological-plausibility assertions into measurable, comparable, and accumulable evidence.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The self-organizing hierarchical models here (Rosenblatt's series-coupled perceptron, Fukushima's cognitron, and the neocognitron) justify their architecture by claiming their self-organized units resemble neurons from the visual neurophysiology papers: orientation-selective, increasingly position-invariant simple, complex, and hypercomplex cells (Hubel and Wiesel) and complex-feature, position and size invariant grandmother-like units of inferotemporal cortex (Gross et al. 1972; Sato et al. 1980). The neocognitron explicitly maps S-cells to simple and hypercomplex cells and C-cells to complex cells. Yet evidence for these claims is purely qualitative, resting on inspecting a few trained weight maps. There is no shared quantitative protocol that probes artificial and biological units with the same stimuli and scores them with the same metrics, no benchmark stimulus battery drawn from the physiology experiments, and no standardized measurement of how tuning properties progress layer-by-layer. So the field cannot observe or audit whether unsupervised self-organization genuinely reproduces neurophysiological tuning statistics, cannot validate the biological rationale motivating these architectures, and cannot compare model variants on common evidence. This is an evidence gap: we lack instrumentation to measure and accumulate comparable evidence for the biological-fidelity claims on which the modeling program rests.

**Method.** Build a neurophysiology-in-silico benchmark and diagnostic that applies the classic single-unit probing protocols uniformly to the artificial units of these models and scores correspondence with reported physiology. First, a curated stimulus battery reconstructs as digital input patterns the stimuli described in the physiology sources: oriented slits, bars, and edges swept over orientation, position, length, width, and contrast (Hubel and Wiesel protocol), stationary versus moving variants, plus complex object silhouettes as used for IT recordings; each class is parameterized so the same sweep can drive any layer. Second, standardized metrics are computed identically for every unit at every stage: orientation tuning curve and half-width bandwidth, orientation-selectivity index, position-invariance index, size-invariance index, stationary-versus-moving preference, and a feature-complexity score. This converts static weight maps into physiology-comparable tuning profiles. Third, a layer-wise fidelity comparison runs the battery on each model after self-organization to produce per-stage metric distributions and compares them to quantitative summaries extracted from the physiology papers, yielding per-stage correspondence scores and an invariance-growth curve across the hierarchy to test the claimed simple-to-complex-to-IT progression. Fourth, as a diagnostic, sweep architectural and training knobs (lateral-inhibition efficacy, plane-wise competition, number of stages, supervised perceptron layers versus unsupervised cognitron rule, and training-set composition) and measure how each changes correspondence scores and the invariance-growth curve. All models are fully specified and directly simulatable on the input arrays they were designed for, and the probing stimuli and target tuning descriptions are given in the sources, so no external data are needed. Deliverables are the reusable stimulus battery, metric definitions, and per-model per-layer fidelity reports.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** The cognitron and neocognitron claim their cells resemble Hubel and Wiesel simple and complex cells and invariant inferotemporal neurons, yet this is asserted only qualitatively and never measured or benchmarked. This matters because the brain-like justification for these models rests on an unverified analogy.

**Method.** Build a virtual electrophysiology benchmark that probes units of the perceptron, cognitron, and neocognitron with digitized stimulus batteries from these sources and scores each layer against biological tuning and invariance statistics to form a layer to cortex map.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** The perceptron (Block, Knight, Rosenblatt 1962), cognitron (Fukushima 1975/1981), and neocognitron (Fukushima 1980), inspired by the Hubel and Wiesel 1962 simple-to-complex hierarchy, all rest on a claim of invariance (recognition unaffected by shift in position) produced by alternating feature-detection stages (S-cells/simple cells) and pooling stages (C-cells/complex cells). Yet the evidence is anecdotal and the field lacks any way to observe, quantify, compare, or predict invariance. The neocognitron is shown on a few hand-crafted patterns; Fukushima notes tiny parameter changes critically influenced performance, with no account of how much invariance the pooling ranges buy or how it grows with depth. The cognitron is called position-dependent and the neocognitron position-invariant, but no graded measure makes this more than a binary anecdote, and Hubel and Wiesel describe complex-cell position tolerance only qualitatively.
