Prove that under a feature-sparsity condition (bounded features per pool window) the deep gradual hierarchy strictly dominates the shallow one: it achieves the same T with exponentially higher N. This is a formal statement of how "selective AND invariant" IT-type responses are possible — invariance built compositionally, not by one large blur.

4. Optimal schedule and predictions. Casting "maximize T subject to selectivity ≥ target" as an optimization over the per-layer pooling radii {ρ_ℓ} yields, via the additivity of costs and multiplicativity of tolerance, an equalized (geometric) growth schedule: constant ratio ρ_{ℓ+1}/ρ_ℓ, i.e., receptive fields should expand gradually and by a fixed factor per stage — a derived explanation for the Hubel–Wiesel simple→complex→hypercomplex RF-growth progression and a concrete prescription for scheduling r_z / pool size layer-by-layer in the neocognitron (small tolerance increments per stage rather than one late invariant readout).

5. Validation. (i) Analytic: prove the bound and the dominance theorem for the lattice model. (ii) Simulation on the neocognitron itself: vary the pooling schedule at fixed final tolerance and measure N (patterns kept separable) and false-binding rate on part-rearranged stimuli, testing the predicted geometric-schedule optimum and the superposition-catastrophe scaling with pool area. (iii) Physiological consistency: check that the predicted per-stage tolerance/selectivity accumulation reproduces the reported combination of position/size invariance and shape specificity in inferotemporal neurons and the graded RF enlargement in striate/prestriate cortex.

This turns the qualitative "compromise" acknowledged by Fukushima and the biological puzzle raised by Gross and Hubel–Wiesel into a derived tradeoff bound, and shows precisely which architectural discipline (many shallow, conjunction-interleaved pools on a geometric schedule) escapes it.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron is presented as autonomous self-organization, yet the paper admits recognizing 10 digits works only when parameters are properly adjusted and patterns skillfully presented, and that small deviations break the network. Its success secretly depends on a designer hand-picking the number of cell-planes, the selectivity and inhibition parameters, and the presentation order. This is a tradeoff: weak selectivity gives shift and deformation tolerance but merges distinct patterns; strong selectivity separates categories but splits deformed instances and exhausts representatives. Fukushima only conjectures that more planes would help, giving no mechanism to set plane count or selectivity. Hubel and Wiesel show cortex uses a finite set of detectors with no designer fixing their number; retinotectal plasticity models show maps forming by activity-dependent competition; IT cortex shows sparse selective cells. Biology allocates resources adaptively; the neocognitron does not, which blocks scaling.

**Method.** Discover the neocognitron hand-set quantities by unsupervised search on internal competition statistics, treating selectivity versus invariance as resource allocation. Adaptive plane allocation: start minimal, keep the winner-per-column representative rule, add a novelty score comparing winner to runner-up; add a new plane when a pattern repeatedly activates cells no representative claims, and prune or merge planes with correlated weights or low win rate. Selectivity tuning: optimize each layer selectivity parameter by gradient-free hill climbing against a label-free objective maximizing discrimination margin subject to an invariance target measured with injected jitter and deformation. Active presentation: pick the next stimulus favoring patterns whose representatives are least stable, removing order sensitivity. Evaluate on the digit task: reproduce the fragile 10-digit case with no hand tuning at a self-discovered plane count, sweep category count to quantify capacity scaling, and ablate each loop for robustness versus the fixed baseline.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 29 turns / 38 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (Fukushima 1980), grounded in the cognitron, Hubel-Wiesel, and inferotemporal grandmother-cell work, is celebrated for learning without a teacher, its warrant being robust autonomous biological self-organization such as retinotectal regeneration (Meyer and Sperry 1974). Paradox: the authors admit that with more classes, small parameter or presentation-order changes critically degrade it, and success needed skillfully presented patterns, tuned parameters, and a fixed pre-set number of cell-planes. So the most teacher-free paradigm is actually the most brittle and covertly engineered, contradicting the robust biology it models. It was shown only on clean single centered patterns, not novelty, clutter, or arbitrary order. Robustness of unsupervised feature formation, not just invariance, is what makes it a credible model of cortical development and a usable recognizer.

**Method.** Relax assumptions to make neocognitron self-organization robust. Add a per-column vigilance test: when no representative S-cell's normalized activation exceeds a similarity threshold, recruit a new cell-plane initialized to that feature instead of forcing a poor match, so capacity grows with data and late-introduced classes get detectors; merge duplicate planes and prune dead ones. Add homeostatic regulation: each plane adjusts its shunting-inhibition gain by how often it wins, spreading representation regardless of order or class frequency and removing the global tuned inhibition parameter. Broaden the regime with randomized order, noise, jitter, variable size, occlusion, and novel patterns. Evaluate variance of recognition across random parameter and order perturbations versus the fixed-plane baseline, sub-linear plane growth with class count, incremental learning without catastrophic reallocation, and a retinotectal-style self-repair test after removing planes. Baselines: original fixed-capacity neocognitron and a recruitment-only ablation without homeostasis.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 13 tool calls

**Motivation.** A striking paradox sits at the heart of the neocognitron line of work. The entire purpose of the neocognitron (Fukushima, 1980), building on the cognitron and on the Hubel & Wiesel simple/complex-cell hierarchy, is robustness: the trained network recognizes patterns "unaffected by shift in position," and tolerant to size change, small shape distortion, noise, and partial occlusion. Yet Fukushima explicitly reports the opposite property for the process that *builds* this robust network. With ten patterns, "a small deviation of the values of the parameters, or a small change of the way of pattern presentation, has critically influenced upon the ability of the self-organized network." In other words, the same system that is engineered to be invariant to variation in its *inputs* is exquisitely fragile to variation in its own *construction* — parameter settings, presentation order, and (in the cognitron reinforcement rule it inherits) which single "representative"/winner S-cell happens to win a column competition. This is a genuine contradiction: robust genesis is promised by "learning without a teacher," but the outcome is reproducible only under skillful, hand-tuned presentation.

Why it matters: (1) The scientific claim of unsupervised self-organization is undermined if the resulting recognizer depends on lucky parameter/order choices rather than on the statistics of the stimuli; results are then non-reproducible and untrustworthy. (2) It sharpens the biological tension — the retinotectal plasticity literature and the orderly hypercolumn maps of Hubel & Wiesel show that nervous systems form reliable, orderly feature maps despite noise and varied experience, contradicting the fragility of the model that is supposed to imitate them. (3) The prior works leave the gap open: the neocognitron only conjectures that simply adding more cell-planes might help (never tested), the cognitron supplies the very winner-take-all reinforcement rule that causes the knife-edge sensitivity, and the perceptron convergence results (Principles of Neurodynamics; four-layer series-coupled perceptron) address supervised learning, not this unsupervised map-formation stability. No source provides a mechanism that makes the self-organization itself robust, nor a way to measure that robustness.

**Method.** Propose "stability-driven self-organization": a robustification of the neocognitron's unsupervised learning that makes the formed network reproducible and insensitive to parameters, initialization, and presentation order, while preserving its input-invariance. Four coupled mechanisms, all implementable in the same simulation framework used by Fukushima:

1) Soft, redundancy-controlled reinforcement instead of a single winner. Replace the "select one representative per S-plane by largest output" rule with graded reinforcement of all strongly co-activated candidate S-cells, weighted by their activity normalized within an S-column (divisive/shunting normalization already present for C-cells). This removes the discontinuity where a tiny activity difference flips which cell — and thus which feature-plane — gets committed, the primary source of order/parameter sensitivity.

2) Adaptive cell-plane allocation with a vigilance criterion. Rather than a fixed 24 planes (which Fukushima suspects is "not sufficient"), monitor how well the best-matching existing S-plane explains a strongly activated local feature; if the match falls below a vigilance threshold, recruit a fresh S-plane seeded on that feature, and periodically merge near-duplicate planes. This guarantees adequate capacity and makes feature-to-plane assignment depend on *what* features occur, not on *when* they first appear, directly neutralizing presentation-order effects.

3) Consolidation via slow commitment and interleaved replay. Accumulate candidate synaptic changes over many interleaved presentations (a slow learning phase) before committing structure (fast phase), so the map converges to statistics-driven fixed points. Ground the lateral, topographic self-correction in the orderly activity-dependent map formation seen in retinotectal plasticity and cortical hypercolumns, adding normalized lateral interactions so nearby S-columns cooperate/compete to form a reproducible, self-healing feature map.

4) A quantitative robustness objective and diagnostic. Define reproducibility metrics: variability of learned feature-to-plane assignments (via matching) and of final recognition accuracy across (a) random parameter perturbations, (b) random initial connections, and (c) shuffled presentation orders. Evaluate the original neocognitron versus each added mechanism (ablations) on Fukushima's digit/letter tasks plus adversarially reordered and noisy training streams, reporting both invariance-to-input (shift/distortion tolerance, which must be preserved) and invariance-to-construction (the new metrics). Success is turning the anecdotal fragility into a measured, near-eliminated property: comparable recognition with sharply reduced outcome variance, without hand-tuned "skillful" presentation. This addresses the paradox by making the mechanism that grants input-robustness itself robust to how it is built.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** A paradox runs through these works about spatial position in recognition. Fukushima treats position-dependence as a defect: the cognitron (1975) recognized patterns but its response was dependent upon position, while the neocognitron (1980) is celebrated because its last-layer C-cells are not affected by the pattern's position at all. Invariance is achieved by a mechanism whose explicit purpose is to destroy location: S-cell feature detection plus C-cell spatial pooling, ending in a grandmother cell that fires regardless of where the object is. Yet the biology cited points the opposite way. Hubel and Wiesel (1962) show cortex is built on a preserved retinotopic architecture; inferotemporal neurons (Gross 1972; the 1980 discrimination study) are invariant yet retain position and size tuning while the animal must localize and act; and the neocognitron justifies its plasticity rule by appealing to retina-to-tectum map formation, whose function (retinotectal neuroplasticity models, 1974) is to PRESERVE position. So the same activity-dependent competitive learning is credited both with building position invariance and with building precise topographic maps: conflicting goals. This plausibly causes the neocognitron's brittleness (parameter sensitivity, one grandmother cell per pattern, failure with multiple patterns), since a system that discards position cannot localize, re-bind identity to place, or segregate objects. Neither Rosenblatt's position-sensitive perceptron, the position-preserving cognitron, nor the position-discarding neocognitron explains how one self-organizing substrate could yield both invariant identity and preserved location.

**Method.** Unify the neocognitron's invariance-by-pooling hierarchy with the retinotectal and Hubel-Wiesel tradition of activity-dependent topographic-map preservation into one self-organizing network where invariance and localization are complementary read-outs of a shared feature substrate. First, keep the neocognitron S/C cascade and its unsupervised representative-cell competitive rule so a what pathway grows position-invariant category cells. Second, reinterpret C-cell pooling: each C-cell also emits a positional tag, the retinotopic coordinate of the maximally responding S-cell in its pool. Route these tags into a parallel where map, a self-organizing topographic layer trained by a retinotectal-style activity-dependent refinement rule (the same competitive plasticity, used to MAINTAIN retinotopy rather than blur it), so one learning principle grows both map-preserving and map-abstracting layers from the same afferents. Third, add reentrant feedback in the spirit of Rosenblatt's four-layer series-coupled perceptron: a last-layer identity cell projects back to re-activate its topographic locus on the where map, closing the binding loop so the network reports both what and where. Fourth, use this feedback for multi-object handling by sequential attention: the identity selects one object's location, that location is suppressed, and the cascade re-runs on the next object. Evaluate on Fukushima's stimuli (X, Y, T, Z, digits) plus multi-object scenes, testing that it matches neocognitron shift-invariant classification while also recovering locations, handles multiple simultaneous objects that collapse the neocognitron, and predicts IT-like units that are identity-invariant yet carry residual position and size tuning with a double dissociation (lesioning the where pathway abolishes localization but spares recognition). Thus position need not be destroyed to obtain invariance: it is factored out as a tag and preserved on a co-trained topographic map, reconciling the cognitron's localization, the neocognitron's invariance, and the retinotectal and Hubel-Wiesel evidence for maintained spatial maps.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** Resource-bottleneck gap. These sources established a still-underexploited property: position-invariant pattern recognition can be acquired WITHOUT a teacher. Fukushima's cognitron and neocognitron show that repeatedly presenting unlabeled stimuli, plus local synaptic reinforcement and complex-cell (C-cell) pooling, builds shift tolerance mirroring the Hubel-Wiesel simple/complex/hypercomplex hierarchy, IT-cortex invariant selectivity, and activity-dependent retinotectal reorganization. Yet the REALIZATION of these models, not the theory, is the bottleneck. Compute/time: the neocognitron was only a serial digital simulation on tiny hand-curated stimulus sets, and its learning step (globally selecting one representative/seed cell per S-plane and reinforcing it) is inherently serial and unscalable. Usability: the number of feature planes and selectivity thresholds must be set by hand per layer, making experiments labor-intensive and non-reproducible. Deployment friction: the global winner-selection offers no path to a parallel physical device that learns online from a live sensor. Thus these models solve the labeling bottleneck but stay blocked by compute, scalability, tuning burden, and deployment. Nothing in the provided works removes these barriers: the perceptron needs supervised correction, and cognitron/neocognitron exist only as small serial simulations. This leaves clear room for a systems contribution.

**Method.** Artifact/System contribution: build a tiled, mixed-signal self-organizing cortical-column array (prototyped on FPGA plus analog front-end, with an open mapping toolchain) that physically instantiates the neocognitron's alternating S-cell/C-cell module stack so unsupervised, position-invariant recognition self-organizes online from a live camera stream, removing the serial-simulation, hand-tuning, and deployment bottlenecks. Components: (1) Column tiles: each tile realizes one hypercolumn/S-column with analog convolution over a fixed local connectable area (e.g. 5x5) implementing simple-cell receptive fields with shunting inhibition as in the neocognitron; tiles replicate across the field for spatial parallelism. (2) Local competitive plasticity: replace the global representative search with on-tile lateral-inhibition winner-take-all within each column, reinforcing the winner's synapses by the local Hebbian r.m.s.-cognitron rule, so learning is fully parallel and streams in real time. (3) Growth instead of manual allocation: an activity monitor commits a dormant plane as a new feature detector whenever all existing winners fall below a floor (inspired by retinotectal reorganization), removing pre-specified plane counts. (4) C-cell pooling tiles: fixed spatial averaging between modules gives progressive shift tolerance so deeper modules acquire IT-like invariance. (5) Self-tuning controller: a lightweight on-device loop adjusts each layer's selectivity threshold and reinforcement rate from running activity statistics (target one-winner-per-plane sparsity), removing manual tuning. (6) Toolchain: a description language to stack arbitrary S/C module depths, map them onto tiles, and log energy, wall-clock self-organization time, and accuracy. Evaluation: stream unlabeled character/shape and digit imagery and measure whether last-layer cells become one-pattern-selective and shift-invariant (reproducing the neocognitron result) but learned online; scaling of self-organization time and energy versus equivalent digital simulation as input size and plane count grow; and robustness of the growth and self-tuning mechanisms. Success: label-free invariant recognition self-organizing at frame rate at scales serial simulation cannot reach, with no manual plane allocation or threshold tuning, turning a proven-but-unscalable model into a deployable edge-vision artifact.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (Fukushima 1980) shows a Hubel-Wiesel-style hierarchy of S-cell/C-cell modules can self-organize without a teacher into a shift- and distortion-tolerant recognizer. But the paper is explicit that its limits are resource-driven, not conceptual. First, fixed capacity limited by memory: it hard-codes 24 cell-planes per layer, states 24 are not sufficient for recognizing ten different patterns, that more planes would likely help, but that simulating more than 24 planes has not been done because of the lack of memory capacity of the computer. Each plane also maintains a full retinotopic activation array, so dense S/C-cell arrays are the true memory wall. Second, high tuning and experimental burden: ten-class success held only provided that parameters are properly adjusted and stimulus patterns are skillfully presented, and a small deviation of parameter values, or a small change in the way of presentation, critically influenced the outcome. Selectivity thresholds, reinforcement speeds and presentation order are all hand-set and brittle. Thus the model's demonstrated capability cannot scale to more classes or larger inputs without prohibitive memory and manual tuning cost. The gap: let such a self-organizing hierarchy consume only as much memory and parameter-supervision as a task actually requires.

**Method.** Build a resource-adaptive, self-regulating neocognitron that keeps the S/C-cell hierarchy, weight-sharing within a cell-plane, and unsupervised winner-take-all representative reinforcement, but makes three rationed resources demand-driven. First, novelty-triggered plane recruitment: start each S-layer with zero or one plane; on each presentation run the existing representative-selection competition, and if the best existing plane's match to a strongly active S-column falls below a criterion, recruit a new plane seeded from that column's afferent pattern using the same reinforcement rule. Planes are added only for genuinely new features, so capacity grows to fit the data, no memory is wasted on empty or redundant planes, and allocation no longer depends on skillful presentation order. Second, homeostatic threshold auto-tuning: replace hand-set selectivity parameters with a per-plane control loop that tracks recent firing statistics and adjusts each plane's threshold to hold a target sparsity, sharpening promiscuous planes and loosening or pruning silent ones, removing brittle manual tuning. Third, sparse retinotopic instantiation: exploit progressive C-cell pooling and spatial sparsity by computing and storing S- and C-cell responses only where presynaptic C-cells are active; since a plane is one shared kernel, the recognizer becomes shared kernels plus a sparse active-site list, collapsing the dominant activation-memory cost. Evaluation: reproduce the original digital-simulation protocol (shifted and distorted digits 0-9, then confusable X/Y/T/Z), measuring accuracy, planes auto-recruited per layer, peak memory, and sensitivity to parameter and presentation-order perturbations, versus the fixed-24-plane hand-tuned baseline. Success: match or exceed baseline accuracy while recruiting only needed planes, using far less activation memory, and staying flat across randomized parameter initializations and presentation orders. All ingredients exist in the source; the contribution is making each demand-driven so the model scales under fixed modest memory and without expert tuning.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The cognitron (Fukushima 1975/1981) and neocognitron (Fukushima 1980), grounded in the Hubel-Wiesel simple/complex-cell hierarchy (1962) and position-invariant inferotemporal responses (Gross 1972; Sato 1980), achieve unsupervised shift-invariant recognition, but their use is throttled by a resource bottleneck the papers themselves flag. The neocognitron recognizes ten patterns only when parameters are properly adjusted and patterns skillfully presented, with small parameter or presentation-order changes critically degrading it, and scaling past 24 cell-planes was impossible for lack of computer memory. Progress is thus limited by memory scalability, training burden, and blind parameter tuning, with no way to predict how much capacity or training a task needs. Prior works give mechanisms but no empirical map of how performance and invariance trade off against these resource costs, leaving the models costly, irreproducible, and impossible to provision rationally.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map (benchmark plus diagnostic) of the resource-performance frontier for these networks. Reimplement the cognitron and neocognitron as described, with S/C-cell modules, cell-planes, winner-take-all reinforcement, inhibitory ratio, selectivity threshold, and connectable-area size. Use standardized stimulus batteries of graded difficulty from the sources: digits 0-9; confusable sets like X/Y/T/Z; controlled shift, size, and distortion suites; and feature-complexity ladders motivated by the simple-to-hypercomplex progression and IT-cortex selectivity. Sweep three resource axes: cell-planes per layer for memory, number and order of presentations for training time and data, and core parameters. Measure accuracy and confusion structure, invariance range, convergence in presentations to stable weights, stability, and per-parameter sensitivity. Deliver empirical scaling laws and phase diagrams predicting minimal cell-planes and presentations as a function of pattern-set size and inter-pattern similarity, a sensitivity diagnostic ranking which parameters and presentation orders dominate failure, a head-to-head cognitron-versus-neocognitron comparison on one battery, and a capacity-planning predictor that estimates required resources for a target pattern set. This replaces trial-and-error tuning with measured up-front provisioning, and is feasible because results were originally simulation-based and modern hardware removes the 24-cell-plane ceiling.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron self-organizes shift-invariant features but its scalability is a resource bottleneck: cell-planes per layer are fixed by hand, 24 was reported insufficient for ten patterns, and more could not be simulated due to lack of computer memory. No principle says how many feature planes a layer needs, forcing wasteful over-provisioning or failure-prone under-provisioning.

**Method.** Derive a formal per-layer capacity theory for simple and complex cell hierarchies. Model each S-layer as a normalized similarity vector quantizer and each C-layer as position quotient pooling. Show winner take all reinforcement is ascent on a competitive learning objective with a separation margin set by inhibition. Prove minimal cell planes per layer equals the covering number of the pooled feature space, with a recursion showing pooling shrinks it. Convert this into a novelty triggered grow and prune rule that builds a minimal covering, giving memory optimal capacity without hand tuning, validated on the neocognitron digit simulation.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (Fukushima 1980), building on the cognitron (1975) and the Hubel-Wiesel simple/complex-cell hierarchy (1962, 1965, 1977), achieves self-organized position-invariant recognition, but the paper itself names concrete resource bottlenecks. Memory and compute: the number of cell-planes per layer is fixed by hand (24), and Fukushima states this was not sufficient for ten patterns, that more planes would help, but that this was not simulated because of the lack of memory capacity of the computer. Expert time and fragility: correct recognition needs parameters properly adjusted and patterns skillfully presented, and a small deviation of parameters or presentation order critically degrades the network. Training cost: self-organization requires uniform repeated presentation of the full stimulus set regardless of what is already learned. These bottlenecks (memory from over-provisioning planes, human tuning time, presentation cost) block scaling to more patterns. Prior works fix plane count, thresholds, and presentation order by hand and never treat their selection as something to discover efficiently, leaving room for an automatic, resource-frugal approach.

**Method.** Replace hand-tuned self-organization with a resource-adaptive procedure cast as optimization and search over plane allocation, thresholds, and presentation schedule, targeting equal-or-better recognition with the fewest planes, least tuning, and fewest presentations. Three mechanisms. Grow-and-prune plane allocation: keep winner-take-all representative reinforcement but make plane count data-driven; if a presented local feature is not covered above threshold by any existing plane, allocate a new S-plane seeded from that S-column, and periodically merge or prune planes with highly correlated response maps, converging to a minimal detector set that spends memory only where features occur. Automatic threshold search: the per-layer selectivity threshold controls granularity and plane count, so run a coarse-to-fine bisection or hill-climb driven by measured last-layer confusions, raising selectivity where categories stay confused and lowering it where planes are redundant, yielding a stable error-minimizing operating point. Active presentation scheduling: choose the next pattern and its shift to maximize expected novelty or current inter-class confusion, concentrating scarce presentations on informative examples and removing hand-crafted ordering. Evaluation reuses the original digital simulation on the paper tasks (digits 0-9 and the confusable X, Y, T, Z set), measuring planes and cells at convergence, presentations to convergence, accuracy, and robustness to parameter perturbation versus the fixed 24-plane uniform-presentation baseline. Success is matching or exceeding accuracy with markedly fewer planes and presentations while tolerating threshold perturbations.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (Fukushima, 1980), extending the cognitron (1975) and modeled on Hubel & Wiesel's hierarchical simple/complex-cell (and hypercolumn) architecture of visual cortex, self-organizes a position-invariant pattern recognizer "without a teacher." But its own conclusion exposes a stark resource bottleneck. First, the number of feature-detecting cell-planes per layer must be fixed and guessed in advance (24 in the paper). Fukushima reports that 24 was already insufficient for reliably recognizing just ten patterns, and that he could not even test larger networks "because of the lack of memory capacity of our computer." Recognition capacity is thus provisioned by worst-case pre-allocation, and scaling is blocked directly by compute/memory. Second, the model works only when "various parameters are properly adjusted and the stimulus patterns are skillfully presented," and "a small deviation of the values of the parameters, or a small change of the way of pattern presentation, has critically influenced" performance. In other words, unsupervised learning is nominally teacher-free but in practice demands expert hand-tuning of inhibition/threshold parameters and expert curation and ordering of the training stream — a heavy experimental, data-access, and deployment burden that undermines the "learning without a teacher" promise. These are the bottlenecks that limit the whole hierarchical self-organizing line of work: you cannot afford to pre-provision cell-planes for the worst case, and you cannot afford an expert to curate presentation order and retune parameters for every new pattern set or noisier, real input. The prior works leave this open: they establish the architecture and the winner-take-all "representative" reinforcement rule but assume a fixed capacity, clean curated presentation, and careful parameter setting.

**Method.** Relax the neocognitron's three fragile assumptions so the same hierarchical self-organization runs under a fixed compute/memory budget, without curated presentation, and under noise. (1) Demand-driven, growable-and-prunable cell-planes. Start each S-layer with a minimal set of planes. Reuse the model's existing representative-selection (per-S-column winner-take-all): when the winning S-cell in a column matches its best existing S-plane below a novelty threshold (normalized correlation of the input patch to that plane's learned weight profile), allocate a new S-plane seeded on that patch instead of forcing it into an ill-fitting plane; symmetrically, merge planes whose learned weight profiles become near-duplicates and prune planes that almost never win. Capacity then tracks the intrinsic feature complexity actually encountered, removing the need to pre-guess or pre-provision cell-planes and letting a small network cover more patterns within a bounded memory budget — the exact experiment blocked in 1980. (2) Self-tuning inhibition and selectivity. Replace the hand-set inhibition efficacy (r_l) and selectivity thresholds with running per-column normalization estimated online from recent S-cell response statistics (mean/variance), so a single adaptive rule replaces per-dataset parameter search, eliminating the reported critical sensitivity to parameter deviation. (3) Presentation-order and noise robustness via replay. Instead of relying on a skillfully ordered stream, keep a small bounded reservoir of high-response feature patches per plane and interleave replayed patches during reinforcement, decorrelating what each plane learns from the accidental order of the input stream; additionally reinforce under injected input noise/degradation so learned features are noise-tolerant. Evaluation: drive the model with an uncurated, randomly ordered, noisy stream of shifted/distorted patterns and compare position-invariant recognition accuracy, final cell-plane count, and total memory against the original fixed 24-plane neocognitron under identical budgets, and against ablations removing each component. Success criterion: match or exceed the fixed network's invariant recognition while (a) self-selecting far fewer planes for easy pattern sets and gracefully growing for hard ones under a memory cap, and (b) requiring no per-dataset parameter tuning or curated ordering — thereby extending the classic hierarchical self-organizing recognizer to weaker-assumption, resource-constrained, noisy, streaming regimes.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Fukushima's neocognitron learns "without a teacher," which should be cheap since labels are the costly resource, but the paper shows this is illusory. For ten patterns the author reports that "a small deviation of the values of the parameters, or a small change of the way of pattern presentation, has critically influenced upon the ability of the self-organized network," and that success required patterns be "skillfully presented" — so a working network demands many hand-tuned simulation runs, costly in compute, time, and expert labor. He also could not test more cell-planes "because of the lack of memory capacity of our computer," using a fixed count (24) that wastes memory yet fails when wrong. The root cause is greedy winner-take-all "representative" seed-cell selection: the S-cell with the largest output monopolizes a feature plane, and which cell wins depends on random initial synapses, parameters, and presentation order, making feature allocation non-reproducible (duplicated features, orphan features never learned) and forcing over-provisioning and re-tuning. No listed source addresses this reliability problem. The opportunity is to remove the resource bottleneck by making teacher-less self-organization reliable and self-scaling.

**Method.** Robustify neocognitron-style S-cell/C-cell self-organization so one hands-off run reliably yields good features with minimal planes. Four components replace winner-take-all seeding. (1) Order-invariant batched reinforcement: accumulate candidate statistics over a full epoch and reinforce from pooled evidence, removing presentation-order sensitivity. (2) Margin-based soft competition: when top candidates in a column are within a tolerance band, reinforce the one in the least-represented plane, decoupling learned features from tiny parameter deviations and random initial synapses. (3) Coverage-driven adaptive plane allocation and pruning: each epoch compute residual coverage error per pattern using existing reinforced templates, add a plane only for persistent orphan features, and merge near-duplicate planes, so plane count grows to the minimum needed instead of a fixed 24. (4) Consensus seeding: run several short passes with different seeds/orders, keep planes recurring across passes, then continue training the consensus set for reproducible feature dictionaries. Evaluate on the neocognitron's own shift-tolerant recognition task (digits/letters with position shift, distortion, noise), measuring reliability: variance of accuracy across random parameters, shuffled orders, and seeds, plus planes and epochs to reach target accuracy. Hypothesis: consistent recognition with lower variance, fewer planes, and no manual tuning, quantifying reduced human, compute, and memory burden while staying faithful to the Hubel–Wiesel S/C hierarchy.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Resource bottleneck. Fukushima's neocognitron (1980), building on the cognitron (1975) and Rosenblatt's perceptron lineage (Principles of Neurodynamics, 1962), showed that a Hubel–Wiesel-style S-cell/C-cell hierarchy can self-organize position-invariant recognition "without a teacher." But the paper is candid about three coupled costs that still throttle progress toward scalable, reliable recognizers built this way:

1. Human/experimental tuning burden. The authors report that with as few as ten patterns, "a small deviation of the values of the parameters, or a small change of the way of pattern presentation, has critically influenced" the outcome, and that patterns had to be "skillfully presented." Self-organization is therefore not an attractor but a fragile trajectory requiring expert, trial-and-error curation of parameters and training curricula — a large, non-scalable labor cost.

2. Compute/memory scarcity. Simulation was capped at 24 cell-planes per layer explicitly "because of the lack of memory capacity of our computer," and the authors could not test whether more planes would fix the ten-pattern failures. The winner-take-all "representative" rule gives no guarantee that the scarce planes are used efficiently (no coverage guarantee), wasting a scarce resource.

3. Costly biological validation. The model is meant to illuminate the brain, yet the reference data — single-unit recordings in cat V1 (Hubel & Wiesel 1962), macaque functional architecture (1977), and inferotemporal cortex (Gross 1972; Sato 1980) — come from extraordinarily expensive, low-throughput, sample-scarce experiments that cannot be re-run per model iteration.

Crucially, an adjacent literature already solved the robustness problem in biology but has stayed disconnected from engineering self-organizing recognizers: explanatory models of retinotectal neuroplasticity (1974) describe how orderly topographic maps form and re-form reproducibly, self-correcting after translocation, rotation, or partial lesion, via activity-dependent Hebbian competition, molecular positional markers/gradients, and systems-matching normalization — with no skillful curriculum or fine parameter tuning. The neocognitron's fragile, ungoverned self-organization leaves exactly the room this developmental theory could fill, but no one has bridged the two.

**Method.** Synthesis/unification. Build a single formal self-organization principle that reconciles three currently separate literatures — (a) the competitive "representative-cell" reinforcement of the cognitron/neocognitron, (b) retinotectal map-formation dynamics (activity-dependent Hebbian competition + positional markers/gradients + systems-matching normalization), and (c) the Hubel–Wiesel columnar functional architecture as the target macro-organization — and use it to make the neocognitron's learning robust and resource-frugal.

Step 1 — Unified update rule. Reformulate each S-layer's plasticity as a common objective in which (i) the neocognitron's winner-take-all reinforcement supplies feature selectivity, (ii) a retinotectal-style lateral competition + activity normalization ("systems matching") term enforces that every plane is claimed by a distinct feature and that the finite set of planes tiles feature space (a coverage/one-feature-per-plane guarantee), and (iii) fixed positional "markers" (analogous to chemoaffinity gradients) anchor which region of feature space each plane is nudged toward, so that organization becomes a stable attractor rather than a presentation-order-dependent trajectory. This single rule specializes back to Fukushima's rule when the competition/marker terms are switched off, making the unification explicit and testable.

Step 2 — Robustness by construction (addresses tuning burden). Because retinotectal models are designed to re-establish order after perturbation, the resulting layer should self-correct. Test this directly: after self-organization, ablate planes, scramble weights, or change pattern-presentation order, and measure whether the map re-forms to the same feature set. Success criterion: near-invariant final feature inventory and accuracy across random seeds, presentation orders, and parameter perturbations — i.e., eliminating the "skillful presentation" and knife-edge parameter dependence the 1980 paper reports.

Step 3 — Frugality (addresses compute/memory scarcity). Use the coverage guarantee to quantify planes-needed vs. accuracy on the same tasks Fukushima used (the ten digits 0–9, and the confusable X/Y/T/Z set). Hypothesis: enforced non-redundant tiling lets far fewer planes than 24 succeed where the ungoverned rule failed, and degrades gracefully rather than collapsing — turning the memory ceiling into a soft trade-off curve.

Step 4 — Reuse scarce physiology as the benchmark (addresses validation cost). Instead of new recordings, treat the existing Hubel–Wiesel V1 simple/complex-cell statistics and the IT-cortex selectivity/invariance reports (Gross 1972; Sato 1980, including behavioral-discrimination responsiveness) as fixed, reusable evaluation datasets. Score whether the unified model reproduces the observed simple→complex receptive-field elaboration, columnar clustering of like-tuned cells, and the emergence of position-invariant complex-pattern selectivity at the top layer — using published data as the resource so validation costs nothing new.

This synthesis addresses the bottleneck by importing biology's proven self-correcting, gradient-anchored map-formation dynamics into the engineering self-organizing hierarchy: it converts fragile, expert-curated training into a robust attractor (cutting labor), guarantees efficient use of scarce cell-planes (cutting compute/memory), and validates against already-collected costly physiology (cutting experimental burden) — all while unifying the developmental-neurobiology, visual-neuroscience, and perceptron/neocognitron traditions under one rule.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. Rosenblatt's perceptrons, Fukushima's Cognitron, and the Neocognitron were motivated by Hubel and Wiesel's visual hierarchy and by inferotemporal findings (Gross et al. 1972, cited in the Neocognitron) that high-level neurons respond to shapes scarcely affected by position OR size. Yet the models fall short of that premise. First, invariance scope is too narrow: the Neocognitron delivers only shift invariance plus small-distortion tolerance, with no mechanism for the size invariance its own cited IT evidence treats as co-equal with position invariance, and none for rotation. Second, the training regime is unrealistic and brittle: it self-organizes on tiny noise-free binary line patterns (4-10 characters) presented one at a time at controlled positions, and the paper concedes correctness holds only provided parameters are properly adjusted and patterns skillfully presented, with small deviations of parameters or presentation order critically degrading results. Its representative-S-cell rule has no homeostatic guarantee of complete, non-redundant feature coverage. These narrow boundary conditions do not transfer to realistic input, leaving open how to build a self-organizing hierarchical recognizer that meets the position-and-size-invariant, robustly trainable target the invoked neuroscience implies.

**Method.** Central contribution: a concrete simulation platform/prototype, an invariance-first self-regulating self-organizing hierarchical recognizer, plus a companion transformed-and-degraded pattern benchmark. (A) Multi-scale channel bank for position and size invariance: instead of the Neocognitron single cascade, instantiate parallel size-tuned channels over an image pyramid, each a Neocognitron-style S-cell/C-cell cascade (S-cells plastic feature detectors, C-cells local pooling, per Hubel and Wiesel). Add a final cross-scale pooling stage where scale-pooling C-cells take the max response of matching feature-planes across all size channels, so a shape recruits the same top-layer cell regardless of scale, instantiating the position-and-size invariance Gross et al. attribute to IT cells. An orientation-pooling stage over rotated S-cell templates adds in-plane rotation tolerance. Weights are tied across channels so a feature learned at one scale is shared. (B) Homeostatic self-organization controller to remove the skillful-presentation and fragile-parameter dependence: replace fixed hand-set thresholds with a per-cell-plane controller that tracks running activation statistics and adapts each plane threshold toward a target firing rate. A recruitment/pruning module monitors coverage: if a pattern excites no plane above threshold, an unused plane is recruited (seeded from current input, echoing representative-cell selection); if two planes become redundant, one is pruned or repelled. This drives complete, non-redundant coverage under arbitrary presentation order and mixed patterns. (C) Benchmark and evaluation harness (the deployable artifact): a reproducible testbed generating pattern sets under controlled transformations (translation, scaling, rotation, noise, multi-pattern scenes), measuring top-layer selectivity across each axis, sensitivity of accuracy to parameter perturbations and shuffled presentation orders, and feature coverage/redundancy. Feasibility: every component is a modest extension of mechanisms already in the Cognitron/Neocognitron (analog cells, competitive reinforcement, C-cell pooling, S-columns), implementable as a digital simulation. Success criteria: sustained selectivity across scales and rotations; correct self-organization under randomized presentation order without retuning, with an ablation reverting to fixed thresholds/curated order reproducing the original brittleness; and maintained recognition under noise.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. The self-organizing pattern-recognition models in this literature — Rosenblatt's series-coupled perceptron, Fukushima's cognitron, and the neocognitron — are all constructed and validated under a single, narrow boundary condition: an isolated, roughly centered pattern presented on an otherwise blank field, one at a time, "skillfully presented," with purely feedforward information flow. Fukushima demonstrates the neocognitron only on isolated digits/letters and explicitly warns that self-organization succeeds only when "the stimulus patterns are skillfully presented" and parameters are finely tuned; a small change in the way patterns are presented "critically influenced" the result. Rosenblatt likewise concedes his machine is merely "shown one letter at a time," and speculates that only cross-coupled systems with feedback from response to association units "might be capable of dealing with the problems of figure-ground discrimination, relations of figures in complex visual fields, selective attention."

This regime does not transfer to realistic vision, where scenes contain multiple, possibly overlapping objects embedded in background clutter with no pre-segmentation. The neocognitron's core learning rule — reinforce the winning "representative" S-cell in each column (unsupervised, winner-take-all) — has no way to know which features belong to which object, so clutter and multiple simultaneous patterns would corrupt self-organization; the model has no figure-ground or attentional mechanism at all. Tellingly, the physiology cited by these very papers points to the missing ingredients that the models omit: Hubel & Wiesel's columnar cortical architecture implies rich reciprocal/recurrent connectivity rather than a strict feedforward cascade (a limitation Fukushima himself acknowledges); inferotemporal neurons studied in behaving monkeys performing discrimination show top-down, behaviorally driven modulation of pattern responses; and retinotectal map formation is activity-dependent. The gap that matters is precisely the transfer from lab-curated isolated stimuli to natural, cluttered, multi-object scenes — anticipated by Rosenblatt but never built.

**Method.** Augment the neocognitron with a reciprocal top-down feedback hierarchy plus an attentional gate that (1) segments one figure at a time in a cluttered scene and (2) gates the unsupervised self-organizing plasticity so only the currently attended figure drives learning — thereby relaxing the "isolated single pattern" boundary condition to multi-object scenes.

Concrete components:
1. Reciprocal feedback pathways. Add backward connections from each C-layer to the S/C layers below it, mirroring the forward receptive-field structure (in the spirit of Rosenblatt's proposed cross-coupled R→A feedback and of Hubel–Wiesel reciprocal cortical connectivity). A partially activated higher-level representation projects an excitatory "searchlight" downward that enhances lower-layer cells consistent with it and, through lateral inhibition, suppresses cells belonging to competing figures and background.

2. Scene-parsing loop. Present a full cluttered field. The forward pass yields several competing higher-layer activations; a winner-take-all competition among them selects the strongest as the current focus. Its feedback pass sharpens and isolates the corresponding figure at the input (figure-ground separation and implicit re-centering against clutter). After that figure is recognized, an inhibition-of-return signal suppresses its location so attention shifts to the next figure, letting the network serially report every object in the scene — the "relations of figures in complex visual fields" capability Rosenblatt named but never implemented.

3. Attention-gated plasticity. Modify the neocognitron reinforcement rule so a representative S-cell's modifiable synapses are strengthened only when the cell lies inside the currently attended, feedback-enhanced region. This makes learning-without-a-teacher robust even though the raw input contains multiple patterns and background: plasticity is both activity-gated (grounded in activity-dependent retinotectal map formation) and attentionally/behaviorally gated (grounded in the top-down modulation seen in inferotemporal neurons during discrimination).

4. Training and evaluation under the realistic regime. Self-organize the network on scenes containing multiple, overlapping, and clutter-embedded characters rather than isolated ones, via digital simulation as in the original work. Test whether it (a) still acquires position-invariant, distortion-tolerant category cells, (b) resists clutter-induced corruption of the competitive learning that would occur without the gate, and (c) can sequentially detect and identify each object in a multi-object field. Ablations (removing feedback, removing the plasticity gate, presenting isolated vs. cluttered stimuli) directly quantify how much the added attention/feedback machinery is responsible for closing the scope gap between curated single-pattern presentation and natural cluttered scenes.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works form a lineage of biologically-inspired self-organizing recognizers: Rosenblatt's four-layer series-coupled perceptron (1962), Fukushima's cognitron (1975/1981) and neocognitron (1980), justified by visual-cortex physiology (Hubel and Wiesel 1962/1965/1977) and category-selective inferotemporal responses (Gross et al. 1972; the 1980 IT discrimination study). Each claims unsupervised learning without a teacher yields position-invariant, distortion-tolerant, brain-like recognition. The Scope Mismatch: these claims were established only under narrow, idealized conditions. The neocognitron's evidence is a handful of hand-drawn patterns (five digits, then X/Y/T/Z, then ten numerals) on a small clean array, tested against essentially one axis (translation plus small distortion), with limited and often stationary presentation statistics; scale, rotation, noise, occlusion, class count, and class confusability go untested. The cognitron is admittedly position-dependent. The biological grounding is itself narrow: the neocognitron paper concedes the strict Hubel-Wiesel hierarchy does not hold in its original form (e.g., monosynaptic LGB-to-complex connections), and the physiological benchmarks came from reduced, small-stimulus preparations. So the operating envelope where these mechanisms deliver invariant recognition, and how well emergent units match cortical tuning, is unknown, limiting honest comparison and biological-fidelity claims for inherited mechanisms such as S/C alternation, lateral inhibition, and C-cell pooling.

**Method.** Build an empirical invariance and self-organization atlas mapping the operating envelopes of these classical models. First, reimplement the four-layer perceptron, cognitron, and neocognitron in one framework using the sources' stated parameters, with identical inputs and scoring. Second, construct a parameterized stimulus battery varying one factor at a time: translation, scale, rotation, elastic deformation, noise, occlusion, contrast, plus class-set properties (number of classes, inter-class similarity, intra-class variability), seeded with the original stimuli (digits, X/Y/T/Z) then expanded beyond them. Third, manipulate the unsupervised training regime: presentation order, class-frequency imbalance, number of patterns, stationary vs randomly shifted presentation. Fourth, compute diagnostics per model and axis: invariance breadth (accuracy vs transformation magnitude with breakdown thresholds), selectivity/grandmother-cell purity, capacity (max distinct patterns before representations merge), convergence stability and order-sensitivity, and tuning-curve/representational-similarity analysis of emergent S/C units. Fifth, cross-map to physiology by comparing emergent tuning to documented cortical properties: orientation/position tuning and growth of position invariance from simple to complex cells (Hubel and Wiesel) and selectivity-with-invariance of IT neurons (Gross et al.; 1980 study), yielding a per-layer biological-fidelity score. Sixth, ablate S/C alternation, lateral inhibition, C-cell pooling/blurring, and unsupervised reinforcement to attribute each invariance or failure to a specific ingredient. Deliverables: operating-envelope heatmaps locating where each model succeeds or fails, a capacity-vs-confusability frontier, and a mechanism-to-property attribution table, replacing anecdotal small-scale validation with a reproducible map of when these foundational self-organizing mechanisms transfer.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The foundational hierarchical recognition models here (Rosenblatt's series-coupled perceptron, Fukushima's cognitron, and the neocognitron) inherit the Hubel and Wiesel simple-complex-hypercomplex hierarchy with an inferotemporal grandmother-cell endpoint, and claim recognition unaffected by shift in position and by small distortion in shape. These guarantees hold only in a narrow regime the papers themselves expose. Invariance is genuinely built in only for translation, and only for small shifts; scale and rotation invariance are asserted merely qualitatively. Distortion tolerance emerges from C-cell local pooling whose window sizes, receptive-field growth, subsampling, and depth are tuned empirically for a tiny hand-crafted character set; the neocognitron paper notes recognition is critically influenced by these choices and that 24 cell-planes per layer were not sufficient. The structurally near-identical cognitron has no position invariance, showing invariance depends delicately on the pooling construction rather than any characterized principle. This is a scope mismatch: the models are presented as general solutions to shift and distortion, yet there is no formal statement of which transformation classes a simple-complex cascade can be invariant to, how much transformation magnitude a given pooling geometry and depth tolerate before selectivity collapses, or the invariance-versus-discriminability trade-off. Consequently the results do not transfer to scale, rotation, or large deformation, and there is no principled way to size an architecture for a target transformation range.

**Method.** Develop a group/semigroup-theoretic formal model of the S-cell/C-cell cascade these papers instantiate, and derive the exact class and magnitude of invariance it can realize. Formalize a stimulus as a function on the plane, an S-cell as thresholded shunting-inhibited template correlation, and a C-cell as a monotone local pooling operator over a window with given overlap and subsampling, weight-shared across a cell-plane; take the transformation set as the Euclidean group of translations, rotations, and scalings, plus a monoid of bounded local deformations. Prove Theorem 1 (translation): exact invariance under the discrete translation subgroup generated by successive pooling windows, with a closed-form tolerance radius equal to the sum over layers of half the pooling width times the product of preceding subsampling factors, predicting maximal tolerated shift from architecture parameters and explaining the pooling-free cognitron's near-zero radius. Prove Theorem 2 (scope/impossibility): spatial pooling alone cannot yield scale or rotation invariance beyond an order pooling-width-over-feature-size limit because these transformations do not commute with local spatial pooling; formalize a cell-plane-orbit condition, that invariance to a subgroup requires feature planes tiling each feature's orbit, giving the number of cell-planes per stage as the orbit covering number for a chosen tolerance. Derive an invariance-selectivity bound: larger pooling raises the invariance radius but shrinks the minimum separable inter-pattern distance, yielding an explicit Pareto frontier that formalizes the neocognitron's informal compromise between differentiating similar patterns and tolerating distortion. Produce a taxonomy classifying invariance mechanisms as pooling-based translation, orbit-tiling scale/rotation, and learning-based deformation tolerance via competitive learning over the deformation monoid, mapping each to requirements on receptive-field size, pooling geometry, subsampling, depth, and cell-plane count. Validate by re-simulating a minimal S/C cascade and the reported neocognitron configuration on stimuli with parametrically controlled shift, scale, rotation, and deformation, checking recognition degrades at the predicted thresholds and that adding orbit-tiling planes restores invariance as the covering-number formula predicts. This replaces hand-tuned, stimulus-specific invariance claims with transferable architecture-parameterized theorems and a taxonomy for sizing a cascade to a target transformation range.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 29 turns / 37 tool calls

**Motivation.** MOTIVATION. Scope mismatch gap in the neocognitron. Fukushima 1980, building on the cognitron 1975 and the Hubel and Wiesel hierarchical hypercolumn model of visual cortex 1962 and 1977 and unsupervised learning without a teacher from Rosenblatt 1962, shows position invariant recognition only under a narrow hand engineered regime. Capacity is fixed at exactly 24 cell planes per layer, admitted insufficient for ten patterns and unscalable due to memory. The network is fragile to small parameter or presentation changes, needs a skillful curriculum of five to ten clean symbols, and uses a hand designed winner take all selection that ignores feature coverage. It works only when a human pre tunes capacity, thresholds, and order for a tiny alphabet. Whether the self organizing principle survives when these must be discovered automatically at scale is left open.

**Method.** METHOD. Build an optimization and search driven self allocating Adaptive Neocognitron with four unsupervised mechanisms. One, novelty driven growth spawns a new S plane only when the best matching column response falls below an adaptive coverage threshold, so capacity is data driven and scales with classes. Two, layer wise budgeting measures per layer residual coverage, greedily adds planes to the worst covered layer, and prunes unused or duplicate planes. Three, feedback controlled tuning sets selectivity, inhibition, and reinforcement speed by closed loop controllers targeting an activation sparsity, removing manual tuning. Four, active curriculum presents the currently worst explained pattern with randomized position, scale, and shape. Evaluate versus the fixed baseline on scaling to many classes, robustness under random hyperparameter and order perturbation where the baseline fails, capacity efficiency of discovered versus fixed planes, and invariance on the original distorted stimuli.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The founding self-organizing recognition models share a scope mismatch. The cognitron, the neocognitron, and Rosenblatt's four-layer series-coupled perceptron validate unsupervised self-organization and recognition only in a narrow regime: a single isolated near-noise-free pattern filling the retina, presented one at a time, with the experimenter choosing presentation order and tuning parameters. The neocognitron paper concedes this fragility: recognizing even ten digits requires patterns to be skillfully presented and is critically influenced by small parameter changes, with only small distortion tolerance. Rosenblatt lists figure-ground discrimination, relations of figures in complex visual fields, and selective attention as problems his single-letter-at-a-time training could not solve. Yet the biological targets these models invoke, size-invariant inferotemporal responses in behaving monkeys and the Hubel-Wiesel hierarchy, arise from cluttered, multi-object, variable-scale, noisy scenes. The shared winner-take-all reinforcement of the most-active representative cell assumes one pattern is present, so it cannot decide which of several co-present objects to learn and cannot bootstrap in clutter.

**Method.** Relax the single-clean-isolated-pattern assumption so self-organization and recognition work in cluttered, noisy, multi-object, variable-scale scenes, using only mechanisms licensed by the sources. Add attentional figure-ground gating via top-down feedback: descending connections from higher C-layers to lower S and C layers (Rosenblatt's cross-coupled feedback, consistent with the Hubel-Wiesel hierarchy), where the strongest-responding high-layer C-cell projects a spatial spotlight that facilitates S-cells of one candidate figure and suppresses those from other objects and background, segmenting one attended pattern. Use attention-scoped learning: restrict winner-take-all representative selection to the attended region so reinforcement targets a single segmented pattern even when many are present. Add noise-robust selection: replace fixed-threshold selection with local contrast normalization via lateral inhibitory cells (extending the cognitron's inhibitory cells and Hubel-Wiesel lateral interactions). Add scale invariance: parallel S-planes tuned to a range of feature sizes with C-cells pooling across scale and position. Add sequential multi-object readout with inhibition-of-return so attention shifts among figures. Evaluate by self-organizing unsupervised on scenes of two or three overlapping, noisy, differently scaled characters and comparing recognition accuracy, segmentation quality, and self-organization stability against the vanilla neocognitron.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The neocognitron (Fukushima 1980) and cognitron (1975/1981) are presented as general learning-without-a-teacher mechanisms for shift- and distortion-invariant recognition, with the neocognitron advertised as a drastic solution to coping with position shift and shape distortion. But the demonstrated competence lives in a narrow, unrealistic regime: only a handful of clean, well-separated patterns presented in isolation, with hand-set parameters (inhibitory efficacy r_l, reinforcement speed q_l, saturation) and a fixed 24 cell-planes per layer. The author concedes the scope mismatch: scaling to ten patterns works only provided parameters are properly adjusted and the stimulus patterns are skillfully presented, and a small deviation of parameter values or a small change in presentation critically influences the ability of the self-organized network. The failure is structural: reinforcement goes to a single winner-take-all representative S-cell per plane, so an early or spurious winner can permanently claim a plane, features get duplicated or left uncovered, and there is no recovery, deduplication, or capacity reallocation. The learned feature dictionary thus depends on luck, ordering, and tuning, and the promised transfer to larger, noisy, cluttered pattern sets is unsupported. Prior work shows what the hierarchy can represent under idealized boundary conditions, not how to self-organize it reliably outside them.

**Method.** Develop a robustified self-organization procedure for Fukushima-style S-cell/C-cell hierarchies, in the same digital-simulation setting, aimed at stable low-variance feature learning under adverse conditions. Four mechanisms replace the fragile assumptions. (1) Consensus-consolidated reinforcement: within each S-column, reinforcement is graded by relative activation (soft competition), and a candidate feature is consolidated only after winning consistently across several recent presentations held in a short template-memory buffer, damping noisy single-presentation winners and making final templates approximately order-invariant. (2) Adaptive capacity: grow a new cell-plane when an input's local features are poorly covered (a novelty/residual test) and merge or prune planes that converged to near-identical templates, self-sizing the network and curing the admitted 24-planes-not-sufficient failure. (3) Homeostatic selectivity control: replace the hand-tuned inhibitory efficacy r_l with a per-plane controller that adjusts inhibition to hold each plane near a target selectivity/tolerance operating point, removing manual tuning of the selectivity-invariance tradeoff. (4) Adversarial training regime: self-organize under injected noise, position/size jitter, partial occlusion, and occasional simultaneous multi-pattern presentation, forcing robust features. Evaluation is stability-oriented and targets the original's admitted failures: comparing original versus robustified self-organization, measure mean and variance of recognition accuracy across randomized hyperparameters, randomized presentation orders, increasing noise/occlusion, and growing class counts (10, 20, 50). Primary metric is accuracy variance across seeds/settings; secondary is planes-used-versus-accuracy. Success is collapsing the sensitivity the original attributes to parameters and skillful presentation while preserving shift- and distortion-invariance.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. The cognitron and neocognitron established self-organizing, shift-invariant hierarchical recognition, but only under narrow, unrealistic conditions: single isolated, near-noise-free patterns shown one at a time; a strictly feedforward cascade; and a fully hand-designed static architecture (number of stages, S/C cell-planes, receptive-field sizes, and connection ranges all set by the designer). Learning is defined as without a teacher, excluding reinforcement or top-down signals, with no mechanism for clutter, figure-ground segmentation, or for the architecture itself to arise. Yet the literatures these models build on describe richer conditions left unused: Hubel and Wiesel document structured functional architecture (orientation and ocular-dominance columns, binocular interaction); inferotemporal recordings show position- and size-invariant grandmother-like units measured while an animal actively performs discrimination, not passively viewing; Rosenblatt's four-layer series-coupled perceptron work explicitly studies cross-coupled feedback from response to association units, selective attention, reinforcement rules, and geometric constraints on organization; and retinotectal neuroplasticity models describe how connectivity itself forms through activity-dependent and chemoaffinity processes. The feedforward, teacher-free, hand-wired, one-clean-pattern regime thus mismatches both the biology and the surrounding theory, limiting transfer to realistic cluttered vision.

**Method.** Synthesis and unification. Build one self-organizing recurrent visual hierarchy integrating four mechanisms currently siloed across the sources. (1) Bottom-up: retain cognitron and neocognitron competitive unsupervised S-cell plasticity with C-cell pooling for local shift tolerance. (2) Top-down back-coupling: add reciprocal higher-to-lower connections, importing Rosenblatt's cross-coupled feedback and selective attention, so a partial recognition hypothesis at deep stages re-weights and gates lower-stage activity, implementing figure-ground segmentation and attentional routing to parse cluttered scenes of multiple, overlapping, noisy patterns and relaxing the one-clean-pattern assumption. The same descending pathway carries an optional graded reinforcement signal, unifying learning with a teacher (perceptron reinforcement) and learning without a teacher (Fukushima competition) into one rule that degrades gracefully from supervised to unsupervised. (3) Developmental self-wiring: replace the hand-set architecture with a retinotectal-style plasticity process governing not only weights but growth and pruning of inter-stage connections and receptive-field extents, so the number of cell-planes, pooling radii, and depth emerge from input statistics plus a chemoaffinity-like topographic bias. (4) Structural prior and read-out: use Hubel and Wiesel functional-architecture regularities (columnar and topographic ordering, binocular combination) as a regularizer on emergent maps, and adopt inferotemporal-style position- and size-invariant category units as the top-layer objective, trained and evaluated during an active discrimination task. Validation: test on cluttered multi-object scenes with systematic position, size, and noise variation; measure invariant recognition and segmentation accuracy versus the feedforward neocognitron baseline; and check whether the emergent architecture reproduces columnar organization and the invariance-gradient-with-depth predicted by the hierarchy.

---

## Query by Committee

*seed paper `query-by-committee-1992`, 11 sources, 64 generated ideas*

### Human idea (ground truth)

**Motivation.** Query learning algorithms have been proposed for various learning problems, but there is little understanding of the general principles by which such algorithms should be constructed. In incremental learning, where training examples are acquired one at a time and each query may carry heavy computational or experimental cost, it is crucial to select examples that maximize what is learned per query. Learning from randomly chosen inputs suffers from information gain that decays toward zero as more examples accumulate, yielding generalization error that improves only via a slow inverse power law. A principled criterion for choosing maximally informative queries—and an understanding of when such a criterion yields dramatically faster learning—is missing.

**Method.** We propose to use the Shannon information of a query as the guiding principle for example selection, defining the information gain of a query as the reduction in entropy (log-volume) of the version space. Since the optimal query bisects the version space into equal-volume halves—yielding 1 bit per query—but the version space geometry is generally intractable, we propose to estimate this bisection using a committee of learners. Specifically, we train a committee of 2k students (weight vectors drawn at random from the version space via the Gibbs algorithm on the current training set), and select as the next query an input on which the committee maximally disagrees—classified positive by half the members and negative by the other half. This principle of maximal disagreement approximates version-space bisection, approaching exact bisection as k grows. We will analyze this algorithm on two tractable models—the high-low game and perceptron learning of a perceptron target—using statistical mechanics (replica) techniques and Monte Carlo simulation, computing the asymptotic information gain and the resulting generalization curves, and comparing against random-input training. We conjecture that maintaining an asymptotically finite information gain per query drives generalization error to decay exponentially in the number of examples, in contrast to the power-law decay of random sampling.

### LLM ideas

#### [method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior works split into two unlinked strands. Tishby-Levin-Solla and Opper-Haussler give a Gibbs ensemble over network weights where information gain is ensemble-entropy reduction and the Bayes-optimal predictor is a posterior majority vote. Separately, Watkin-Rau, the 1990 example-selection paper, and Baum show active example and query selection saves many labels (about 42 percent fewer wasted examples), with Fedorov optimal-experiment theory as the principled criterion. Gap: query selection was studied only for a single perceptron in the thermodynamic limit with realizable noise-free data, using geometric heuristics rather than a true Bayes-optimal information-gain rule, and was never built into a working tool for finite, multilayer, noisy nets.

**Method.** Build an active-learning query-engine artifact: a workbench that samples K weight vectors from the Gibbs posterior via Langevin MCMC, scores candidate inputs by committee vote-entropy as expected information gain (a network-general Fedorov D-optimality criterion), queries an oracle at the top input, re-samples, and tracks generalization via ensemble free-energy differences. Exposed inverse temperature handles noise and unrealizability; an entropy-plateau monitor flags capacity limits. Validate by reproducing the single-perceptron 42 percent saving, then extend to finite N, multilayer, noisy oracles, and ship an architecture-agnostic API.

#### [unsteered (blank)] — claude-opus-4-8, 28 turns / 39 tool calls

**Motivation.** Active query learning for perceptrons (Kinzel-Rujan; Watkin-Rau) selects the next input orthogonal to a single estimate J, giving only constant-factor gains with a power-law learning curve. Opper-Haussler show generalization is governed by the version-space posterior and that the ideal example halves the version-space volume; Fedorov formalizes querying the point of maximal predictive variance. Gap: no work chooses queries from the posterior version space rather than one point estimate, nor quantifies the gap to optimum; a point orthogonal to one J does not bisect the version space, and analyses are limited to the noise-free case.

**Method.** Build a statistical-mechanics theory and committee algorithm for posterior version-space active queries. Rule: pick the input where the posterior predictive is maximally uncertain (posterior label probability one half), bisecting the solution volume, one bit per query. Implement by sampling a committee from the Gibbs version-space ensemble (Opper-Haussler majority net, Langevin sampling, or repeated MinOver solutions) and querying where votes split fifty-fifty. Extend the Watkin-Rau replica calculation to committee-bisection, tracking overlap and version-space entropy versus alpha, predicting near-exponential error decay and a computable gap over single-estimate selection. Extend to the Ising perceptron freezing transition and to noisy cases via a finite-temperature posterior, validated by simulation.

#### [method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works advocate active example selection (query learning) as the most efficient way to train perceptrons: Watkin and Rau derive a 42 percent reduction in wasted examples by querying near the decision boundary; Baum uses boundary halving to evade NP-completeness limits; Fedorov gives D- and G-optimal design criteria. Separately, Opper and Haussler compute the exact Bayes-optimal generalization error and a factor sqrt(2) gap to the Gibbs learner; the Statistical mechanics review shows annealed and high-temperature theories fail for unrealizable (teacher-student mismatched) rules; Tishby, Levin and Solla relate prediction error to sufficient training size; Gardner capacity and VC bounds set limits. The gap: every gain from active selection is proven only for noise-free, realizable targets in the large-N limit, and each is tied to one learner. No source measures, under one protocol, whether these gains survive label noise, model mismatch, and finite N; how close practical queries get to the Bayes-optimal and optimal-design ideals; or when active selection hurts (querying a noisy or mismatched boundary over-samples the most misleading region, exactly where annealed theory fails). This matters because active learning is used where labels are costly and imperfect, yet its robustness there is untested here.

**Method.** Build an empirical map (a phase diagram of active learning for perceptrons) via a teacher-student Monte Carlo benchmark measuring generalization curves G(alpha) on a common grid, using the review's validated setup (N about 100 to 1000, quenched averages over many training sets). Vary four axes. (1) Query strategy: random baseline; minimal-margin boundary query (Watkin-Rau); Fedorov D-/G-optimal design on the current estimate; Gibbs-ensemble committee disagreement; Baum boundary halving.
