(2) Learner: Hebb, optimal stability (MinOver), a single Gibbs/Boltzmann sample, and a Bayes-optimal ensemble estimate, so errors are referenced to the exact Bayes curve and the sqrt(2) gap. (3) Realizability: Ising teacher with spherical student and vice versa, continuous versus discrete targets. (4) Corruption: label-noise level and input-distribution shift. Per cell record: the G(alpha) curve and its asymptotic form; sample complexity to fixed target errors; wasted examples versus the alpha=1 bound; gap to the Bayes-optimal and Fedorov-optimal ideals; and degradation slope versus noise. Cross-check finite-N results against source predictions (the 42 percent figure, the transition near alpha=1.245, the sqrt(2) factor) to locate where thermodynamic/annealed theory holds or breaks. Deliverables: (i) a comparative map marking, per strategy-rule-mismatch-noise cell, whether active selection beats, matches, or underperforms random sampling; (ii) cheap online diagnostics (ensemble disagreement, margin-distribution skew, estimated noise rate) fitted to predict beforehand whether boundary-focused selection helps or backfires; (iii) a measurement of how much of the Bayes/optimal-design ideal each query rule recovers at finite N. This turns scattered idealized claims into a condition-resolved robustness map and a rule-of-thumb for deploying active selection.

#### [method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Three traditions in the provided sources address the same question (which examples should a learner see) but none unifies them. (1) Statistical-mechanics learning theory (the Seung/Sompolinsky/Tishby review; Tishby-Levin-Solla's Gibbs ensemble; Opper-Haussler's Bayes-optimal analysis) gives a version-space posterior, an ensemble entropy, a one-step information-gain notion, the halving relation V_{m+1} <= (1/2)V_m, and mostly power-law learning curves eps_g ~ 1/alpha, but only for RANDOM examples. (2) Query/active selection (Watkin-Rau "Selecting examples for perceptrons"; Baum's examples-and-queries) shows large speedups (a predicted 42% fewer wasted examples; midpoint binary search) but uses ad hoc single-hypothesis heuristics with no optimality principle and no bound on achievable speedup. (3) Fedorov's Theory of Optimal Experiments gives a mature design theory (D-/G-optimality, information matrix, Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence) built for regression, never connected to version-space generalization. The gap: no formal principle defining the optimal query in the stat-mech setting, no proof relating the perceptron heuristics to it, no bridge to optimal-design theory, and no bound on the best possible speedup (an active-learning analogue of the VC/sample-complexity bounds noted in the Bayesian-learning source). Closing it would put active learning on principled footing and connect literatures that reinvented the same idea in isolation.

**Method.** A formal-derivation contribution: one objective, three theorems, one bound, unifying version-space learning, perceptron query heuristics, and optimal design. Setup: following Tishby-Levin-Solla and Opper-Haussler, define the Gibbs posterior p_m(J) on the version space of m labeled examples, with volume V_m and entropy S_m. Define the query objective as expected one-step information gain of an unlabeled query x with unknown label s: DeltaI(x)=S_m - E_s[S_{m+1}(x,s)] = H2(pi_+(x)), where pi_+(x) is the posterior-predictive probability of label +1. Theorem 1 (optimal query = Bayes-point bisection): DeltaI(x) is maximized when pi_+(x)=1/2, splitting the version space into equal halves through the Bayes point and achieving V_{m+1}=(1/2)V_m with equality in expectation, the maximal reduction. Corollary: Watkin-Rau's rule (pick xi orthogonal to the current estimate J) and Baum's midpoint query are the MAP single-hypothesis approximations of this Bayes-point bisection; derive their suboptimality gap via the order parameter R(alpha). Theorem 2 (equivalence to D-optimality): in the large-alpha regime where p_m(J) is asymptotically Gaussian with covariance proportional to the inverse Fisher matrix M_m, expand DeltaI(x) ~ (1/2)[log det(M_m + beta x x^T) - log det M_m], exactly Fedorov's D-optimality criterion; thus greedy info-gain query learning equals sequential D-optimal design, and by Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence the optimal query measure is G-optimal, minimizing worst-case predictive variance and hence worst-case generalization. Theorem 3 (learning-curve bound and speedup): from S_m <= S_0 - sum DeltaI_k derive (a) query-complexity bound m >= (S_0 - S_target)/max_x DeltaI(x), an active-learning analogue of VC/Bayesian sample-complexity bounds; (b) optimal bisecting queries force exponential decay eps_g ~ exp(-c alpha) versus power-law 1/alpha for random examples; (c) explicit c for spherical and Ising perceptrons from the replica free energy (reusing Watkin-Rau's entropy S(alpha,kappa)), recovering the 42% reduction as the random-vs-optimal prefactor ratio. Noisy extension: replace the sharp version space with a temperature-T Gibbs weight; DeltaI reduces to a noise-scaled Fisher log-det (D-optimality weighted by label reliability), and the residual Bayes-vs-Gibbs gap reproduces Opper-Haussler's sqrt(2) factor, giving optimal query rates under noise. Feasibility: all ingredients exist in the sources (Gibbs/version-space formalism, replica entropies for spherical/Ising perceptrons, the halving relation, Fedorov's information-matrix theory); the contribution is analytic and validated against Watkin-Rau and Baum as limiting cases.

#### [method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical mechanics yields exact learning curves G(alpha) for perceptrons (Seung/Sompolinsky/Tishby review; Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal perceptron; Tishby/Levin/Solla information-gain/entropy). Active example selection helps: Watkin-Rau (analyzing the Kinzel/Ruajn scheme) show querying near the current single student's decision boundary cuts wasted examples (~42% for Ising perceptrons). Fedorov's optimal-experiment theory supplies adaptive-design machinery (D-/G-optimality, equivalence theorem, iterative mix-in), and Baum shows queries speed learning. Gaps: (1) existing perceptron query rules are greedy and one-step, placing the next example on a single point estimate's boundary, not optimizing the whole trajectory under a budget; Watkin-Rau even note that WHEN one starts selecting matters (selecting only for alpha>0.7 keeps most of the gain) but never optimize this scheduling. (2) The criterion is never tied to the Bayes posterior, though Opper-Haussler show the Bayes predictor beats the single-student one by up to sqrt(2) and flag that error vs. one-step information gain is unresolved -- maximizing information gain need not minimize error. (3) All analyses assume a realizable, noise-free teacher, with no principled way to reallocate a query budget once boundary queries stop shrinking the relevant version space. This leaves open a unified optimal-experimental-design theory of query allocation for perceptrons that is non-greedy, posterior-based, and noise-robust.

**Method.** Frame perceptron active learning as an optimal experimental-design and budget-allocation problem solved with statistical-mechanics objectives. (1) Represent the learning state by the Bayes posterior (version space) through order parameters: teacher overlap R, self-overlap q, and posterior anisotropy; use the replica/annealed free energy to predict, for a candidate query with projection profile rho, the one-step change in both ensemble entropy (information gain) and true error G=(1/pi)arccos R. (2) Define a Fedorov-style criterion adapted to this setting: maximize shrinkage of the version space along the generalization-relevant teacher-aligned direction, i.e. maximize dR/dalpha per query, and derive the error-optimal profile rho* versus entropy-optimal and single-student-boundary profiles. (3) Since order parameters obey ODEs in alpha given rho, treat the whole run as optimal control: choose a schedule rho(alpha) over a fixed budget to minimize terminal G(alpha*) by calculus of variations / dynamic programming, yielding a non-greedy allocation. (4) Implement with a Fedorov iterative update mixing the current design with a query matching rho*, sampled by rejection over a posterior committee's disagreement band -- a Bayes generalization of boundary sampling. (5) Extend the free energy to noisy/unrealizable teachers; use the annealed approximation to detect when boundary queries saturate and automatically reallocate budget to bulk queries, predicting the altered phase transition. (6) Validate by deriving predicted learning curves for random, greedy-boundary, entropy-optimal, and error-scheduled sampling, each paired with single-student and Bayes predictors, and test with finite-N simulations on spherical and Ising perceptrons; success is reaching target G with fewer queries than greedy boundary sampling and staying efficient under noise.

#### [method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 15 tool calls

**Motivation.** The prior work establishes two powerful but disconnected threads. (1) Query / self-selecting example learning: Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons," 1992) and the earlier Kinzel–Rujan example-selection idea show, via replica statistical mechanics, that a perceptron which chooses its next question ξ^{p+1} orthogonal to its current estimate J^(p) — i.e., right on its own decision boundary — dramatically accelerates learning (a 42% reduction in "wasted" examples, and faster-than-power-law approach to perfect generalization). Baum's polynomial-time algorithm similarly drives queries toward the separating hyperplane. (2) Bayesian/information-theoretic analysis of generalization: Opper & Haussler compute the Bayes-optimal perceptron learning curve and relate generalization to the one-step information gain, and Tishby–Levin–Solla formalize learning as reduction of ensemble entropy (information gain).

The critical, unaddressed limitation is that every one of these query/selection results assumes a NOISE-FREE, REALIZABLE oracle: labels are exact and the teacher is a single perceptron the student can match. This is exactly the regime where aggressive boundary-seeking queries are most dangerous. Querying points on the current decision boundary means querying inputs whose true labels have the smallest margin and are therefore the most likely to be corrupted by output noise or by teacher/student model mismatch. Intuitively, the greedy strategy that is optimal in the clean case should collect maximally unreliable, low-margin examples under noise, potentially destroying its advantage or even producing a residual error floor. Yet the noisy/unrealizable case has only been analyzed for PASSIVE, randomly drawn examples (Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby), and classical optimal experimental design (Fedorov) treats noise but only for linear-regression-style designs, not the nonlinear threshold classifier or the statistical-mechanics order-parameter regime. This matters because real oracles (human labelers, physical experiments, noisy measurements) are noisy and imperfectly realizable, so the whole promise of "learn faster by choosing your own examples" is untested precisely where it would be used. There is thus room for a theory of when, and how, self-selecting perceptrons should query under noise.

**Method.** Relax the noise-free, realizable assumptions underlying the query-learning results and build a statistical-mechanics theory of self-selecting perceptron learning in noisy and unrealizable regimes, together with a noise-robust query rule.

1) Extend the order-parameter/replica calculation. Take the Watkin–Rau framework (order parameters R = J·B and the query distribution induced by choosing ξ near the student's boundary) and add a teacher noise model: output label flips with probability η (and, as variants, Gaussian output noise, and a "complex teacher" the spherical/Ising student cannot represent — the unrealizable case). Recompute the generalization curve G(α; η) for the greedy boundary-seeking strategy. This yields a concrete, testable prediction: the point where greedy active learning stops beating passive random sampling, and the noise-dependent residual error floor / plateau it incurs, quantified as a function of α and η.

2) Derive the optimal query margin. Instead of always querying at zero margin (on the boundary), parametrize queries by their projected margin m = ξ·J^(p) and ask which m maximizes the EXPECTED one-step information gain about the teacher B given the noise model — directly generalizing the information-gain quantity that Opper–Haussler and Tishby–Levin–Solla relate to generalization. Under noise, informativeness (highest near the boundary) trades off against label reliability (highest far from the boundary), so the argmax is a finite optimal margin m*(η, R) > 0 rather than the boundary itself. Deriving m*(η,R(α)) as an explicit function of the current overlap and noise level is the core theoretical deliverable and the mechanism by which the method "works under weaker assumptions."

3) Move from a point estimate to the noisy Bayes/posterior ensemble. Replace the single weight vector J with the Gibbs/Bayes posterior over weights (the object Opper–Haussler analyze), and define the query as the input maximizing posterior label disagreement weighted by reliability — a noise-aware, version-space / query-by-committee reading. Carry the posterior into the replica average so the analysis is self-consistent: examples selected from the posterior change the posterior. This gives an "active Bayes-optimal" learning curve to compare against the passive Bayes-optimal baseline and the greedy active baseline.

4) Validate and delimit. Simulate spherical and Ising perceptrons at several η and under a mismatched teacher, comparing (a) greedy boundary queries, (b) the optimal-margin rule m*, (c) posterior-disagreement queries, and (d) passive random sampling. Map out the phase diagram in (α, η) showing regions where active learning helps, is neutral, or hurts, and confirm the predicted optimal margin and error floors.

This addresses the gap by taking results proven only for clean, realizable oracles and making them function — and prescribing what to actually query — under label noise and model mismatch, i.e., under the realistic conditions in which self-selecting learners are deployed.

#### [method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across these works, a learner generalizes far faster if it queries its own inputs rather than accepting random ones. Baum's polynomial-time algorithm locates a point on the target hyperplane by binary search between a + and a - example; Watkin & Rau (extending Kinzel/Rujan example selection) show a perceptron that queries inputs on its own current decision boundary wastes ~42% fewer examples and beats every random-sampling rule. The shared principle: query where the model is most uncertain (nearest the boundary), where a label is most informative. But all these efficiency results assume a noise-free, perfectly realizable teacher. Opper & Haussler compute the Bayes-optimal learning curve explicitly for noise-free examples, and the statistical-mechanics literature (Seung/Sompolinsky/Tishby; Tishby-Levin-Solla's Gibbs/version-space formulation) treats output noise and unrealizable rules only for random example streams, never for actively selected inputs. This is the gap: the inputs that maximize information (on the boundary, teacher margin |B·S|->0) are exactly the ones whose labels are least reliable under noise and where a mismatched student is most systematically wrong. So query-learning speed-ups rest on the most brittle possible sampling strategy — under label noise or model mismatch, boundary-seeking queries amplify errors, induce sampling bias, and can drive the weight estimate away from the teacher (Baum's binary search fails on a single mislabeled query). No source characterizes or repairs this failure mode, yet active selection is precisely where the most labels are saved, making a silently unreliable query scheme a trustworthiness problem worth solving.

**Method.** We propose a robustified Bayesian query-selection scheme for perceptron learning plus a statistical-mechanics analysis quantifying its reliability gains over standard boundary-seeking active learning. Setup: a student perceptron learns teacher B in the thermodynamic limit (N->inf, p=alpha*N) with two corruptions absent from prior work: output label noise (flip probability lambda or Gaussian teacher-field noise) and model mismatch/unrealizability (finite-margin or extra-structure teacher). The learner maintains the Bayes posterior / Gibbs ensemble over weights (Opper-Haussler and Tishby-Levin-Solla version-space formulation), summarized by order parameters R (teacher-student overlap) and q (student-student overlap). Robust query criterion: instead of selecting the input maximizing raw expected information (which drives queries to the student boundary J·S=0), select inputs by a risk-adjusted objective that integrates over the noise model, maximizing expected posterior-error reduction rather than raw information. Since a boundary label is near a coin flip under noise, this prefers queries at a strictly positive stability margin kappa*>0, where labels are both informative and reliable. Three concrete levers: (1) tunable query-margin kappa (query inputs with predicted field J·S ~ kappa rather than 0), whose optimum we derive; (2) query redundancy, averaging r nearby/repeated queries per point to suppress label noise, trading query count for reliability; (3) an online unrealizability detector monitoring posterior entropy / training-error growth (available in the Gibbs formulation) that adaptively raises kappa and r to prevent catastrophic drift. Analysis: using Watkin & Rau's replica recursion (which already contains a Gaussian noise-like term whose width narrows with R) and the Gibbs free-energy machinery of the statistical-mechanics-of-learning framework, derive learning curves e_g(alpha) for random sampling, standard boundary query learning, and robust margin-plus-redundancy query learning, under noise lambda and a finite-margin unrealizable teacher. Predictions to establish: standard query learning plateaus or worsens past a critical alpha under noise (error amplification/sampling bias), while the robust scheme keeps an efficiency edge over random sampling and degrades gracefully; and there is an optimal kappa*(lambda,alpha) and r minimizing e_g. Validate with simulations on Ising and spherical perceptrons (the two Watkin-Rau regimes) with MinOver/Bayes-optimal students, comparing final generalization, run-to-run variance (a direct brittleness metric), and label budget. How it closes the gap: prior works either exploit boundary queries assuming no noise, or analyze noise only for random sampling. This is the first query strategy and analysis that makes active example selection robust, turning the most brittle sampling regime into a controlled, reliable one and providing a principled knob (query margin plus redundancy) for the efficiency-versus-reliability trade-off.

#### [method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** These sources contain four disconnected notions of an informative example: Fedorov D-optimality, Watkin-Rau near-boundary selection, Baum bisection queries, and Tishby version-space volume reduction, alongside Bayesian/VC information-gain bounds and Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal learning curves. They are never unified, and the truly optimal query strategy is never analyzed with exact average-case tools, so we lack a principled architecture-general selection rule and an exact theory of how much active learning can help.

**Method.** Unify the criteria under a single Maximum Expected Information Gain objective, defined as expected version-space log-volume shrinkage, equivalently the mutual information between the hypothesis and the unknown label under the Gibbs posterior of Tishby et al. and Opper-Haussler. Prove reductions showing this one objective specializes to Fedorov D-optimality in the linear-Gaussian limit, to version-space bisection for realizable binary classification (query where the Bayes predictive probability is nearest one half, recovering Baum midpoint queries and Watkin-Rau near-boundary selection), and to the average information-gain term bounded by VC dimension in the Bayesian sample-complexity result. Then use replica and Gibbs-ensemble analysis for spherical and Ising perceptrons to compute exact active-learning curves when queries follow this rule (implemented as maximal disagreement of hypotheses sampled from the posterior), determining whether error decays as a power law or exponentially and how much it beats the Watkin-Rau and D-optimal heuristics. Show Baum geometric decay is the worst-case shadow of the average-case result, and validate all reductions and curves by simulation.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Three communities have independently answered the same question — "which input is most worth querying?" — without connecting their theories. (1) Statistics produced rigorous optimal experimental design (Fedorov 1972): D-optimality (maximize the determinant of the Fisher information matrix), G-optimality (minimize worst-case predictive variance), and the Kiefer–Wolfowitz D-equals-G equivalence. (2) The statistical-mechanics-of-learning community (Gardner; Opper–Haussler Bayes-optimal perceptron; Watkin–Rau "Selecting examples for perceptrons"; Baum's examples-and-queries algorithm) describes learning via order parameters (teacher–student overlap R, version-space volume) and replica learning curves, but its query rules are heuristic: Watkin–Rau pick examples orthogonal to the current estimate J (42% fewer "wasted" examples), Baum does binary search, neither derived from any optimality criterion. (3) The Bayesian/information-theoretic community (Tishby–Levin–Solla's Gibbs-ensemble "information gain = KL reduction of accessible configuration volume"; Haussler et al.'s sample-complexity bounds via information theory and VC dimension) measures example value as posterior-entropy reduction. These are three views of one object: Fedorov's log-determinant D-criterion equals Tishby's log version-space-volume reduction, which drives the order-parameter dynamics the physics learning curves describe. No work fuses them, so physics query heuristics ignore provably optimal design criteria, optimal-design theory ignores the exact Bayes-optimal ensemble and replica learning curves, and the information bounds never become a constructive query rule. Bridging them yields principled, near-optimal active learning with closed-form learning-curve guarantees, and lets us test whether the classical equivalence holds in the learning setting.

**Method.** Build an open Active Learning Workbench for perceptrons and layered nets whose central contribution is a system that operationalizes optimal experimental design inside the statistical-mechanics/Bayesian ensemble. Five components. (1) Posterior tracker: represent the learner as the Gibbs ensemble over weights consistent with labels (Tishby–Levin–Solla), summarized for perceptrons by order parameters (overlap R, self-overlap q) and, in general, by Monte-Carlo/Langevin samples; provide the Bayes-optimal predictor (center of mass of version space, per Opper–Haussler) plus baselines (Hebb, MinOver, Boltzmann/Gibbs). (2) Unified query scorer: score each candidate input x by design criteria re-expressed on the ensemble — Bayes-D equals expected reduction in log version-space volume (information gain), averaged over candidate labels weighted by the Bayes predictive probability p(y|x); Bayes-G minimizes the resulting worst-case predictive variance/generalization error; the greedy one-step rule maximizes Bayes-D and recovers Watkin–Rau's orthogonal-to-J heuristic as its leading-order approximation. (3) Analytic learning-curve module: extend Watkin–Rau's replica operator that inserts the (p+1)-th pattern to the case where the pattern is chosen by each criterion, yielding coupled ODEs for R(alpha) and generalization error G(alpha) under Bayes-D, Bayes-G, boundary-heuristic, and random sampling, for spherical and Ising perceptrons. (4) Simulation/benchmark harness: finite-N experiments for spherical/Ising perceptrons and a small two-layer net (matching Opper–Haussler hidden-unit approximations), comparing empirical curves to analytic predictions and across strategies; metrics include generalization error vs. alpha, examples wasted, information gain per query, and sample complexity implied by Haussler-style information/VC bounds. (5) Equivalence test: check analytically and empirically whether the Kiefer–Wolfowitz equivalence carries into the learning-curve regime (does Bayes-D also minimize worst-case generalization error?). This addresses the gap because the artifact is the bridge: Fedorov's design criteria, the Bayes-optimal ensemble, and Tishby's information gain become one scoring function evaluated three ways, validated by replica curves and simulation, replacing heuristic query rules with a principled criterion and quantifying how far heuristics such as the 42% figure sit from optimality. All required ingredients appear in the cited sources, and the perceptron setting keeps both analytics and simulation tractable.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Three streams in these sources circle the same question—which training example is most informative—yet never connect, a bridge opportunity. (1) Statistical mechanics of learning (Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby 1992; Tishby-Levin-Solla 1989; Opper-Haussler 1991) gives a Gibbs posterior over a network's version space, an explicit information gain as entropy reduction from the prior, and replica learning curves; Opper-Haussler flag generalization-error versus one-step information gain as open but never use it to pick examples. (2) Physics example selection (Watkin-Rau 1992; Baum 1991 queries) shows big gains (about 42% fewer wasted examples) via an ad hoc rule—query orthogonal to the current estimate J (maximally ambiguous input)—with no optimality criterion or guarantee. (3) Classical optimal experimental design (Fedorov 1972) is a mature theory for this: D-optimality on the information matrix, the Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence theorem, and convergent sequential algorithms; Haussler 1991 adds information-theoretic/VC sample-complexity bounds. None of this has met the network Gibbs posterior or replica analysis. Gap: the physics heuristic lacks Fedorov's optimality criteria, equivalence theorems and convergence guarantees, while design theory has never been posed on the network posterior or given average-case learning curves. Bridging them explains why the maximally-ambiguous query works, makes it principled and guaranteed, and predicts how fast generalization falls under optimal querying.

**Method.** Recast active example selection for perceptrons as sequential Bayesian optimal experimental design on the statistical-mechanics Gibbs posterior, analyzed via replica theory. (1) From the Gibbs posterior over weights after m examples, define a candidate input x's expected one-step information gain as the mutual information between its label and W (expected entropy drop); for a binary perceptron this equals the binary entropy of the posterior vote split, maximal at 50/50. Show analytically that maximum information gain implies x orthogonal to the posterior-mean J, deriving the Watkin-Rau heuristic as the information-optimal query. (2) Define a posterior information matrix as the Hessian of the log error-partition-function, cast greedy querying as sequential D-optimal design, and prove a network analog of the Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence theorem: the information-maximizing query equals the query minimizing the maximum posterior predictive variance equals the maximally ambiguous point—giving a monotone-improvement convergence guarantee, with Baum's binary search as the 1-bit-per-query limit. (3) Via replica theory compute order parameters under the D-optimal query stream versus random sampling for spherical and Ising perceptrons, and for both Gibbs and Bayes-optimal (Opper-Haussler) predictors; predict asymptotic decay and test whether optimal design beats the 42% saving and turns power-law into exponential decay. (4) Chain per-query information gain against Haussler's VC/information bounds to bound queries needed for target error, and validate by simulating the sequential design algorithm (optimal-stability plus posterior sampling), probing label noise and replica symmetry.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Four separate communities in these sources independently formalized the same idea, choose the most informative example rather than a random one, but never connected. First, statistical-mechanics of learning (Watkin and Rau on selecting examples for perceptrons; statistical mechanics of learning from examples; the Bayes-optimal perceptron paper; consistent inference of probabilities in layered networks) derives average-case learning curves G(alpha) in the thermodynamic limit, shows selecting the next question orthogonal to the current estimate J yields a predicted 42 percent reduction in wasted Ising examples, and links generalization error to one-step information gain and entropy. Second, optimal experimental design (Fedorov) selects the point maximizing the variance function or information-matrix determinant via D and G optimality with sequential update. Third, PAC query learning (Baum) gives worst-case distribution-free query-complexity guarantees for membership queries to layered threshold nets. Fourth, Bayesian information-theoretic sample complexity relates entropy reduction to VC dimension. The physics rule of querying where the estimator is maximally uncertain is structurally the same as Fedorov's maximum-variance G-optimal rule and greedy information-gain selection, yet these equivalences are never stated and the average-case physics predictions are never reconciled with worst-case PAC and VC bounds. This is a Bridge Opportunity: disconnected literatures studying one phenomenon with incompatible objectives (learning curve versus information-matrix determinant versus worst-case query count versus entropy) and assumptions (thermodynamic limit, Gaussian regression, distribution-free adversary, Bayesian prior), with no shared benchmark. Since no source spans more than one framing, it is unknown when the physics predictions hold, how they compare to optimal-design criteria, and where worst-case theory diverges from average-case behavior, a gap that matters because practitioners have no principled map of which active-learning criterion wins in which regime.

**Method.** Build an empirical map comparing the four communities' example-selection principles on a common family of learnable problems, overlaying each community's own theoretical prediction. Step 1: cast every rule in one notation for the perceptron and linearly-separable-Boolean problem (spherical and Ising, realizable and noisy) and Baum's depth-2 threshold nets, implementing a random baseline, stat-mech selection orthogonal to the current estimate J, Fedorov G and D optimal maximum-variance design with sequential update, greedy maximum information-gain and entropy-reduction, and Baum-style membership queries; record proven equivalences explicitly. Step 2: sweep dimension N (finite-N versus thermodynamic limit), sample ratio alpha equal to p over N, noise level, task type, and input distribution (uniform versus skewed and adversarial), estimating learning curves G(alpha), information gain per query, and compute cost over many targets and seeds with confidence intervals. Step 3: on the same axes overlay each theory, namely replica G(alpha) and the 42 percent Ising prediction, Fedorov optimality, Baum's order nk query PAC bound, and information-theoretic plus VC-dimension bounds, quantifying agreement and divergence such as finite-N deviations, loose versus tight worst-case bounds, and noise breaking predictions. Step 4 deliverables: a phase diagram of which criterion minimizes error and cost per regime; a correspondence table linking stat-mech uncertainty selection, Fedorov G-optimality, and information gain with empirical evidence of when they coincide or diverge; and diagnostics quantifying the average-case versus worst-case gap. This creates the first common empirical footing connecting these disconnected literatures and is feasible because all tasks are small, exactly simulable perceptrons and shallow nets as in the sources.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 31 tool calls

**Motivation.** Two disconnected literatures answer the same question: which example should a learner query next to learn fastest? Classical optimal experimental design (Fedorov; Kiefer-Wolfowitz) offers rigorous objectives (D-optimality on the information matrix, G-optimality on max predictive variance), an equivalence theorem that D- and G-optimal designs coincide, and convergent sequential algorithms, but only for linear Gaussian regression, never for zero-one-loss classifier learning curves. Statistical mechanics of learning (Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby; Tishby-Levin-Solla Gibbs version-space posterior; Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal perceptron) gives exact learning curves via the student-teacher overlap R, and active-query heuristics (Watkin-Rau; Kinzel-Rujan) that pick examples on the decision boundary with a claimed 42 percent saving, but purely heuristically. A third stream (Haussler-Kearns-Schapire; Opper-Haussler one-step information gain) links information gain to generalization but gives no query rule. Bridge opportunity: no framework casts statistical-mechanics active learning as optimal experimental design, derives the physics heuristics as provable optima, and predicts learning curves. Connecting these would yield a principled query law with convergence guarantees, sample-complexity bounds, and predicted exponents.

**Method.** A formal-derivation, thermodynamic-limit optimal-experiment-design theory for classifier learning curves. (1) Use the Gibbs version-space posterior over perceptron weights and a query design measure rho over inputs, the analogue of Fedorov design measure, with order parameter R and error eps equal to (1/pi) arccos R. (2) Define Bayesian D-optimality (maximize expected posterior-entropy reduction, i.e. one-step information gain) and Bayesian G-optimality (minimize the max over inputs of posterior predictive disagreement). (3) Prove an equivalence theorem, the thermodynamic-limit analogue of Kiefer-Wolfowitz: via the replica-symmetric saddle point for spherical and Ising perceptrons, the D- and G-optimal query measures coincide and concentrate on the posterior-mean decision boundary, deriving the Watkin-Rau and Kinzel-Rujan heuristic as provably optimal and identifying wasted examples as the uniform design sub-optimality gap (recovering 42 percent). (4) Turn expected information gain into an ODE for d eps over d alpha, integrate to get the optimal-query learning curve, and compare its exponent to random sampling and the Opper-Haussler Bayes bound. (5) Give the Fedorov iterative update at the maximal-disagreement point, prove monotone convergence, and combine with Haussler-Kearns-Schapire information and VC bounds to bound queries needed for target eps. All ingredients exist in the sources and are analytically tractable in the perceptron model.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 22 turns / 34 tool calls

**Motivation.** Statistical-mechanics learning (Watkin and Rau; Seung and Sompolinsky) selects examples using only a single point-estimate weight vector, ignoring the full posterior. Bayesian work (Opper and Haussler; Tishby) shows version-space volume and information gain govern generalization but only analyzes them passively. Fedorov optimal experimental design gives principled sequential criteria but only for linear regression. Baum shows queries are cheap. These are disconnected; bridging them enables a principled posterior-based query rule for perceptrons.

**Method.** Maintain the Gibbs posterior over perceptron weights consistent with labels, sampled as an ensemble. At each step choose the query maximizing expected information gain, the classification analog of Fedorov D-optimality, biased toward the largest posterior-covariance eigendirection. Select queries by optimizing this objective over input space via gradient ascent or pool screening as costly resources. Derive the learning curve by replica theory and compare to random selection, the Watkin-Rau heuristic, and the Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal bound; validate on spherical and Ising perceptrons and extend to Baum depth-2 query nets.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Three research communities represented in these sources have independently developed the idea of *choosing which examples a learner sees*, yet they have never been connected, and each was worked out only under idealized assumptions.

(1) The classical statistics of optimal experimental design (Fedorov, "Theory of Optimal Experiments") gives a rigorous, general machinery for sequentially selecting experiments to minimize the variance/entropy of estimated parameters: D-/G-optimality, the Kiefer–Wolfowitz equivalence theorem, and Fedorov's iterative rule "add the point where the prediction-variance function d(x,ξ) is maximal." But this theory is built for linear regression with additive Gaussian noise.

(2) The statistical mechanics of learning community independently invented active query learning for perceptrons. Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") show a 42% reduction in wasted examples by querying inputs that minimize the aligning field |J·ξ| (i.e. querying near the current decision boundary); Baum constructs queries geometrically by bisecting between + and − examples; Opper & Haussler compute the Bayes-optimal predictor and note a link to "one-step information gain"; Tishby, Levin & Solla frame learning as a Gibbs ensemble whose "information gain" is entropy reduction. But every one of these active-selection results assumes a *noise-free, realizable* teacher and the ability to place a query *anywhere* in input space.

The bridge that is missing: the perceptron "minimize the field" heuristic is nothing but Fedorov's "query where prediction variance is maximal," specialized to a noiseless classifier — yet no one has stated this equivalence, nor used the mature experimental-design theory to justify, generalize, or correct the physics heuristics. Meanwhile the same review that studies active selection (Seung/Sompolinsky/Tishby) shows that once rules are *unrealizable*, quenched disorder and spin-glass phases make generalization behave qualitatively differently and non-monotonically — exactly the regime in which greedy uncertainty sampling is most likely to be misled, and precisely the regime the active-learning papers never touch. This matters because real learners face label noise, model mismatch, and a fixed pool of inputs they cannot freely design, so we do not know whether the celebrated efficiency gains of query learning survive, vanish, or reverse.

**Method.** Build a single framework — "statistical-mechanical optimal experimental design for learning" — that identifies active example selection with sequential Bayesian optimal design on the Gibbs/version-space posterior, and then *relaxes the idealizing assumptions* under which all prior active-learning results were derived.

Step 1 — Unify. Represent the learner not by a single weight vector but by the Gibbs posterior over networks at temperature T (following Tishby–Levin–Solla and the Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby ensemble; Opper–Haussler's Bayes-optimal predictor is the T-averaged output). Define a design criterion = expected reduction in posterior entropy (information gain) from labeling a candidate input. Show analytically that this criterion (i) reduces to Fedorov's D-optimality and the equivalence-theorem "maximum prediction-variance" rule in the linear-Gaussian limit, and (ii) reduces to the Watkin–Rau "minimize |J·ξ|" rule and Baum's boundary-bisection in the noise-free realizable perceptron limit. This makes the two literatures special cases of one design functional.

Step 2 — Relax the assumptions, one regime at a time, and compute typical-case learning curves with the replica/annealed methods already used in these sources:
  (a) Label noise / stochastic teacher: introduce a teacher temperature and re-derive the optimal design. Prediction: greedy boundary-querying (which places all mass on the noisiest, most ambiguous inputs) becomes suboptimal exactly as Fedorov's theory warns; there is an optimal "query softness" that spreads queries away from the boundary, and an optimal matching between query temperature and inference temperature T.
  (b) Unrealizable rules (model mismatch): here entropy reduction in *parameter* space (D-optimality) diverges from reduction in *generalization error*, because the posterior concentrates on a wrong minimum. Replace the parameter-space criterion by a predictive-error criterion (integrate Tishby–Levin–Solla's prediction error into the design objective) and compute whether active selection still helps; predict a phase where uncertainty sampling is actively harmful because it chases unrealizable structure, connected to the spin-glass phases reported for unrealizable rules.
  (c) Pool-based / fixed input distribution: forbid free query construction (the unrealistic power Baum and Watkin–Rau assume) and instead select from inputs drawn from p(S). Introduce this as a constrained design measure (Kiefer–Wolfowitz designs are exactly measures over a compact input space), and quantify how much of the 42% saving survives when queries cannot be placed arbitrarily.
  (d) Finite-N corrections to the thermodynamic-limit predictions.

Step 3 — Validate against the known anchors: the framework must reproduce Watkin–Rau's 42% and Opper–Haussler's Bayes-optimal √2 factor in the noiseless realizable limit, and Fedorov's D-optimal designs in the linear limit, then extend beyond all of them. Deliverables: (i) a design–inference duality theorem linking experimental-design optimality to Bayes-optimal generalization; (ii) closed-form or numerically-solved learning curves showing when active selection helps, saturates, or reverses as functions of noise level, degree of unrealizability, and pool constraint; (iii) a corrected, noise-robust query criterion that interpolates between "minimize the field" and D-optimal spreading. This directly addresses the gap: it makes the physics active-learning results function under weaker assumptions (noise, model mismatch, restricted queries, finite size) by importing and generalizing the classical optimal-design theory they were unknowingly re-deriving.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Four streams in these sources all address which examples a learner should acquire and how well it will then generalize, yet were developed in isolation: statistical-mechanics of learning and example selection (Seung/Sompolinsky/Tishby 1992; Opper and Haussler 1991; Watkin and Rau 1992, showing orthogonal example selection cuts wasted examples about 42 percent); query learning in computational learning theory (Baum 1991, boundary membership queries with polynomial-time guarantees); classical optimal experimental design (Fedorov 1972, D and G optimality and information-matrix maximization) framed for linear regression and never connected to neural nets; and Bayesian and information-theoretic generalization theory (Opper and Haussler Bayes-optimal classifier, noted as hard to implement; Tishby, Levin and Solla posterior Gibbs measure; Haussler, Kearns and Schapire sample-complexity bounds). No work links Fedorov design theory and the Bayesian posterior view to statistical-mechanics query strategies. Critically, all concrete active and query strategies assume a noise-free realizable teacher and greedily pick the single most-uncertain boundary point, yet Seung et al. show that for unrealizable or noisy rules learning curves become non-monotone, approximations fail, and spin-glass phases appear. Greedy maximum-uncertainty querying concentrates on the boundary where label noise dominates, so under noise or mismatch the most efficient known algorithm wastes queries or degrades generalization, a brittleness none of the streams analyze because they never connect.

**Method.** Build a robust Bayesian active example-selection framework for perceptron learning that unifies Fedorov-style optimal design, the posterior Gibbs measure, and statistical-mechanics learning-curve analysis. First, represent knowledge as the Gibbs posterior over weights at temperature T rather than a single estimate, tracking the order parameter R equal to J dot B and the posterior spread analytically. Second, the bridging design criterion: replace greedy boundary querying with a Fedorov D and G optimality analogue on the posterior predictive, picking the query maximizing expected reduction in Bayes generalization risk rather than raw boundary information, importing optimal-experiment-design theory into neural query learning. Third, robustification: introduce an explicit noise and mismatch parameter (a label-flip rate eta, or a mismatched-architecture teacher as in Seung et al.), and make selection a minimax or Bayesian-averaged design maximizing worst-case expected risk reduction over an uncertainty set of eta, weighting candidates by predicted label reliability; this penalizes noise-dominated boundary queries, prevents boundary-collapse, and reduces to the Watkin and Rau orthogonal-query rule in the noise-free limit. Fourth, use replica and annealed machinery to derive generalization error versus alpha for random sampling, greedy margin query, plain Fedorov design, and robust minimax design, for Ising and spherical perceptrons, as functions of alpha and eta, predicting where greedy querying becomes non-monotone or diverges. Fifth, connect achieved query complexity to information-theoretic and VC bounds for worst-case reliability, and validate with finite-N simulations measuring query efficiency and, crucially, variance and worst-case error across increasing noise and teacher-student mismatch.

#### [opportunity=Bridge Opportunity; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** Four disconnected literatures here attack one question — how to learn a rule with as few and as well-chosen examples as possible — yet each invented its own incompatible notion of how informative an example is, and none reconciled them. (1) Classical optimal experimental design (Fedorov, 1972): informativeness via the Fisher/information matrix and D/G-optimality, framed for linear regression, never connected to neural learning. (2) Worst-case query learning (Baum, 1991): queries by geometric binary search on separating hyperplanes, an algorithmic view with no statistical information measure. (3) Statistical mechanics of learning (Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby, 1992; Watkin & Rau 1992, predicting a 42% example reduction from self-selected queries; storage capacity, 1989): average-case learning curves and order parameters (version-space volume), treated separately. (4) Bayesian/information-theoretic theory (Tishby–Levin–Solla, 1989, Gibbs ensemble with information gain; Opper–Haussler, 1991, average one-step information gain; sample-complexity bounds via mutual information and VC dimension, 1991): posterior-entropy measures, connected neither to Fedorov nor to physics active-learning curves. This fragmentation matters: the physics ~42% reduction has never been shown to be the same object as the Bayesian information gain governing sample-complexity bounds nor as D-optimality. We thus lack a single transferable active-learning criterion with average-case learning-curve predictions, finite-sample guarantees, and a tractable algorithm — a clear bridge opportunity to unify these streams around one quantity.

**Method.** Construct one master criterion — expected information gain in the Gibbs posterior ensemble — for query selection, prove the four literatures' criteria are its special cases or limits, then use the unification for analytic learning curves and a practical algorithm. Step 1: adopt the Gibbs ensemble over weight vectors J from the training set (Tishby–Levin–Solla; Opper–Haussler) and score a candidate query x by expected posterior-entropy reduction, i.e. mutual information I(label(x); J | data) — exactly the one-step information gain of Opper–Haussler and the ensemble entropy of Tishby–Levin–Solla. Step 2, reconciliation theorems: (a) for realizable noise-free perceptron learning the gain equals expected log-shrinkage of version-space volume, the statistical-mechanics order parameter, so the Watkin–Rau self-selection rule is its greedy maximizer; (b) in the smooth Gaussian-field (large-margin/noisy) regime a Laplace posterior approximation turns the gain into the log-determinant of the posterior Fisher information, recovering Fedorov D-optimality, with G-optimality as minimizing worst-case predictive variance; (c) maximizing the gain concentrates queries at maximal posterior label uncertainty (decision boundary/minimal margin), reproducing Baum's binary-search-on-hyperplane strategy as the deterministic noise-free case. Step 3: use replica/order-parameter machinery (Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby; storage-capacity work) to compute analytically generalization error versus example number for the information-gain active learner on Ising and spherical perceptrons, comparing random sampling, D-optimal design, and Bayes-optimal passive learning, showing where curves coincide and where active selection changes exponents or removes plateaus. Step 4: combine the equivalence with the mutual-information/VC sample-complexity bounds to prove the greedy learner attains, up to constants, the information-theoretic lower bound on examples, giving the 42%-type speedups a finite-sample, distribution-free footing. Step 5: instantiate a tractable maximum-posterior-predictive-uncertainty query rule (estimate posterior label variance from a small Gibbs/Langevin sample of J, query the maximizing input); validate on Ising and spherical perceptrons that learning curves match replica predictions, that it recovers and extends the ~42% reduction to optimally-stable/noisy cases, and that via its D-optimal limit it matches classical optimal-design behavior on a linear-regression control. The result unifies optimal experimental design, query learning, statistical-mechanics active learning, and Bayesian information-theoretic sample complexity.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The works give sharp learning-theory predictions under incompatible idealized assumptions, with no shared instrumented testbed to observe, compare, audit, or accumulate evidence on whether they hold at finite N and across regimes.

**Method.** Build an open benchmark platform implementing each paper's selection strategy behind one interface over a common teacher-student perceptron generator, logging empirical learning curves against closed-form predictions, sweeping N and noise, and publishing a reproducible evidence repository.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 16 tool calls

**Motivation.** A cluster of results shows that a learner can do far better than passively absorbing random examples: Baum's query algorithm bisects near the decision boundary, Watkin & Rau's "selecting examples for perceptrons" predicts a 42% reduction in wasted examples by querying points orthogonal to the current weight vector, Opper & Haussler compute the Bayes-optimal generalization curve and relate it to an "average one-step information gain," Fedorov's optimal experimental design offers D-/G-optimality criteria, and Tishby et al.'s consistent-inference framework identifies the natural currency of learning as the reduction of ensemble entropy (the accessible configuration / version-space volume). The statistical-mechanics-of-learning review and the storage-capacity and statistical-theory-of-a-rule works supply exactly solvable learning curves for the perceptron teacher–student setting.

The evidence gap: each of these selection principles (query-near-boundary, Bayesian information-maximization, D-optimal experimental design) is proposed and analyzed in its own idiom (replica computations, Bayesian posteriors, variance/information matrices, worst-case VC bounds), and each reports its gains against a different baseline. The field therefore has no common instrument to *measure* how efficiently any given query strategy actually extracts information, no way to *benchmark* competing strategies against the true achievable optimum in the same units, and no diagnostic to *audit* when and why a heuristic (e.g., always querying at the boundary) becomes wasteful (correlated, redundant, or noise-amplifying queries). Claims like the "42% reduction" cannot be placed on an absolute scale, and evidence about different regimes (spherical vs. discrete Ising weights, noise-free vs. noisy teachers) cannot be accumulated or compared. This matters because it is impossible to know how much room for improvement remains, or which principle to trust, without such a measuring stick.

The listed works leave this open: they provide the solvable model and each piece of the machinery (entropy currency, Bayes-optimal bound, exact learning curves) but never combine them into a shared measurement and benchmarking framework for query/example-selection strategies.

**Method.** Build a solvable "information-efficiency measurement instrument" for example-selection strategies, using the perceptron teacher–student problem in the thermodynamic limit as the testbed where a ground-truth optimum exists.

(1) Common currency. Adopt Tishby et al.'s ensemble entropy — the reduction of the log accessible version-space (Gardner) volume, equivalently posterior entropy — as the single measurable quantity. For any strategy, express its behavior through the standard order parameters (student–teacher overlap R(alpha) and volume/entropy S(alpha)) obtained from the replica calculation, exactly as in Watkin & Rau. From these define the marginal per-query information gain g(alpha) = -dS/dalpha.

(2) Absolute reference. Compute the Bayes-optimal one-step information gain g*(alpha) from the Opper–Haussler optimal generalization curve, which is the maximal achievable entropy reduction per example. Define the measurable "information efficiency" eta(alpha) = g(alpha)/g*(alpha) in [0,1], and an aggregate efficiency (area under the eta curve). This turns every strategy into a number on the same scale and lets the "42% reduction" and similar claims be re-expressed as efficiency relative to the true optimum.

(3) Unified strategy family. Instantiate the same instrument on a menu of selection rules cast as different scoring functions on candidate inputs: (a) query-near-boundary / minimal-margin (Watkin & Rau, Baum), (b) Bayesian maximum expected information gain / query-by-committee style disagreement (Opper–Haussler), (c) Fedorov D-optimal / maximum-variance design mapped into the perceptron's information matrix, and (d) random sampling as the passive baseline. Each rule is fed into the same order-parameter equations (or Monte-Carlo version-space sampling in finite N) so that all are measured identically.

(4) Benchmark and audit. Produce learning-curve benchmarks plotting generalization error and eta(alpha) for every strategy together with the optimal bound, across controlled conditions: spherical vs. discrete Ising weights (where entropy can go negative and a first-order transition to perfect generalization occurs), output noise, and input-distribution mismatch. Use eta and its breakdown to diagnose failure modes — e.g., quantify query redundancy/correlation by measuring the shortfall g(alpha) << g*(alpha) that arises when boundary queries become mutually informative-poor, and identify regimes where a heuristic actually underperforms random sampling.

(5) Validation and accumulation. Cross-check the analytic replica predictions against finite-size simulations (perceptron learning with MinOver/optimal-stability constructions and posterior sampling), and package the whole thing as a reproducible benchmark suite with the version-space-entropy efficiency eta as the standard reported metric, so that future selection strategies can be dropped in and compared on the same absolute, information-theoretic scale.

This addresses the gap by supplying the missing shared instrument: it observes and measures information extraction in interpretable bits, benchmarks heterogeneous strategies against the exact optimum, audits when heuristics waste queries, and lets evidence accumulate across noise levels and network types — all within an exactly solvable model where the answers can be trusted.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** The sources predict perceptron learning curves under many separate assumptions but never compare them on one empirical platform, leaving an evidence gap about finite-size corrections, query gains, and assumption validity.

**Method.** Build a benchmark that simulates perceptron learning across model classes, learning rules, example-selection strategies, and system sizes, measures generalization curves, and compares them against the closed-form predictions of the sources to map finite-size corrections, query gains, and where replica assumptions break down.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** These works sharply characterize how much a learner knows: generalization error e_g and learning curves in the review (statistical-mechanics-of-learning-from-examples-1992), version-space volume V and one-step information gain in the Bayes-optimal analysis (generalization-performance-of-bayes-optimal), ensemble relative entropy as information gain in the Gibbs framework (consistent-inference-of-probabilities-in-layered-networks), and entropy with teacher overlap R(alpha) used to prove example selection cuts wasted examples by about 42 percent (selecting-examples-for-perceptrons; improving-a-network-generalization; Baum queries; Fedorov optimal design). Evidence gap: each informative quantity is defined via things unobservable during real training, namely the true teacher (order parameter R, hence e_g), exact version-space volume, or worst-case VC and information-theoretic bounds (bounds-on-the-sample-complexity). The field has predictions of information gain and selection efficiency but no instrument: no observable, teacher-agnostic way to measure how many bits an example contributed, audit whether a strategy is at the information-theoretic optimum, diagnose when a strategy stalls due to unrealizable or noisy targets rather than saturation, and compare strategies and architectures on one normalized scale so evidence accumulates. The review only notes the annealed approximation could estimate e_g from training error for random examples; no observable measure exists for the actively-selected non-random streams these papers advocate. Active-learning claims like the 42 percent figure rest on knowing the teacher and cannot be validated where it is unknown.

**Method.** A formal derivation turning the unobservable information-gain quantities of these papers into one observable, normalized instrument. Formal model: adopt the Gibbs posterior over hypotheses of consistent-inference and its zero-temperature version space from the Bayes-optimal paper; define realized per-example information gain g(x) as the drop in ensemble relative entropy, equivalently the log-shrinkage of version-space volume, when candidate x is queried. Central theorem: with q(x) the posterior probability the ensemble labels x positive, prove expected gain equals binary entropy H2(q(x)) and Bayes error contributed equals min(q,1-q); both are estimable with no teacher by sampling a committee from the Gibbs posterior via finite-temperature stochastic training runs and measuring vote disagreement at x. Bounds and audit metric: derive g(x) at most one bit, equality iff q(x) equals one half (x on the current boundary), recovering the version-space halving bound of Opper-Haussler and the boundary-selection rule of Watkin-Rau as the maximizer; define observable efficiency eta as realized gain over maximal gain in the unit interval, and prove cumulative gains lower-bound version-space shrinkage and upper-bound achievable e_g, extending the review's train-error-to-e_g estimate from random to arbitrary actively-selected streams. Diagnostics: show that under unrealizable or noisy targets committee disagreement saturates so eta collapses toward zero while training error stays positive, a computable signature separating saturation from unrealizability and overtraining. Validation: because the perceptron models have exact analytic learning curves, validate the observable estimators against ground truth where the teacher is known, then apply where unknown, and instantiate a benchmark reporting eta-curves for random sampling, boundary selection, Baum queries, and Bayes-optimal querying, recovering the 42 percent wasted result from observable committee disagreement alone.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The corpus contains two mature but disconnected bodies of work. First, statistical mechanics yields exact generalization curves and information-theoretic bounds for learning from randomly sampled examples: inverse-power-law and exponential learning-curve tails (Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby review), the Bayes-optimal error and its sqrt(2) advantage over Gibbs learning (Opper-Haussler), Bayesian sample-complexity bounds tied to VC dimension, and consistent-inference predictions for layered nets. Second, several works propose active example/query selection to beat random sampling: Watkin-Rau predict a 42 percent reduction in wasted examples by selecting questions orthogonal to the current estimate, Baum's polynomial-time algorithm uses membership-query bisection, and the classical theory of optimal experiments frames this as experimental design. The evidence gap: each selection strategy is analyzed in isolation for a single idealized model (usually a noise-free, realizable perceptron) and measured on its own terms. The field has no common yardstick to measure how much of the information-theoretically available error reduction a strategy actually harvests, to compare heterogeneous strategies on equal footing, or to audit when active selection's advantage survives label noise, model mismatch (unrealizable rules, where the review shows approximations fail and error can be non-monotonic), and over/under-parameterization. This matters because active learning's promise is efficiency, yet we cannot say how close any strategy comes to the achievable optimum nor where the savings evaporate. The exact SM learning curves are the missing measuring stick, but no work uses them to benchmark selection strategies.

**Method.** Build an optimization-driven benchmarking framework that uses exact statistical-mechanics learning curves as a ground-truth yardstick and casts next-example selection as adaptive optimization. First, metrics: for each model use the SM/Bayes-optimal curve e_g*(alpha) as reference; define information yield as realized error reduction per query divided by the maximum reduction implied by e_g* at the same alpha, and sample-complexity gap as extra examples needed to reach a target error relative to the Bayes-optimal curve. Second, a benchmark suite spanning axes the corpus isolates but never crosses: Ising vs spherical perceptrons, realizable vs unrealizable teachers, noise-free vs label-noise, and learner type (Hebb, optimal-stability/MinOver, Gibbs, Bayes committee), with large-N simulations compared to analytic predictions. Third, selection-as-optimization: at each step choose the next example by searching input space to maximize a tractable surrogate of expected information gain, unifying prior heuristics as instances: minimal-margin selection placing the query on the current boundary, query-by-committee/version-space bisection using weights sampled from the Gibbs posterior, and Baum membership-query bisection; use candidate-pool screening plus geometric or gradient search to place queries efficiently and scale to large N. Fourth, resource allocation: when cheap random labels and expensive designed queries coexist, treat the mixed budget as scheduling optimization, adaptively allocating queries versus random draws to minimize error per unit cost and comparing the realized cost-error frontier to the theoretical optimum. Fifth, audit: run all strategies across the suite, overlay realized curves on SM benchmark curves, and report information-yield and sample-complexity-gap heatmaps over noise, realizability, and parameterization, testing falsifiable hypotheses such as that minimal-margin selection is fragile under label noise while committee bisection degrades gracefully, and that under model mismatch active selection can underperform random sampling in the non-monotonic regimes the review flags. The output is a reproducible protocol and metric set letting future selection algorithms be benchmarked against the achievable optimum rather than evaluated in isolation.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 26 tool calls

**Motivation.** Evidence gap: the field cannot measure or benchmark whether active query learning actually helps or silently hurts generalization once idealizing assumptions are dropped. Watkin and Rau show analytically a 42 percent reduction in wasted examples for a perceptron that greedily queries inputs on its current decision boundary; Baum's examples-and-queries algorithm and the maximum-information heuristic assume a clean realizable oracle; Opper and Haussler and Tishby-Levin-Solla provide Bayes-optimal and version-space prediction measures. But every result on the value of active selection assumes noise-free labels, a target realizable by the student, and the large-N limit. The review Statistical mechanics of learning treats noise and unrealizable rules only for passive random sampling. This matters because greedy querying concentrates queries exactly on the boundary, where labels are noisiest and an unrealizable target most misleading, so the mechanism that makes it efficient may make it self-defeating. No theory, metric, or benchmark exists to observe when this happens, nor evidence comparing greedy versus Bayes-optimal querying under realistic conditions.

**Method.** Relax the noise-free, realizable, and large-N assumptions of existing statistical-mechanics analyses of query learning and convert the relaxed theory into a diagnostic benchmark. First, take Watkin and Rau's replica order-parameter machinery and the Opper-Haussler version-space formulation and re-derive the generalization curve for query learning under three graduated relaxations: label noise where the oracle flips the teacher label with probability lambda so boundary queries get the least reliable answers; architecture mismatch with an unrealizable target such as a reversed-wedge perceptron or committee machine giving an error floor; and finite N with leading corrections. Second, compare three selection rules under each condition: passive random sampling as baseline, greedy boundary querying, and Bayes-optimal querying that maximizes expected version-space information gain using the full posterior. Third, define benchmark metrics as functions of lambda, mismatch, and N: the asymptotic error floor, the break-even sample size where active first beats then may lose to random, a query-safety margin measuring drift onto the noisy boundary, and a robustness ratio of active advantage under noise over active advantage when clean. These pinpoint the regime where greedy querying flips from beneficial to harmful and test whether posterior-aware Bayes-optimal querying stays robust where greedy fails. Fourth, validate against finite-N Monte Carlo simulations of Ising and spherical perceptrons scanning noise, mismatch, and N. The deliverable is a phase-diagram benchmark annotated with which strategy is safe plus closed-form metric expressions, giving the first auditable account of when active example selection helps versus hurts.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Active example-selection for perceptrons (Watkin-Rau) gives large efficiency gains but is derived only in noise-free, realizable teacher-student settings. Querying near the boundary is exactly where label noise and teacher-student mismatch cause most harm. The field lacks any way to measure, benchmark, or diagnose when active selection helps versus hurts generalization under noise, and lacks a reliability audit. The SM review flags noisy rules but not active selection; Bayes-optimal and active-learning results are noise-free. The provided works supply the machinery to both measure and fix this but never combine it.

**Method.** First, build a robustness benchmark: extend the replica learning-curve calculation for boundary-querying to label noise and teacher-student mismatch, measuring the gap between idealized and noisy curves and the point where selection underperforms random sampling. Second, robustify the query rule: sample at a tunable margin offset from the boundary rather than exactly on it, score candidates by Bayes posterior variance to reduce single-point leverage, and down-weight noise-dominated queries. Third, add an online diagnostic using the prediction-probability criterion and committee disagreement to detect harmful regimes and revert to random sampling. Validate analytically and via finite-N simulation.

#### [opportunity=Evidence Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 32 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works analyze learning from examples in disconnected communities lacking a common yardstick. Worst-case learning theory (Baum examples-and-queries algorithm; VC and PAC bounds in the Gyorgyi-Tishby review and the Bayesian-information VC paper) counts queries and bounds error via one combinatorial number. Typical-case statistical mechanics (the review; Watkin and Rau example selection) predicts generalization curves and speedups like a 42 percent reduction in wasted examples. Bayes-optimal analysis (Opper and Haussler) tracks version-space volume shrinkage and one-step information gain. Consistent inference (Tishby et al.) measures ensemble entropy relative to a prior. Optimal experiment design (Fedorov) maximizes a Fisher information matrix. Each claims efficiency gains from selecting examples but reports them in incommensurable units. The review flags that PAC is worst-case while statistical mechanics is typical-case, yet no shared measurement framework lets us observe whether the predictions agree, how far achievable typical speedups fall below worst-case bounds, or whether a gain in one framework transfers to another. The field cannot accumulate comparable evidence on when, and how much, active example selection helps.

**Method.** Synthesis in two parts. First, adopt version-space log-volume or entropy (the Bayes posterior or statistical-mechanics Gibbs ensemble entropy, as in Opper-Haussler and Tishby et al.) as one accounting variable, and prove the four literatures selection rules are instances or bounds of maximizing expected posterior-entropy reduction per example: Baum binary-search queries as one-step volume halving; Watkin-Rau selection as thermodynamic-limit phase-space shrinkage; Opper-Haussler information gain as the exact entropy decrement; Fedorov D-optimality as its Gaussian Fisher-information approximation. This yields one expected-information-gain functional plus conversion formulas among their headline metrics. Second, build a common benchmark on the canonical perceptron family (spherical and Ising, tunable N, realizable and noisy targets), instrumenting every method to emit the same observables versus alpha and N: generalization error, ensemble entropy, and examples or queries to reach a target error. For each, compute four reference curves on identical problems: replica statistical-mechanics typical case, Bayes-optimal information-gain optimum, VC and PAC worst-case envelope, and Monte-Carlo simulation of concrete selectors. The deliverable is a gain spectrum placing all paradigms on shared axes, quantifying the worst-case versus typical gap, cross-paradigm agreement, and the fraction of predicted speedup realizable at finite N.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** These works collectively compute *how well* perceptrons and layered nets generalize, but leave an unexplained, fragmented picture of *why the value of a training example varies so dramatically across settings*. Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") find self-selected queries "waste" fewer examples, predicting a 42% (constant-factor) reduction for one perceptron, yet the Ising perceptron instead shows a discontinuous jump to perfect generalization; Opper & Haussler show the Bayes-optimal rule beats a stochastic (Boltzmann) rule by only a factor approaching √2; Baum's query algorithm turns exponential example needs into polynomial ones. The statistical-mechanics review reports that annealed/high-temperature/replica-symmetric approximations predict learning curves well for *realizable* rules but "fail in general" for *unrealizable* ones, attributing this only vaguely to quenched disorder. Fedorov's optimal-experiment theory offers an information-matrix / D-optimality account of why some designs (queries) are more informative, but it has never been connected to these statistical-mechanics learning curves.

The explanation gap: there is no unified, mechanistic account of *why* the informational value of an example — and hence the speedup from active selection — depends jointly on (a) which learning rule is used (Hebb vs optimal-stability vs Gibbs vs Bayes-optimal), (b) whether the rule is realizable or unrealizable/noisy, and (c) finite N versus the thermodynamic limit. Existing results are asymptotic, rule-specific, and analytical, so they cannot tell a practitioner why active learning delivers a mere constant factor in one regime, an exponential gain in another, or why the elegant asymptotic theory silently breaks down. This matters because example selection is the most efficient known training strategy, yet we cannot predict when it will help or explain the mechanism behind its wildly varying payoff.

**Method.** Build a concrete, reproducible measurement platform — a "learning-curve observatory" — whose central contribution is an instrumented simulation system that empirically decomposes the *value of an example* into mechanistic quantities and confronts the competing theories on identical ground.

Components of the artifact: (1) A unified learning-rule library implementing the algorithms scattered across the sources on a common interface: Hebb, pseudo-inverse, MinOver/optimal-stability (maximal margin κ), Gibbs/Boltzmann at tunable temperature T, and Bayes-optimal prediction realized by Monte-Carlo sampling of the version space (consistent weight vectors). (2) A pluggable example-selection module: random sampling; the Watkin–Rau minimal-margin/orthogonal-to-J query; query-by-committee disagreement using the sampled version-space ensemble; and a design derived from Fedorov's optimal-experiment theory (choose the next input maximizing the D-optimal information-matrix gain) — directly bridging optimal-experiment design and statistical-mechanics learning. (3) A per-step instrumentation layer logging the quantities the theories invoke but never observe together: version-space volume and its shrinkage per query (estimated by hit-and-run/rejection sampling of consistent weights), order parameters R = J·B and the replica overlap q, generalization error G, empirical information gain (entropy reduction) per selected example, and the eigenspectrum of the accumulated example correlation/information matrix. (4) A scenario sweeper varying N (to expose finite-size corrections to thermodynamic predictions), the realizable/unrealizable axis (teacher–student architecture match, label noise, capacity mismatch), and α = p/N.

Protocol: for each (rule, selection strategy, realizability, N) cell, record the measured learning curve alongside the corresponding analytic prediction (annealed, high-temperature, replica-symmetric, Bayes √2 bound, Watkin–Rau 42% prediction). The platform then produces the missing explanatory account as concrete outputs: (i) a causal decomposition attributing each rule's speedup to how much version-space volume a selected example removes and how efficiently the rule converts that volume into overlap R; (ii) an explanatory phase map delineating where active selection yields constant-factor versus exponential gains, tied to whether queries can drive the version space through the storage-capacity transition; and (iii) a quantitative diagnosis of *why* asymptotic approximations fail for unrealizable rules, by correlating prediction error with measured version-space fragmentation and information-matrix ill-conditioning. Feasibility is high: all algorithms are single-layer or small-committee perceptrons already simulated at N=100–200 in the sources, and the instrumentation uses standard Monte-Carlo sampling, so the system can be built and the sweeps run on modest hardware.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 22 turns / 30 tool calls

**Motivation.** Active query-based example selection is reported to dramatically accelerate perceptron learning (Watkin and Rau 42 percent reduction; a shift from power-law to near-exponential decay; exponential boundary bisection), and Opper and Haussler show Bayes-optimal beats Gibbs by a factor approaching root-two while flagging an undeveloped link between generalization error and one-step information gain. Tishby et al define information gain as version-space volume reduction, and Fedorov optimal-design theory offers D and G optimality criteria not invoked here. The gap is explanatory: no mechanistic theory says why these query heuristics attain their rates, whether they maximize information gain, why selection converts algebraic into exponential decay, or how this ties to the root-two gap and classical optimal design.

**Method.** Build a statistical-mechanics theory of active perceptron learning where the query rule is a tunable functional.
