Track teacher-student overlap and posterior version-space volume as functions of load via replica computation. Parameterize queries by the stability at which patterns are drawn, giving differential equations for the overlap and the error decay per rule. Using the information-gain definition, derive expected one-step gain versus query stability, solve for the information-optimal query, and compare to boundary, bisection, and Bayes-optimal rules. Identify the feedback that turns power-law into exponential decay and derive the crossover informativeness; check whether the residual constant recovers root-two. Map onto the Fedorov framework via the Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence to explain why boundary querying is near-optimal and where it breaks. Validate against finite-N Monte Carlo for Ising and spherical perceptrons.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works show that actively selecting or querying examples for perceptrons improves learning, but the reported gains are wildly disparate and unexplained. Watkin and Rau show only a constant factor gain for the Ising perceptron but power law gains for spherical ones, with the Ising case jumping discontinuously to perfect generalization at a critical alpha. Opper and Haussler show Bayes optimal beats Gibbs by only root two. Yet the examples and queries paper shows queries drive error down exponentially. So the payoff ranges from constant factor to changed power law exponent to discontinuous jump to exponential. This is an explanation gap: each result is derived in isolation for one architecture, rule, and heuristic, with no unified causal account of what structural property decides whether active selection helps a little, changes the convergence law, or triggers a qualitative speedup. Explaining this would tell practitioners when query design pays off and unify statistical mechanics learning curves, Bayesian information gain, and Fedorov style optimal experimental design.

**Method.** Build one controlled teacher student testbed and map the active learning advantage across four axes: weight structure Ising versus spherical; inference rule Hebb, pseudoinverse, maximal stability, Gibbs, Bayes optimal; selection strategy random, minimum margin, maximum information gain via Fedorov D optimal, and query by committee; and label noise. For each cell, measure the generalization curve by large N simulation plus replica predictions where tractable, and classify its asymptotic form as power law exponent, exponential rate, or first order jump. Define gain functionals such as the ratio of alpha to reach a target error versus random and the change in exponent or rate. The diagnostic core is to log along each trajectory candidate mechanistic order parameters: version space entropy and its shrinkage rate, teacher student overlap, the margin distribution of selected examples, and information per query. Then correlate gain type with these to test hypotheses. H1: exponential or discontinuous gain needs vanishing margin queries plus a bounded entropy version space from discrete weights that keeps per query information order one. H2: continuous weights allow only a changed power law exponent as per query information decays. H3: noise saturates gain by flooring version space entropy. Structural interventions such as discretizing spherical weights and capping margin confirm drivers causally. The output is a labeled empirical map plus validated structural predictors explaining why active learning speedups across the prior works differ.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across these works, active/query-based example selection for perceptrons produces puzzlingly heterogeneous outcomes that lack a unified explanation. Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") show that choosing questions orthogonal to the current weight estimate (queries lying on the decision boundary) gives only a CONSTANT-FACTOR benefit for the spherical perceptron — a 42% reduction in "wasted" examples — while leaving the asymptotic power-law generalization curve unchanged; yet for the Ising perceptron the same style of selection interacts with the discontinuous jump to perfect generalization. Meanwhile, for PASSIVE (random) learning, Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby provide a clean taxonomy: the asymptotic form of the learning curve (inverse power law, nontrivial power law, exponential tail, or discontinuous transition) is dictated by the exponent z in the version-space entropy s(ε)∝ε^z near ε=0, distinguishing smooth (continuous-weight) from nonsmooth (discrete-weight) networks. Opper & Haussler hint at a governing "one-step information gain" and quantify a √2 gap between Bayes-optimal and Gibbs/Boltzmann learning; Tishby–Levin–Solla cast generalization as a free-energy/entropy contraction of the posterior ensemble; and classical optimal experiment design (Fedorov, via Kiefer–Wolfowitz D-/G-optimality) offers a variational language for "most informative" probes. Despite these ingredients, there is NO causal/mechanistic account of WHY and WHEN actively selecting examples changes the ASYMPTOTIC FORM (exponent) of the learning curve versus merely rescaling its prefactor. Why does boundary-querying buy only a constant factor for the spherical perceptron but qualitatively reshape learning for discrete-weight networks? What is the optimal query and the best achievable rate? This explanatory gap matters because it decides whether active learning is fundamentally more powerful or only marginally cheaper — and the listed works compute isolated cases without deriving the underlying principle.

**Method.** Construct a formal theory of active perceptron learning as sequential Bayesian optimal experiment design over the version-space Gibbs ensemble, and derive from it a predictive dichotomy for learning-curve forms. (1) Model: define the posterior/Gibbs measure on weight space and its relative entropy S(α) (following Tishby–Levin–Solla), together with the exact map e_g=(1/π)arccos R between generalization error and target-overlap order parameter R used throughout the SM literature. (2) Variational characterization theorem: derive the expected one-step entropy reduction ΔS(x) for a candidate query x and prove it is maximized by the maximally ambiguous query — the input on the current posterior's decision boundary (margin→0), i.e., Watkin–Rau's orthogonality prescription — thereby unifying their heuristic with Opper–Haussler's "one-step information gain" and with Fedorov D-optimality (a perceptron analogue of the D/G-optimality equivalence). (3) Flow equations: using replica/annealed averaging, derive the deterministic ODE for S(α) and R(α) under greedy optimal querying, a query-driven counterpart of the SSNT saddle-point equations. (4) Central dichotomy theorem: introduce a "query-informativeness exponent" q that measures how the best attainable per-query information gain scales as the version space contracts (e_g→0), controlled by the density of achievable maximally-ambiguous inputs — which depends on whether inputs are drawn from a fixed distribution or can be synthesized, and on weight-space smoothness. Prove: (a) when informative queries are limited by a fixed input measure with bounded boundary density (continuous weights), optimal querying preserves the SSNT power-law exponent and only improves the prefactor — quantitatively recovering the constant-factor (≈42%) speedup for the spherical perceptron; (b) when the version space can be split by a constant fraction per query (freely synthesizable inputs, or discrete/Ising weights where entropy has a finite floor, SSNT z≥1), S decreases linearly in the number of queries, yielding an exponential curve e_g∝e^{-cα} and/or an earlier discontinuous jump to perfect generalization. (5) Matching lower bounds: combine the entropy-contraction rate with the information-theoretic/VC-dimension Bayesian sample-complexity bounds (Haussler et al.) and the storage-capacity constraints (Gardner's capacity works) to certify that no query strategy beats these rates, establishing that the achievable rate equals min(information supplied per query, version-space geometric contractibility). (6) Validation: check the derived curves reduce, in the appropriate limits, to Watkin–Rau's 42% spherical result and Ising behavior, Opper–Haussler's √2 Bayes factor, and the SSNT passive taxonomy — turning three disparate empirical observations into consequences of a single derivation. The theory explains the "why" (informativeness supply vs. entropy geometry) and provides a constructive optimal-query rule with provable rate.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 25 turns / 34 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query-based example selection greatly speeds perceptron learning (Watkin-Rau 1992 zero-margin selection; Baum 1991 queries), yet no theory explains why the zero-margin heuristic works, whether it is optimal, or how active selection reshapes the learning-curve exponent that Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby (1992) derived only for random examples. Optimal experimental design (Fedorov 1972) and information objectives (Tishby-Levin-Solla 1989; Opper-Haussler 1991) are never linked to the version-space picture, so the optimal query strategy and achievable acceleration are unknown.

**Method.** Formulate adaptive query selection as sequential optimal design. Track order parameters R and version-space entropy S, and choose each query by its margin m. Optimize m analytically under several objectives (version-space-volume reduction, error decrement, information gain, committee disagreement) using replica averages, deriving the optimal margin schedule and testing whether zero-margin is optimal. Feed the optimal query into the saddle-point equations to derive the learning curve, explaining when a power law becomes exponential and quantifying acceleration. Compare Gibbs and Bayes learners, and validate with simulations and Watkin-Rau numerics.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 10 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query (example-selection) learning is the most efficient training scheme in this literature: Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") and the related selection method show a perceptron that continually picks its own next example — the input on its current decision boundary (orthogonal to the student vector J, the maximally ambiguous point) — reaches perfect generalization faster, gaining a constant factor (~40%) toward the theoretical minimum. This is rationalized by optimal experimental design (Fedorov, D/G-optimality) and the information-gain/entropy-reduction view (Tishby–Levin–Solla; Opper–Haussler one-step information gain and Bayes-optimal analysis).

But all of these rest on the same idealizations: noise-free labels, a realizable perceptron teacher, exact knowledge of the student's current estimate, and the thermodynamic limit. This leaves an EXPLANATION GAP. First, there is no mechanistic account of what governs the magnitude of the query advantage. Second and more important: the feature that makes boundary-adjacent examples maximally INFORMATIVE in the clean case also makes them maximally UNRELIABLE once labels are noisy or the rule is unrealizable, because near-boundary labels are most likely corrupted — so the sign of the query effect under realistic conditions is unknown. This matters because Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby show noisy/unrealizable rules behave qualitatively differently (non-monotonic curves, spin-glass phases, decisive quenched disorder), and Opper–Haussler explicitly leave the noisy extension open. No work explains WHEN active querying helps, WHEN it fails, and WHY it may reverse relative to random sampling.

**Method.** Build a statistical-mechanics theory of query learning under weaker, more realistic assumptions, and use it to explain the informativeness-vs-reliability tradeoff. (1) Relax the setting: introduce (a) a stochastic teacher that flips outputs with a probability growing as the example nears the teacher boundary (margin-dependent reliability), plus a fixed-rate flip variant, and (b) unrealizable-teacher variants (architecture mismatch), matching the regimes SST show to be distinct; also allow an imperfect student estimate rather than exact J. (2) Relax the query rule from a point to a family: select the next example at a tunable margin kappa_q from the current student's decision boundary. kappa_q=0 recovers Watkin & Rau's orthogonal query; kappa_q to infinity recovers random sampling, so the theory interpolates between the known limits. (3) Solve: use the Gardner-style replica method (as in Watkin & Rau and SST) to derive coupled order-parameter equations for teacher-student overlap R(alpha) and generalization error G(alpha) as functions of loading alpha, noise level, unrealizability, and kappa_q, for spherical (optimal-stability/MinOver) and Ising perceptrons. Use SST's high-temperature and annealed approximations as tractable limits, cross-checked against replica-symmetric solutions and direct simulations (as Watkin & Rau validated). (4) Explain: decompose the expected one-step information gain (Opper-Haussler/Tishby entropy reduction; Fedorov D-optimality criterion) into a geometric-information factor (peaked at the boundary, kappa_q=0) times a label-reliability factor (suppressed at the boundary under noise). Their product has an interior maximizer kappa_q*(noise, alpha)>0, giving a mechanistic account of the optimal query margin. Predict a critical noise/unrealizability threshold beyond which classic boundary querying is worse than random sampling, and derive the optimal adaptive schedule kappa_q*(alpha). Deliverables: closed-form learning curves G(alpha) across noise and kappa_q; the optimal-margin law kappa_q*(noise); a phase diagram of query-helps vs query-hurts; and the reliability-vs-information decomposition that explains, not merely measures, when and why active example selection works — converting query learning from a clean-case heuristic into a theory valid under noisy, unrealizable, imperfect-estimate conditions.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 18 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query-based active example selection is the headline sample-complexity result in statistical-mechanics learning theory: Watkin & Rau (1992) predict ~42% fewer wasted examples for the Ising perceptron via continual selection, Baum (1991) shows queries evade NP-hardness limits, and the idea descends from optimal experiment design (Fedorov 1972). But every such analysis assumes a realizable, noise-free target, so querying near the current decision boundary maximizes information per example. This leaves an explanation gap: there is no mechanistic account of why the active-learning advantage appears and, crucially, why, when, and how much it should erode or reverse under unrealizability or label noise. This matters because Seung, Sompolinsky & Tishby (1992) show unrealizable rules produce qualitatively new physics: non-monotonic generalization, spin-glass phases, and failure of annealed/high-temperature approximations. The mechanism that makes greedy querying fast (concentrating examples at the boundary) concentrates them exactly where noise and model mismatch are most corrupting; Watkin & Rau even note an effective per-example noise term whose width shrinks with teacher-student overlap, yet never analyze the noisy case. No source combines active selection with the spin-glass/unrealizable regime, so it is unknown whether the celebrated speedup is robust or a brittle artifact of the noise-free idealization.

**Method.** Part 1, mechanistic explanation: extend Watkin & Rau's continual-selection replica calculation to controlled unrealizability by adding teacher label noise and capacity mismatch (overlap R cannot reach 1), using the unrealizable-rule setup of Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby, finite-temperature Gibbs learning of Tishby-Levin-Solla, and Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal learning as baseline. Track order parameters R, q, stability kappa, and the effective per-example noise width, and derive the generalization curve for greedy query-at-zero-margin selection. Output a phase diagram over noise, mismatch, and temperature marking where greedy active learning accelerates, turns non-monotonic, or enters a spin-glass phase where selection hurts, explaining how boundary-concentrated querying amplifies noise. Part 2, robustified rule: replace maximize-information-per-example with a risk-adjusted criterion selecting examples at an optimal offset from the boundary, maximizing expected information minus a noise-amplification penalty computed online from current order parameters; add two stabilizers flagged by the theory, enforcing positive optimal stability and finite-temperature Gibbs averaging to stay out of the spin-glass regime. Offset and kappa adapt from R, q, and estimated noise width, reducing to standard greedy querying in the clean limit. Validation: compare random sampling, greedy querying, and the robust rule in spherical and Ising perceptron simulations across noise and mismatch levels, measuring mean generalization error, run-to-run variance as brittleness, and agreement with predicted phase boundaries. Success means the theory predicts the crossover where greedy querying loses to random sampling, and the robust rule keeps most of the speedup in the clean limit while removing degradation and variance under noise and mismatch.

#### [opportunity=Explanation Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Active example selection accelerates generalization dramatically but unevenly, and no one explains why the speedup varies. Watkin and Rau predict a 42 percent reduction in wasted examples for the Ising perceptron and find optimally selected examples for spherical perceptrons generalize far faster than random ones. Opper and Haussler show the Bayes-optimal predictor beats the Gibbs learner by a factor approaching sqrt(2) and flag the unexplored link between generalization error and one-step information gain. Tishby, Levin and Solla recast learning as entropy reduction, shrinking accessible version-space volume. Fedorov's classical Theory of Optimal Experiments gives a mature calculus of query design: D-optimality, G-optimality, Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence. These literatures developed in isolation, leaving an explanation gap: no unified causal account of why active selection helps, why it sometimes yields only a constant-factor gain, sometimes changes the learning-curve exponent, and sometimes yields near-exponential generalization, and how this depends on hypothesis-space geometry and learner type.

**Method.** Build a unifying framework identifying the statistical-mechanics version-space entropy with the information matrix of classical optimal experimental design. Map the Gibbs-ensemble version-space log-volume onto Fedorov's information matrix, so reducing accessible volume becomes a determinant or variance functional; show maximizing Opper-Haussler one-step information gain is a D-optimality step and Watkin-Rau boundary selection is a G-optimality step, using Kiefer-Wolfowitz equivalence to explain why they coincide. Using replica order parameters already computed in these papers, derive per-query entropy reduction as a functional of the chosen criterion, predicting when it yields a constant factor, changes the exponent, or drives an Ising first-order transition to perfect generalization. Reconcile the sqrt(2) Bayes-vs-Gibbs gap as the difference between prediction-optimality and design-optimality. Validate by recovering known cases and issuing new falsifiable predictions, such as the criterion converting the spherical Bayes power law to exponential decay, testable with existing replica calculations and simulations.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Classic active example-selection and query-learning results promise large label savings under idealized noise-free, realizable teacher-student assumptions with a perfect oracle: Baum's membership queries, Kinzel-Rujan and Watkin-Rau near-boundary and freezing selection (about 42 percent savings), and the Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal curve. This is a reliability gap: the efficiency criterion, querying the most ambiguous lowest-margin points, is exactly what makes it brittle. Such points have the worst signal-to-noise ratio, so label noise, oracle error or bias, or teacher-student mismatch is maximally amplified, can collapse the version space onto the wrong hypothesis, and biases the rule toward unrepresentative inputs. Prior works do not quantify this failure, guard against it, or provide calibrated uncertainty or reproducible evaluation of when active selection catastrophically fails. Since active learning is used precisely when labels are scarce and error-prone, this brittleness matters. The statistical-mechanics learning curves and Gibbs posterior-ensemble formalisms in the same literature provide tools to diagnose and repair it, but have never been combined for this purpose.

**Method.** Build a reusable software artifact, a robust active-query workbench, combining a stress-testing benchmark and a risk-aware query controller. Benchmark: instantiate the teacher-student perceptron (Ising and spherical), implement classic strategies as baselines (random sampling, near-boundary selection, maximum-stability freezing selection, Baum membership queries), and inject controlled seedable failure conditions: label-flip noise, systematic oracle bias, and teacher-student mismatch. Use analytic statistical-mechanics learning curves (Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal and selection curves) as ground-truth references, and report catastrophic-failure rate, worst-case error, and calibration. Central artifact, a risk-aware controller: maintain a Gibbs version-space posterior ensemble over student weights (grounded in Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal classification and Tishby-Levin-Solla consistent inference). Instead of greedily querying the most ambiguous point, select the query maximizing expected posterior information gain subject to a robustness constraint bounding worst-case posterior distortion from an incorrect answer, penalizing high-leverage low-margin queries whose payoff collapses under noise. Replace hard consistency constraints with soft probabilistic updates using an explicit oracle-noise model, a posterior over the label-flip rate, so one wrong answer cannot irrecoverably freeze the version space. The ensemble yields calibrated per-prediction uncertainty and an abstention self-diagnosis signal flagging when the learner leaves its reliable regime. Workflow: wrap the controller as a drop-in module, evaluate against all baselines across the noise, bias and mismatch grid and a simulated fallible human oracle, measuring the trade-off between sample efficiency and catastrophic failure and calibration. This turns idealized noise-optimistic criteria into a robust, uncertainty-aware, reproducible system and delivers the first benchmark quantifying when classic active-learning speedups break down.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap] — claude-opus-4-8, 19 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** Active example-selection methods for perceptrons (Watkin and Rau; Baum) gain efficiency by querying points near the current decision boundary, and the information-gain and Bayes-optimal analyses reward the most ambiguous queries. All assume a noise-free oracle. But near-boundary points are exactly where labels are least reliable, so greedy selection harvests the most corruptible labels and can turn information gain into misinformation. The statistical-mechanics review shows noise and unrealizability cause non-monotone curves and spin-glass phases for passive sampling, but this is never combined with active selection. The gap is an uncharacterized reliability hazard: active learning may be brittle and worse than random sampling under label noise, with no theory of when it is safe.

**Method.** Extend the statistical-mechanics theory of example selection to a noisy oracle. Model a perceptron learning a teacher where each queried label flips with probability eta. The learner queries points at an adjustable margin kappa from its current boundary estimate; kappa near zero is greedy boundary-hugging, large kappa is random sampling. Using replica and annealed methods, compute the generalization curve versus load alpha, noise eta, and margin kappa, capturing that low-margin points are flipped more by noise. Derive the eta-alpha phase diagram and the critical curve beyond which greedy selection makes generalization worsen and passive sampling wins. Derive the information-optimal margin kappa-star trading information against noise reliability, and add replicated queries with majority voting plus a Bayes-posterior acceptance step to reject inconsistent labels. Validate with Monte Carlo simulations comparing greedy, kappa-star, and passive sampling, quantifying when active learning is safe.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 14 tool calls

**Motivation.** The prior works establish that actively *selecting* or *querying* training examples dramatically accelerates perceptron learning: Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") predict a ~42% reduction in the examples "wasted" by continually selecting one's own questions; the neural-net queries paper shows membership queries can even evade NP-completeness barriers; and "Improving a network's generalization ability by selecting examples" advances the same program. These gains, however, are all derived under strongly idealized assumptions: a realizable, matched teacher–student perceptron, noise-free labels, and the thermodynamic limit. The Bayes-optimal analysis is likewise explicitly "noise-free," and the statistical-mechanics survey shows that as soon as rules become *unrealizable* (architecture mismatch or noise-corrupted data), the well-behaved picture breaks down — annealed/high-temperature approximations fail, learning curves become non-monotonic, generalization error need not decrease, and genuine spin-glass phases with degenerate, high-barrier minima appear.

This exposes a Failure/Risk gap that none of the sources close. The very mechanism that makes active selection efficient — concentrating queries on maximally informative points near the current decision boundary, i.e., the most *ambiguous* region — is also the region where label noise, input noise, and teacher–student mismatch have the largest relative effect. There is therefore a strong prior reason to suspect that aggressive example selection is *brittle*: it may amplify the influence of noise and mismatch and produce generalization that is worse, and less predictable, than plain random sampling. Because active/query learning is exactly the regime where labels are expensive and one cannot afford to average over many redundant points, silent brittleness is a real reliability hazard. Yet no systematic map exists of *when* selecting examples helps versus hurts, how the crossover depends on noise and unrealizability, or whether the onset of brittleness can be diagnosed before it corrupts the learner.

**Method.** Construct a systematic empirical map — a "robustness phase diagram" — of example-selection strategies for perceptron learning under controlled failure conditions, combining the two toolkits already present in these sources: analytic statistical mechanics (annealed and replica-symmetric averages over quenched example disorder, as in the survey and the Bayes-optimal paper) and matched large-N Monte Carlo simulation of the training dynamics.

(1) Strategy axis. Implement a common family of learners/selectors on identical data: (a) random sampling (baseline), (b) maximally-informative boundary queries in the Watkin–Rau style, (c) membership queries as in the neural-net-queries paper, (d) query-by-committee / version-space disagreement selection, and (e) the Bayes-optimal reference algorithm as the "best possible" standard against which the others are scored. A tunable "aggressiveness" knob interpolates each active strategy toward random by mixing a fraction f of random examples with (1−f) selected ones.

(2) Perturbation axis (the benchmark). Systematically vary the failure factors flagged by the survey and Bayes-optimal paper: output label-flip noise rate, input (question) noise, and degree of teacher–student unrealizability (architecture mismatch: e.g., Ising-vs-spherical weights, or a teacher of higher complexity than the student). Sweep these against the load α = examples/weight.

(3) Measurements. For every (strategy, aggressiveness, perturbation, α) cell, measure the generalization error curve and derive: a Brittleness Index = worst-case excess error of an active strategy relative to random at fixed label budget; the crossover locus where active selection stops beating random (and where it turns actively harmful); and indicators of the pathologies the survey associates with unrealizability — non-monotonic learning curves and evidence of spin-glass-like degeneracy/high-barrier trapping under selected data. We also record *where in input space* queries concentrate to test the hypothesis that boundary concentration drives noise amplification.

(4) Diagnostics and mitigation. From the maps, extract cheap, observable early-warning signals of impending brittleness (e.g., collapse of committee disagreement, over-concentration of queries in a shrinking margin band, or a rise in empirical-vs-generalization error gap) and validate whether they predict the analytic crossover. Finally, use the map to test a simple robustifying prescription — the random/selected mixing knob f — and chart the minimal randomization needed to recover graceful degradation across the noise and mismatch grid.

The deliverable is an empirical/analytic atlas that turns the currently untested optimism of query learning into quantified reliability guarantees: a map of the regimes where selecting examples is safe, where it silently fails, and how to detect and defuse that failure.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** The most efficient learners in these works choose their own inputs: Baum's examples-and-queries algorithm drives a binary search toward the separating hyperplane, and Watkin and Rau's statistical-mechanics analysis of selecting examples gains about 41 percent efficiency by querying inputs with near-zero margin to the current decision boundary. Fedorov's optimal-experiment theory (D- and G-optimality via the information matrix) justifies such greedy information-maximizing designs, and Opper and Haussler give the Bayes-optimal decoder such designs feed. Crucially, all these query and selection results assume a noise-free oracle: Opper and Haussler explicitly study noise-free examples, and Watkin and Rau assume a deterministic teacher. This is a Failure/Risk gap. Boundary-greedy querying concentrates samples exactly where labels are least reliable, because near the true boundary the correct label is inherently ambiguous, so a flip there is maximally misleading. The statistical-mechanics review already warns that noise yields unrealizable rules and nontrivial phase behavior, yet no source combines active example selection with noise. Practitioners thus inherit a silent hazard: active learners are assumed to always beat random sampling, but under label noise their defining property of proximity to the boundary can bias the version-space posterior and make them worse than passive learning. No provided work characterizes when active query learning breaks down under noise, nor gives a robust query objective. This matters for the reliability and reproducibility of any system trusting an active learner or a costly, noisy oracle.

**Method.** Develop a derivation-first theory of noisy query learning for the perceptron, fusing Fedorov-style optimal experimental design with the version-space Gibbs-ensemble statistical mechanics of Tishby-Levin-Solla, Watkin-Rau, and Opper-Haussler. Step 1, Model: a teacher B and a learner that selects the next input to maximize information about B, but with margin-dependent output noise p_flip(m), where m is the query distance to the true boundary and p_flip decreases from one half at the boundary to zero far away, formalizing that near-boundary queries are least reliable; setting p_flip to zero recovers existing noise-free results. Step 2, Formal object: compute the Fisher information, equivalently the expected version-space volume reduction, of one query at margin m under noise, and derive that noisy information is non-monotonic in m, vanishing both far away and at the boundary and maximized at an interior optimal margin m-star that depends on noise, obtained in closed form as a noise-corrected D-optimality condition maximizing (1 minus 2 p_flip(m)) squared times the marginal information at m. Step 3, Phase-transition theorem: extend the Watkin-Rau replica order-parameter calculation for the teacher-student overlap R to include p_flip, derive learning curves for boundary-greedy versus random sampling as functions of load alpha and noise, and prove a threshold theorem giving a critical noise eta_c(alpha) above which boundary-greedy querying yields asymptotically larger generalization error than passive random sampling, a negative-value-of-active-learning result, also determining whether the noise-free first-order freezing transition to perfect generalization survives, is delayed, or is destroyed. Step 4, Robust optimal design: derive the query distribution maximizing noise-discounted expected information gain, querying at posterior margin m-star rather than at the boundary, as an explicit corrected D-optimal objective, couple it to the Opper-Haussler Bayes decoder to define a robust Bayes-active learner, and prove it recovers the fast alpha-inverse rate in the noise-free limit yet never underperforms random sampling above eta_c. Step 5, Validation: verify m-star, the eta_c crossover, and the robust learner curves against finite-N perceptron simulations with label noise, as Watkin-Rau validated their noise-free predictions, confirming the theory predicts both the failure regime of naive active learning and the safe operating region of the robust design.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 19 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query-based example selection is the most efficient known way to train perceptrons but hides a reliability gap. Watkin and Rau 1992 gain about 42 percent efficiency by choosing each question to lie on the current decision boundary where the projection onto the estimate is minimized, and the 1991 example-and-query binary search likewise drives queries onto the boundary, while Fedorov optimal-experiment theory places designs where uncertainty is largest. The trouble is that the most informative query sits at the input whose true label margin is smallest, hence the label most likely corrupted by a noisy or adversarial oracle. Boundary querying amplifies label noise, and one wrong answer near the boundary can flip the separating plane or derail the search. These analyses assume a perfect oracle and report only noise-free efficiency; the statistical-mechanics learning curves, the Bayes-optimal perceptron analysis, and Bayesian version-space results give tools to quantify uncertainty, yet none asks when aggressive querying becomes less reliable than random sampling. This matters because the efficiency gain is harmful if it collapses under realistic noise, and no prior work characterizes or repairs this failure.

**Method.** Cast query selection as a noise-aware optimization in the statistical-mechanics and Bayesian framework, validated by simulation. Model an oracle whose label-flip probability grows as the true margin shrinks plus a baseline rate. Using the overlap order parameter and the replica margin distribution, derive for a query placed at signed distance m from the current boundary both its expected posterior information gain and its misinformation risk, which diverges as m approaches zero, and combine them into a robust acquisition score rewarding information and penalizing noise-induced posterior variance. Search over m for the optimal offset as a function of overlap and noise, so the learner queries at a controlled distance off the boundary rather than exactly on it, adapting the offset online, a noise-robust analogue of Fedorov iterative design. Split the label budget between new informative queries and repeated majority-voted queries at uncertain points, deriving the optimal replication count. Screen queries with a committee sampled from the version-space posterior, discarding labels the committee margin distribution deems inconsistent with the noise model. Finally compute replica learning curves comparing random, greedy boundary, and optimized querying for Ising and spherical perceptrons across noise levels, predicting a threshold beyond which greedy querying generalizes worse than random while the optimized scheme stays robust, confirmed with MinOver and Bayes-optimal Gibbs simulations measuring generalization error and its variance.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query-based active example selection for perceptrons is celebrated as the most efficient known training scheme: Watkin and Rau's replica analysis shows that continually selecting the next example on the current estimate's decision boundary pushes generalization about 40 percent closer to the theoretical bound than random sampling. Yet every such analysis assumes a perfectly realizable, noise-free teacher, hiding a reliability gap. The queried examples lie essentially on the boundary, which are exactly the examples whose labels are most easily corrupted by output noise, input noise, or teacher-student mismatch. The most informative query is thus the least trustworthy label, so aggressive query learning risks amplifying noise, destabilizing the estimate, and generalizing worse than random sampling. No source characterizes when this happens or how to prevent it: Opper and Haussler's Bayes-optimal perceptron analysis is explicitly noise-free; the statistical-mechanics theory of noisy and unrealizable learning (Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby; Gyorgyi-Tishby) treats only random passive examples; and Fedorov's optimal-experiment theory assumes homoscedastic Gaussian noise, not the heteroscedastic boundary-concentrated label-flip noise that active selection provokes. This matters for the safety and reproducibility of active learning in realistic deployment regimes.

**Method.** Relax the noise-free, realizable assumptions of active perceptron learning and derive a noise-robust query rule by extending the replica machinery. First, model a teacher B and student J with heteroscedastic label noise whose flip probability grows as an example's teacher-stability shrinks toward the boundary, plus variants for input noise and teacher-more-complex-than-student mismatch. Second, generalize Watkin and Rau's operator that averages the log-volume over the next selected pattern: rather than constraining the query to lie on the boundary, constrain it to a tunable margin m and carry the label-noise distribution through the replica average, yielding the overlap R(alpha) and generalization G(alpha) as explicit functions of noise level and margin m for Ising and spherical perceptrons. Third, optimize m to trade off information gain against label reliability, deriving the information-per-reliable-example-maximizing margin and its generalization curve, and embed this in a Bayes-optimal posterior/version-space framework rather than a single estimate, linking sample complexity to the information-theoretic and VC bounds of Haussler-Kearns-Schapire under noise. Fourth, predict analytically the noise threshold beyond which greedy boundary queries fall below random sampling, and show the robust-margin rule (optionally with repeated boundary querying to average out flips) restores an advantage. Fifth, validate with simulations of MinOver/optimal-stability and Bayes-Gibbs learners across output-noise rates and student/teacher capacity ratios, comparing random, greedy-active, and robust-margin-active selection. This makes prior active-learning theory operate under weaker, noisier, unrealizable conditions and converts the identified brittleness into a tunable robustness knob.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior works show a perceptron learns far more efficiently if it actively selects its own examples. Watkin & Rau and earlier example-selection work show that querying inputs on the current decision boundary, with an optimal-stability construction, gives a ~40% reduction in wasted examples toward the information-theoretic bound. Baum's query algorithm uses the same idea, with binary-search queries at the separating hyperplane. Fedorov's optimal-experiment theory and the Bayes-optimal classification analysis supply the maximize-information-per-query rationale. Failure/Risk gap: all these query strategies are derived and validated in the noise-free, realizable-teacher regime. Their core mechanism, concentrating queries on the boundary where the teacher field goes to zero, is exactly where a real teacher's label is least reliable: under label noise the flip probability peaks near the boundary, and under an unrealizable target the boundary is where student and teacher irreducibly disagree. So the informativeness that makes boundary queries powerful becomes a liability: aggressive selection amplifies noise, biases the weight estimate, and by pruning version space with corrupted constraints can trigger the empty-version-space collapse the statistical-mechanics-of-learning study shows dominates unrealizable rules. That study treats noisy and unrealizable rules only for passive random examples, and the Bayes-optimal analysis is explicitly noise-free, so no source characterizes when active query selection helps versus hurts under unreliable labels, nor offers a robust query rule. A noisy oracle is the realistic case, and a scheme optimal noise-free yet worse-than-random under mild noise is untrustworthy.

**Method.** Develop and analyze a risk-averse, noise-aware query-selection rule for perceptron learning and map when active learning is safe versus brittle. First, a noisy teacher model: extend the Watkin-Rau replica setup to a teacher whose label flips with probability that grows as the teacher field goes to zero (boundary noise), plus uniform-flip and unrealizable-target variants, making the reliability cost of boundary queries explicit. Second, a margin-offset query rule as the robustification: instead of the boundary query, place each query at a tunable offset margin from the current estimate; small offset gives high information but unreliable labels while larger offset gives labels the student can trust. Via the replica method derive the generalization curve and the student-teacher overlap as functions of the offset and noise level, then solve for the offset schedule that maximizes noise-discounted information gain, defined as information per query weighted by one minus the flip probability. The prediction is that offset going to zero recovers the noise-free speedup while the optimal offset grows with noise, trading information for reliability. Third, reliability weighting and redundancy: for queries near the boundary, allocate repeated oracle calls aggregated by majority vote or posterior averaging, and update via a Bayes-optimal posterior-mean or finite-temperature Gibbs rule rather than hard optimal-stability, softening corrupted constraints, using annealed and high-temperature approximations to set temperature and repetition budget. Fourth, a safety monitor: track version-space consistency such as freezing or negative-entropy onset, and when inconsistency signals noise or unrealizability, automatically raise the offset and relax the stability constraint to avert the discontinuous collapse noise-free algorithms ignore. Fifth, validation: produce a phase diagram in the sample-size versus noise plane delineating the active-learning-safe region where the robust rule beats random sampling, quantify the crossover noise at which naive boundary querying becomes worse than random, and confirm with finite-size simulations of the Ising and spherical perceptron with label noise.

#### [opportunity=Failure / Risk Gap; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query-based active learning is the most sample-efficient paradigm in this literature: Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") predict a ~42% reduction in wasted examples by continually selecting questions near the current decision boundary, and Baum ("Neural net algorithms that learn in polynomial time from examples and queries") shows membership queries turn intractable net-loading into provable polynomial-time PAC learning. But both rest on a fragile assumption: a realizable, noise-free target. The very mechanism that makes these methods efficient (driving queries to where the current hypothesis is most uncertain) is a failure/risk hazard: under label noise or model misspecification (the unrealizable-rules case flagged but explicitly NOT analyzed in the "Statistical mechanics of learning from examples" review), boundary-concentrated queries oversample exactly the inputs whose labels flip most, amplifying bias, producing overconfident brittle learners, hurting reproducibility across noise realizations, and possibly making active learning worse than random sampling. A parallel literature quantifies calibrated uncertainty but only for passive random examples: Opper & Haussler's Bayes-optimal perceptron ensemble and Levin/Tishby/Solla's consistent-inference Gibbs ensemble both yield a posterior with a well-defined prediction probability, and Fedorov's optimal-experimental-design theory gives a principled information-matrix criterion for where to sample. These streams have never been unified: active query methods lack calibrated risk control and a noise theory, while the Bayesian and optimal-design frameworks were never pushed through the active, nonlinear, noisy perceptron setting. This matters because active learning is deployed exactly when labels are scarce, expensive, noisy, and mismatched, precisely when existing guarantees silently fail.

**Method.** Build one statistical-mechanics framework, robust Bayesian active learning, unifying query selection (Watkin-Rau, Baum), the Bayes-optimal Gibbs posterior ensemble (Opper-Haussler; Levin et al.), and optimal experimental design (Fedorov), analyzed under label noise and unrealizable targets. Step 1: represent the learner as the Gibbs/Bayes posterior ensemble over weight space rather than a single weight vector, giving a calibrated prediction probability and a committee-disagreement measure for any candidate input. Step 2: reconcile the two notions of informative example by showing analytically that Watkin-Rau's geometric rule (query orthogonal to the current estimate) is the single-hypothesis limit of maximizing expected posterior information gain or committee disagreement, which is the threshold-unit generalization of Fedorov's D/G-optimal design applied to the ensemble information matrix, fusing three heuristics into one tunable objective. Step 3: inject noise and mismatch into the generative model (output-flip rate p; target drawn from a richer class than the student) and, using the replica method already used in the provided perceptron analyses, derive order parameters and joint learning curves for both generalization error and a calibration/overconfidence measure as functions of number of queries, p, mismatch, and a query-aggressiveness parameter beta controlling how tightly queries concentrate at the boundary (beta=0 recovers random sampling; beta to infinity recovers pure greedy querying). Step 4: locate the failure regime by finding the phase boundary in (beta, p, mismatch) space where greedy active learning's asymptotic error crosses above random sampling and the posterior becomes systematically overconfident, giving a quantitative diagnosis of the hidden brittleness. Step 5: prescribe a safe strategy, a temperature-controlled query distribution sampling points with weight proportional to exp(beta times disagreement), and solve the variational problem for the beta*(p, mismatch) that minimizes generalization error subject to a calibration constraint, a Bayesian noise-aware analogue of Fedorov's iterative D-optimal update. Step 6: validate on the sources' own testbeds, Ising and spherical perceptrons (Hebb and optimal-stability rules) and Baum-style membership-query nets, comparing random sampling, pure greedy active learning, and the temperature-controlled rule across noise levels, measuring sample efficiency, generalization, calibration, and run-to-run variance, confirming the predicted phase transition and recovery of the 42% gain only in the low-noise realizable corner. This closes the gap by giving active query learning the calibrated risk control of the Bayesian ensemble and the principled criterion of optimal experiments, and yields the first theory of when and by how much active learning becomes unreliable under noise and misspecification.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 12 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** A puzzle runs through these works. Actively choosing the most informative question looks like a clear win: Watkin and Rau call boundary example-selection the most efficient training algorithm yet devised (42% fewer wasted examples for the Ising perceptron; near-exponential speedups for spherical ones), echoing Fedorov's D-optimal design of querying the max-variance point, and Baum shows membership queries let nets escape NP-hardness limits. The shared prescription: query the maximally uncertain, near-boundary point. Yet the same statistical-mechanics literature undermines this. First, these strategies choose queries relative to a single point-estimate hypothesis J, but Opper and Haussler prove a single perceptron (Gibbs/Boltzmann) is generalization-suboptimal by about sqrt(2) versus the Bayes posterior average, which no single perceptron realizes, so we ask the most informative question about a biased boundary. Second, Seung, Sompolinsky and Tishby show metastable low-overlap phases and first-order transitions (the discontinuous jump at alpha_c=1.245 for discrete weights); greedy querying can entrench poor-learning states. Third, Watkin and Rau's own reinterpretation shows boundary points are exactly those most corrupted by label noise, so maximally informative is also maximally noise-amplifying, and Baum-style synthesized queries can be off-distribution. This contradiction has never been reconciled, no tool exists to map when aggressive querying helps or hurts, and no strategy queries with respect to the Bayes posterior rather than one hypothesis. It matters because active learning is deployed precisely in noisy, imperfectly realizable settings where clean-case guarantees may silently fail.

**Method.** Build an open, reproducible active-learning simulation platform for perceptron learning that instantiates every query strategy in these papers, introduces a posterior-based strategy, and serves as an experimental instrument to resolve the puzzle. Component one, a posterior-ensemble engine: maintain a committee of K weight vectors sampled by Monte Carlo/Langevin dynamics from the Gibbs posterior at inverse temperature beta over weights consistent with observed labels, on the sphere or hypercube. Prediction is the committee majority vote, a tunable realization of Opper and Haussler's n-hidden-unit majority approximation to Bayes (beta to infinity gives the realizable limit, finite beta the noisy regime, K=1 recovers Gibbs/Boltzmann). Component two, a pluggable query module with four strategies: random examples; single-hypothesis boundary querying (Watkin-Rau/Kinzel-Rujan, inputs orthogonal to current J); Baum-style synthesized boundary queries allowed off-distribution; and a new posterior/committee-disagreement query that, from a candidate pool, picks the input maximizing committee vote entropy, a computational realization of Fedorov's max-variance D-optimal point and Tishby-Levin-Solla one-step information gain taken over the full posterior instead of one hypothesis. Component three, a noise-and-mismatch module: controllable label-noise rate, target/hypothesis geometry mismatch (spherical vs Ising), varied alpha=P/N. Component four, on-line instrumentation: generalization error G(alpha), overlap R=J.B, committee entropy, wasted-example counts, and detectors for metastable trapping and first-order jumps. Validation and use: first certify the platform by reproducing the sources' analytic predictions (42% Ising reduction, sqrt(2) Bayes-vs-Gibbs gap, alpha_c=1.245 transition). Then sweep noise rate, alpha, beta, geometry, and K to produce a query-strategy phase diagram identifying where each strategy wins. Central hypothesis: posterior/committee querying dominates single-hypothesis boundary and random sampling under noise and near first-order transitions, because it queries the unbiased Bayes boundary rather than the biased Gibbs boundary, hedges across competing metastable basins, and implicitly down-weights noise-fragile isolated boundary points while staying on-distribution. This unifies disparate analytic results into a deployable tool and explains the paradox: greedy boundary querying is optimal only in the clean, realizable, single-basin regime, and posterior-referenced querying is its robust generalization. It is feasible with standard perceptron simulations (N up to thousands, committees of tens, cheap MC sampling) using the sources' closed-form benchmarks for correctness checks.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction] — claude-opus-4-8, 16 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle and contradiction in the statistical-mechanics theory of perceptron learning. Two established results conflict. First, active learning works best at the boundary: Watkin and Rau (1992), building on Kinzel-Rujan, show the most efficient strategy is to query the maximally ambiguous example, the input orthogonal to the current student weight vector J, giving about 42 percent fewer examples because each query is maximally informative about the teacher B. Second, the estimate defining that boundary is provably suboptimal: Opper and Haussler (1991) show a single point hypothesis, the Gibbs or Boltzmann rule, is about sqrt(2) worse than the Bayes-optimal predictor that averages over the whole version space. So the celebrated query rule is built around the boundary of a demonstrably suboptimal learner and ignores the posterior spread that Bayes-optimality and the Gibbs-ensemble formulation of Tishby, Levin and Solla (1989) say governs generalization. The contradiction sharpens under label noise: querying at the boundary deliberately picks the inputs whose labels are least reliable, so the rule that is optimal noise-free should become the worst as noise grows, since maximal informativeness and maximal label-unreliability coincide at the boundary. None of the provided works resolve this: the query papers assume noise-free realizable data and a point estimate; Opper-Haussler and Tishby et al. give the Bayes and Gibbs machinery but never use it to choose examples; the 1992 review does not treat active selection. This leaves open the genuinely optimal query when the learner is Bayes-optimal and labels are noisy, and predicts a transition from beneficial to harmful active learning.

**Method.** Develop a replica theory of Bayes-optimal active learning for the perceptron under label noise. Setup: a student learns a teacher perceptron B from examples whose outputs flip with rate eta. Following Tishby-Levin-Solla, represent beliefs as a Gibbs ensemble over weight space whose temperature is tied to eta, giving the noise-consistent version-space posterior of Opper-Haussler; the predictor is the Bayes-optimal posterior-averaged classifier, not a single J. New query criterion: instead of Watkin-Rau's rule of picking the input orthogonal to J, select the next input to maximize expected reduction in generalization error under the full posterior predictive, a noise-aware analogue of version-space bisection. An input's value is its committee disagreement, the closeness of the posterior predictive to one half, discounted by expected label reliability under eta. Parameterize this as a query margin, the distance from the current boundary at which examples are solicited, with zero margin reproducing the boundary rule. Analytical treatment: extend the Watkin-Rau self-consistent replica ansatz but track two order parameters, student-teacher overlap R and posterior spread q, with queries generated from the posterior predictive rather than a single J; derive coupled learning-curve equations for R and q in terms of loading alpha, noise eta and margin using Opper-Haussler version-space volume integrals. Predictions: noise-free, recover and improve on the 42 percent saving, compounding the sqrt(2) Bayes advantage; with noise, solve for a strictly positive optimal margin so the best query is offset from the boundary, quantifying the informativeness-reliability tradeoff; find a critical noise level beyond which boundary querying does worse than random sampling, a phase transition resolving the paradox; characterize how the Ising perceptron freezing transition shifts under noise. Validation: compare analytic learning curves with Bayesian Gibbs-sampling simulations, using MinOver and optimal-stability students as controls, across noise levels and margins, and extend toward hidden-layer machines flagged by Watkin-Rau.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** A paradox runs through this body of work. The active/query-learning literature declares that letting a learner pick its own examples is the most efficient training algorithm yet devised: Watkin & Rau (Selecting examples for perceptrons) predict a 42% cut in wasted examples for the Ising perceptron and more for optimal-stability spherical perceptrons; the earlier Improving a network's generalization ability by selecting examples, Baum's examples-and-queries algorithm, and the D-optimal experimental-design tradition (Fedorov) all agree: choose the most uncertain, near-boundary points and learning accelerates. Every one of these results assumes a realizable rule (teacher matches student) and noise-free labels. The statistical-mechanics results warn that these are exactly the dangerous assumptions. Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby show that for unrealizable rules and quenched disorder the clean approximations fail: learning curves turn non-monotonic and spin-glass phases with degenerate, barrier-separated minima appear. Opper & Haussler and Tishby-Levin-Solla recast learning as Bayes-optimal inference and one-step information gain, and Gardner's version-space analysis shows how each example shrinks the solution volume. The contradiction: query strategies deliberately select points of maximal label uncertainty (minimal stability, nearest the boundary) - precisely the points most likely to be mislabeled or unrealizable, and thus most able to inject misleading, disorder-amplifying constraints. So the always-beneficial premise may invert and querying may hurt, yet nobody has located the crossover. The evidence is also non-comparable across papers (Ising vs. spherical; Hebb, optimal stability, Gibbs, Bayes), giving conflicting speedups, so we cannot yet say when or why selecting examples helps versus harms.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map - an active-learning phase diagram - of when self-selected examples help, do nothing, or hurt, in the analytically solvable perceptron-in-the-thermodynamic-limit setting shared by these papers. The map is spanned by four axes drawn from the sources. (1) Selection strategy: random baseline; minimal-stability/maximal-uncertainty query (Watkin & Rau and the earlier selection paper); one-step maximal-information-gain/Bayes query, operationalizing Opper & Haussler's average one-step information gain, Tishby-Levin-Solla's entropy-based gain, and the perceptron analogue of Fedorov D-optimal design; and version-space bisection (Gardner volume halving). (2) Realizability: from a matched teacher through increasing architectural mismatch using the unrealizable-rule constructions of Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby. (3) Label noise: output-flip probability from 0 upward, exploiting the boundary-variable-behaves-like-Gaussian-noise observation in Selecting examples and Opper & Haussler's note that their methodology extends to noise. (4) Inference rule: Hebb, optimal stability (MinOver), Gibbs/Boltzmann at temperature T, and Bayes-optimal. For each grid cell compute the learning curve of generalization error as a function of alpha two ways for cross-validation: replica/order-parameter calculations where tractable (tracking teacher-student overlap R and the stability distribution of accepted examples) and large-N Monte Carlo elsewhere. Extract diagnostics: (i) query advantage A = e_g_random minus e_g_query, whose sign partitions the noise-by-mismatch plane into helps/neutral/harms regions with mapped transition lines per strategy and rule; (ii) stability/margin distribution and mislabeling fraction of the points each strategy actually queries, testing the hypothesis that harm arises when queries concentrate on corrupted near-boundary points; (iii) spin-glass/replica-symmetry-breaking and non-monotonicity signatures, testing whether querying itself induces the disorder-driven pathologies flagged by SST; and (iv) a myopia gap comparing greedy one-step information-gain querying against its realized long-run generalization, locating where the single-step Bayes/Fedorov-optimal choice is globally suboptimal. The deliverable is a comparative atlas plus a practical decision map: given noise level, degree of mismatch, example budget, and inference rule, it states which selection strategy (including plain random sampling) minimizes generalization error and explains the mechanism, converting fragmented, assumption-specific speedup claims into one calibrated picture of when selecting examples is a boon versus a trap.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Puzzle: the Gibbs-ensemble view (Tishby-Levin-Solla) says a labeled example can remove up to one bit of version-space entropy, promising large acceleration from example selection, yet every concrete optimum gives only constant-factor gains and keeps error near c/alpha. Opper-Haussler show Bayes-optimal inference on random data beats Gibbs by at most sqrt(2); Watkin-Rau and Kinzel selection cut only the prefactor (42 percent for Ising) while the spherical perceptron stays 1/alpha. No source unifies these: selection uses a point-estimate query rule with non-Bayes updates, and Bayes-optimal inference is studied only on random data; Fedorov optimal design targets regression variance, not discrete-label version spaces. Is the 1/alpha exponent a real barrier or an artifact of optimizing the wrong objective with the wrong inference?

**Method.** Build a statistical-mechanics theory of Bayes-optimal active learning for the perceptron. Using the Gibbs posterior, distinguish and prove two query criteria: a point-estimate rule maximizing uncertainty of one estimate versus an ensemble-optimal rule maximizing expected reduction of posterior entropy, the classification analogue of Fedorov D-optimality. Derive expected one-step information gain as the binary entropy of the posterior predictive probability, prove it is maximized when that probability equals one half so the query bisects the posterior by mass, and show this equals the point-estimate rule only for isotropic posteriors, which fail at finite alpha, explaining the constant-factor-only gains. Couple this rule with Bayes inference and derive, via the replica method used by Watkin-Rau, the self-consistent overlap and entropy flow and learning curve. Prove a one-bit-per-query bound and a separation theorem that ensemble-optimal plus Bayes beats random plus Bayes and point-estimate query plus optimal stability. Specialize to spherical and Ising perceptrons, predict whether the power-law exponent breaks, give a committee-vote estimator, and validate by simulation.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 20 turns / 30 tool calls

**Motivation.** Boundary querying (asking the perceptron's most uncertain input) is called the most efficient training rule, yet gains are puzzlingly small and inconsistent, and it ignores uncertainty orthogonal to the estimate, unlike Fedorov optimal design.

**Method.** Treat active learning as optimal experimental design: parametrize queries by margin and orthogonal exploration, use replica order-parameter dynamics to optimize the query schedule minimizing generalization error, benchmarking greedy boundary queries against a Fedorov D-optimal criterion.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Query/active-learning theory for perceptrons (Watkin-Rau 1992; Baum's examples-and-queries; Kinzel-Rujan selection) achieves large speedups by querying minimal-margin points on the current decision boundary. But Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby (1992) show unrealizable/noisy learning behaves qualitatively differently, and Opper-Haussler show Bayes-optimal uncertainty is largest at the boundary. Thus the strategy that is optimal in the noise-free realizable case targets exactly the least-reliable points under noise. It is unknown whether active selection still helps or backfires under noise, because query-learning results assume a perfect oracle while noisy/unrealizable analyses assume passive random sampling. The two never meet.

**Method.** Extend the replica statistical-mechanics framework of query selection to noisy, unrealizable, pool-based settings. Introduce a query-aggressiveness parameter kappa_q controlling the margin of queried points (kappa_q=0 = boundary-seeking Watkin-Rau/Baum; large kappa_q = passive random). Add margin-dependent label noise (flips concentrated near the boundary) and an unrealizable teacher. Use the Bayes-optimal posterior predictor (Opper-Haussler; Tishby-Levin-Solla) as student and query scorer. Compute generalization error versus alpha, kappa_q, and noise via replica theory for Ising and spherical perceptrons, predicting an information-vs-reliability tradeoff with an optimal margin kappa_q*(noise) and a crossover where aggressive boundary-seeking becomes worse than passive learning. Derive an adaptive online rule choosing the query margin that maximizes expected posterior-variance reduction under estimated noise, reducing to Watkin-Rau in the clean limit. Validate with simulations comparing passive, aggressive, fixed-margin, and adaptive strategies across noise levels and realizable/unrealizable teachers.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** A striking contradiction runs through the active-learning results in these works. When a perceptron is allowed to choose its own training inputs, it learns dramatically faster than from random examples: Watkin & Rau show a 42% reduction in "wasted" examples for the Ising perceptron and large gains for spherical perceptrons; Kinzel/Rujan's example-selection scheme and Baum's example+query algorithm show similar or exponential speed-ups. The mechanism is always the same: the most informative query lies exactly on (or orthogonal to) the learner's current decision boundary — the point of maximum ambiguity, minimum margin |J·S|≈0 — because its label maximally constrains the target direction B (Watkin & Rau's Figure-1 geometry; Fedorov's D-optimality intuition).

Here is the puzzle. Every one of these optimality claims assumes a noise-free oracle. But the statistical-mechanics literature on the same models tells us that low-margin inputs are precisely where labels are least trustworthy: in the realistic "output noise"/unrealizable regime (Seung–Sompolinsky–Tishby; the noise extension noted by Opper & Haussler), the probability that a teacher label is flipped grows as the true margin |B·S| shrinks toward zero. So the very examples active learning prioritizes as "most informative" are simultaneously the "least reliable." This suggests a surprising failure that none of the provided works analyzes: the selection rule that is provably optimal at zero noise may be maximally brittle under noise — concentrating the query budget where the label signal-to-noise is worst, corrupting the estimate J, and potentially yielding generalization worse than passive random sampling. Watkin & Rau, Kinzel/Rujan and Baum all assume a perfect oracle; Opper & Haussler compute the Bayes-optimal learner but only for noise-free random examples; the review treats noise only with passive sampling. Whether, and how, informative-example selection can be made trustworthy under label noise is therefore an open and important question, since real oracles (human experts, physical experiments) are noisiest exactly at the ambiguous boundary points active learning seeks out.

**Method.** We propose a noise-robust active example-selection theory for perceptron learning, analyzed with the replica method of Watkin & Rau and validated by simulation, that turns the brittle boundary-querying strategy into a reliable one and characterizes when active selection helps versus hurts.

(1) Noise model tied to margin. Teacher B produces label sign(B·S), flipped with probability f(|B·S|) that increases as the true margin shrinks toward the boundary (plus a uniform baseline rate). This encodes the source of the paradox — boundary queries are the noisiest — and connects to the unrealizable-rule setup of the review.

(2) Robustified selection rule with a tunable stability offset. Instead of querying at the current estimate's boundary (J·S=0), query at a controlled distance |J·S| = κ_q from it. κ_q interpolates between the existing "maximally informative" scheme (κ_q=0) and conservative, high-reliability queries (large κ_q, borrowing Gardner's optimal-stability geometry). We solve for the offset κ_q*(f, α) that maximizes expected information per reliable label.

(3) Noise-aware posterior and information criterion. Following Opper & Haussler's Bayes-optimal posterior and Tishby–Levin–Solla's consistent inference, we fold the flip model f into the version-space/Gibbs posterior so near-boundary evidence is automatically down-weighted. The query is then chosen to maximize expected posterior information gain under the noise model — a noise-discounted, Fedorov-style D-optimal criterion — rather than raw ambiguity. We also study a redundancy variant: spend queries repeating nearby ambiguous points and majority-vote to average out noise, quantifying the breadth-versus-redundancy trade-off.

(4) Analytic predictions and validation. Using replicas we compute the order parameters R(α) (overlap J·B) and the generalization error G(α) as functions of noise level f, offset κ_q, and query budget. We predict a crossover/phase transition: below a critical noise level naive boundary-querying still beats random sampling, but above it naive selection becomes worse than random, whereas the offset/noise-aware scheme stays monotonically better and degrades gracefully. Predictions are checked against Ising and spherical perceptron simulations, as in Watkin & Rau.

This directly resolves the gap: it identifies the regime where the "optimal" noise-free strategy fails, and supplies a concrete, feasible selection rule (margin offset + noise-aware posterior + optional redundancy) that restores robustness and trustworthiness of active learning under label noise, with quantitative guarantees on generalization.

#### [opportunity=Puzzle / Contradiction; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 15 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works prescribe the most informative next example in contradictory ways. Watkin and Rau (Selecting examples for perceptrons, 1992) prove that querying at the current decision boundary (margin near zero, maximum uncertainty) is the most efficient scheme yet devised, cutting wasted examples by 42 percent. Fedorov (Theory of Optimal Experiments; D and G optimality) prescribes the opposite: high-leverage points maximizing Fisher information about the weights, not low-signal boundary points. Opper and Haussler show Bayes-optimal passive learning beats Gibbs by a factor approaching sqrt(2) and tie generalization to one-step information gain; Tishby, Levin and Solla formalize information gain as ensemble-entropy reduction; Baum and the VC sample-complexity bounds show queries give exponential PAC speedups. Yet none states which query maximizes the expected drop in Bayes generalization error, nor whether uncertainty sampling is Bayes-optimal or a heuristic. The puzzle: two rigorous optimal-query theories point in opposite directions (boundary versus high-leverage), and the Bayes/information stream that should adjudicate has never been connected to either. This matters because active-learning gains hinge on the query criterion, so the wrong choice wastes the theoretical advantage, and evidence conflicts on whether uncertainty sampling is optimal.

**Method.** Build one statistical-mechanics theory of optimal query selection unifying uncertainty sampling (Watkin-Rau), Fedorov D-optimal design, and Bayes/information-theoretic learning (Opper-Haussler; Tishby-Levin-Solla). Use the realizable teacher-student perceptron of Opper-Haussler; represent the learner as the Gibbs posterior and Gardner version-space measure, summarized by order parameters R and q via the replica method of Watkin-Rau. Following Tishby-Levin-Solla, define a query-value functional equal to expected ensemble-entropy reduction, the mutual information between the candidate label and the weights under the current posterior, expressed as a function of only the query projected-margin distribution, from which both the boundary criterion and the Fisher-information D-optimal criterion can be read off. Maximize it to get the Bayes-optimal query and show it reduces to Fedorov D-optimality in the diffuse low-alpha regime and to Watkin-Rau margin-zero uncertainty sampling in the concentrated high-alpha regime, predicting a crossover in alpha and locating where uncertainty sampling is suboptimal, thus dissolving the contradiction as two limits of one criterion. Compute the resulting generalization-decay exponent and place the Opper-Haussler sqrt(2) factor, the Watkin-Rau gain, and the Baum PAC speedup on one phase diagram, and combine Bayes-optimal estimation with Bayes-optimal querying to test whether gains multiply. Validate by simulating online query learning on spherical and Ising perceptrons, comparing D-optimal, uncertainty, and unified-optimal rules against random sampling, and probe robustness under label noise.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 22 tool calls

**Motivation.** Resource bottleneck: the dominant cost in training a neural classifier to good generalization is label acquisition, since each label must come from an expensive oracle such as a human expert, a wet-lab measurement, or a physical experiment. The prior works show this bottleneck is severe yet avoidable. Watkin and Rau predict a 42 percent reduction in examples wasted when a perceptron continually selects its own examples. Baum shows oracle queries cut example counts by large factors and stresses queries are available in practice via a human expert. Opper and Haussler quantify the Bayes-optimal generalization curve bounding how much information a given number of labels yields. Levin, Tishby and Solla give an entropy and information-gain measure where labels are informative insofar as they shrink the accessible version-space volume. Fedorov gives D-optimal iterative algorithms for choosing maximally informative measurements. These results promise savings but are fragmented and idealized: each is tied to a specific model class, assumes a known input distribution and the thermodynamic limit, and none is embodied in a system a practitioner can run against a real costly oracle. None provides a budget-aware policy deciding which labels to buy and when the marginal value of another label no longer justifies its cost. Baum also flags that synthesized membership queries are often unanswerable for a human oracle. So the theory says savings are possible, but the bottleneck persists for lack of a deployable system turning these information-maximizing principles into a usable, cost-driven labeling workflow.

**Method.** Build an active-labeling training system, a query engine, that turns the fragmented selection theory into a deployable budget-driven workflow, where the system is the central contribution. It has five parts. First, a version-space posterior module maintaining a committee of networks Gibbs-sampled from weight configurations consistent with the labels seen so far, using the accessible-volume formalism of Levin, Tishby and Solla and approximating the Bayes-optimal posterior of Opper and Haussler; the committee estimates remaining uncertainty. Second, a value-of-information estimator that scores each candidate input by expected reduction in posterior entropy via committee disagreement, combined with a Fedorov D-optimality criterion and a statistical-mechanics learning-curve prediction of generalization gain per label, then divides by labeling cost. Third, a query source that is pool-based by default, ranking a large unlabeled pool rather than synthesizing inputs, with optional constrained near-boundary synthesis, which addresses Baum's concern that queries must be realistic and answerable. Fourth, a budget-aware controller that, given a fixed budget and per-label costs, allocates queries greedily by value-per-cost and uses the fitted learning curve to halt when predicted marginal error reduction per unit cost drops below a threshold. Fifth, an instrumentation harness logging labels-to-accuracy curves against random sampling and against theoretical predictions. Evaluation deploys the system against simulated expensive oracles such as teacher perceptrons and small layered nets, and a human-in-the-loop oracle, measuring realized label savings and checking whether achieved reductions reproduce predicted factors like the 42 percent figure and the Bayes-optimal curve. The artifact unifies version-space entropy, Bayes-optimality, and optimal experimental design into one cost-aware pipeline a practitioner can point at any costly oracle to cut the label-acquisition bottleneck, with quantitative guidance on when to stop paying for labels.
