#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck] — claude-opus-4-8, 7 turns / 13 tool calls

**Motivation.** Across these works the binding constraint on learning is a resource bottleneck: labeled examples (equivalently, oracle/expert queries or physical experiments) are expensive, so the central question is how to reach a target generalization error with the fewest labels. The statistical-mechanics learning curves (Seung/Sompolinsky/Tishby review; Opper & Haussler's exact Bayes-optimal G(α); Gardner-style version-space volumes; Tishby/Levin/Solla predictive inference) all describe how error falls with the ratio α = examples/weights, and show that even the *optimal* passive learner needs α of order 1 — i.e., O(N) labels — with a slow tail. Two lines attack the bottleneck by choosing examples: Watkin & Rau ("Selecting examples for perceptrons") and the Kinzel–Rujan-type rule select the next example on the decision boundary of the *single current best estimate* J(p), predicting a ~42% saving; Baum's query algorithm constructs synthetic membership queries by bisection. But each leaves a resource gap. (i) Boundary selection uses only a point estimate, discarding the posterior uncertainty that Opper & Haussler show is what the Bayes-optimal learner actually exploits, so it under-uses each costly label. (ii) Both methods are strictly sequential: one query, then full retraining, then the next — so the number of oracle-interaction rounds and retraining cycles equals the number of labels, which is the true cost when the oracle is a slow expert, a wet-lab assay, or an expensive simulation. (iii) Baum's constructed queries can be unnatural/unanswerable inputs (the membership-query pathology), and no work connects the classical optimal-experimental-design machinery (Fedorov: iterative D-/G-optimal designs that add the point of maximum predictive variance and provably converge) to version-space classification learning. There is therefore room for a query-selection theory that squeezes maximum information from every label *and* collapses the number of interaction/retraining rounds, using only realistically available inputs.

**Method.** Develop and analyze "Bayes-optimal batch experimental design" for perceptron learning, unifying Opper–Haussler's posterior-over-version-space view with Fedorov's optimal-design algorithm, and evaluate it with the replica/statistical-mechanics tools of Watkin–Rau and the SSC review. Steps: (1) Maintain the Bayes posterior (Gibbs ensemble on the version space consistent with the labels so far), represented cheaply by a small committee of weight vectors — reusing Opper & Haussler's result that an n-hidden-unit majority committee approaches Bayes-optimal as n grows — so no exact posterior computation is needed. (2) Define a per-candidate information utility for inputs drawn from a *finite realistic pool* (not synthetic points): the posterior label ambiguity, i.e., the committee-vote disagreement / posterior label variance for x. This is the classification analogue of Fedorov's maximum-predictive-variance point and strictly generalizes Watkin–Rau's boundary rule (which is the degenerate one-member-committee case), thereby using each expensive label to bisect the whole version-space volume rather than only the neighborhood of one estimate. (3) Turn single-point selection into *batch* selection to cut interaction/retraining rounds: adapt Fedorov's iterative design update, greedily adding to the batch the pool point of highest residual ambiguity while penalizing redundancy between points whose version-space cuts are nearly parallel (a diversity/D-optimality term on the induced information geometry), so a batch of k points jointly slices the posterior into near-equal informative pieces. Label the whole batch, retrain once, repeat. (4) Derive predicted learning curves G(α) and, crucially, the number of batch rounds to reach target error, using replica theory as in Watkin–Rau for both spherical and Ising perceptrons, and compare against three baselines: random (passive Bayes-optimal), single-estimate boundary selection, and Baum-style membership queries. (5) Validate with simulations, measuring the two resource axes explicitly — total labels and total oracle-interaction/retraining rounds — and characterize how batch size k trades a small label overhead for a large reduction in rounds. The approach addresses the bottleneck by (a) extracting more information per costly label via the full posterior, (b) using only answerable pooled inputs, and (c) amortizing the sequential interaction and retraining cost through principled batching, yielding a feasible, analyzable active-learning scheme grounded entirely in the provided works.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 21 tool calls

**Motivation.** The practical bottleneck across these works is the cost of labeled examples and oracle queries: labeling requires expensive experts or physical experiments, and most new random questions give no new information, so examples are wasted (Selecting examples for perceptrons; Neural net algorithms that learn from examples and queries). Three separate traditions each promise to cut this cost: statistical-mechanics active example selection (a predicted 42% reduction in wasted examples for the Ising perceptron), classical optimal experimental design (D-/G-optimal iterative designs in Theory of Optimal Experiments), and query/halving strategies for multilayer nets. The sample-complexity literature (VC/information-theory bounds; Bayes-optimal generalization; Statistical mechanics of learning from examples) supplies the learning-curve yardsticks. The gap: every savings claim is derived inside one idealized model under convenient assumptions — the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity), replica-symmetric ansatze, a realizable teacher-student match, and a noise-free oracle. The stat-mech review itself warns these approximations fail in general for unrealizable rules and finite systems, and the Bayes-optimal result is explicitly noise-free. No one has measured how much predicted query savings survive at finite N, under noisy labels, under teacher-student mismatch, or across learning rules, nor how much extra compute the selection oracle itself costs. A practitioner with a fixed labeling budget therefore cannot tell whether active selection's operational and computational overhead buys real savings in their finite, noisy, misspecified setting, or backfires by over-querying ambiguous points. The listed works leave this comparative, condition-resolved measurement open.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical map answering when active example selection actually pays, via a controlled factorial simulation study on a common learning-curve yardstick that unifies the three selection traditions. Factorial axes: (1) Model: spherical perceptron, Ising perceptron, and a small committee/two-layer net. (2) Learner: Hebb, optimal-stability (MinOver), stochastic Gibbs/Boltzmann, and Bayes-optimal. (3) Selection strategy: random baseline, maximum-uncertainty selection near the decision hyperplane (Selecting examples), D-/G-optimal iterative design (Fedorov), and query-by-halving (poly-time query paper). (4) Stressors, the core novelty: finite size N with finite-size scaling toward the N-to-infinity theory, oracle label noise, teacher-student mismatch/unrealizability, and input-distribution shift. Measurements: per cell, estimate the empirical learning curve G(alpha) and a sample-savings ratio (examples needed by random selection divided by examples needed by the strategy to reach a target generalization), averaged over many teachers and training sets. Overlay the analytic asymptotics (the 42%/41% Ising savings, spherical exponents, Bayes-optimal sqrt(2) factor, VC/information bounds) to quantify a prediction gap equal to theoretical minus measured savings as a function of N and each stressor. Add a cost-adjusted savings metric that debits the compute of running the selection oracle, reporting savings per unit of total (query plus compute) budget. Deliverables: (a) an empirical phase map over the stressor axes marking regimes where selection helps, is neutral, or hurts, for example predicting that noise makes uncertainty sampling over-concentrate on ambiguous inputs and collapse savings; (b) finite-size scaling curves showing how fast measured savings approach the thermodynamic-limit theory; (c) a compact budget-aware decision rule telling practitioners which strategy is worth its overhead given their N, noise level, and suspected misspecification. The study is fully feasible from simulation alone, uses only models and estimators already defined in the sources, and turns three isolated savings claims into one validated, actionable cost map.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 8 turns / 17 tool calls

**Motivation.** In perceptron / feed-forward network learning, the binding resource is not compute but *labeled examples*: each label can require a costly oracle — a human expert producing an answer for a synthesized input (Baum 1991, "queries by appeal to a human expert"). Random sampling squanders this budget, because once a rule is partly learned "most new questions give no new information," and near-perfect generalization needs many redundant examples (Watkin & Rau report a large fraction of examples "wasted"). Active example selection promises far fewer oracle calls, but the theory that would let us minimize this cost is incomplete in three ways. (1) The statistical-mechanics active-learning result (Watkin & Rau 1992) analyzes only a specific *greedy* heuristic — place the next query on the current decision boundary, orthogonal to the estimate J — implicitly assuming an isotropic version space, and it proves no optimality nor any budget guarantee. (2) It assumes free synthesis of arbitrary boundary inputs (membership queries), which is exactly the expensive/infeasible operation in practice, and says nothing about the realistic pool-restricted or noisy-label regimes. (3) The classical, mature theory of optimal experimental design (Fedorov 1972: D-/G-optimality through the information matrix), which was built precisely to spend experiments frugally, is never connected to the Bayesian version-space / information-gain machinery available here (Opper–Haussler 1991 version-space subvolume relations; Tishby–Levin–Solla 1989 ensemble entropy and "information gain"). As a result there is no formal, resource-aware answer to the practically central question: what is the minimum number of oracle queries needed to reach a target generalization error, which query achieves it, and how much is lost when inputs cannot be freely synthesized?

**Method.** We develop a formal, resource-aware theory of query-efficient perceptron learning that unifies optimal experimental design with the statistical-mechanics/version-space picture, and derive the queries and bounds it implies.

1. Objective. Cast learning as Bayesian inference with posterior equal to the normalized measure on version space (Opper–Haussler), with ensemble entropy S(m)=log V(m) (Tishby et al.). Define the query-selection objective as the *expected reduction in ensemble entropy* (expected one-step information gain) of labeling input x. Using Opper–Haussler's subvolume relation V(σ^{m+1}) = min-subvolume of V(σ^m), prove that the optimal single query bisects the version space (V(σ^{m+1})=½V(σ^m)), extracting exactly 1 bit — the maximum possible per label.

2. Equivalence theorem. Under a saddle-point/replica Gaussian approximation of the posterior on weight space, derive that maximizing expected entropy reduction is equivalent to Fedorov's D-optimality: maximizing the determinant of the posterior precision (Fisher-information) matrix. This is the core formal contribution — a bridge showing statistical-mechanics active learning IS optimal experimental design in the appropriate limit.

3. Anisotropy correction (new prediction). From the equivalence, derive that the truly information-optimal query is NOT Watkin–Rau's boundary-orthogonal input but one *tilted toward the principal eigenvector of maximal posterior variance*. Give a closed form in terms of the order parameters (overlap R and the within-version-space covariance of J); recover Watkin–Rau as the isotropic special case.

4. Query-complexity bound. Because each optimal query yields ≤1 bit and target error ε maps to version-space volume through the known generalization relation G(R) (Gardner/Watkin), derive a lower bound m*(ε) on required queries and the fastest achievable ε(m) decay, contrasting it with random-sampling scaling.

5. Resource-aware extensions and taxonomy. Extend the derivation to (a) the pool-based setting where inputs are drawn from a distribution and cannot be synthesized on the bisecting hyperplane — derive achievable per-query information as a function of pool density near that hyperplane, giving an explicit degraded bound; and (b) noisy labels, via a modified subvolume-splitting factor. This yields a conceptual taxonomy of three regimes — membership-query-optimal, pool-optimal, noisy-optimal — each with its own query-complexity bound.

6. Validation. Compute replica learning curves and run simulations on Ising and spherical perceptrons comparing random sampling, greedy Watkin–Rau selection, and the anisotropy-corrected optimal design, checking that the corrected design saturates the derived bounds and outperforms greedy selection.

This directly targets the labeled-query bottleneck by prescribing provably information-maximal queries and quantifying, for the first time in this setting, the minimum oracle budget and the penalty imposed by realistic (pool/noisy) constraints.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Labeled queries are the scarce resource in perceptron learning; existing statistical-mechanics learning curves assume unlimited random examples and greedy boundary-query heuristics are not budget-optimal, noise-aware, or pool-constrained.

**Method.** Optimize the active-learning query distribution as an experimental-design measure using replica learning-curve predictions to allocate a fixed labeling budget minimizing generalization error.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 9 turns / 19 tool calls

**Motivation.** Labeled examples are the scarce, expensive resource in learning a rule. The active-selection results here promise large label savings: Watkin and Rau (1992) predict up to 42% fewer wasted examples by requesting the label of an input on the current decision boundary (perpendicular to estimate J), and Baum (1991) learns in polynomial time by querying constructed points such as midpoints and points on separating hyperplanes. But these gains rest on two idealizations that break in real deployment. First, free synthesis or unlimited filtering of inputs: the boundary-aligned-example rule assumes one can construct arbitrary inputs or reject an infinite stream until a boundary example appears, whereas in practice one has only a finite pool of naturally distributed unlabeled inputs and cannot synthesize arbitrary vectors. Second, noise-free labels. The complementary statistical-mechanics work that handles noise, Opper and Haussler's Bayes-optimal perceptron and Seung, Sompolinsky and Tishby's review, treats only passive random sampling. No listed work characterizes how much of the active-learning label savings survives under a finite pool and noisy labels. This matters because of a hidden tension: near-boundary inputs are the most informative but, under output noise, the least reliable, so naive boundary-selection may amplify noise and waste the very labels it aims to save.

**Method.** Relax the two idealizations of the active-selection theory and re-derive both the learning curve and the optimal selection rule for a finite pool with noisy labels, unifying Watkin-Rau selection analysis with Opper-Haussler Bayes-optimal and noise framework. Setting: a teacher perceptron B; a finite pool of p_pool = beta*N unlabeled inputs from the input distribution; a label oracle returning sign(B dot S) flipped with probability lambda; the learner may pay for only m = alpha*N labels with alpha at most beta, the scarce resource, and estimates J via the Gibbs or Bayes-optimal posterior. Selection rule: replace pick the input perpendicular to J (which needs infinite supply and is noise-blind) with, from the current pool, request the label maximizing expected posterior information gain about B under the noise model. For a perceptron this reduces to a noise-aware margin band: prefer small absolute J dot S but discount points so close to the boundary that their noisy label is near a coin flip, giving an optimal nonzero target margin that grows with lambda. It is deployable without knowing B: estimate margins from J and estimate lambda from label disagreement on repeated queries. Analysis: extend the Watkin-Rau replica calculation along two axes, first finite pool size beta, adding an order parameter tracking depletion of near-boundary inputs as they are consumed, and second noise lambda, giving an effective margin threshold. Compute the generalization curve G(alpha; beta, lambda) and its asymptotics and produce a phase diagram of regimes where pool-based selection still beats random, where finite beta erases the gain, and where noise inverts the optimum so that selecting at a nonzero margin is best and naive boundary-selection is worse than random. Derive the optimal label budget alpha given beta and lambda. Validation: mirror Watkin-Rau by checking replica predictions against simulations of pool-based spherical and Ising perceptron learning with controlled label-flip noise and finite pools, benchmarked against the Bayes-optimal passive baseline. This maximizes generalization gained per expensive label under realistic finite-pool, noisy conditions that prior active-learning results assumed away.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 27 tool calls

**Motivation.** The binding constraint in these works is the cost of acquiring labeled examples. The poly-time examples-and-queries paper motivates active learning because each label may need an expensive oracle. Watkin-Rau shows querying informative points cuts examples wasted by about 42 percent versus random sampling, and Fedorov optimal-experiment theory does the same classically. This label cost is the resource bottleneck making active learning attractive. But every efficiency result assumes a noise-free realizable oracle, and efficient strategies place queries on the current estimated decision boundary, exactly where the true label is most ambiguous and a noisy or mismatched oracle is most likely wrong. The Bayes-optimal perceptron paper is explicitly noise-free, and the statistical-mechanics review shows that with unrealizable or noisy rules learning develops spin-glass phases, non-monotonic curves, and breakdown of standard approximations. So boundary-hugging active learning is brittle: a few mislabeled boundary queries lock in errors and destabilize the estimate, so practitioners distrust it and revert to expensive random sampling. No work here gives an active query strategy robust to oracle noise and mismatch, nor a theory of how much informativeness must be traded for reliability.

**Method.** Build a robustified active example-selection scheme for perceptrons trading per-query information for immunity to label noise and target mismatch, with a noise-optimal operating point derived by statistical mechanics. The robust query rule replaces the margin-zero boundary query with a tunable safety-margin query, drawing the next question at a controllable overlap offset kappa from the current estimate J, generalizing the Watkin-Rau near-zero-overlap constraint to a target overlap kappa. Larger kappa gives larger true margin, so labels are less likely corrupted while still more informative than random examples. Repeated noisy queries near a location are aggregated and denoised using Bayes-optimal posterior version-space estimation from the Opper-Haussler framework rather than trusting one boundary label. The theory extends the Watkin-Rau replica computation of order parameters R and generalization G to include an oracle noise rate p and an unrealizable target component, the regimes the statistical-mechanics review flags as failure-prone, giving G as a function of alpha, kappa, and p plus expected one-step information gain per query. We solve for the robust-optimal margin kappa-star maximizing noise-averaged and worst-case information gain per label subject to a stability constraint against catastrophic misalignment, a Bayesian robust version of Fedorov D-optimal design where the information matrix is regularized by noise-induced label variance. For validation we predict a graceful-degradation frontier where kappa-star grows with p, interpolating between greedy boundary querying and near-random sampling, and loss of the discontinuous sudden-perfect-generalization transition under noise. We simulate Ising and spherical perceptrons with noisy and mismatched oracles, comparing greedy margin-zero active learning, random sampling, and the robust scheme on labels-to-accuracy and failure rate. The payoff is retaining most label savings while eliminating catastrophic failures, making active learning trustworthy enough to genuinely reduce labeling cost under noise.

#### [opportunity=Resource Bottleneck; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 19 turns / 33 tool calls

**Motivation.** Acquiring labeled examples is the real cost of learning in these works. Baum's query paradigm shows each label may need an oracle or expert, and statistical-mechanics results show random sampling wastes many examples since most carry no new information. Cutting labels is the bottleneck, but knowledge is split across four disconnected literatures: optimal experimental design (Fedorov D-/G-optimality, iterative maximum-variance-point updates) for linear regression only; SM learning-curve theory predicting exact payoffs (Watkin-Rau 42 percent; Bayes-optimal sqrt2) but via hand-crafted perceptron heuristics; the Bayesian ensemble entropy/information-gain framework (Tishby-Levin-Solla) lacking a concrete rule or savings prediction; and query learning (Baum) with worst-case bounds detached from generalization curves. No single cheap, analyzable rule tells which example to label next, generalizes beyond linear models, and predicts savings.

**Method.** Unify the four streams into one information-optimal example-selection framework. Represent the learner as the Gibbs posterior ensemble of Tishby-Levin-Solla. Define the criterion: select the query maximizing expected ensemble-entropy reduction (information gain), and prove this generalizes Fedorov D-optimal design (in the linear-Gaussian limit it maximizes the Fisher-information determinant, recovering the maximum-variance-point rule) while for classification it becomes maximum committee disagreement, recovering Watkin-Rau near-boundary selection and Baum boundary queries. Derive learning curves via replica calculations with the example distribution set to this self-consistent query distribution, quantifying labels saved and the gap to the Bayes-optimal sqrt2 bound. Implement a practical algorithm: sample a committee from the Gibbs ensemble by MCMC, score candidates by disagreement (Monte-Carlo info-gain), label the most informative, iterate, needing no NP-hard search and applicable to layered nets. Validate against replica theory and simulations on Ising and spherical perceptrons, and on a small multilayer net show reduced oracle queries for target accuracy.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Artifact / System] — claude-opus-4-8, 17 turns / 24 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior active-learning theory (Watkin-Rau 42 percent gain, Baum queries, Fedorov D-optimal, Opper-Haussler Bayes optimal, stat-mech learning curves) assumes N to infinity, noise-free realizable single-layer perceptrons, and arbitrary oracle queries; real settings are finite-N, noisy, unrealizable, and pool-based, so the promised gains are unverified and the theory is not a usable tool. This scope mismatch matters because active/query learning is exactly where sample efficiency is decisive.

**Method.** Build a modular active-example-selection platform implementing theory-derived acquisition criteria (Watkin-Rau margin, committee-disagreement Bayes-optimal voting, Fedorov D-optimal), a finite-temperature Gibbs soft version space for noise robustness, a pool-based query mode with manifold-projected synthesized queries, and a finite-N calibration and benchmark harness comparing empirical learning curves to asymptotic theory across perceptrons and small multilayer nets.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch] — claude-opus-4-8, 24 turns / 34 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope-mismatch gap. Prior active and query-learning theory for perceptrons derives its efficiency from unrealistic boundary conditions. Watkin and Rau (Selecting examples for perceptrons) obtain a 42 percent example reduction and a shifted discontinuous transition to perfect generalization by constructing each next example to lie exactly on the current student decision hyperplane, and Baum uses membership queries that fabricate input points and receive noise-free oracle labels. Both assume a realizable, noise-free, matched teacher in the large-N limit with inputs synthesizable anywhere in feature space. These assumptions are narrow: real inputs come from a fixed natural distribution and can only be selected, not manufactured; synthetic boundary queries fall off the data manifold and cannot be labeled by real oracles; labels are noisy; teachers are often unrealizable. Seung, Sompolinsky and Tishby show unrealizable rules under passive random sampling behave very differently, with non-monotonic curves and spin-glass phases, yet no one has analyzed selective example choice once the idealized query model is dropped. So it is unknown whether the promised savings survive, or reverse due to selection bias toward ambiguous noisy points, in the regime that actually occurs: pool or stream selection from a natural distribution with noise and mismatch.

**Method.** Build a statistical-mechanics replica and order-parameter-flow theory of stream-based selective sampling for perceptrons under the natural-distribution constraint. Examples arrive i.i.d. from the true input distribution; a Gibbs student at temperature T, or a Bayes posterior over version space following Opper-Haussler and Tishby-Levin-Solla, decides from the input alone whether to spend a label, accepting when the margin is below a threshold for uncertainty sampling, or when two Gibbs students disagree for a pool-compatible query-by-committee. Unlike Watkin-Rau, inputs cannot be forced onto the boundary; we only filter the isotropic stream. Extend the Watkin-Rau and Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby replica machinery by treating accepted examples as a selection-biased effective distribution concentrated near the current boundary, deriving coupled equations for student-teacher overlap and stability along two axes, examples seen and labels spent, giving learning curves in labels-per-generalization plus an acceptance rate. Solve three regimes: a realizable noise-free baseline to reproduce then relax the 42 percent result, label noise, and an unrealizable teacher, testing whether the freezing and discontinuous transition and the savings persist, degrade, or invert. Optimize the acceptance-threshold schedule via the information-gain and D-optimality logic of Fedorov optimal-experiment theory adapted to a filterable rather than constructible design space, and compare achievable label scaling to passive sampling and the idealized membership-query bound. Validate with finite-N MinOver and Gibbs simulations on natural streams.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Empirical Mapping] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** The provided works build a powerful theory of learning curves and of smart example acquisition. Watkin & Rau (Selecting examples for perceptrons) predict a ~42% reduction in examples by having a perceptron continually select questions near its current decision boundary; Baum's query-learning algorithm proves polynomial-time PAC learnability using membership queries; Fedorov's optimal-experiment (D-optimal) design underpins maximizing information per query; and the Bayes-optimal / statistical-mechanics analyses (Opper-Haussler, Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby, Tishby-Levin-Solla) give exact generalization curves and asymptotic power laws.

The problem is a Scope Mismatch: nearly every quantitative promise is derived under a tightly bundled set of idealizations - the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity), replica symmetry, a REALIZABLE and NOISE-FREE teacher whose architecture matches the student, i.i.d. spherical/uniform inputs, and (for Bayesian results) a matched prior. Real problems violate all of these: finite networks, model mismatch, label noise, structured/anisotropic/clustered inputs, mismatched priors. Crucially, Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby already show that once rules become unrealizable the high-temperature and annealed approximations fail, generalization error stops being monotonic, and spin-glass phases appear - yet the active-selection results (42% savings, query speedups) were never re-examined in these broken regimes. There is a real danger that boundary-focused selection actively hurts once idealizations fail: sampling near an unstable or noise-corrupted boundary can amplify label noise and chase mismatch artifacts. No systematic characterization exists of WHERE the theoretical learning curves and, especially, the active-learning advantage survive, degrade, or invert as each idealization is relaxed. This matters because practitioners inherit optimistic idealized conclusions without knowing their domain of validity.

**Method.** Build a systematic empirical regime atlas measuring how (a) the theoretically predicted learning curves and (b) the advantage of active example selection over random sampling behave as each idealizing assumption of the prior theory is relaxed, one axis at a time and in combination.

1. Controlled testbed. Reuse the teacher-student perceptron setup common to all sources so the idealized theory gives exact reference predictions. Simulate at finite N and sweep to large N to probe the thermodynamic-limit assumption.

2. Factorial grid of assumption violations (map axes): Realizability (teacher=student vs. capacity/architecture mismatch, e.g. multilayer teacher vs. perceptron student, the unrealizable rules flagged by Seung et al.); Label noise (clean vs. graded output-flip and input noise); Input structure (i.i.d. spherical/uniform vs. anisotropic/correlated Gaussians, clustered, low-dimensional-manifold inputs); Prior/model mismatch for the Bayes-optimal algorithm; Finite size N.

3. Learners and acquisition strategies in every cell: random sampling (baseline), Watkin-Rau/query-style boundary (uncertainty/margin) selection, Fedorov-style D-optimal/maximum-information selection, and Bayes-optimal selection. Use corpus constructions (Hebb, optimal-stability/MinOver) and small multilayer nets for direct comparability.

4. Measurements/diagnostics: empirical generalization curves e_g(alpha) with confidence bands; an example-savings ratio (the empirical analogue of the 42% figure) mapped as a heatmap over the grid, flagging cells where savings vanish or go NEGATIVE (active worse than random); a theory-fidelity gap quantifying deviation of measured curves from high-temperature, annealed, and replica predictions as a function of distance from the idealized point; structural signatures (non-monotonicity, learning-transition sharpness, effective ruggedness/degeneracy as a spin-glass proxy) correlated with where active selection fails.

5. Output. A quantitative diagnostic map/benchmark that (i) delineates the region of assumption space where classic learning-curve predictions and the active-learning advantage hold, (ii) identifies the specific violations that break them (noise+boundary selection; unrealizability; anisotropic inputs), and (iii) yields empirically grounded decision rules and cheap computable indicators (e.g., estimated boundary noise level, realizability gap) for when to trust active selection versus revert to random sampling. This converts the sources' narrow idealized guarantees into an empirically charted map of their real operating envelope.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Formal Derivation] — claude-opus-4-8, 11 turns / 20 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior statistical-mechanics theory of query/example selection for perceptrons (Watkin and Rau 1992; Kinzel and Rujan; Baum; Fedorov 1972) shows that querying examples on the student's current decision boundary minimizes version-space volume and cuts the examples needed to learn by up to about 40 percent. But this is derived only under a realizable teacher, noise-free labels, and the thermodynamic limit. The review Statistical mechanics of learning from examples (1992) shows these assumptions are fragile: under label noise or architecture mismatch (unrealizable rules), generalization becomes non-monotonic, overtraining appears, and spin-glass phases with degenerate frozen minima arise. The Bayes-optimal analysis (Opper and Haussler 1991) is likewise restricted to noise-free examples. This is a scope mismatch: boundary examples are exactly the inputs whose labels are most likely flipped by noise or misclassified under mismatch, so the strategy proven efficient in the idealized regime may be actively harmful, spending the query budget on the least informative examples and biasing the frozen student. No work characterizes when selection helps versus hurts under noise and unrealizability, nor what the optimal query distribution is there.

**Method.** A formal-derivation contribution extending the replica/annealed statistical-mechanics formalism of example selection to noisy and unrealizable teacher-student perceptrons. (1) Model: teacher-student perceptron with output-noise level eta (label flip probability, generalizing the paper's own U-as-noise order parameter) and unrealizability delta (teacher component outside the student's reachable subspace, or a majority-vote hidden-unit student as in Opper-Haussler). (2) Generalized selection family: replace the boundary rule query-where-field-is-zero by a one-parameter family querying inputs at target margin m, with m=0 recovering prior work. Derive saddle-point self-consistency equations for overlap R(alpha) and generalization error as functionals of (m, eta, delta), reusing the Watkin-Rau constrained-integration machinery but retaining the noise width and an unrealizable bias term. (3) Optimal-query theorem: minimize the derivative of generalization error over m to get the optimal margin m*(eta, delta, R); prove m* increases with eta, so under noise optimal queries retreat from the boundary into a trust region. (4) Threshold theorem: derive a critical curve eta_c(alpha, delta) above which boundary selection generalizes strictly worse than random sampling, and identify a selection-induced bias term that pushes the unrealizable student into a spin-glass frozen minimum different from the passive optimum. (5) Bound: using information-theoretic and VC arguments from the Bayesian-sample-complexity source, show per-example information near the boundary is capped by the label entropy H(eta), an upper limit no query rule can beat. (6) Validation: the resulting phase diagram in (alpha, eta, delta), the m*(eta) trust region, and the eta_c reversal are testable with the same simulations that validated the original selection results, adding only noisy or mismatched teachers. Deliverable: a taxonomy of accelerate/neutral/harmful regimes plus an optimal-margin objective and threshold theorem, turning the narrow noiseless-realizable result into a scope-correct theory of active learning.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Optimization / Search] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 26 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior optimal query work relies on unrealistic assumptions. Watkin and Rau query exactly on the decision boundary, Baum synthesizes arbitrary inputs with perfect oracle labels, and Opper and Haussler and Fedorov assume noise free data and free choice of design points. But real tasks offer only a finite pool of sampled inputs, and boundary points, which are the most informative, have the noisiest labels. This trade off is uncharacterized, so reported gains may vanish in noisy pool limited settings where active learning is actually needed.

**Method.** Cast query selection as optimization over a query margin and a query budget under boundary dependent label noise and pool based inputs. Keep the Gardner and Watkin Rau perceptron setup with overlap R and generalization G, but add a noise channel where an example of stability t is mislabeled with probability peaking at the boundary, and require candidates to be drawn from the input distribution so the learner may only label points whose projection onto its current estimate lies in a chosen margin band. Extend the Watkin Rau replica analysis so the new example sits at a tunable margin rather than at zero, deriving the learning rate as a function of margin and noise. Solve for the margin that maximizes expected version space entropy reduction net of noise corruption, which predicts a nonzero optimal margin reducing to zero only in the noise free limit. Treat oracle calls as a scarce budget and derive an adaptive margin schedule maximizing final generalization for fixed budget. Implement a pool based committee algorithm that maintains a version space sample, screens candidates by expected entropy reduction discounted by a boundary distance reliability factor and an online noise estimate, greedily selects the best within budget, and re tunes the margin. Validate analytically and by simulation by comparing generalization for random sampling, boundary querying, and margin optimized pool selection across noise levels and pool sizes, testing the predicted crossover where boundary querying underperforms random sampling under noise.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Relax / Extend Scope] — claude-opus-4-8, 18 turns / 28 tool calls

**Motivation.** Prior active example-selection theory for perceptrons (Watkin and Rau 1992; the Kinzel-Rujan boundary-query scheme; Baum's query bisection) assumes a noise-free realizable teacher and unrestricted membership queries, and prescribes querying points on the decision boundary; Opper and Haussler's Bayes-optimal analysis is likewise noise-free. Yet the boundary region is exactly where labels are least reliable under margin-dependent noise and where an unrealizable student cannot track the teacher. The Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby review shows noisy and unrealizable rules give spin-glass phases and non-monotonic learning curves, but only for random examples. Thus the boundary heuristic may underperform random sampling once noise and unrealizability are admitted, and no theory says when active learning helps. Fedorov's optimal experimental design handles noise via the information matrix but is never linked to perceptron query learning. The scope mismatch is that selection theory holds only under noiseless realizable free-query assumptions that fail where sample efficiency matters most.

**Method.** Build a noise-aware active-selection theory that relaxes the noiseless realizable free-query assumptions. Model teacher and student weight vectors at large N with load alpha, adding margin-dependent output noise whose flip probability rises as the teacher field shrinks, plus unrealizable teachers. Replace boundary querying with an information-gain D-optimal criterion maximizing expected version-space-volume reduction under a noise-corrected likelihood, using the Bayesian ensemble of Tishby-Levin-Solla and Bayes-optimal prediction of Opper-Haussler. This yields an optimal nonzero query margin that trades informativeness against label reliability and reduces to the boundary rule only at zero noise. Use replica and annealed theory to compute the teacher-student overlap and generalization error versus load for random, boundary, and optimal-margin protocols, predicting a harmful-active-learning phase removed by optimal-margin querying. Extend from membership queries to finite-pool selection to quantify how the optimal margin and the gain degrade with pool size. Validate with finite-N MinOver and posterior-sampling simulations.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Robustification] — claude-opus-4-8, 13 turns / 23 tool calls

**Motivation.** Scope mismatch in query-based example-selection for perceptrons. The efficient training rules here (Watkin and Rau's self-selecting perceptrons with about 42 percent fewer wasted examples, the Kinzel/Rujan and Gardner-Derrida constructions, and Opper and Haussler's Bayes-optimal generalization analysis) all obtain their gains under narrow, unrealistic assumptions. First, noise-free labels: Watkin and Rau select each query so that J dot xi is near zero, the point of minimal stability and maximal ambiguity where the student is least confident; this maximizes information per example only if labels are trustworthy. Opper and Haussler explicitly assume noise-free examples and only remark that noise can be extended without significant change, with no analysis. Yet realistic output noise flips labels with probability rising sharply as stability z goes to zero, so the maximally informative queries are exactly the maximally corrupting ones, and the speedup is bought in the regime where the strategy is most brittle. Second, exact realizability: the analysis leans on a sharp freezing transition to perfect generalization that loses meaning under noise or mismatch. Third, synthesizable boundary-hugging queries drawn from an assumed uniform input distribution, a membership-query assumption unrealistic for real data. No source characterizes how much query speedup survives noise or offers a rule that degrades gracefully, which matters because active learning is advocated for expensive-label settings where labels are noisy and boundary-hugging is dangerous.

**Method.** Develop a noise-robust, uncertainty-aware query-selection theory for perceptron learning that robustifies the boundary-hugging rule against label noise and model mismatch, using the same replica statistical-mechanics framework as Watkin-Rau and Opper-Haussler for direct comparability. Step 1, noise model: introduce output noise with flip probability eta(z) increasing as query stability z equals J dot xi approaches zero. Recompute expected information gain per query as a function of z discounted by label reliability, so the noiseless optimum at z equal zero is replaced by a strictly positive safety margin z-star depending on noise level and current overlap R. Step 2, replica derivation: extend Watkin-Rau's order-parameter recursion, which already reads the selection variable as a Gaussian noise narrowing as R grows, to include eta(z), solving self-consistently for the query-stability distribution that maximizes robust information gain at each alpha; this yields learning curves and an optimal margin that starts far from the boundary at high noise and tightens as confidence rises. Step 3, committee robustification: replace the single-estimate margin criterion with disagreement among weights sampled from Opper-Haussler's Bayes-optimal posterior version space, querying where a Bayesian committee disagrees but the label is still likely reliable, removing dependence on a single freezing point and staying meaningful under mismatch. Step 4, realism and fallback: a pool-based variant selects, from finite real data-distributed inputs, the point whose stability is closest to z-star rather than a synthesized ideal boundary point; a mixture policy blends selected and random examples in a proportion tied to posterior uncertainty, guaranteeing performance never worse than random sampling and bounding the downside of pure boundary-hugging under noise. Step 5, validation: compare generalization curves, the freezing transition, and asymptotic error against random sampling and the original noiseless rule for Ising and spherical perceptrons across noise levels, with replica predictions checked by Monte-Carlo simulation. The target is retaining most query speedup at low noise while avoiding noise-induced collapse at moderate noise, matching random-sampling robustness while preserving active-learning efficiency. This relaxes the noiseless, realizable, and synthesizable-query assumptions and converts select where confidence is lowest into select where robust information gain is highest, reducing brittleness and downside risk.

#### [opportunity=Scope Mismatch; method=Synthesis / Unification] — claude-opus-4-8, 14 turns / 25 tool calls

**Motivation.** Active/query learning theory for neural nets (Watkin-Rau 1992; Baum 1991; Opper-Haussler 1991) assumes a noise-free oracle, a realizable target, and the thermodynamic limit, yielding results like a 42% reduction in wasted examples. But Seung-Sompolinsky-Tishby (1992) show these idealizations break under noise and unrealizable rules, where annealed approximations fail and spin-glass phases appear. Separately, Fedorov's Theory of Optimal Experiments (1972) offers a mature, noise-aware theory of informative-measurement selection, but only for linear/nonlinear regression, disconnected from the version-space/Gibbs picture, SM learning curves, and info/VC sample-complexity bounds (Haussler 1991; Tishby-Levin-Solla 1989). No framework predicts active example selection in the realistic noisy, unrealizable regime.

**Method.** Build a Bayesian information-gain framework unifying Fedorov optimal design, SM Gibbs learning curves, version-space Bayes optimality, and query heuristics. Central object: the finite-temperature Gibbs posterior P_beta(W|D), with 1/beta encoding noise/unrealizability (a soft version space). A query's value is the expected ensemble-entropy reduction, i.e. mutual information I(label(x);W|D). Reconciling limits: (a) beta to infinity collapses to Opper-Haussler's hard version space, recovering Watkin-Rau's orthogonal-query rule and Baum's queries; (b) a Laplace approximation yields a Fisher matrix, making entropy reduction equal Fedorov's D-optimal design with computable iterative algorithms; (c) cumulative information gain lower-bounds query count via Haussler's info/VC bounds. Program: redo the replica/SM learning-curve calculation with the query-generating measure set by information gain, extended to finite beta, teacher-student mismatch, and Ising/spherical perceptrons with Gardner capacity constraints. Test whether the 42% active gain survives under noise, whether optimal queries shift from boundary to interior under unrealizability, and agreement with Bayes-optimal error; validate on the perceptron analytically and Baum's depth-2 nets by simulation.
